Lecture 1

Restoring What Was Lost

We're crowded now but we won't be for long. It never fails; by the third week this will be half empty. That's true. They see no reason why they should hang around, and so they don't come. Or they wisely drop. And I have a cold which is very unphotogenic.

This is a class in religion, and it is frankly, unashamedly, unabashedly religion. The teaching is hortatory and protreptic. You know what protreptic means, don't you? It urges, it persuades, it says, "Come in my direction." Trepo, of course, is "to turn" and pro is "to turn in a direction, to go forward, to urge forward, to urge to come over here. " Protreptic is come over to this side. It's a snake oil pitch, a carnival pitch. That's protreptic or exhortatory or hortatory. Protreptic is the word that Aristotle uses. The Latin is hortatory-exhortation, that type of hortatory. We are going to try to persuade you to do something. We're going to try to twist your arm. Such courses are not to be found in public institutions and should not be because they frankly pressure people. But they do have courses elsewhere that deal with religion that is historical, and philosophical, and cultural.

I just had lunch with I.E.S. Edwards who is a very eminent Egyptologist, and, of course, you can't get out of religion in that. If they forbid the teaching of religion in public schools and have a course in Egyptology, you say, "forget it," because Egyptian is nothing but religion. But at Chicago they try to teach Egyptian without religion. (I've got to slow down, don't I?) It's like teaching Hamlet with Hamlet left out. Oh, it's deadly. I mean they won't touch it with a forty-foot pole because they don't want to get involved in anything like that, although it's a private institution. So they all have to deal with religion if the courses are historical or philosophical or cultural; it's indispensable.

But this is not that kind of religion. The gospel is not religion as it is commonly understood. It is an interesting thing that in all modern European languages, they all use the same word for religion, whether it's in Russian, or Spanish, or Dutch, or English. You don't use the native language. There is no native word. Everybody says just religion. And why is that? Why don't they appeal to the roots of their native language, their native idiom, to tell what they want? Because that would force them to define what they were thinking about, and if we just use this common word "religion," it's a great help. It's a safe word. There's no agreement on the source at all. It has been very much studied, of course. The ancients argued about where it came from. The moderns do, and they still don't agree what the source is. There are two main theses. Well, the roots are very clear. Some say there are the two: religio, religo. But it's generally agreed today that the word is religare, which is strange. What it means, of course, is to bind the ligaments, ligation, and this sort of thing-anything you bind together.

And religio means to bind back to something, to tie together with something else; and nobody knows what it is we are supposed to tie back to. St. Augustine and all had their theories about it. The one accepted today is that it always ties up with another world, or with heaven, or it ties you up with something beyond this world. You get out of this world, and so that puts it in a department by itself. You see, in any subject at all, you must deal with a thing that you can deal with-that you see, hear, handle, talk about, etc. But when you talk about religion, you've brought in an element that you are not allowed to bring in in any other subject. And yet this is the most important thing there is.

And so we borrow this safe word that comes from the Romans-religion, which we leave undefined. The Roman religion was a state religion of forms and observances, the Book of Mormon says. It was relieved by unmitigated superstition. That's a very interesting thing, you see. There was superstition, but how religious were they? It was also interesting going through hundreds and hundreds of the effects in the pockets of German soldiers, prisoners of war taken on the battlefield. That's where they're the most accessible. They will tell you anything you want. But it's important to have them turn their pockets out and show what they have. They were just loaded with charms and rabbits' feet, and all sorts of superstitious trinkets, etc. Very superstitious. In about one out of two or three hundred you would find a Bible.

In World War I, they tell me, every soldier had a Bible. It was routine for everyone to have a Bible. But in World War II they didn't have Bibles; they didn't believe in anything in particular, but were they superstitious! Did they cling to their rabbit's foot! It's a surprising thing, you see. The two aren't the same. That comes nearer to religion as we understand it there. But the Romans would accept anything under the name of religion without arguing. Ludwig Deubner in his work on Roman religion in Chantipie says it is true to say that there is not another civilization of the ancient world whose religion is not somewhere or other involved in the religious life of the Romans. They borrowed anything they could. They would just pick it up, but wouldn't take it too seriously. If it was nice they used it, etc. They had everything. Apuleius and Petronius, writings like that, remind you so much of rock stars. Those stories are about the typical Roman novella in the time of Augustus in the Golden and then in the Silver Age. They were about wandering tramps. Most novels are wandering stories. But they were typical rock stars. They would wear these outrageous outfits, etc. They were very superstitious. It was always a matter of horoscopes, and stars, and charms. Everything that happened they interpreted as having some superstitious connection that had to do with them. But their dress, their action, their morals were exactly like that particular element today, the MTV people. But you read something, as I say, in Apuleius, or better still in Petronius (a marvelous work), or any of the five satirists, and you see that that's the level of their religion. They're quite pious really, like some of their songs are quite pious. That's the nice thing about it; anything goes. Horace jokes genially about it, you know. Horace is the greatest of the satirists. Everyone is talking religion, and he says in his famous ode called Nil Admirare, "Don't get excited; don't worry about anything, the next world or anything like that."

It starts out, "Nil admirarie, not to get worried or upset. That's my religion; that's about all you can worry about. If you come over and visit me, we will have a nice time chatting. And you will find that I'm just a pig from the sty of Epicurus." Epicurus taught just enjoy yourself. There is nothing else to do. If you die, there is nothing you can do about it. If there is going to be a hereafter, oh so good; you will be that much ahead. But that was the philosophy of Epicurus, you know, and they were Epicureans. And so Horace says good naturedly, "Come over." He tells about a miracle story, and he says, "Let the Jew, Apelles, believe that."

This was the time of Augustus, you see. This was the very time that Christ was born, and anybody who believed anything was a Jew. The reason he uses Apelles is that Apelles was a famous actor. Everybody was wild about Apelles because he was a clever actor. (You see I've already started wandering from the subject; we'll get back to it.) The people at that time were obsessed by theatromania, the theater mania. Everything was spectator sports. It's TV today. But they were just as obsessed as we are today. It is a sign of ultimate decline and fall, this theatromania. All those notes I've collected on theatromania and never used I'm going to use sometime. Theater mania; theatron, of course, means "to look at, to watch, to show." We're all watchers and viewers today. Very few participants in sports or anything else, thanks to TV. They had the techniques for it too. It wasn't TV, you may have known; but they had their setups for that. But as George Wissowa, which is the standard twentieth century work on Roman religion, says, it's all public. Everything is public. And it's all cult, and it's what everybody did and everybody went along with. There was plenty of reverence.

I say this religora, it binds you to something. There's a famous work of Lucretius, De rerum natura, on the real nature of things. He says if you get to know what things really are, you'll give up religion and all its foolishness. The purpose of his writing, as he says, is to liberate the spirits of men from the bonds or ties, the nodes, but it's a knot in which you are tied. But it's the same word as this one, religio, you see. Religio is really a knot that ties people up. They can't be free; they can't think freely; therefore, by giving you a scientific picture of the world, I will liberate you, as he puts it; I'll dissolve, I'll untie the knots.

Well, suffice it to say that we have gone one way and the world the other. We're not taking that idea of religion at all. So what we begin with is this one theme. They have taken many precious things away from the scriptures, from the record. We have one book that restores them, and only one. It is not the Book of Mormon. It's not the Doctrine and Covenants. It's the Pearl of Great Price that restores the many precious things that were taken away and cause people to stumble.

I collected a number of relevant passages years ago. There are just a couple of short ones here from the Book of Mormon. 1 Ne. 13:23 . He sees the book in a vision, and he says it is very close to the Old Testament we have now, but they have taken away a lot of it and there is not much left. "The book that thou beholdest is a record of the Jews, which contains the covenants of the Lord, which he hath made unto the House of Israel and it also containeth many of the prophecies of the holy prophets;Ã‚Â…" So they had a good edition of the Old Testament. "Ã‚Â…it is a record," he goes on here, "like unto the engravings which are upon the plates of brass,Ã‚Â…" Remember the bronze plates which were brought down. That was the record that Lehi had. He says that's about the same thing. He saw that later the Gentiles would have the Bible, the Old Testament, very much like the bronze plates that his father had, except that there are not so many. We don't have even as many as he had, and lots of people came after him, you see. His friend was Jeremiah, and there was a lot written after him that wasn't recorded in the Book of Mormon. Well, "Ã‚Â…nevertheless, they contain the covenants of the Lord unto the House of Israel; wherefore, they are of great worth unto the Gentiles [as well as the Jews]." They are of great worth to everybody.

And then Professor Albright, who has talked here and has an honorary degree from here; the most distinguished biblical scholar America ever produced, William Foxwell Albright. I'll never forget what he said as we came from the airport, "My, it feels good to get away from those anemic Protestants," he said. Well, the Pearl of Great Price is not anemic. It carries a wallop. As Professor Albright puts it, "Our Hebrew texts have suffered more from losses than from glosses." Glosses are changes and corrections by scribes-alterations, notations, those things. That isn't what has damaged the Bible. It's losses, the stuff that has been removed. That's where it really suffers. And he proceeds to illustrate the point from a number of books showing he says, "Future translations will have to expand the text substantially, including some passages of great importance in their content that are missing from the Bible because they have been removed." They are being restored now.

And this is our original proposition in the Book of Mormon: "They have taken away," says Nephi, "many parts that were most precious." And these are to be restored by bringing forth other books and records, he says. Well, do we have them?

1 Ne. 13:26 : "Ã‚Â…they have taken away from the gospel of the Lamb many parts [in the New Testament, you see] which are plain and most precious; and also many covenants of the Lord have they taken away." This is all from the thirteenth chapter. And it is the great and abominable church, but remember they were all taken away before the Roman Catholic Church appeared at all. By the great and abominable church, he tells us, in 1 Ne. 22:13, that designates any who fight against Israel. It doesn't pinpoint any particular church here. Any who fight against Israel are the great abominable, he tells us. 1 Ne. 13:29 : "And After these plain and precious things were taken away, it goeth forth unto all the nations of the Gentiles;Ã‚Â…" The records didn't go forth from Palestine until after they had been plundered and the objectionable parts removed.

Now this is the result of it. Next verse: "Ã‚Â…because of these things which are taken away out of the gospel of the Lamb, an exceeding great many do stumble,Ã‚Â…" That's what happens, of course. We walk; we are able to get along, but we stumble. We don't agree on what we read.

We can't get over passages, etc. And because of this disagreement it shows that we are not walking in a straight and simple path, in the plain path, as the scripture says; in the plain way, which incidentally is a good Egyptian expression. "Today, the whole scholarly world is, by its own admission, stumbling around in the dark." I'm quoting from C.H. Dodd who has also visited here. He says, "A new integrating pattern is what we need." He wonders what can possibly be the point of the entire corpus. We've lost everything is what he is telling us here in editing that big book on the New Testament. And, Nephi tells us again here that there were certain things he was not allowed to write down because he said they would be written down by John. 1 Ne. 14:26 : "Ã‚Â…and they are sealed up to come forth in their purity, according to the truth which is in the Lamb, in the own due time of the Lord, unto the House of Israel."

Well, has that time come? Yes it has. We had a lot given us from the very first. That's in the fourteenth chapter of Nephi. So we have here these books of restoration. The most important restoration of books to date has been the Pearl of Great Price because it contains nothing but manuscripts, firsthand writings purportedly, just brief excerpts, but adequate for their purpose. Some of them are not so brief, but they are excerpts from the writings of the founders of the great dispensations of the past. Eight different writers have contributed what purport to be their original writings. Now in 1830 nobody could test anything like that, but this is what is so significant about the Pearl of Great Price now.

The Book of Mormon doesn't restore lost parts of the Bible. It contains passages not handed down in our Bible that they had from their bronze plates. You find them quoted by Abinadi and Helaman and 3 Nephi, and they've come up in documents that have been found since the Book of Mormon came forth. But its purpose was not to restore these lost passages of the Bible. They come in incidentally when Abinadi quotes the Book of Enoch. Helaman and 3 Nephi quote some marvelous passages. And it was all written after the Old Testament. Malachi was the only book that had to be added. The Old Testament had already been completed then, so the Book of Mormon is not going to give us anything new. And the great doctrinal teachings-such as those of Alma, Nephi, Ammon, Moroni, Mormon-are all direct revelations. They are not restored from old books. We're not talking about that particular theme now. These are direct revelations we have coming down. And the Jaredite story; that's very interesting because that deals with the scattering. That's one of the most interesting books we have. You notice I use the word scattering, not the dispersion. That's used of the other dispersions of Israel when it was scattered. That's the scattering of the people at the fall of the Tower of Babel when the nations were scattered at that early time after the Flood. That's a very important period, the scattering. I say it's not a diaspora. It's not the spreading abroad of Israel. The scattering is the best word to use because it distinguishes that particular period.

Now the interesting thing is, incidentally, that there is no literature from the scattering. We don't have any Jewish or Christian literature from that at all. The only book we have of the scattering is the Book of Ether in the Book of Mormon. That's from the scattering. But another interesting thing. We don't have from any holy sources books on the scattering. Yet it is the most richly documented of all subjects in the other literature, in the secular literature, of the Babylonians and the Greeks-and especially contemporary literature, I mean the old stuff, Egyptian and Babylonian. There you really get into it. They talk about it in detail. They talk about the wanderings and the scatterings all over the place; of course, the fourteenth chapter of Genesis, but that's before the Tower of Babel. This is the big scattering we are talking about. So again the Book of Mormon is unique there, but this is not what we are talking about. It doesn't restore what was lost from the Bible because that story never was told in the Bible. That's one by itself; it is peculiar. Likewise, the Doctrine and Covenants is for the modern church. In your own time sections have been added to the Doctrine and Covenants from the vision of Joseph F. Smith. But it is the Pearl of Great Price alone to date that restores precious parts that were removed from the scriptures.

Now, how do you know that anything has been taken out of the Bible? Well, the genizas are good example of that. We used to say a lot about genizas in Book of Mormon courses especially. You know what a magazine is. It comes from makhzan or makhza from the root "to store." We put things in a magazine. It is the same root. But a geniza is a peculiar institution, a Jewish institution. You are not supposed to destroy a text of the Old Testament, the holy writings. The copies were used in the synagogues, and when they were used up and worn out they had to be replaced. But you could never write a word from memory. You had to copy down exactly as it was there word for word. So it was actually the continuation of the same manuscript. There was no loss whatever. But what would you do with the old manuscript? It couldn't be burned. It couldn't be thrown away. It couldn't be cut up. So what you did is make a geniza. That was a hole in the wall (and there are many of these) of the synagogue, in the mud or brick or stone. You put the old text in there and you covered it up. In the beginning of this century, Paul Kahle and others started looking for genizas. The Damascus fragment was discovered, which belongs to the Dead Sea Scrolls, which indicated that these things were there. They started tapping the walls around old synagogues in Cairo. Cairo is a very important one because it produced a valuable geniza. And in Romania and all the places where there had been old synagogues. There are some very old ones scattered all over the place. Many of them had been converted to mosques, you see, but the walls were still intact. And so by hearing the hollow sound you could come out with a text that was a thousand or two thousand years older than any text that had been known. Then you could see if they had altered or changed anything. So the Cairo Geniza is perhaps the most valuable. It serves the same purpose as the Dead Sea Scrolls. They were salted away in caves. Here you have the cave number four, which is the most interesting when you go in there.

First, they made careful copies on new clean sheets of parchment or sometimes papyrus. Usually it was parchment. Then they rolled them up carefully and put them in a linen bag. They tied the bag up and covered it with pitch, just as if there was a funeral and someone was being buried.

Incidentally, the Ascension of Moses confirms this. Then they were put into specially made jars. You've seen pictures of these Dead Sea Scrolls jars with a lid that seals hermetically. They put lead or pitch around the top and sealed it hermetically. Then the jars were lined up at the back of a dry cave in the Judean desert where it never rains. It's as dry as Death Valley. Then the jars were covered with dry sand. Then it would be preserved until a later time, as the texts tell us themselves,"until the due time of the Lord when these things shall come forth again." It was thought formerly that they never did anything like this. I used to teach for four years at Scripps College before the war, and shared a class, the junior humanities, with Edgar Goodspeed who had retired from the University of Chicago. He had come out there to teach in California where it was nice. So we shared this junior humanities class. He'd have it one day of the week and I would have it the next. He insisted at that time that nothing was written in the New Testament before the fourth century. The New Testament was not an ancient book at all. It didn't go back to the apostles in the time of Christ because they didn't have writing. The Jews didn't write at the time of Christ. According to him, there was no writing at that time. And then he died, and a month later they started discovering the Dead Sea Scrolls. Ten thousand different documents have been found, all written by Jews at the time of Christ in a very flowing, free hand. Scriptures and all sorts of things, tremendous treasures, came out all of a sudden. They didn't think it was so, but all of sudden there it was.

So we know that things have been taken out of the Bible now. It's a remarkable thing. One of the very first discovered was a text of Isaiah, which, of course, was the most important. They thought more of that, just as we do. It's the number one Old Testament prophet and text. It has more to do with the restoration of the gospel. This text was complete and in perfect condition. We still have it. I have a photographic copy of it. The interesting thing, though, is that in three thousand places it reads differently from the Masoretic text, the Hebrew text, we have now. Nonetheless, nothing really basic has been changed. It was handed on with remarkable faithfulness and veracity, and it's a correct and reliable document. Two thousand years didn't spoil it at all. Until the 1950s, about 1951, the oldest text we had of the Old Testament was a ninth century text. We didn't have anything older than ninth century A.D. Here was a text a thousand years older than any other text of the Bible to tell us what had been lost and what had been changed. Now, remember that over three hundred caves have been found with documents in them, all over the place. Just imagine, before then nobody knew there was anything at all there. But this answers the question, how do we know things have been taken out of the Bible. You can find out there.

How do we know they were precious? Now, Catholics and Protestants insist that anything really important was never lost. Of course, they have to insist that because if something really was lost, they would be in an awkward situation. They don't believe in revelation, and a serious loss would mean that the present-day teachings are to be called into question. How do they know their interpretations are correct? The Jesuit formula is what you preach, preach from the housetops. They say, "See, the Lord told the disciples to preach from the housetops. No secrets, nothing held behind."

And yet when he met with them behind closed doors, he said, "Ã‚Â…it is given unto you to know the mysteries of the kingdom of heaven, but to them it is not given.Ã‚Â…Therefore, speak I to them in parables:Ã‚Â…(Matthew 13:11,13). Well, I have collected and written extensively on that subject of the reticence of the apostles and the teachings that were held back-and they were always the most important ones. Of course, as the Lord says, you do not cast your pearls before swine or your dessert to the dogs. This was not a term of contempt for the animals at all, but the pigs wouldn't have any particular use for pearls. And if you give your dessert to the dog (he uses the word kunarion, a word of affection for your pet puppy dog), it will make him sick. It's not for him; he can't digest it. So these things are held back.

Well, that's very clear here. Now you can tell. These documents that come forth show us that a great deal was not divulged by the apostles. Remember, in 1948, the same time the Dead Sea Scrolls were discovered, the Nag Hammadi texts were also discovered. The Dead Sea Scrolls were discovered at Qumran and the others at Nag Hammadi, the blessed female camel. This is on the middle Nile. Seven years ago two of our men, Kent Brown and Wilfred Griggs, went back and dug a couple of seasons in the Nag Hammadi site. We don't really know where the texts were found. They turned up in the markets. Peasants found them, and they had been burning these documents to heat their huts for untold years. They were found in much the same condition as the Dead Sea Scrolls, buried in the same way. They go back to the first or second century, and they are in book form-real books with leather bindings and all the rest going way back. They are codices. And they just found one new codex, not a very big one. It's supposed to be a James. Well, there are other Jameses in there-the writings of James going way back to the time of Christ. I hear it's up for sale for three million dollars. If you want to go over to the Bookstore, they will give you a slight reduction.

You see, these things are still coming up. But these things were not all preached from the housetops. They were precious, and they were held back. The more precious, the more they were held back because you are not to kick these things around. They are not to be made known to everybody. That's why we keep the temples closed to the general public, not that people can't find out what goes on if they make any effort at all. But that isn't the point at all. The point is that you keep your own private integrity, and these things are not to be made what the Jews called hellal. There's a lot in the Old Testament about that. You must not make these things hellal. It means vulgar, for regular discussion, etc. because what does it do? Well, it distorts things. Everybody starts arguing about it. They give their own interpretations. Then things become distorted, and they are treated as trivia, subjects for popular conversation, or movies, or anything else. That's what hellal means. It means defiled by regular public use. It has lost its sacred nature.

Well, how highly valued were they in that case? The answer is that they were valued highly enough that people fought furiously to defend them from being removed from the scriptures. There was a tremendous fight about that. Fight against whom? Who wanted to remove them? Well, the swarming sectary teachers in the second century.

Everybody claimed that he had the only teaching that was given to the apostles. This is what the Gnostics did, you see. Secretly, after the resurrection, the Lord came and taught the apostles, and Eusebius tells us-quoting Hegesippus, a very early historian-that as soon as the last eye witness to the old church had died, then all these people who had been lying low came out of the woodwork. They all claimed they had the original teachings, the secret teachings that had been passed by. These are who the Gnostics were. They claimed to have the secret knowledge, the secret teaching which Gnosticism is. Everybody had his own version and claimed to be the only one. The same thing happens later in Free Masonry. They didn't want these things. Why did they want them out? Why did they want them to be done away with? The pretenders.

And there were no winners in this fight. The winner, of course, was the one who was taken over by the schools that had the big backing, the big money, etc. And some of them were pretty good people-smart, serious and conscientious. But why did the pretenders want these precious things to be done away with? Because they were afraid of them. Why were they afraid of them? Because they deal with the terrible questions. And I'll say a good deal about the terrible questions here. Why the terrible questions? Because they were fundamental and they had no answers to them. Why didn't they have any answers? Because there was no revelation. Why no revelation? Well, let's go back to our I Nephi, the passage we started out with. And Moroni is good on that. Yes, the last book in the Book of Mormon tells us why they were having no more revelation. And don't think they didn't miss it. They just were wild. They did anything they could to get revelation. They used drugs and hypnosis and everything else. There are good accounts of that in Irenaeus and Epiphanius. But right at the end here, he says, "And I would exhort you my beloved brethren, that ye remember that he is the same yesterday, today, and forever, and that all these gifts of which I have spoken, which are spiritual, never will be done away, even as long as the world shall stand, only according to the unbelief of the children of men (Moro. 10:19 )." If they start misbehaving themselves and not believing, these things will become lost. And then he tells us in verse 24 here. "And now I speak unto all the ends of the earth-that if the day cometh that the power of the gifts of God shall be done away among you, it shall be because of unbelief. And wo be unto the children of men if this be the case; [and this is a marvelous statement here]for there shall be none that doeth good among you, no not one."

In the oldest and best account of the First Vision that we have, what the Lord said to Joseph right at the beginning of the conversation was, "Behold, the world at this time lieth in sin, and there is none that doeth good, no not one. And mine anger is kindling against the inhabitants of the earth to visit them according to this ungodliness." Now we are going to see the results of that.

So, many precious things were taken away from the Old Testament as from the New Testament. For example, the Pearl of Great Price is such a clear-cut case. R.H. Charles who was the principal editor of the Book of Enoch for many years, says that nearly all writers of the New Testament were familiar with it.

He discovered that there were no less than 128 quotations in the New Testament from the Book of Enoch. But the world didn't have any Book of Enoch. What had happened to it? It is quoted as a genuine production of Enoch by Jude in the New Testament and as scripture by Barnabas, one of the seven apostolic fathers. And with the early fathers and apologists it had all the weight of a canonical book. It was scripture as far as the early church was concerned. It was included in the Bible and belonged there, but there is not a trace of it there now.

But when the men of the schools became the leaders of the church, accommodating to the contemporary philosophy, there were many things in Enoch that they did not like. Charles himself doesn't like them; he puts it as tactfully as possible here. He says, "But our book contained much of a questionable character." This was the fourth century, of course. That was the time when the University of Alexandria won a complete victory over everything. That's the Athanasian Creed. The Book of Enoch fell into discredit. Under the ban of such authorities as Hilary, Jerome, and Augustine (three of the four Latin doctors of the church-their highest authority), it gradually became lost. Notice, they banned it. They would not allow people to use the Book of Enoch, and they were the authorities. It was Hilary who made more decisions than anybody else. He said that if a thing isn't found in the scripture, you can be sure that it never happened. Unless a thing is mentioned in the Bible, it didn't happen. Now that is absolute dependence on the infallibility of the scriptures.

Joseph Smith started elaborating and bringing all these other things in, and it offended people sorely. It was the worst offense he could possibly have made. "Why do we need all this stuff?" they say. I've heard members of the Church say the same thing. But under their ban it fell into discredit and became lost to the knowledge of western Christendom until over a century ago when Sir James Bruce brought some copies back from his expedition to the sources of the Blue Nile, which he actually discovered. He brought fragments of the Book of Enoch back with some big texts, beautiful ones. And nobody knew anything about it before that. It was lost completely. But the early church treasured it as a regular scripture. It is quoted in the Book of Mormon too. This is an interesting thing, you see, because it wasn't known at that time.

But the objection to it when it came back in our own time was almost as strong as in ancient times. What was the reason for this? R. H. Charles says that the law had claimed to be the highest and final word from God. They could tolerate no fresh message from God. Again, you can see why. We have to regard the Bible as complete, final and total without one word of error because as soon as we admit that there might be one verse wrong here and there, or something incorrect, then we are in the soup because we never know when a verse is really telling us what we are supposed to learn. Is it right or is it wrong? This leads to all sorts of stuff. After all you have eight thousand ancient manuscripts of the New Testament, and no two are alike. Now which one are you going to follow? Well, you start introducing things like that and you are in trouble. So there are many precious things to be restored.

The Latter-day Saints have never realized what they have here. Neglect, I think, is an understatement as far as that goes. When the Joseph Smith papyri were acquired by the Church in 1967, a high authority, a relative of my wife, in fact, who was editor of the Deseret News at that time, said, "Why did Joseph Smith do that crazy thing? Why does he have to get us involved in all this Egyptian stuff?" Why does that bother us? His idea of what was important and news was a new mall or a fashion salon opened in ZCMI or something like that. Now that's news, but why are we bothered with all this ancient stuff?

And another thing is that we have always treated it as something that can be tossed off in three easy lessons. It's only sixty pages long. When I first came to BYU, many professors felt that you couldn't possibly find enough in the Pearl of Great Price to fill a whole quarter of instruction. So they divided it up with the Doctrine and Covenants. I see that we haven't taken it seriously. That is an understatement; contempt is a better word.

But we must affirm the gospel on two fronts, and this is where it's particularly valuable. The removal of the precious things by the teachers who didn't like them plunged students of the scriptures into endless theological argument. Because of the removal of the precious things, they do stumble. A very interesting case of that is Gregory the Great in 600 A.D. He was a great man and was bishop of Rome at that time. He was also called Pater Superstitionum, the Father of Superstitions. Well, why? Because he added all sorts of things that were not in there before. He was the one that made official the doctrine of purgatory, igneus pergatorius, the purgatorial fires. He noticed that people weren't completely good or completely bad. The idea was very simply that when you die you go straight to heaven or straight to hell. There's no place in between. This was the teaching of the Alexandrian School that was simplifying everything. People aren't one hundred percent good or one hundred percent bad. We are all in between. That's why we all need repentance. It's not the good guys and the bad guys-that syndrome we have today. That simplifies things, doesn't it?

So in between was purgatory where you go to be tested and tried. This was a teaching of the early church, but it wasn't purgatory there. The fires and flames purge out all impurities and sin acquired in life as Hamlet's father said. Then you are ready to pass on because you have been purged. There's why it was called the purgatorium. Early Christians had the same thing, but they didn't call it purgatory. They called it the anapausus, the frigidarium. The Egyptians use much the same expression. There's a very eloquent picture of it up in the museum now in the Ramses exhibit. 1 Anapausus means pause a little, take a little rest. After this life, you go to a place where you rest and take a breather. No judgment or anything at that time. But if you haven't been very good, you're going to worry at that time just the same. Alma makes it very clear when he talks about that to his son, as you know. The Roman term for it was refrigarium, the refrigerator, the place where you cool off, rest and relax. It was a green room. Just as we pass into this world through a Garden of Eden, we pass out of it through a garden into another world.

To soften the culture shock, you go through this state. There are some very interesting writings on that. No one pays any attention to them.

But notice that Gregory way along in 600 not only introduced that, he introduced all sorts of doctrines. They started paying more attention to miracles. They started having masses for the dead. He's the first person who introduced that. Why? Because there were great gaps. People asked questions, and there was no answer they could get from the Bible. Why not? Because they had removed all those parts. It all comes back to us now in these later documents, you see, what he was trying to supplant.

But that isn't the big thing now; that isn't the thing we value the Pearl of Great Price for. What is this big brouhaha, the scientific one, that has come forth? It burst out with particular fury in 1859 and after. That's when The Origin of the Species was published, and from then on to this very day, that has been the big issue. Why? Again because precious things have been taken out of the scriptures. What is the merit of The Pearl of Great Price? It has restored those precious things to the scriptures that will answer about any question you can ask about prehistoric man, time schedules of millions of years, and all that sort of thing. You would be surprised how well it handles them.

So if we had some of the precious things, it might be a different story, we might say. We wouldn't have to have this argument, this evolutionary conflict and the like. But we do have the precious things in The Pearl of Great Price, so we do tell a different story. And as a result of that we do get ourselves into trouble with the world. A good example right now of what The Pearl of Great Price has given us is that movie called "The God Makers." It just shows how offensively out of line our teachings are with conventional Christianity which wants to play it safe.

I'm going to use Hempt now. Now the purpose of religion, as I said, is to answer the terrible questions, so-called. They still bear that name. And the best definition I know of is found here in the Clementine Recognitions, a very old work. This is the first volume of the Patrologia, published in 1857. This is a battered one. We have the Patrologia here, about one thousand volumes of the writings of the early fathers. The purpose of the Patrologia, which was sponsored by the Vatican many years ago, was to publish every scrap of early Christian literature there was in chronological order. What an advantage that would be. It would be the earliest writings of all, and the earliest writing in them is where we begin here. This first volume is the writings of the so-called Clementines. Clement was the first, some say the third, bishop of Rome. The Recognitions is a novel, the first Christian novel. It is very early in the second century. It is called Recognitions because it deals with a very popular form in the ancient world. As the ancient world began to collapse (and it is so much like our own), the recognition became the most moving form of literature, and it became very common. It becomes the main theme in the comedy of manners, in the Latin comedy of Plautus and Terence, taken over from Menander, the Greek popular writer.

These were the popular plays, the popular TV soaps. But they always dealt with recognition. Families are scattered and they come together and recognize each other-the glad moment of recognition. Always moving, a real tear jerker. The parted lovers meet in the end. And so they were called the Recognitions.

This one is about a family that was scattered and was brought together by the church. This was at the time of Christ. How easily they were scattered because life was so insecure. There was no security for anybody, not even the very rich. They were in greater danger than anybody as you read from Varro. There was no defense against terrorism. And since that has become a theme in our day, let me refer you to the beginning of his great work De re rustica, on farming. In the days of the Republic before the Empire came in, a great land owner by the name of Varro wrote a book on farming. It begins with a number of exceedingly rich men, met in the lobby of an office building in Rome. There is a large map of Italy on the wall. They start pointing out their various holdings in it. One man says very casually, "This is my river." Another says, "This is my range of mountains here." The lands were worked by sharecroppers who were cheaper than slaves because they wouldn't have to feed them. The sharecroppers would have to feed themselves. They were the old Roman soldiers.

The wealthy men are waiting for the big man to come before the consultation can begin. He is the chairman of the board. They wait and get impatient as they talk about their business affairs. A servant comes in very upset and says, "I'm sorry, he has been murdered." They ask what happened, and the servant says, "Well he was going through the crowd with his attendants, and one of the little knife people came out of the crowd and slipped a knife into him and said, 'Oh, my, I've made a mistake; I'm sorry.' Then he ran off and disappeared into the crowd."

He had assassinated the wrong man. But here the richest man in Rome couldn't defend himself. That's what happens when you have terrorists. So if you think we are living in new times.Ã‚Â…What did they do about it? They appointed a man called Augustus who was very smart and very capable. He helped things out some. The Roman history is a very interesting one because it parallels our own.

Well, now we get to the terrible questions and the Clementine Recognitions which take us right to The Pearl of Great Price. We will continue with that the next time. [p.1]

^The Ramses II exhibit from Egypt was in the Bean Museum on BYU campus when Dr. Nibley taught this class.

Lecture 2

Allegory and Rhetoric

This is the latest I have ever been, but if you are in a conversation with Professor Edwards and he starts reminiscing, when do you break off? He's good though. He's the only man alive who knew all the great ones in the early days of Egyptology.

Now we brought up this proposition that The Pearl of Great Price restores lost teachings of the gospel, the lack of which had caused everybody to stumble. (Is this a Turkish bath in here, or something? I can't see anybody.) Now as a result of these lost parts, we are told that there was great disagreement as to what the scriptures meant, and this has kept the world in turmoil for centuries. After the mid-nineteenth century, an issue largely ignored by laymen (before then it had always come up among the doctors of the schools), became the main object of controversy.

So we have these two, the doctrinal differences and the scientific ones. The question now is whether the scriptures have any value at all. First, it was how to interpret them. Now, do they have any value at all when we are dealing with the real facts of life, besides moral value, of course? The teachings would have to be restored on the basis of scientific reality or dropped entirely. This is what they interpreted. From the second century on, the schoolmen had accommodated their teachings to the teachings of the schools. It was one long, undignified retreat. Every time the school, that is the latest science of the time, changed its opinion, the theologians would change theirs to match it. It has been going on ever since then.

So the teachings of the schools were completely dominated by rhetoric. Anciently, they were the schools of rhetoric. Success schools, business schools, is what they all turned out to be. That's another story, but it's a very important one. Now the first casualty of this loss, of course, was literalism, given rights in the decline and fall. This is the situation. The most learned of the fathers by a very singular condescension have imprudently admitted the sophistry of the Gnostics, acknowledging that the literal sense is repugnant for every principle of faith as well as reason. You must never take anything literally in the Bible. They deem themselves secure and invulnerable behind the ample veil of allegory. You say a thing one way, you mean it another way. Allegory is different meanings. The classic example from the scriptures is where they speak of God coming and going. It can't possibly mean that. It's given another interpretation. It doesn't mean that he has eyes; it just means there's an awareness there. He can't be like us in any sense. When it says we are in His image, they would say that's allegorical. The very first of the fathers that started this, were men like Origen and Irenaeus Their favorite term was the "visible image of the invisible God." He is in the image of man, but he is invisible. Of course, an image is something that you can see. But an invisible image is what God is, they claimed. It's like saying soundless music or odorless perfume. It's a contradiction of terms to say an invisible image. Behind the veil of allegory is where they have lived ever since. Joseph Smith came along and gave us a very different story. He says he really saw the angel and gives us a clinical account, how he looked, where he stood, etc., just like the first chapter of Luke.

Well, there was a limit here. Allegory could not explain what we have mentioned before, the terrible questions. We go to the first volume of the Patrologia for that. What made them terrible was the insistence on a literal answer. You have to give me a straight, literal answer to this. Allegory can mean what you say it means. We started with the story of Clement in Rome when he was a young fellow. He was the first and oldest writer after the New Testament. We mentioned before there's this great collection, the Patrologia. We have them all, along with the Oriental Patrologia and others. It's over a thousand volumes. It's all the writings of the early church in chronological order, and number one is our friend Clement. He's going to be very important here. He tells us his story. And we mentioned that it was a recognition. This was a very common theme and reflects the conditions of the world. We have this today. It began with the new comedy, second and third centuries B.C., with Menander and was picked up by the Romans who made a big thing of it (Plautus and Terence). It was the story of families that had been broken up, and then they recognized each other. Recognition is a big tear-jerker, as you know. The breaking up is interesting, how kids were constantly being kidnapped, little children in crowds at festivals. The new comedy was picked up by Shakespeare. That's The Comedy of Errors. It was simply taken from Plautus. It's the two twins separated at different times. Shipwreck was a common one. There were lots of merchants in it, lots of traveling, the recognition by the locket. This was all through the Middle Ages, these tokens of recognition. It was the standard theme of the blood and thunder operas. A spoof by Gilbert and Sullivan makes a big thing of the recognition. The babies got mixed up at birth, and as a result the captain should have been Jack Tarr, and Jack Tarr should have been the captain. But they were twins and the nurse got them mixed up. They were separated, and you recognize them by the letter, or the token, or the wrappings, or things like that. It gives a picture of a restless world. We talked about theatromania. They were always having these big shows, big markets, etc., and children were always getting swiped. Just as people were always getting assassinated, as we mentioned last time Varro's Re rustica.

A child in a crowd might easily disappear, and it happened all the time. They were sold as slaves or in the thriving international sex business. It was very much like today, and it got worse and worse. Finally, the whole thing collapsed. But this was a recognition of a family that was broken up and the way they found themselves together. We have stories of this in our church history. Sooner or later they all joined the church, and that brought them all together. They never would have found each other if they hadn't independently joined the church. So he starts out telling his story, and it's a very important one because this tells us what the terrible questions are that only our Pearl of Great Price can answer.

So he starts out like, "I, Nephi, having been born of goodly parents." He writes, "Ego Clemens in urba Roma natus. I, Clement, born in the city of Rome, from my earliest age was a studious child and constantly concerned with certain questions. From my boyhood, I could not break myself away from thinking about these things."

It's quite common. Questionnaires show that the part of the population that worries most about nuclear war is small children. Children six, seven and eight years old worry all the time about it, which surprised the experts. Well, Clement was always worried about the big things. He says, "I don't know exactly where I started doing that or where the idea came from, but I worried about it all the time. My mortal condition bothered me." And these were the questions. First of all the big one: "whether I would have any life after death at all." If you have answered that, you have answered an awful lot, and if you can't, then you are "in the soup." You have nothing much to look forward to, and it becomes very discouraging. But this is the big question we find that nobody can escape from. You think you can, but you can't. We will refer to that later.

Well, that led to another question, a corollary of that. Well, if I live after death, will I remember? Will I be myself after death, or would I turn into some other creature? Transmigration. What's the point of living after death if you cease to be yourself and start to be somebody else? That is part of many religions, as you know, and that wouldn't satisfy him at all. So whether there is any life for me at all after death; and secondly, after I leave will I be the same person? Well, Clement plainly and simply says, "Would I have any memory of this life after, or would I turn into another person entirely?" And the third one was, "or would I forget absolutely everything? Would I simply be going into complete oblivion?" Shakespeare's last stage of all is "second childishness and mere oblivion. "Sans teeth, sans eyes, sans taste, sans everything." The last of the seven ages of man is "his second childhood and mere oblivion." Well, the next step from that is into nothing at all, you see. Shakespeare dwells morbidly on that in As You Like It. But this worries him, oblivion and silence to look forward to. Or would I probably remember something of this life?

Well, along with this are the conditions which continued existence implies and requires; namely, you have to have a physical substratum, cosmology. So the next question he asks is the normal one: "quando factus hic mundus. When was the earth created?" Was it created? And what existed before that? Was there anything before the earth? Notice he goes into questions of cosmology from questions that seem to be theology. But if you are going to live hereafter, this is literalism. It has to be literal, first of all. He is going to tell us that the doctors of the church could give him beautiful allegories, but that didn't mean a thing. As the first apologist of the church says, "You use these things, but to us Christians they mean nothing. A thing is either what you say it is, or it isn't."

What was the world, and what was there before it? It must pass away too if it was created out of nothing. When would that be? Could it exist further?

Is that something completely beyond our thought, so we just have to drop it and not think about it. He said he would like to have forgotten it, but you can't forget about it. As A. E. Housman says, "When men betimes are sober, they think by fits and starts. And when they think, they fasten their hands upon their hearts." If you didn't have to think about it, that would be fine, but you can't escape from it.

Well, he worried about this. He started to lose weight. He got sick, just from brooding about the big one. It dogged him all the time. "My passion, my desire for this immortality just wouldn't let me alone. It wouldn't let my mind be quiet," he says. He was going around this way, and he went to the schools. His family was wealthy, so he visited the schools of the philosophers to find an answer. "I visited the philosophers' schools, and what did I hear there? Nothing but dogmatic assertions and rebuttals, and nothing but a constant wrangling and display of wits. Always displaying their skill in syllogisms, binding words together. If A equals B, B equals C, therefore God is at the end of a chain of syllogisms. And the main purpose was to show off, he says. Everyone showing his versatility. That was what they got paid for, and that's what they did. But they never really came to grips with the subject. They were always giving opinions and definitions, but what about the things themselves? He said it had nothing to do with that. "It wasn't to get into the real nature of things, but simply to show the cleverness of the defenders," he said. The schools didn't please him at all because were they really asking those questions? We're not supposed to ask them. That's really the only question we ask if we have any science, art, literature, which all try to get a picture of the world. We'll see that later.

Well, what did he do then? He started to worry about hell. This is the old Roman hell, the pagan hell. Incidentally, this is at the time of Christ because he is living in the time of the Emperor Tiberius. This is the Golden Age of Rome. Very prosperous, he can have anything he wants; but he is just worried sick. Everybody was worried sick. They started importing all sorts of religions because the state religion didn't satisfy people very much.

But what about going to hell? He heard about that. He heard these stories about pyriphlegethon, the fiery flood; and Tartarus, and Sisyphus and Tityus being tormented there. And Tantalus. You know his story. Any way he looked at it, it was scary. In Shakespeare's play Measure for Measure (which they are putting on here this spring), when Claudio doesn't want to be executed, he expresses his objections to his sister and says,

This sensible warm motion to become

A kneaded clod; and the delighted spirit

To bathe in fiery floods, or to reside

In thrilling region of thick-ribbed ice;

You see there's Dante. The hell is ice there. It's not hot. Of course, the conventional Roman hell was fire.

To be imprisoned in the viewless winds,

And blown with restless violence round about

The pendent worldÃ‚Â…

Remember Francesca da Rimini; that was their punishment. Satan is the prince of the air. His great curse, according to very early tradition, is that he can't put his feet anywhere. He has no solid ground. He has no body. This can be very annoying. It can give you vertigo.

Ã‚Â…or to be worse than worst

Of those that lawless and incertain thoughts

Imagine howling!

Notice this is purgatory. He recognizes that it's lawless. It's not in the scripture, and it's uncertain. You can't be sure about purgatory. And it has people howling. Remember Hamlet says, "You churlish priest, a ministering angel shall my sister be when thou liest howling." Then it goes on:

-'tis too horrible!

The weariest and most loathed worldly life

That age, ache, penury, and imprisonment

Can lay on nature is a paradise

To what we fear of death.

It's the worst thing you can possibly think of. So this is what scares the daylights out of him, and this is what he was worried about. What about answering these questions? Philosophy wouldn't touch them at all. And remember that shortly after this, Christian theology went completely over into philosophy. You couldn't be a bishop unless you had been trained in the rhetorical schools and knew all the tricks. That very soon became the rule.

Anyway he goes on here. These worried him. He says, "I knew they were just fairy stories, but there was just a chance. If they weren't true, then couldn't I be free to go out and have some fun?" Why behave in that case? Remember in The Book of Mormon Korihor based his whole doctrine on this one thesis that when the man dies, that is the end thereof. Therefore, they lifted up their heads and rejoiced in their wickedness. There was nothing to restrain them. It was just a moral teaching, anyway. When a man dies that is the end thereof, and all of Korihor's mischief evolves from that.

Well, Clement didn't want to misbehave. "What should I do?" he says. Then he decided that he would go to Egypt. He had money, so he went to Egypt. And then he decided to make friends with the hierants and the prophets in the temples there and go around and inquire.

They would be perfectly willing for money to do something for him. They were very indigent in the temples at that time. He would pay them to invite some spirit from the other world and then he would know that there is something hereafter. "They can teach me something about the hereafter. I know the Egyptians are always talking about it," he says. "That will at least solve the one question I want to know, whether the spirit is immortal. And I don't mean just in the spirit. I wanted to see if they could let me hear something and see something. Then I would be sure." But merely to talk about it as philosophy wouldn't satisfy him, he said. So he decided to go.

He had a good friend who was in the philosophy school, and he said, "Don't do that, whatever you do. It would be a very dangerous thing." He told him, "If nothing happens, then you will be desperate. If you don't go, then there is always the possibility that there might be something. Just leave it at that and don't do it."

He goes on with the story. He says at that time news and rumor were going around the city. It was being evangelized that the kingdom of God had appeared in Judea somewhere and there was a prophet preaching there, and that he backed up his preachings and claims by most singular prodigies and wonders. These rumors grew, and he didn't know what to make of them. He paid no attention until one day there was a street meeting. Right at the most busy crossroads in the center of Rome, here was this character preaching to all the people standing around. He was proclaiming that the Son of God had appeared in the region of Judea, promising eternal life to those who would listen to him-that he had been sent from God the Father, etc. Clement said that he spoke about matters both temporal and eternal. That was Barnabas who had come on a mission to Rome. There were some students from the university hanging around, and they started making fun of him because he spoke with a Jewish accent. They were wits, so they laughed at him and exercised some syllogisms. For example, why did God make a flea with eight legs when he made a huge elephant with only four legs to hold it up? Wouldn't that be silly of God? Then they would all roar and laugh.

Barnabas said, "Well, I could answer you and I'd like to argue with you, but I have been sent on a mission here. I can only teach what I have come to teach, and I should not be distracted. You can listen to it or you can leave it alone, but that's what I have to do."

Then they started yelling, and somebody threw rocks, and there started to be a mob. So Clement grabbed Barnabas by the hand, he says here, and dragged him into an alley nearby and they ran and got away from the mob. Then he took him home. Barnabas was very much discouraged because he hadn't had any success on his mission in Rome at all. He had to go back to a general conference which was being held in Caesarea. Clement said, "I can't go with you now. I have some business affairs to settle, but as soon as I do, I'll take a ship and follow you. Caesarea, you say; all right I'll go there."

There's a fascinating description of what the conference was like there. It's not like the other literature at all. I mean the typical church literature, miracle literature. Peter is a very real character. He takes a swim in the ocean every morning, and he has a very good sense of humor. Clement wants to see him more than through the crowd. He finally gets a conversation with Peter who tells him some wonderful things. Peter answers the questions that he had when he started out. First, he says, "What about my father and mother? They haven't heard this; will they be damned because they never had the chance?" Peter said, "Now, you are asking me to tell you things that you will learn later on, but I'm not going to tell you about now. You will understand what our work for the dead is like when we get to that part." And Peter satisfies Clement's mind on all things.

These are the questions he asks, you see. And so we start out this one with the literalism. I have arranged here, which I have never used before, in chronological order this argument. We only need to touch it at a few points to show what this is. Namely, the things to watch are this. The early church was very literal minded. The doctors hated it and got rid of it. And then they had to restore a lot, but they restored it in a different department. They brought it back again after the manner of the schools. It has always been changing since. But the main thing was to keep it allegorical so that we would be safe, as Gibbon says, behind the broad veil of allegory.

One of the first things Peter says to Clement is this: "We affirm there is absolutely nothing evil in matter." Because this was the doctrine, you see. God had to be immaterial and had no contact with material at all. This was Neoplatonism, not Platonism. Plato was different. But Neoplatonism was that matter is bad and evil, and spirit is everything that isn't matter. So the only definition that they ever gave of God was the asomaton, "that which has no body." The bodyless one was God. That was all you could say about him, as Origen says. And you could see they had a legitimate reason for regarding material and the flesh as loathsome because that's the way everybody was behaving in those days. It was a period of decline, and they took it for granted that if people had bodies they would misbehave and do anything that felt good. That's what they were doing, and we have a rich literature on that, of course. The idea was that anything material was absolutely defiling; it was vicious. So you get to the Neoplatonists with the doctrine that even any contact with matter would defile God himself. Well, how could God have made the universe then? He made a physical world, after all. Oh no, that was made by the thirty Eons. So there are thirty steps between God and the final creator. And that was a mistake, you see. That was Sophia. The final Eon was Sophia, and she misbehaved. Being female, she wanted to rule things. She went wild in space, and the Lord gave her no more support. She had a terrible abortion and brought forth a lot of filthy matter, and from that the world was made. So God is not responsible for this world, not at all. He is pure unmatter, according to Neoplatonists. They are rabid on that particular subject. That is why it is so revolutionary when Peter says, "We affirm absolutely that there is nothing evil in matter," thus countering the favorite claim of the Neoplatonists and Gnostics.

The earliest Christian apologist, Aristides, will not waste his time arguing about abstractions, he says. "We Christians simply will not accept any allegorical explanations." That's all they use in the Middle Ages later on. There are the four interpretations of things that Thomas Aquinas gives us. There are the seven trophies of Tyconius, the rhetorical devices used in the schools, which Saint Augustine says are the keys to Christian preaching. We adopt those. We adopt the method of rhetoric preaching, using all sorts of tricks. The seven means of saying something when you mean something else. These become the principal tools of Christian teaching, according to St. Augustine, the greatest of all Christian teachers, both for Protestants and Catholics. And he used them certainly. That's in his great work called De Doctrina Christiana, "The Teaching of the Christian Faith." But before him Aristides said, "We just will not accept allegorical explanations. They are simply myths and nothing else, because you make them up."

But the next apologist to follow him was the famous Justin Martyr, the first person to join the church who was a real first-class philosopher from the outside. They were very proud to get him. He never laid aside his philosopher's robe that he acquired in the schools. He was the first philosophical martyr. He's called a martyr for that reason. But he had grown up in the schools. And he turns the whole thing around. He insists that the Christians have as much right to allegory as the Greek myths do. "We have as much right to the myths as you do. It's really the Greeks who taint their allegory with a touch of the physical," he says. "The Greeks imply that there might be something physical in their myths where there's nothing physical in any of ours," he said. So he had shifted from the days of Aristides.

Some influential teachers who followed wanted to have it both ways. Early Christians said that God and the heavenly spirit world must be thought of as physical. It's interesting that Bousset, the very stout Catholic defender of this position, says, "Nevertheless, the early Christians thought of God and the spirit world as physical," while he himself does not share the view.

Later on, at the end of the second century, there was Clement of Alexandria, and he became the big name. Quoting Bousset still: "In Clement of Alexandria the old teachings began to shrink to a purely intellectual content until he spiritualizes the sacraments to the point of being incomprehensible, nothing but a mere game of words." Well, Bousset, the most eminent Catholic writer, says that's what it became. Did they ever get out of it? No, this has become the rule ever since.

Tatian, who has given us his four versions of the gospel, informs us that it was the pagan philosophers who laughed at the Christian anthropomorphism. Tatian is another very early one, and he says, "What the pagan philosophers laughed at in Christianity was its anthropomorphism" (the idea that God should look like a body), showing that the Christians did teach that-the anthropomorphic God. The schools of the philosophers laughed at it, so they turned around and joined the schools. Still, Tatian ends up on their side, as does Minucius Felix who comes a little later. Next to anthropomorphism, the first thing the intellectuals and Gnostics denied was the physical resurrection. They could not tolerate that idea. We read in the newly found Bodmer Papyrus, number ten (a Coptic papyrus found in Egypt from the very early days of the church). It's supposed to be Third Corinthians, Paul's third letter to the Corinthians. Some people think it is genuine.

But it's against these Gnostics. The great sin of the Gnostics was to dematerialize everything, and this is what we find in the Bodmer Papyrus, for example. It gives a number of false doctrines taught by the Gnostics, and the first one is that there is no physical resurrection. The second is that God created a physical world; we can't have that. They just ruled out everything physical. The early apologist Athenagoras objects. He said, "Life would be an utter waste without the resurrection. It's the resurrection which gives everything in human life its meaning."

And the greatest of all theologians was Origen. He was born and grew up on the campus of the University of Alexandria. You notice he has a good Egyptian name (born of Horus). He flourished in 230 A.D. and was a dedicated student and a phenominal person. When he became a Christian, he took over the doctrines, but he couldn't agree with a lot of them. But he does give us what they are. He tells us, "This is what the Brethren taught. This is what they used to teach in the church." And so he is a valuable source. Back in the year 80, Celsus had written a scathing attack on the church, and Origen years after gave a reply. But in the process of replying to Celsus, he has to tell the things that Celsus accused the Christians of teaching. So this reply to Celsus is the best source we have of what the earliest Christians taught, next to the Bible itself and the Clementine Recognitions.

Origen is honest enough to report what the early Christians believed. Because he was always giving away what they believed, he lost his chance to become a saint or a doctor of the church, although he is the most influential. He contributed enormous writings. Augustine is nothing but a paraphrase of Origen who lived earlier. He kept seven stenographers (beautiful girls, incidentally) at work taking dictation all day long. He answered all the questions in the book. He was enormously productive. Keeping the seven beautiful stenographers working is something to be said in his favor. He is the one that says, "All you can think of of God is that he has no body. He can have nothing physical about him at all." Origen himself was so averse to it that he wouldn't eat anything. Somebody gave him an inheritance of some sort, and he would spend a penny or two a day for a few grains of wheat. He would eat them and vomit them up, disgusted with his own physicalness. It was anorexia, you see. People really do get as crazy as that. He had that problem, and he looked it. He was very skinny and cadaverous. But that's not what the early Christians believed.

When he was seventeen years old, they were running down Christians in Alexandria. His father didn't want him to go leave the house, so he hid all his clothes. Origen was very modest, so that was effective. But more than anything else, Origen will not allow holiness to anything corporeal. He says the resurrection can only be spiritual, a non-physical resurrection. There it is again, your asomaton, your invisible image, your unphysical resurrection. "When God's voice spoke from the heavens, it was not a real voice," he says, "since only the elect heard it." Already he is allegorizing-the great Origen. "The doctrine that God and the Son go to and from the earth is quite offensive," he says, "but only if one takes it literally." Well, no doctrine is offensive if you don't take it literally.

There's nothing wrong with saying, "thou shalt not commit adultery," or why not say, "you shall commit adultery?" Don't take it literally though; then you're all right, you're safe. And this is what they are doing, deliteralizing everything. "Now, we know as well as you do," he tells his pagan opponent, Celsus, "that God does not move; so how could he go from place to place? In the Parousia, 'the return of the Lord,' God does not descend to the earth in a topical sense but only in a mental sense." You think of Him as being here and He is here. Well, that is the way he talks.

Lactantius is the first of the Latin fathers and the best Latinist. He left the church as so many of the earlier ones did. St. Augustine did too for nine years. He said, "We don't have the old doctrine anymore." Lactantius left the church and joined a pious group that tried to live the original order of Christianity. There were lots of people doing that in his time. This is only the sixth volume of the Greek Patrologia, you see. Lactantius wrote, "Whoever desires the highest good, let him desire to live without a body, for matter is evil."

After noting that the Scribes and Pharisees reject the Old Testament references to Christ because they would not accept his being born in a physical body, Hilary can still affirm that the physical resurrection of the righteous is a contradiction. (Now this is St. Hilary who lived up in Gaul and had great influence on the editing of the scriptures.) To rise with sinful bodies would be to be saved into nothing, he would say. A physical resurrection is only for the wicked; they deserve it. This is what the great Hilary was teaching about the resurrection. It was just for the wicked. Isn't that a promise?

Rufinus is very important. He translated all the earliest Greek writers in the fourth century into Latin so the Latin people could read them. Clement of Rome has come to us in writings that Rufinus has given us. The Greek part has been lost in some of them, not all of them. He says that after the resurrection all will be spirit, no bodies. Well, what's the point of having a resurrection? Here's another one of those flat contradictions.

Eusebius goes all the way. "Any appearance of God must necessarily be an intellectual and a spiritual one," he says. Bluntly, "Christ did not die." This is Eusebius, the father of church history. He is the greatest source we have for church history. He was a personal friend of the Emperor Constantine. He tells us about the Council of Nicaea, and he was there. He lived in 325 A.D. (It's awfully warm in here, isn't it? I can't think. Will everybody go to sleep and make it unanimous? I know a few people are doing it. Then it doesn't bother me if I stumble around here because then nobody is bothered, and neither am I.)

But I think these references are interesting. I raise them in chronological order. This is the progress of the church. It starts very early. Can you imagine Eusebius of Caesarea saying bluntly, "Christ did not die; he did not suffer the death of a common criminal." He said, "Prophets, apostles, and high priests are still in the church, but we see them all with the pure eye of our minds." Notice, we don't need literal prophets or apostles; we don't need a high priest; we see them in our minds, and that's adequate. That's better than the other kind. They use that argument a great deal. He mocks one Marcellus for teaching that God has a body like a man and his foolish teaching that Christ made human flesh immortal through the power of the resurrection. These are all quotations from Eusebius. He applauds the nobility and good taste of the Greeks and Romans, the pagans, for interpreting their own deities allegorically. "We Christians should take a lesson from them and interpret our gods the way they do," he says.

Notice, they knew that the Greeks and Romans didn't take their night life of the gods very seriously. Those were inventions of the poets very largely. But this became the main weapon of the church, idolatry, plurality of gods. They never believed in that, but they had to have something to make it appear that they had departed from the old ways and that they were more righteous and more severe, and spiritual, etc. "We don't believe in all those idols and all those immoral gods." Well, as I say, the ancients didn't either. Their gods were something very different in the mysteries, the ordinances and various celebrations. Now we have great literature on them. We didn't have it before. Before now we have been going on the poets.

Eusebius says it is unholy to say that matter is unbegotten or was only organized at the creation. Notice what they were preaching-that matter was not created and was only organized at the creation. It wasn't created out of nothing; it was organized, which is what we teach. He says that's what the early church taught, but it's unholy to say it. "The real human is an intellectual, bodyless, immaterial, rational being, and as such only is he in the image of God," he wrote. And then he reveals himself in the seventh book of the history. He says that it's the simple, old-fashioned Christians who believe in the old literalism. We're much too intellectual for them; we leave that all behind. We don't need that anymore.

What marks the fourth century, as Alfoldi puts it, is "the victory of abstract ways of thinking-the universal triumph of theory, which knows no half measures. The Gnostic idea of the body as a prison is entirely at home with the doctors of the church. They love it because matter is vile."

Now there was a very righteous old guy. He and his followers got quite close to the early church, and they suffered a lot of persecution for it. They were noted for their old-fashioned ways. He left the main church because of the immorality of the clergy and stuck to a literal interpretation of the scriptures. Even to believe, says Epiphanius, that we actually are in God's image. That actually refers to Adam's body. Three exclamation points Epiphanius puts in there. He listed over eighty of these heresies that carried over from the third century. He is writing in the fourth century. Everyone had his own doctrine and was preaching in his own way. But the thing to do was to leave out anything literal, physical, or material-anything that smacks of Mormonism.

St. Augustine goes all the way. "Christ is with us if we believe. His dwelling in you is more real than if he were outside you before your eyes."

It's more real to have Jesus in there than out there. "When everything dissolved into abstractions, it left an uncomfortable feeling behind. The generation that rids itself once and for all of the old literalizers of which Jerome speaksÃ‚Â…" Jerome lived in a monastery in Bethlehem for fifteen years and knew the Christians at Jerusalem. There were old groups that still hung on. He called them the old-fashioned Christians, the literalizers. They would take you up to the top of the Mount of Olives and show you a plot that the church owned where they said the temple was going to be built when Christ came again. This was the sort of thing they taught, and Jerome was shocked. "Whom Jerome speak of crave no other, the presence of physical objects to tie them to the reality of the scriptures." (That's in the fourth century.) In his day everybody started making a pilgimage because they wanted contact with something that was physical. They went to the temple site, but Jerome says, "Don't go to the site of the temple in Jerusalem. Does not the sepulcher of the Lord appear more venerable to you? In the resurrection you have heard that all things will be without bodies. (This is from the eleventh volume of the Patrologia.) There is a passage that says this is what we will do when we are resurrected. Of course, we must be resurrected physically because the scripture says we will. So you will rise up with your body, but as soon as it is completely incorporated and put together, it will start to melt and dissolve and presently go away so you won't be bothered with it anymore.
