So that accounts for the resurrection and makes it spiritual at the same time. He says, "All things will be without bodies for they will have no need of them, and all matter will return to the nihilum [the nothing] from which it was once made. All matter came out of nothing originally and with the resurrection will all be restored, and then it will return to nothing."

Jerome admits though that Tertullian, Victorinus, Lactantius-the earlier fathers, all believed what the old Christians believed: that the Saints would reign with the Lord after the resurrection in the flesh. Yet he approves of pilgrim ladies who piously lick the place where the Lord lay in the holy sepulcher as they groan and sob. In the fourth century people started making pilgrimages to Palestine, and the church tried to stop them. Do you know there is not a single writing of the Catholic or Protestant churches that encourages pilgrimages to the Holy Land because it is an act of no faith. If you really think that these things have been taken care of and the church is established, you wouldn't have to go back there to look for some tangible contacts. That argues a lack of faith in the institution. The church fought it all the time, but the pilgrims started going and continued to go. They would go up to the top of the Mount of Olives, the place where the Lord would come back again, and look up to heaven and weep. That was where the Lord was last seen, and they wanted a physical contact with what was there. Jerome says, "Go to the holy sepulcher instead. Don't go to the place where the Lord left."

Incidentally, Lady Sylvia, a very rich Gallic woman of the fourth century, went on foot to all these places in a long flowing black robe. She had a terrific personality and scared everybody to death. She visited all the holy places, but at the same time she visited the place where Andromeda was chained to the rock and Perseus rescued her. That was just as real to her.

Although it didn't belong to the gospel, she felt she might as well see the place, in her attire of course. And she also visited the cell of Sarah and saw the cradle of Isaac and things like that. "And what goes on at the tomb of the Saints," Jerome said, "such a howling and yelling and lamentation you can't imagine." Just like there were at some festivals in India where people yell their heads off and rant and carry on. What strange pilgrimages. No wonder they shouldn't go.

"Why should we not honor relics in view of the Psalms?" he asks. "Why should dead bones be considered pollution?" It's odd that they turned to bones because they wouldn't accept the physical and the literalness of the gospel. Then they turned and went full blast for relics. They had to be tangible things-a cradle, a house, especially bones. "Why should they be considered pollution?" Jerome says. "Elijah raised a dead body, and it wasn't a dead body anymore after he did that." Well, anyway these go on and on.

St. Basil, annoyed by the physical component of Christ, too physical to be the highest, said, "I believe the resurrection to be the transition from his physical gnosis to his incorporeal theoria. That's a pure Aristotelian expression. Theoria is pure contemplation, just mind and nothing else. St. Basil is one of the three great Cappadocians and one of the four doctors of the church. There are only eight doctors, and he is one of them. They laid the foundations of Christian doctrine. And he says, "I believe the resurrection to be the transition from this physical gnosis to incorporeal theoria." No body, nothing but just mind.

"Angels do not have tongues or bow the knee," said Severus. "Away with the thought. They are figures of speech. Christ ate and drank after the resurrection only to show that he was not a fantasy, that he was physical." (That's a strange thing.) The wine and food really represented his teachings. He didn't really eat, but he had to represent that he wasn't a fantasy, so he ate to show that he was physical; but what he really ate was his teachings. That's the way they argue. But you notice that there is a formidable defect here, and only the Pearl of Great Price can fill this gap for the present time. We'll get on to particular things here. We have never referred to this before, but I think it is important because we don't realize that. In the controversies that are raging today, they talk about the crazy Mormons being Gnostics ("The God Makers," etc.). That's exactly the way they talked about the early Christians. It was the doctors of the church who talked that way, and they are the ones to whom the Christian world is dedicated. John Knox and Luther quote these fathers as the authorities just as often as they quote the Bible. They prove everything by these fathers. None of the first and second century. All after the fourth century.

Lecture 3

Literalism

You notice that I am mentioning sources all over the place. Some of those sources are available here. The main reason I'm giving you all this stuff is that they have not been available. Nobody has ever paid any attention to them, as a matter of fact. They've never been viewed from this point of view, and we have been collecting them because our whole thesis this semester with the Pearl of Great Price is that it represents a great gap, a vast amount of scripture that has been neglected and lost, but has now been found, and we refer to that. Now these fathers refer to the lost scripture and refer to earlier times. We talked about the early Christians; then the later times when they threw it all away; then after that when they tried to get it all back again coming in at a different angle. We were talking about literalism.

Now as to the sources, half the books from last semester are still on reserve. All the sources I mentioned were the fathers before the Nicaean Council in 325 A.D. They are the early fathers. They are not the apostolic fathers which come immediately after the church, the seven of them, but the so-called ante-Nicaean fathers. They are available in one collection. We have three copies of them in the library, actually. I spent a whole hour at the library this morning, and they weren't able to locate any one of them. But we do have three sets of the ante-Nicaean fathers and they should be on reserve on the fourth floor. Then there are other sources that we mention as we go. That includes the apostolic fathers too.

We were talking about the importance of literalism here. This is where we differ from all the other churches in taking it literally. Well, why shouldn't you take it literally? Gregory of Nyssa puts it this way, "If we soar in language and stand above the arch of heaven, we shall find there the supracelestial earth and also those who gape after sensual enjoyment and ask shall we have teeth or other members hereafter? The answer is yes since the scriptures are perfectly clear. We shall have all our members, but we will not make use of them."

You had to have your cake and eat it. They knew the scriptures made it clear that there would be a physical resurrection, so you had to have your eyes, ears, teeth, organs, and dimensions; but you would never have any use of them because you would be resurrected. You had to be a spiritual being then. This is an awkward answer. Now Gregory of Nyssa was one of the greatest. Professor Jaeger spent all the last years of his life editing Gregory of Nyssa who had already been edited by Benedictus Nursia. It's a massive work which you can't find here. You'll find it in the old edition of the Patrologia, but it has never been translated. You won't find it anywhere, so we won't bother about that. But he was a very important father. He was especially interested in teleiotes, the idea of being initiated. The temple ordinances were an obsession with him. He could never get them out of his system, but he didn't know what they meant.

The great John Chrysostom is one of the doctors of the church. There are only eight doctors of the church-four Greek doctors and four Latin doctors. They are the ones that really give us the basic theology of the modern Christian world-Catholic, Protestant, and all the rest of them. They are ones who are constantly cited, for example, in the controversies of the Scholastics and also in the controversies of the Reformation. The reformers referred to them every bit as much. In fact, they used the doctors more than the priesthood before them in the Middle Ages who were not nearly so learned. Notice they don't include some of the great ones like Origen, but they include two cousins, Gregory of Nazianzus and St. Basil, and then this John Chrysostom and Athanasius, of course. He was present at Nicaea, and he was the one that gave the Christians the Athanasian Creed. This creed tells you that God is three, and yet he is not three, he is one; the Father and the Son are the same, yet they're not the same; they're different, yet they're not different. And it clarifies things beautifully and led to hundreds of years of controversy, the Nicean Creed.

So those are the four Greeks and the four Latins. Notice that they come very close together. Two of them are cousins. Then the Latin fathers are even closer together. There's the great St. Augustine; and his teacher was Ambrose, the bishop of Milan. Ambrose was a rhetorician, long before he joined the church. His rhetoric was always interesting. Augustine converted him finally. Then there was his friend and contemporary, Jerome. They died just fifteen years apart. Jerome spent fifteen years in Palestine, lived in a monastery at Bethlehem, and translated the Bible-the official Vulgate Catholic Bible. Incidentally, we wouldn't have any Bible at all today without him. The King James translation goes back to the Vulgate because at the time the King James was made, nobody could read Greek very well. They didn't understand it at all. And Hebrew was almost out of the question. Look at how they laughed at Scaliger. They were good scholars, but there were problems. In the Beza manuscript there was one verse missing at the end of Mark, and the great Erasmus supplied it by writing what the Greek words would have been. In that one short verse, he made nine bad grammatical mistakes, which shows you how well they understood the text anyway.

The writers of the King James version, which is the most glorious prose work in the English language, leaned heavily on Luther's Bible, and Luther leaned heavily on the Vulgate, the Latin Bible, because that much they could read. But they had late texts. This isn't a class in the Bible, but this is why these people believed these things and why they lost all the stuff we have in the Pearl of Great Price that is so important. You have those three doctors, and then way off in 600 A.D. you have Gregory the Great whom we mentioned before. He was called Pater Superstitionum, "the Father of Superstition," because he introduced all sorts of things into the picture that were necessary to fill in the gaps that the loss of the earlier teachings had left.

John Chrysostom left us lots of writings, and he is a wonderful writer. He says, "How can God have a tangible body? Such a mystery can be viewed only with fear and trembling. We can't understand it." Elsewhere he says he doubts that the angels can understand it; nobody can.

Well, actually Origen uses that argument. But they never could break away from it because the scriptures wouldn't allow it. John Chrysostom (which means "golden mouth") was the great orator of his time and lived to the beginning of the fifth century. He says, "The old Christians had to have physical miracles and presences, those simple-minded boobs. We don't believe like the early church." They were proud of having advanced from the primitive doctrine. That's why there was another de-eschatologizing of Christianity after World War II because the German Lutheran scholars were very much disillusioned by the terrible things that had happened. So led by Rudolf Bultmann they said, "Well, we must de-eschatologize." In fact, Albert Schweitzer in his Leben Jesu started that. He says, "After all, they were primitive people. They didn't know A from B. We'll have to get rid of the superstitious part of the Bible-the miracles and the idea that Christ is the Son of God." He was a great moral teacher, and that was as far as you could go. And so you had the de-eschatologizing-taking all the supernatural element out of religion, and what do you have left?

But then in the 1950s and '60s they had this reaction, along with the Ecumenical Council. How can we say we are Christians if we don't believe what the early Christians believed? Call yourself anything you want, but don't call yourself a Christian because you say those people were crazy and didn't know what was what. But already you have that in writers like Jerome. He talks about the old-fashioned Christians. So Chrysostom says, "How about these things? We can't go along with the old Christians. They had to have physical miracles and presences." You will not find translations of John Chrysostom either, too bad. We have him in the Patrologia here, two different editions. He has seventeen volumes in it. It's a massive work, and you can find out all sorts of things.

Do you realize that the fourth century is far better documented than the fourteenth century, or even the nineteenth century in church history? It's marvelous; we have everything that went on at the time when the church was lost, when Constantine took over. The fourth century libraries are tremendous. We have all their personal letters and reflections. Eusebius' table talk with the Emperor Constantine, and Constantine explaining to him what he has in mind. It's like some intimate, in-depth reporter for a TV documentary. We have all these notes from the early church at this time of transition. What value! And nobody ever reads them. Why not? Well, because the church was set on its way. Its pattern is established. No minister ever looks at these or even knows of their existence. It's very puzzling and rather embarrassing to talk to them. But they feel that has all been settled now. They have their creeds, and the concern of any minister or pastor is with his flock and the duties he has to carry out as far as going into the fine points of doctrine is concerned.

So Joseph Smith came along with all this, claiming that he had literally seen the Father and the Son. Imagine what a tremendous cultural shock that would be. If he wanted to say the worst thing he possibly could, that was it. So John Chrysostom repeatedly invites the members to see angels and martyrs with the eyes of their intellect and not to expect that angels and martyrs were real people. He said that the older Christians had to have that sort of thing. "Those simple souls lacked the intellectual capacity for the incorporeal [or non-material] gifts. They had to have miracles because, unlike us, they had not learned to see with the mind and faith alone." Just as if the Christians of the fifth century were an improvement on the days of the apostles. They actually speak patronizingly of the apostles. "We go to school and we understand these things."

And then here is a very frank statement, "If we no longer have revelation, we have something better." And what do you think is better than revelation? "We have something better than revelation," he says. "We've gone away from that old physical stuff to a spiritual realm; we have the bones and relics of the martyrs." Talk about going back to the physical, the tangible. Only this time they're not even alive; there's just a lot of dead rubble here. You can see them by the thousands around the Mediterranean, these dried, withered bones-a rather hideous sort of thing. That's far better than revelation, he says, to have those bones which the demons fear.

He uses one argument for the fact that they still have the Holy Ghost in the church though. He says, "When I go into a big church very late at night, and it is dark, I sort of get scared." That proves there's a Holy Ghost, you see, because there is a spiritual experience-a thrill of spirit. He says, "Any city that contains the bones of the Saints is absolutely impregnable." Of course, they believed that until the cities started falling before the Moslems like moths before the flame. And this was from a man who held physical things in utter loathing. "The church is all because it does not exist physically, but in the spirit. Heavenly things, being incorporeal, are seen only in the intellect. The Lord is near; he is here, but never visibly. That is not what is meant by the coming of the Lord or his appearance," he says. They called that chiliasm which is the belief that the Lord would come and reign for one thousand years. They believed it in the early church, and then it became a heresy. Four hundred thousand Donatists in North Africa were put to death because they believed in it, with the approval and by the recommendation of the great St. Augustine who himself was an African. He was the bishop of Hippo. These Donatists were teaching the wrong thing, so they got rid of them. The emperor's troops went out, urged on by Augustine, and slaughtered four hundred thousand of them for believing that Christ would come again. That's a strange procedure, isn't it. If you want a reference, this is in the sixtieth volume of the Patrologia, twenty-sixth column. He goes on, "But that is not what is meant by his coming. After the resurrection, the Apostles no longer asked the Lord what would be the sign of his coming or the end of the world. They knew he had already arrived, and he would not answer save by promising the Holy Ghost. He would never come again." He goes on like this.

Then Nicephorus comes next, and Gregory the Great is the latest of the great doctors of the church. They want it both ways. They say in the seventy-ninth volume of the Patrologia, "Since there is no such thing as a bodiless body or an imageless image [we talk about the body of Christ and about man in God's image], what's the solution? Christ must still have a body, but one having nothing in common with what we can possibly imagine as a body." St. Nilus says the same thing. And the Venerable Bede, who died on my birthday (no coincidence at all) in the year 735 in England, was one of the greatest of all the fathers.

He wrote in England, and he was finishing his Gospel of John as he expired. He was trying to write quickly and get to the last verse before he died. He said that it was to teach the Apostles that Christ appeared to them in the palpable body and ate with them. To teach them what, if he had no body, but was a spirit? Can you follow that line of argument? He came to them; he asked for food to eat; he brought the fish and cooked it on the fire; and he ate with them. Whenever he appeared after the resurrection, he asked for food and ate with them. He said, "A spirit hath not flesh and bone as you see me have." That was to teach them that he had no body of flesh and bone, but was a spirit. Well, we don't want to be slowed down with this kind of logic too long.

Do they still follow this crazy line of argument in Christianity today? Well, there's an article by Wuttke in the Zeitschrift fur die neutestamentliche wissenschaft which says, "The allegory with the self-congratulating pleasure of thought manipulation; the games which become ideas, which become a mechanical idea of verbal inspiration." You see, by using words and learning phrases, that's the equivalent of inspiration. "Ã‚Â…a mechanical idea of verbal inspiration. To be rapt away from matter, that is the longing of the Christian Greek; it is St. Ambrose's cup of the spirit, which from heaven is held out to the earth."

Now, there are two brothers Hugo Rahner and Karl Rahner who say that the longing of the Christian is "to be rapt away from matter," to be taken away from matter. They still cling to that. They are the two most eminent Catholic theologians today. They say the main object and desire of being a Christian is that you won't be contaminated or concerned with matter at all, with the physical universe. And Arnold Lunn, who in the 1930s was the stoutest defender of the Roman Catholic theology, says, "Thomas Aquinas, starting from the universally admitted truth that it is certain and obvious to our senses that some things are in motion, proceeds to deduce the existence of God." Something is in motion; is there nothing literal in that? Well, then he says, "If one resorts to the easy, self-contradictory expedient of denying the manifold of finite things has any existenceÃ‚Â…" As Lovejoy writes, all problems disappear at a stroke if you say that. We make it just allegory; we get nowhere. Arnold Lunn cites Jerome's remark that the idea of God sitting on a throne is superstitious nonsense. Thomas Aquinas is the greatest medieval philosopher, and he says that it's superstitious nonsense, yet his own blessed assurance is Aquinas starting out from universally admitted truth that if it is obvious to our senses that things move, then God exists. He proves the existence of God purely logically by the fact that things move. Arnold Lunn entitled his big work The Revolt from Reason-that everybody but the scholastic philosophers of the Middle Ages had no theology and no religion at all because they revolted from reason in wanting to talk about a physical God, physical heaven and things like that. That was the revolt from reason. The idea of God in heaven is superstitious nonsense.

But things have changed now. Scholars at the beginning of this century, we read in a recent Expository Times, the official document of the Episcopal Church in EnglandÃ‚Â…Incidentally, I had a conversation with I.E.S. Edwards yesterday. The thing that impresses him so much [p.6] about being here (aside from the fact that he couldn't get coffee in the cafeteria) is that you people seem to take your religion much more seriously than the Anglicans do. My answer was, "Everybody takes religion more seriously than the Anglicans do." It's not considered self-respecting to take it too seriously with them. But he's a great man.

So at the beginning of the century, scholars began devoting themselves to the rediscovery of the Jesus of history who had nothing of the supernatural about him, we are told in this article. This started out especially with Albert Schweitzer. Ten years later, it had been not only abandoned but discarded with contempt, only to return twenty years after that with the doctrine we have today: The materials in the Gospels have survived only as expressions of faith, not as historical data. Jesus has become a mere saving event; he has ceased to be a person. He cites Rudolf Bultmann. "It was a choice between that and the common view that Jesus was a teacher of ethics who represented the highest level then reached by Judaism and that later Christology is mainly the work of Paul." It doesn't go back to Jesus at all. So we get these things that set us apart, back to Bultmann and the rest.

I have so many friends who are devoted to Reinhold Niebuhr. He says, "The biblical eschatology, the miraculous part of the Bible, the supernatural, the hereafter etc. must be taken seriously but not literally," You don't take it literally, you see, but you take it seriously. Well, if you don't take it literally, you don't take it seriously, as Aristides said. It was like Yigael Yadin, who was a commander in the Israeli war in 1948. He was here and we were down at Kent Brown's house. We talked all night with Yigael Yadin, who was the editor of the Dead Sea Scrolls. His father discovered them. He died recently, but he was the commander of the Israeli forces at that time. He said, "In Israel we don't believe in miracles, but we count on them. We never could have got through these wars if it hadn't been for miracles," he said. "So we don't believe in them, but we count on them."

E. M. Dahl was a Danish scholar who collected all this stuff-the biggest single collection of writings on early Christianity. He reaches the conclusion of anthrosomatism; it is a real body that rises in the resurrection (that Christ had), but not the same body that we have here. And he says, "This assumes that the mind of modern man is more likely to have the right insight into truth and reality than that of his ancient forebears." That's the basic thesis: modern man with his education surely knows more than those people knew. But about these things?

Soggin is a very active Christian archaeologist, and he says, "Why not call a spade a spade. There are those today who will go right back to the old literalism. The story of salvation only exists when we are dealing with reality, not with later artificial workings-over. As Hesse says, "only that interests us which actually happened. Everything else, not at all or incidentally." It doesn't interest us at all if it didn't actually happen, which is exactly what Aristides says at the beginning. And E. C. Rust says, "We must still hold to historical, bodily, and even physical resurrection without being crudely material." There you are again; we want to believe in the physical resurrection, but not crudely material. Nothing like that. It reminds us of Bottom in A Midsummer-Night's Dream. He is going to take the lion's part, but he will frighten the ladies with his terrible roar. He says, "No, I'll be careful not to frighten the ladies. I will roar as gently as any sucking dove." It's the same thing here. We don't want to be too material, but we must be material about it. St. Augustine could never explain infant damnation. These children weren't baptized, so they had to be damned. He says, "They'll be damned, they'll be damned, they'll be damned all the way-but with a light damnation." They have to have it both ways.

If we recognize this literalism, it takes us to this very important basic doctrine of a preexistence, on which there is an awful lot. You see how the preexistence comes out if we accept the literal existence-if we don't believe in creation out of nothing. This emphasis on preexistence and seemingly redundant repetition is driving home the realization that knowing who we really are is the only thing that can put everything into its proper perspective. And, of course, it's a big thing in the Pearl of Great Price. The key to understanding knowledge is that we are eternally spiritual. Now, what did the early church teach about that? The Egyptians were great on it.

As Donner says, The basic teaching which we find in the Proverbs of Solomon-the speaker was in the presence of God before the foundation of the earth-is found among the Egyptians; and you see a lot of it there. The earliest texts are the pyramid texts, aside from the Shabako Stone. These are the oldest books in the world, and they are huge. Incidentally, as Professor Edwards tells me (he is the authority on the pyramids, and he is here now), they are working on the pyramids of the fifth and sixth dynasty now, and they are discovering new pyramid texts everyday. We have thousands of them, and this is all back before 2400 B.C. This comes from the Old Kingdom. This is what they believed way back there. These are the oldest documents in the world, and it isn't just a few fragments; it's a library.

In the pyramid texts we read this, for example: "The pharaoh was conceived by his father when there was as yet no heaven, nor earth, nor people, nor birth of the gods, nor were there any dead. And every individual existed when the plan of the ancient Lord of heaven was not yet formulated." It speaks of a primordial mother, how the one became three, and how before all that we were together in his presence. "My name is the son of the Primordial God." These are from the pyramid texts and go right over into the coffin texts without a break here. "I existed before I was born, when the gods did not exist, when as yet there was no bird trap, when the cattle were not yet lassoed. I was formerly; I was of yesterday, a great one among the great and noble ones." The ordinary person puts this on his coffin (well, this is nobility here). One thinks of Abr. 3:23 . He saw many that were great and noble. "Thou art one of them," the Lord tells Abraham. Well, you get this all through the Egyptian stuff. Here is a coffin text: "Before I was born by hand or born of woman, he created me in the midst of his perfection, which caused to jubilate those who shared in the secrets." When we were created in the preexistence, remember the sons of God shouted for joy and all the morning stars sang together. That's the creation hymn, and it's often referred to in these writings.

The thing most often referred to in early writings is this creation hymn-the hymn of joy that was sung at the creation to celebrate the plan which was now accepted and would begin to be operative. And we were there. There's also a great deal of that in the Dead Sea Scrolls. But the fact that this is in the very earliest Egyptian stuff is interesting.

We mentioned the Clementine Recognitions before. When Peter is explaining the gospel to Clement for the first time, he tells about the plan "which He of his own good pleasure announced in the presence of all the first angels which were assembled before Him. Last of all He made man whose real nature, however, is older and for whose sake all this was created." That's the preexistent man. Moses 6:51 says..."I made the world and men before they were in the flesh." And so we have many passages of this from the early writings. Going back to the Clementine Recognitions, "This world was made so that the number of spirits predestined to come here when their number was full could receive their bodies and again be conducted back to the light." So if they were conducted back after they finished their testing here, they must have been there before.

The Pastor of Hermas is the earliest of the seven apostolic fathers we mentioned before. This was discovered at the end of the nineteenth century. It teaches that the Christian should so live to be ready to renounce the prince of this world at any time and betake himself back to the Lord-the Lord having sworn by his glory that after a certain set day of salvation on this earth, salvation would be impossible. So you have to behave yourself now.

I'm going to read from the Odes of Solomon, and we will let this do for the early Christian stuff. The Odes of Solomon were discovered in 1906 by Rendel Harris way up east of the Tigris, almost in central Asia. They are the earliest Christian hymn. They go right back to the beginning and refer to the Dead Sea Scrolls. You get these people wandering back there from Palestine. They go down and become Mandaeans, down near the mouth of the Tigris and Euphrates. There are the Hymns of Solomon and the Odes of Solomon. Now they are checked against early Hebrew and Christian writings, and they are the oldest and the best. Not only that, it's a beautifully lyrical hymn. The Odes of Solomon deal with the temple ceremony, and they are in Syriac. I included a long translation of them in the back of a book of mine. (The best thing about it is the back. I may be a terrible compiler, but I do supply you with a lot of stuff that you can't get everywhere.) That is the book called An Egyptian Endowment or the Message of the Joseph Smith Papyrus. I noticed today there are four copies still on the shelf, and there are some on reserve. Don't pay attention to what I say, but look in the back. There's an appendix with five or six parallels showing that what the Egyptians did in their temples was exactly what the early Christians and Jews did, according to these documents. There you will find the Odes of Solomon. This is from it: "Peace was prepared for you before ever your conflict, your testing, was upon this earth, for I know them and before they came into being, I took knowledge of them, and on their faces I set my seal. Who is there that is not subject to them? They are mine and by my own right hand I set my elect ones [He is talking about the Church, about Israel].

Another passage: "All that have seen me were amazed. And I was regarded by them as a strange person. He who brought me up is the most high in all his perfection. He who brought me down from on high also brought me up from below. Grace of the elect, and who shall receive it save those who trust it from the beginning. The love of the elect, and who shall put it on except those who possessed it in the beginning. Then they tried to slay me. But I did not perish because I was not their brother. Nor was my birth like theirs. For I was older than they could remember. In vain they attacked me. They fought to destroy the memorial of him who was before them. And while I praised among the praising ones, I was great among the mighty ones. For according to the greatness of the most high, so he made me in his own likeness. And in his own newness he renewed me. And he annointed me from his own perfection. Hallelujah! All who see me will be astonished. For from another world, from another race am I."

Then chapter 41 is the creation hymn, hailing the Messiah. You compare it with early Christian liturgy from the eighteenth volume of the Patrologia. We have the Oriental Patrologia too which is very valuable, in which the congregation identifies itself with preexistent design. And the Sophia Christi, "All spirits are ageless and equal as to creation, but differ in degrees of power." The fact that one person is as old as another doesn't mean that one can't be greater than another. That's a very interesting thing that's explained in The Pearl of Great Price when we get to that. You think it isn't fair if one person came first? No, nobody came first. Intelligence never was created, neither can be. "Man also was in the beginning with God." Then why is God so much greater? Well, that's a very interesting problem in The Pearl of Great Price. I've been quoting here from the Sophia Christi and the Gospel of Truth. These are all very old and very recent findings. "One's true name is that given to him by the Father in the preexistence." That's the name by which you will be known when you go back. "How can anyone understand whose name was not called out in the beginning? He remains ignorant to the end, is a plasma of forgetting and will pass away with it."

And here is the doctrine of anamnesis too. If anybody has the true knowledge, it is certain that he is from above because when he is called, he hears, he understands, he answers and he turns toward the one who calls him. There is this recognition, you see. You recognize the voice of the Father if you are righteous. This is a Platonic doctrine of anamnesis. Again we don't want to get sidetracked. There's a wonderful passage from the Gospel of Thomas about all the sufferings you have to go through in the church, all the beatings you have to take. And this you also find in the Hodayot, the Thanksgiving Scroll of the Dead Sea Scrolls. You have to suffer a lot, but when you get a chance to know what you were before and how much is at stake, you would look upon this with nothing but rejoicing. It's your big chance and you wouldn't miss it. You're so glad to come here. And the Gospel of Thomas says, "When you come to know yourselves, then you will be known, and you will know that you are the sons of the living Father. But if you do not know yourselves, then you are in poverty." Jesus says in this same writing, "Blessed is he who was before he came into being, and blessed are the solitary elect. For you shall find the kingdom because you came from it. You shall go there again."

Incidentally, this is the Gospel of Thomas which contains sayings of Jesus that are not in the Bible, and all churches, Protestant and Catholic, have accepted at least fourteen of these sayings as authentic. These are original, authentic sayings of Jesus that were lost, and now they are found. And in the latest Revised Standard translation of the Bible, they are included. So go to the Gospel of Thomas to find out what they used to believe. These things got lost, and the evidence is that they are showing up now. You can get this at the BYU Bookstore. You might be able to get it in paperback. It used to be quite cheap-the Gospel of Thomas from Nag Hammadi. This is from the congregation of the early church in Egypt before the apostasy hit it. It came out in 1949-50 and caused an immense sensation. I guess most of you can't remember it. But it was nothing but the Gospel of Thomas and was as big news as King Tut. It was in the newspapers every day. Go back and look in Time, Life, and Fortune. You see nothing else, but the Gospel of Thomas. That was a big thing.

You will find an interesting article by John Allegro who was one of the most insightful students of the Dead Sea Scrolls. He lost his job at Harvard for this article that was in the Atlantic Monthly in 1960. The scrolls were discovered at the end of the 1940s. In the 1950s there was a big excitement about them. And then all of a sudden, nothing. They were suppressed. Were they actually suppressed? Yes, they were, and that's what Allegro shows in this article. The Jews don't like him, and the Christians don't like him. Nobody likes him because it isn't what they had been teaching. For the Jews, it is much too Christian. You don't have Jews with the sacrament, and a presidency of three, and twelve apostles before the time of Christ. That's what Professor Cross at Harvard, who has lectured here a good deal, calls the "church of anticipation." It's as if the whole Christian church was there ahead of time. Well, you get that in the Book of Mormon. The Jews were offended to find out that there was so much Christianity in Judaism, and the Christians were offended to find out that the Jews were doing these things before Christ came. The Catholics were especially offended because it robbed them of their originality. They felt that Christ had to be original. He had to bring something that wasn't known before. Well, Christ fulfills the prophets and brings more. In the Book of Mormon is where you find that beautiful blending of the two.

And so in the Gospel of Thomas there are all sorts of things that people don't like today. "When you come to know yourselves, then you will be known and you will know that you are the sons of the living FatherÃ‚Â…" And then he says down here, "Blessed are the solitary elect; you shall find the kingdom because you came from it. And Jesus said, 'If they say to you from where do you have your origin, say to them [this is one of the unwritten sayings of Jesus], We have come from the light where the light has originated through itself. It stood and revealed itself in their image.' Lord, do I and man belong to the material world? The answer is you and your children belong to the Father who existed from the beginning. Your spirit came down from above from the imperishable light; for this reason the lower powers cannot approach you. But all who have known this road are immortal amidst mortal men." You have this satisfaction.

And then there is the Gospel of Philip which is equally old and equally important. From the aspect of the temple, it is very important. "The Lord said, 'Blessed is he who was before he came into being for he who is and was shall be.'" It continues, "The exalted nature of man is not revealed but in secret to those who have been initiated and who know." This is an important thing.

When these new writings turn up, so many of them begin with the title "The Secret Teachings of the Lord to the Apostles After the Resurrection." He taught them secretly after the resurrection, and they did not divulge these things. They were not shouted from the housetops, as the Jesuits insist. You see the Christian world can't admit that there had been any secret teachings, let alone any lost teachings-because if there were, where are they now? Whatever happened to them? Now they admit that there was a great deal and now it is possibly turning up.

From the Gospel of Philip again, this is the Cabalistic teaching: "At the Council in Heaven every spirit appeared before God in the very same form they were later to take in the human body. God examined them one by one, and many hesitated to come here and to be exposed to contamination." They knew it would be a hard life, and they didn't want to risk the test. They didn't feel good about it. There's a great deal about that, incidentally. I have some good ones here. Two weeks ago I had occasion to go through the old Slavonic text. It was a very good refresher course for me. In the Second Book of Enoch (remember Enoch was the great scribe), "Write all the souls of men, however many of them are born, for all souls are prepared to eternity before the foundation of the world."

In 1912 R. H. Charles got together all the writings that were known before the Dead Sea Scrolls and Nag Hammadi. This is a standard collection of Old Testament apocryphal writings. It is on reserve here. If you have any questions about the Old Testament, look up R. H. Charles, The Apocrypha and Pseudapigrapha of the Old Testament, volume 2. He says, "The Platonic doctrine of preexistence of souls is here taught. We find that it had already made its way into Jewish thought in Egypt." So through Egypt, Christians and Jews, he says, both adopted this. But the fact is they had it independently; though, as we saw, it was an Egyptian doctrine.

The doctrine was accepted and further developed by Philo. We mentioned Philo before; he is the one that shows how the Jews lost literalism. He turned everything into the abstractions of the schools. Josephus says it was an Essene doctrine, so it would belong to the Dead Sea Scrolls which were unknown at the time this was written. The idea is that spirits are immortal and endure forever and ever. Man is a spiritual, eternal being without beginning or end. And he gives a number of examples here, "He says it became the prevailing doctrine in later Judaism [the doctrine of preexistence] until modern normative Judaism got rid of it. You will find it in the Beresheit Rabbah and the Tanhuma, etc. "The doctrine of preexistence as taught by the Essenes, by Philo, the Talmud and the Cabala," says Meyer, talking about that.

The Apocalypse of Baruch is a very valuable one that goes way back. You'll find that in R. H. Charles. It says, "The multitude of those who should be born was numbered and for that number a place was prepared where the living might dwell." And this is another view: What are we here for then? Was our coming down here a fall or a calamity? Well, the Gnostic doctrine was that we are here in prison. We are being punished. "Man's descent from heaven at the moment of conception with his human form and divine seal (showing that he had existed before)Ã‚Â…" But unlike Origen and the Gnostic schools, the Cabala does not regard life as a fall or exile but as a means of education and a beneficial trial. As Nephi says, "Our time here became a time of probation." That's what the Cabala teaches. But Origen, the Gnostics and the Christians went off in this direction-that it is a fall, an imprisonment, and a curse. Well, just like "Adam brought the curse of man's first disobedience and the fruit of that forbidden tree whose bitter taste brought death into the world and all our woes." It was utter calamity that brought us here. In the Jewish teaching that was not so. It's a benefit, a time of education and learning, and a time of testing. So in the third chapter of the Book of Abraham we read, "We will make an earth whereon these may dwell, and we will prove them herewith to see if they will do all the things whatsoever we shall command them. They who keep their first estate shall be added uponÃ‚Â…"

Another book we are going to cite is from the Zohar. This is a very important writing which has been neglected by the Jews; they come back to it. They used to think it was just an invention of Moses of Leon in 1240 A.D., a work of the Middle Ages, but now they know it is very old. It has come out in a number of editions. I have a beautiful, complete Hebrew edition of the Zohar that just came out. It's full of not just biblical teachings, but things that might have been in the Bible. Remember the canon of the Bible is a very arbitrary sort of thing. Many things are in there that shouldn't be, and things are left out that should be there. But the Zohar tells us here: "All men before they lived on earth were present in heaven in the identical form they possess in this life, and everything they learn on earth they knew already before they came to this world." That's an interesting thing; how could you be tested if you knew it already. This becomes a very interesting problem in physics. According to the Talmud, this world is only a marshaling area, a sort of freeport, while that world above is the true dwelling. We have just left it temporarily to be tested here. "All spirits which are to enter into the body exit from the day of creation of the world until the earth shall pass away."

Now a powerful passage in the Zadokite Document from the Dead Sea Scrolls tells how God condemned the wicked in the preexistence by not counting them among those chosen. "From of old, from the days of eternity and before they were established, he knew them and abhored their generations. With exactitude he set out their names, but those whom he hated he caused to stray." Remember in the Pearl of Great Price, Satan was a liar from the beginning.

This expression that is used is another important one: "from eternity to eternity." In the Serek Scroll of the Dead Sea Scrolls, 2:1, it uses the expression me'olam le'olam. It's more than an ecstatic outbreak "from ever to ever." The Christians use it, and Barnabas uses it in his [p.13] epistle eight. Barnabas-remember he is one of the seven apostolic fathers, one of the earliest fathers after the New Testament. He says, "From eons unto the eons means that you come out of the eons and you go into the eons." You have an eternity behind you, and you have an eternity before you. This is the bottleneck you pass through to determine for a long time to come which way you will take hereafter. He says, "The way of light is the Lord from eons into the eons; the way of life leading from one eternity up to the other one to come. The eschatology is inconceivable without the protology. Typical of this common background to Jews and early Christians is the prayer of Anna in the Pseudo-Philo. "Hast thou not, O Lord, examined the heart of all generations before thou formedst the world?" In the Secrets of Enoch in the Slavonic Enoch (this is one very close to the Joseph Smith Enoch), the Lord says to Enoch, "Sit down and write the names of those who are not yet born and the places which are prepared for them forever; for all the spirits were prepared before the foundation of the earth." Enoch speaks here, "I swear unto you, my children, that before man was made in the womb of his mother, he was prepared; and how each has sojourned in this age that a man might be tested in the balance while he was here." There's your probation and preexistence too. "There has indeed been prepared in advance a place for every human soul."

We'll go back to our friend Gregory of Nyssa again. "The soul had a previous existence and a life of its own where, even as in this life, it was given its free agency by the Creator. And such as grew weary at doing good entered this life at a disadvantage, having passed the test less satisfactorily than others." Now this is writing which is accepted by Origen, the earliest Christian theologian, and he says that this is what the brethren taught. "I don't believe it," he said, "but the brethren in the early church taught it." Now the fact is when we are born into this world it's with unequal advantages, isn't it? Some are born blind, lame, crippled; it's terrible. Some are born into poverty; some into riches. He says, "How does that happen? They used to teach that before we came here life was a test too, and when we passed the test, we came into this world. Our life here is a reward for our performance before we came here." He says that would certainly explain the inequality of people being born. Or as Gregory of Nyssa says here, "The soul had a previous existence for even as in this life, it was a free agent." Remember there was a Council in Heaven. They voted and some preferred this and some preferred that. You were perfectly free to take your way there. You had your free agency. And such as grew weary in doing good entered this life at a disadvantage because they passed the test all right, but less satisfactorily than others passed it. Well, there's an interesting thesis that Origen developed. Needless to say, it was slapped down.

Well, Basilides, also a contemporary, says that suffering in this life is punishment for sins in the preexistence, not by way of denying that there was preexistence, by insisting that the opportunity to suffer here, even martyrdom, is rather a reward earned before, an opportunity for greater glory. Persecutions are not to punish the saints but to sanctify them. Then back to Origen again, "The spirit stands for progress and by definition evil is refusal to accept progress. This is the principle of apostasy that you refuse to progress, and when you dug in your heels in the other world, you came here at a lower level. "Learn this one thing," wrote Cyril of Jerusalem, "that before coming to this cosmos, the spirit did not sin, but that we came down sinless here and now. And now we sin by choice."

Well, which is it? Is it a matter of sin up there? We had to come down here and take on flesh, I suppose, to be tested in a particular way. No, it's the level of performance that we are judged on in coming down here. And that's another interesting thing-that wonderful passage where the Lord says that one will be more intelligent than another. Don't resent it because you know perfectly well why you are not more intelligent than you are. We only use one thousandth of our potential anyway. So you can't complain that somebody is ahead of you. You might be far far better than you are. So don't worry about him; just improve yourself. And this is the gospel of repentance. Well, the time is up and we are still bogged down in these fundamentals. But they are the fundamentals that are treated with peculiar address and clarity in the Pearl of Great Price, and as far as I know, nowhere else.

Lecture 4

Preexistence

Now, to add a little excitement and change of pace, I'm going to wear my other glasses today. That's how exciting this is. I don't know how long they've got to go on wasting footage on the same haggard pan here. It's not going to do any good. "Age cannot wither nor custom stale his infinite monotony." That's from Shakespeare. It's from Antony and Cleopatra. She wasn't monotonous. Shall we talk about Cleopatra, "who burns upon the water"?

We were talking about Clement's questions, and this is very relevant to what we are doing. The first question, he does not want "to die and go we know not where; to lie in cold obstruction and to rot." That's a future he does not relish. It bothers him, he says. Since he was a small child, this idea had been after him all the time. So that was the first question he asked, would he live hereafter? He went to all the doctors of the schools in Rome, and there were lots of schools in Rome. The answers he got were well, yes you will live in the flowers, or you will live in memory, or you will be absorbed into the great sea of being. He didn't find that satisfactory at all. He wanted something literal, and we went into literalism. So he went and talked with Peter, and Peter said, "Yes, this is absolutely literal; we absolutely deny that there is anything evil in matter. That's part of the picture." This was a revolutionary teaching. Then we saw that the early teachers of the Church, the early Christians, all taught that; they were literal minded. The doctors of the schools weren't. By the fourth century they had gotten rid of that completely and would have nothing to do with matter. Matter was entirely evil, the Neoplatonic doctrine. You get someone like Plotinus, and any contact with matter would defile even God himself. To this day the Christians believe in a nonliteral conception of things. Well, if he was to exist as an individual (this was important to be himself), then there must have been a preexistence. If he was a spiritual, eternal being, he must have existed before, and the thing that bothered him as much as anything was this idea of preexistence. We saw last time that preexistence was also an important part of the picture. We have all these references here and will just refer to a couple of them.

In the first volume of the Patrologia there is an interesting note by Migne. He edited it. He speaks for the fathers of the church on this subject, I Clement 1:28. "Clement asserts that the spirit is older than the body, according to one of the four interpretations of the fathers." He says the fathers interpret this four ways. Did we preexist or not? Remember this is all essential to our handbook of terrible questions which is the Pearl of Great Price. The first thing that Migne says is one school taught that the spirit was before the body (it was preexistent); a second that the body came first and the spirit after that; still others that they came into existence together. That school has a special name. And still others that are not willing to make any [p.2] assertion at all. Along with which opinion should be mentioned the errors of Pythagoreans, the Platonists, the Gnostics, and the Origenists (the Hermetic School). Origen for a time firmly believed in the preexistence, and then he changed his mind. Pythagoreans believed in reincarnation, metempsychosis, that you did exist before but it was in another shape or being, an animal or something. This is no satisfaction because if you change your identity completely, you are somebody else and you really haven't continued to exist. I may have been a chair in the preexistence, but that doesn't give me any satisfaction now. Or to think that I will be a cockroach hereafter gives me no great comfort either. That is not me continuing. They feel that very strongly. The Egyptians are particularly strong on this. That's why this emphasis on the name. It was the name to help preserve his identity. It was himself and nobody else that continued. You had to have hereafter, but you had to preserve your identity as well.

But Plato really believed that we existed before and that we still have dim memories of it. How do you know it was good, beautiful and true? Because you saw it before. You have a memory of it when you come here. Every recognition of truth is a recognition of something you knew and learned before, and we are moving toward that state. Plato went all the way there. But Origen changed places. He said that this was the teaching of the venerated elders, the earliest Christians, the brethren in the early church. But he has equal respect for the doctors of the schools because he was born and raised actually on the campus of the University of Alexandria. His father was a teacher, and he was born there. He could never get that out of his system, so he was perplexed and didn't know what to do. He began by fervidly preaching the doctrine of preexistence and ended up by denying it. But in the sixth century it flared up again in his name (the Origenists) in Palestine among the monasteries there and in Egypt too. It had a revival at that time as the true teachings of the church.

But when you get to Hilary in the West, in the fourth century, he says that preexistence stumps him completely. "It is an impious and an alien study, you should not even think about it." He says, "How can anything exist before it is born? How is that possible?" They are giving rational answers, you know, not scriptural ones. Or in that case, why bother, but he did bother. He couldn't leave it alone. In many places St. Augustine accepts the doctrine, apparently because of the appeal of Plato (anamnesis). He believed it up until the year 410 A.D. Would you say that was a crisis year? We are quoting a study by De Wulf here, "After that he hesitates and does not cease to hesitate between traducianism and creationism." Traducianism is the doctrine that the spirit and the body were created at the same moment. Creationism is that they were created separately and then were joined together. Traductio means "carried over together." Incidentally, we have to do something to make this more photogenic. I'm going to bring a visual display next time. Oh, it will stagger you. It will be a most unusual book.

So St. Augustine hesitates between traducianism and creationism, though he preferred the latter because it explained the transmission of the original sin. "He repeats endlessly," says this writer, "that he has not yet made up his mind."

This is typical; he never made up his mind. This is interesting because St. Augustine is the maker of western theology. The Protestants are as dependent on Augustine as the Catholics. But he never made up his mind. His mother wouldn't let him, for one thing. He was a very pious and moral young man, but his mother wouldn't let him marry the mother of his illegitimate child. He had a child to whom he was very devoted, but his mother said, "No, you will ruin your career chances." This was St. Monica. She would say, "Put it off; don't get married yet because when you've had your pleasure, then will be the time that you can repent and get forgiven and carry on. Meantime, don't let a family interfere with your career." And he tells us quite frankly that the objection to sex was not sex at all. It was the danger of having children. Sex was okay if you didn't have a family. This was the great St. Augustine. He says "Ã‚Â…think of having squalling children and the smell of cooking cabbage and having to change diapers and shrieking wives in the house, short patience, etc. You have to be free to concentrate every thought on God. Think always on God and nothing else." You must cling to God regardless of what and keep him constantly in mind; nothing but God. In that case you can't bother about marriage because it is a distraction. It's marriage that's bad, not letting off steam once in awhile. He thought that was all right, and he did. When he was seventy years old, he tells us in the Confessions that this was his daily prayer. "God give me chastity and continence, but not yet. Sed non modo, let me wait awhile." In other words, he wanted to have his cake and eat it. This was the purpose of rhetoric; this was the whole spirit of the times he lived in, the fourth and fifth centuries. He was amazed when he saw the whole civilization collapse before his eyes. He couldn't understand why it was so. But I think he may have contributed to it as much as anyone.

Well, he believes in the preexistence perhaps, following Cicero and Apuleius. But in 394 A.D. he first rejected the idea, popular with the Gnostics, that the soul sinned in the preexistence. He gave that up first, and then he gave up the preexistence entirely. But he didn't condemn the hypothesis of preexistence itself at that time. "In short," says this particular study, "Augustine truly does not know. It is perhaps temerity to want to penetrate a mystery reserved to God himself," he says.

Then there was an official statement by the church. There was a synod held in Africa in 523 A.D. by a number of bishops. They were in exile from Sardinia, actually. They sent a synodal epistle around stating this is the answer to the question: "The question we should either leave in silence or consider without contention since the holy scriptures give no clear statement." There's the idea, you see, many precious things had been removed. The schools turned Plato around. The Neoplatonists got rid of that part of Plato's teaching. They didn't like that at all. The epistle continues, "It should be investigated with caution, especially since it's possible for the faithful to ignore it without disadvantage."

Incidentally, when we quote these councils of the church, and there have been many of them, we have the original records and notes edited by Nursia and the great Mansi. We have the complete Mansi here. Now Mansi is a complete record of all the councils of the church that [p.4] were held (the Greek and Latin), what was said, who presided at the meeting, what was done. For these times we have remarkably full records. We know everything that went on in the church at that time-bishops that were questioned, troubles that came up in wards, etc. And they go into tens of thousands of pages. We have them all here under the collection of Mansi. If you want to read the edited version, though it is many volumes, that's the Hefele. Professor Jaeger, who is supposed to be the best classical scholar of his time, told me, "I always read my Mansi in Hefele." It's easier to manage. If you are interested in any council of the church, you can find out about it there. They are chronologically arranged, and they take place at different places. You find some most interesting things going on.

So they said it could be ignored. You don't have to worry about the preexistence necessarily. This was a teaching that had enormous impact. It makes all the difference on how we look at our situation on earth, doesn't it? Brigham Young said that more Latter-day Saints apostatize because of the doctrine of preexistence than for any other reason-more than polygamy, more than the trials and hardships, more than the teaching of the Law of Consecration. This comes as a surprise and shows how completely it had been wiped out of the Christian consciousness. What turned people off was this very rational doctrine of preexistence which is being accepted much more widely. The churches are taking a hard second look at it now because it is very essential to understanding our situation. But it was not in the cards in the nineteenth century.

And back to our old friend Augustine again. We have all the works of Augustine. There are a lot of them, and they are enormously influential. Remember, Luther himself was an Augustinian friar. It was the doctrine of anamnesis, the intimations of immortality, that was supposed to have moved Augustine who first accepted it and finally rejected it, as I said. In his book of Retractions, one, eight, and two, he says, "The trouble is it does not agree with science." Notice, it's not the scriptures, but science. Science does not prove the preexistence. That's why St. Augustine rejected it. That's the sort of thing you can only know by revelation and intuition, I suppose. As Father Eggersdorfer says in a very good study, he does not reject it on scriptural or religious grounds, but only because he finds it illogical. He says it's not a scientific doctrine. On the contrary, what bothers him is that the scriptures keep bringing it up. "Revelations 4:6 talks about familiar animals found around the throne of God," he says in his epistle number 124.

Incidentally, we have 195 letters of St. Augustine. He was the most important man in the church. He was the bishop of Hippo, and everybody sent letters to him asking questions. We have the answers he gave to these questions. And the funny thing is when they ask him basic questions of doctrine and marriage, and things like that, he never refers them to any higher authority. He doesn't say go to Rome, go to Milan, or go to the head of the church. He knows no head of the church to go to at that time. There was no uniform church. Of course, the Roman church claims it was their own; everybody else does. But St. Augustine didn't know of that clearing house. He doesn't say, "Take your doctrines to a higher source."

Never once refers them to a higher source. And very often he says, "I can't answer your question, and there is nothing we can do about it. The question stumps me."

And his friend Jerome was living in the East. Remember, Jerome gave us the Vulgate, the Latin Bible, which is the official Bible of the Roman Catholic Church and the one that was used more in translating the King James than any other. Luther stayed close to it. He cites the example, along with John Chrysostom, that Paul knew everything that existed before this world. But it cannot be uttered. What is harder than to find out what the beginning or the end of a thing is? Moses knew and so did Solomon who says, "I shall remember the things from the beginning of the world." But they don't tell us what it is. In the end Augustine dismisses it with a one-liner, a very famous one-liner that Luther uses about the preexistence. It's a nervous and unsatisfactory solution. "What was God doing before the creation?" he says. "Making a hell for people who ask that question." Too many questions, he says. Then he adds his own solution in the Confessions, eleven and twelve: "I don't know because I don't know." Well, there we have to leave it.

And again here, Bousset, the great Catholic preacher, says Clement of Alexandria (third century) expressly rejected the old doctrine of preexistence (note that he says the old doctrine) because it conflicted not with the scriptures but with Greek philosophy and culture. Everything must be tested by the schools, later on by Aristotle. You do not change the doctrine to agree with the scriptures. You change the scriptures to agree with Plato. This was done all the time. If you had a conflict between Plato and the scriptures, the scriptures were always adapted. There was this constant accommodation. That's why they were able to get along with the schoolmen and the advancement of science as they understood it. They did not accommodate the new philosophy to the scriptures, but the other way around. It shows which was the real authority. It was not the scriptures but the Greek philosophy and culture. Clement mocks the old-fashioned orthodoxers, as he calls them, and their fear of the higher culture and new advanced education. In other words, they were fundamentalists, and he said, "We can't change that."

The final decision was not with Plato and the Stoics though, but with Aristotle. Here we are quoting a new study by Florovsky on this subject. This is Aristotle: "God has given independent being and life neither to the nature of soul itself nor to the nature of body separately but rather to men composed of soul and body. They cannot be separated. The soul and body cannot exist apart." You cannot have had preexistence. Well, what happens after you die then? I guess that's pretty near to Seventh-Day Adventist doctrine. They teach soul sleep. You go to sleep, become completely unconscious until the resurrection. The soul has no awareness or consciousness, or in other words no existence. So what have you gained by that? You start up where you ended and continue right on. Soul sleep is completely pointless doctrine. It's like your odorless perfume. You cease to exist, but you cease to be aware of the fact that you are not existing. Then all of a sudden there you are. It might have been a million years; it might have been ten minutes. Who cares? It makes no difference. You just weren't there.

This is some doctrine. I don't know how you can build great faith on that.

Severus of Antioch says, "In the fashioning of men the rational soul is not created before the body nor the body before the soul." And so it goes. Lactantius, the first Latin father, mocks the doctrine of preexistence as a Stoic and Pythagorean doctrine. "Oh, wonderful and singular Pythagorean memory of another life. Oh, miserable us in our total oblivion. Why don't we know that we lived before?" he asks. He is being very caustic here.

In a very early writing, Philastrius says, "It is also heresy to say that before the body of man was created there was no spirit." It's heresy to say that the spirit was created before the body, but it is also heresy to say that before the body, there was no spirit (well, now what are you going to do about that?), "Ã‚Â…and that man is in the image of God's body and not the spirit of man. When the prophet says that God said 'let us make man,' it was future as in making not of flesh but of the spirit which he declared to be rational. And the male and female has nothing to do with the sex of corporeal matter but of the rule of the spirit." And so it goes on and on.

Jerome says, "If we accept that doctrine, it will destroy the whole allegorical value of the scripture," which is that these things happened spiritually not physically. "What Origen said was foolish. The angels have their degrees not by merit but by creation. The angels had no preexistence either. It is a depraved and wicked Gentile error when Origen says the spirit of man partakes of the nature of divinity."

Well, the definition of what is divine is what's eternal. The eternal is divine; the divine is eternal. That covers everything beautifully if you think of it, but he says, "It is a depraved and wicked Gentile error when Origen says the spirit of man partakes of the nature of divinity, as if the maker and the made could be of the same nature. As if they could have anything at all in common. It is utterly vicious to make God in any way like man." So how are you going to discuss God or say anything about him? Then Jerome is going to tell all about what God is like after saying you can't possibly define him. Chrysostom does the same. If Jerome's statement, "it is utterly vicious to make God in any way like man," were so, then upon becoming incorruptible there's only one conclusion to be drawn. They would become as gods who were not so before. That amounts to making created things resemble God. Now such were the absolutes of the fourth century schools. No created thing can resemble God. They deal in absolutes all the way. And when you have an absolute, there's no argument at all. As Copernicus said, "There is no proportion between the infinite and the finite." There is nothing you can say that they have in common. There's nothing you can say about one that will apply to the other, so forget about it.

Well, you would think this would put an end to thought, bring down the curtain, and give us a few years of peace. Don't believe it for a minute. It's just the sort of thing the philosophers love to feed on. You could hold endless committee meetings on a subject like this. hundreds

They had hundreds of councils and fought like cats and dogs. It kept them from being bored like the academic feuds of the universities, ancient and modern. They start these feuds going and they go for hundreds of years. They are the only things that make life interesting. Someone wrote a very good essay on that subject. He wrote The Life of Queen Victoria, etc. It was the famous argument between Dr. Arnold and Dr. Bentley at Oxford which went on for almost a century. The author's main argument is against the idea that Adam and Eve came down and took bodies as punishment, the favorite Gnostic and Origenistic idea. We don't go with that either.

All right, let's get it official now. The Fifth General Council of the church in the year 593 made this statement which Christians stick to, to this day. It's issue number 204. They argued 203 problems for days and days before they got to this one. It was just like a state legislature or any other legislature.

"If anyone says or holds that the psyche (soul) of the Lord existed before and was united with God before his incarnation from the Virgin and his earthly birth, let him be anathema." He will be cursed if he claims that even Christ existed before he came down and was born to the Virgin. Now isn't that a statement? Christ says he was with the Father and left his glory to come down. "The glory I had with thee before the world was." All that counts for nothing here. It's the doctrines of the schools that count. "If anyone holds that the psyche of the Lord existed before and was united with God before his incarnation from the Virgin and his earthly birth, let him be anathema." That statement you will find in Denzinger. Incidentally, he is a very convenient source. This is the official statement as it stands at present of all the doctrines of the Roman Catholic Church. Look it up in Denzinger. This is in Denzinger Versus Origen 593, number 204. They are in that order.

And Father Danielou, the last person to treat this, died recently under very strange circumstances. He was a cardinal and a very able man. He saw very clearly the weakness of the position that the Christian churches take today, how weak they are in ignoring these things and passing them by. He says the weak point in Origen's theory is his idea that there is more than one existence. What, no afterlife? He cites Canon One of the Fifth General Council in 593, a key date: "If anyone teaches the mythical doctrine of the preexistence of the soul and return to the Father's presence [' that they may again return to our presence and have everlasting life'], and the doctrine that follows from it, let him be anathema [wiped out of the books, not considered, cursed]." And they were teaching it.

Methodius, a person who enjoys great prestige among the doctors, says Plato and Origen were all wrong "because it is against nature for a soul to exist without a body or a body without a spirit. You have to have them both." In other words they don't believe in spirits. Well, if you had to figure these things out rationally, we would never have arrived at them. And in this treatise he treats the resurrection from every angle. He treats it from Jonah, Moses, and Elijah on the mountain, but he never once makes reference to the resurrection of the Lord. Isn't that something? That's in Methodius, the eighteenth volume of the Patrologia, if any of you are [p.8] burning with curiosity. So the early church believed in the preexistence; the later churches and the official churches today do not believe in it. But we do because it was restored by Joseph Smith. Incidentally, the most beautiful and moving description of this is the oldest. I brought the text here, but I haven't been into Syriac for a long time. So I'm going to use translations. Five different people have made translations besides me, so all I can do is pass the buck to them. I compare the various translations carefully. This is a thing called The Pearl. It was found in 1906 by Rendel Harris way up east of the Tigris. Its dating is interesting because Alfred Adam, a Catholic scholar and very able Orientalist who lives in Jerusalem, dates it between 50 and 70 A.D. That means it could have been written within 25 years of the Crucifixion. Walter Crum, who is the great authority on Coptic documents, places it right there in the first century. You can't get an older Christian document than this. The Pearl is meant to be a romanticized thesis, something like the story Added Upon. Now we have rather mediocre (that's a flattering word) musicals about the preexistence. We sentimentalize it. I don't know how much the author of The Pearl cleared on this, but it is the oldest Christian hymn we have and it is certainly the most remarkable.

It starts out, "In my first primeval childhood (he's in heaven, you see, before he came here), I was nurtured (quoting Eliza R. Snow, you recognize) in the royal house of my father with loving care in the midst of abundance and glory." This is where he came from. "My parents sent me forth from our home in the East, the source of light, supplied with all necessities." So he comes down to get the pearl and bring it back with him. The pearl is the integrity which he preserves during the trials and tests of this life so he can continue hereafter to be a member of the royal family and assume his throne and crown above. "Ã‚Â…making a packet for me from our rich storehouse. It was sizable but light enough for me to carry. I came from the house on high as a great treasure, bringing with me precious stones from India and Kushan and gold and silver, and they girt me with a diamond.

There's an interesting footnote on adamus, on the diamond, here. You see, the word Adam was associated with diamond. Adamantis means that which cannot be damaged, that which is immortal. There's a long story behind that. Diamond is the urim and thummim, the sacred stone that was given to Adam when he came down. So the name Adamantis was given to him-the prince of stones.

Continuing with The Pearl: "They removed from me the garment of light which they made for me above." The idea is that when you lived in heaven you had a heavenly garment; and when you left, it was put aside, carefully folded and put under the Father's throne to be kept there until you came back if you were worthy to receive it again. A good deal is said in this Syriac stuff, for example, about the great tragedy of sinning in this life because you lose all that you invested. What you have up there represents long terms of investment and work to achieve such a state. You left it all there on reserve, on deposit, the crown and the garment carefully folded and deposited (in the throne, some say) in a special chest, for you to resume when you return. And if you can't take it back, you have lost all that you worked for before, which makes this earthly trial a key experience, a terrifying sort of thing if we miss it. You can miss it, and it's [p.9] so important not to. Continuing: "They removed from me the garment of light which they had made for me and my purple robe made exactly to fit me." And it would fit no one else. This is interesting. It is your robe, and it has certain marks on it. "They made a covenant with me and wrote it in my heart lest I go astray." So you have covenanted with your parents above in this test. He was instructed to go down to Egypt and bring back the pearl-this integrity. It's in the sea guarded or surrounded by a fire or poisonous, snorting serpent-the dragon. This was the dragon or the serpent of the Garden of Eden guarding this thing. But he has to get it away from him. He has to outwit the dragon and get the pearl. "Returning the pearl, he may resume his garment of light and his outer priestly mantle and become heir to our kingdom along with our Second One." The Second One is the second heir to the throne. He will become an heir along with the Son. Not the second born, it says, but the next or second in line to the throne, second in glory, successor. That is the first born, Christ, the elder brother.

So if you perform properly when you go down there, you can come back and resume your garment and "become heir to our kingdom along with our Second One." So when he comes down, he has two guides or guardians. Remember they always come in threes. They are official couriers who know the way and are sent down with him up to the point where they bid him goodbye and he is on his own. Remember, the two guides say, "We will leave you now, but we will return later." Then the minute Adam finds himself alone, that is the time Satan strikes and tries to win him over. That's what happens here. The two guardians are sent with him. That's standard; in the eighteenth chapter of Genesis, the Lord appears with the three men, always the three.

He arrived at Maishan where the merchants of the East gather. This is the dividing line where the goods are all transshipped, like the upper or second cataract on the Nile where all the goods of central Africa are transshipped to go into Egypt. It was a new world in those days. In fact, at the cataract Senuseret III, 2000 B.C., set up a famous monument which still stands. The people south of it may not go north of this line, and the others may not go south of it.
