Then the calling of the mission of the Son and the plan, both of which were kept secret until then, were revealed to the elect before they came here. It was revealed again in the Book of Mormon.

It's not too much to say that the dominant theme of the Thanksgiving Hymn, the Hodayot, is the ecstatic contemplation of the wonder of man's participation in the heavenly affairs back to the beginning. The most wonderful of the Dead Sea Scrolls is a collection of thanksgiving hymns. Now here is hymn six or hymn F from the Hodayot scrolls published in the early 1950s: "Thou hast caused me to mount up to an eternal height and to walk in an inconceivable exaltation, and I know that there is a hope for everyone whom thou didst form of the dust in the presence of the eternal assembly, and that the sinful spirit whom thou hast purified of great sin may be counted with the hosts of the saints and enter the society of the congregation of the sons of heaven. Thou didst appoint unto man an eternal share with the spirits that know, to praise thy name in joyful unison with them and to recount thy wondrous works in the presence thereof." This is the great creation hymn it's referring to when the sons of God shouted and the morning stars sang together.

Well, the whole point of this is that man actually belongs by prior appointment to that community of the elect who share in the knowledge of the plan and who shouted for joy at the foundation of the world. In the hymn preceding this, God is hailed as the Prince of the Gods and King of the venerable ones. And we remind ourselves that this is neither a Gnostic nor a pagan production. This was orthodox Judaism back in those early days.

In the first chapter and twenty-fourth verse of the Clementine Recognitions when he meets Peter, Peter tells him of the plan of God which he announced as his own will and desire in the presence of the first angels and which he established as an eternal law for all. He gave it to them as the law in the beginning. In one of the Coptic texts they give it the characteristic Gnostic twist. "My father, the joyful glorious light," says the Psalm of Thomas, "summoned all the eons of peace [the first angels have now become mere abstractions; they are simply the eons of peace] all his sons and all his angels and established them that they might rejoice in his greatness and share it. All bowed the knee before him in saying his praises together, hailing him as the illuminator of worlds." It's the same picture, but this is a very early group in Egypt. Were they interpreting this mystically? Whatever it was, they knew about the council. This is the very same picture we get from the Clementines where it says that he summoned all the angels of peace, all his sons and all the angels and established them that they might rejoice in his greatness. Remember verse 39 in the first chapter of Moses, "For behold, this is my work and my glory-to bring to pass the immortality and eternal life of man."

Then there's the newly discovered Creation Apocryphon. Incidentally, we have all these Coptic texts here. And, fortunately, Coptic is not the most difficult language in the world. It was never required of ministers until 1950 at least. I recommend Coptic because it's fun. It hangs together beautifully, and it's not hard. But anyway the Coptic text tells us "on that day began the discussion in which gods, angels, and men participated [when they were to discuss the creation] and the decisions of the discussion were then carried out by gods, angels and men [' we will go down'] and they saw that their plan was being followed." This is all told in the Book of Abraham, as you know, third and fourth chapters. "But Prince Jabub did not understand the power of faith."

Again, what do we read in the Book of Moses where it says Satan sought to destroy the plan of the world for he knew not the mind of God. That's the most encouraging thing in the scriptures to me. Satan has a terrific game going and he wins hands down all the time, but he knows not the mind of God, so he's not going to win. "But Price Jabub did not understand the power of faith and so was denied authority over matter which the others shared." That's a very interesting quotation. The power of faith, it will be recalled, was the power, we are told, by which the worlds were created. So we get the same picture from the Coptic source and from the Dead Sea Scrolls source. They were written about the same time.

And then the earliest writings after the New Testament were the seven apostolic fathers. The last of them to be discovered was the Pastor of Hermas. The unimpeachable Pastor of Hermas is quite as specific. This is in 120 A.D. Hermas was the brother of the bishop of Rome supposedly. This is a lamentation on the speed with which the apostasy has overtaken the church. Everything is going down the drain, and it's a cry of distress with the others, Barnabas and Ignatius. In 2 Clement, he's standing on a headland watching a friend swimming out at sea. The friend is getting farther and farther away from shore. He's calling to the church, "Come back before it is too late. You're going in the wrong direction." At last his voice is carried away by the wind. It's very much like the last prophets in the Book of Mormon and the Jaredites.

So the Pastor of Hermas says, "Behold God constructing the world in accordance with the great council [some manuscripts have 'the most honored council'], creating the beautiful world and turning it over to his chosen ones that he might carry out his promise to them which he gave them in the midst of great glory and rejoicing; that is if they kept his laws which they accept in great faith." Well, there's the situation exactly: "we will prove them herewith." It will become a time of probation. The plan is placed before them. They vote on it, etc. They would return to his presence and partake of eternal life if they keep his laws. It was to be a test and probation. This life became a time of probation, as Nephi said, which they accepted in great faith. They all voted to come down.

The Mandaean version (very old work from Mesopotamia) is very interesting because it calls the creator Ptah-il, gives him an Egyptian name, combining the archaic Egyptian and the Semitic names. While giving the familiar account of the great council, it adds the important detail that three messengers were sent down to supervise the work and to instruct Adam; these three being glorious angels who were later to live upon the earth as ordinary mortals and prophets. They were to be apostles later on. You find that text in Leipoldt.

I'm going to skip to something very old now. Here's this Eliade. I mentioned him before. He has written many books on this. Just a number of writings to generalize here. Mircea Eliade is Romanian, I think. He was at Swift at the Theological School in Chicago when I was over in the Oriental Institute, but we never met. He had a student who was LDS, and he asked where I was teaching, where this Nibley character was. He said, "He's over at the Oriental Institute just across the quad." But the people at the Oriental Institute never talk to the people in the Theology School. That was Swift Hall, the library. I was doing some very interesting stuff in Swift Library there. It's supposed to be the theological seminary for Chicago, and they had no Catholic books or journals. We have a great collection of them here, one of the best in the country. It's better than you will find at Notre Dame. Somebody gave Notre Dame eight million dollars for books, and they spent every penny of it on books about Dante. I remember it made my friend Frank Barry so furious because they spent eight million on one writer alone. So the people in the two never met. But in this article on cosmogonic myths, he compares them. His business is prehistoric shamanism and world religions.

You can get a very good comparison of world religions, throughout the world, beginning with so-called primitives and right down to the big religions today. It's the collection of Joseph Campbell called Masks of the Gods. He was here three years ago and gave some very fine talks, and we had some nice talks with him. If you want to review the general situation of world religions from the beginning, you can't do better. The book is very readable, and there are at least three volumes out.

But Eliade says this, "Wherever you look, you will always find this central myth of the creation which describes the beginning of the world, including the origin of plants, animals and man; and marriage, family and death. Taken all together, these myths in origin constitute a fairly coherent history." So wherever you go throughout the world you will find about the same story. We are not talking at this time about the process of creation. We are just talking about the council.

W. G. Lambert is perhaps the foremost surviving Babylonian scholar. He is the biggest editor of Babylonian texts today. He says, "The first major conclusion is that the epic of creation is a norm of Babylonian or Sumerian cosmology (you'll find it there), and it's a sectarian aberration." We find these things spread around everywhere.

Hermann Grapow is the editor of five big volumes of the Egyptian dictionary. It took him years. There are three million entries in it. He worked with Erman. It was financed by the Kaiser in Berlin and it went for years and years. It is still the standard Egyptian dictionary. He has written in this on the world before the creation. If you turn to the Egyptian text on the subject, you will find exact parallels from the Old Testament; from the Enuma Elish, the old Babylonian epic of creation; from the Rig-Veda, which is the Indo-European work. In 1700 B.C. it entered into India, but before then it was up in central Asia. Sanskrit is the related language to it. So you will find this both in the religions of Buddhism and Hinduism. And also the Wessobrunner prayer in Old High German. You will find it in the sagas, and you will find it in the Voluspa. Gylfaginning is a marvelous one if you go into the sagas. Their account of the creation is something. And always it's with the council here and their discussion. In the Lokasenna is one of the best descriptions of the council. Loki, you see, is Satan who will not accept the plan. He comes in and denounces it. He starts exposing the private lives of all the gods, and they don't like that. They keep saying to him, "Shut up. Don't tell us any more. We don't want to hear that." So he is cast out. But notice the point is that these are international. You will find it in the Pyramid Texts in the Book of the Dead, in the Greek and Roman temple inscriptions, etc.

These lines from Hesiod's Theogony are good. This is the oldest Greek story of the creation-the beginning of the gods. It's very much in line with the rest of them; some Enoch literature. Some would place him before Homer, but his date is easy to remember. It's 777. He's is supposed to have reached his prime in the year 777 B.C. But I think Milton must have lifted this line here: He hails the muse and asks the muse to teach him. "Tell me how first in the beginning the gods and the earth came into existence, or how first these things were planted down on earth and the gods set up court on Olympus." But that's almost exactly the way Milton puts it.

Of Man's first disobedience, and the fruit

of that forbidden tree whose mortal taste

brought death into the world, and all our woe,

Sing Heavenly Muse, that on the secret top

of Oreb, or of Sinai, didst inspire

That shepherd who first taught the chosen seed

In the beginning how the Heavens and Earth

Rose out of Chaos: or, if Sion hill

Delight thee more, and Siloa's brook that flowed

Fast by the oracle of God, I thence

Invoke thy aid to my adventurous song,

That with no middle flight intend to soar

Above th' Aonian mount, while it pursues

Things unattempted yet in prose or rhyme.

See, he calls the muse to instruct him how things began in the beginning, and this is the way the epic story and the creation stories begin. Almost the identical words are used in Hesiod. We mentioned last time that Milton was condemned because in his work his angels were too literal or too physical. But the oldest Greek account we have starts out "quoting Milton" word for word. What do you know about that?

We don't have much time, but I'm going to talk about the oldest book in the world. We mentioned it before. I suppose we should have a closeup of this. Can you all see it from here? It's reproduced here; it's rather tiny. This is the Memphite Theology or the Shabako Stone. I think it was Breasted's greatest achievement to discover the importance of this. If you want to know about it, read Breasted's book called The Origins of Religion (of course, it's long out of date now; 1912). I say this is the oldest book in the world, and what a religious document it is. Do you expect this to be primitive mumbo jumbo, a lot of hocus pocus, wonders, and miracles, etc.? Ah, just wait. Shabako was the third king of the twenty-fifth dynasty. Interesting relationships to the Book of Mormon here. His name means "wild cat," and he came to the throne about 716 B.C. You say is this the oldest in the world. Ah, just wait. The twenty-fifth dynasty was founded by Kashta, and then his son was Piankhi. It's very funny. The twenty-first dynasty was founded by a person called Korihor whose son was Piankhi. That's a very funny name; you don't invent a thing like that. It wasn't discovered until the 1870s that Piankhi is a name that we have in the Book of Mormon. Korihor was a priest of Amon who usurped the power of the state. His son Piankhi became king. In the Book of Mormon when they set up the old-fashioned courts, they bring in a man called Korihor. Alma says, "This is the first time this has happened since the old world." They restored these things. You start comparing these names and some amazing names turn up. They certainly impressed Professor Albright.

Well, they were Ethiopian kings. The one that followed after him was Shebitka. Then comes the famous Taharqa. He practically delivered Jerusalem from the Assyrians. So this dynasty was held in great esteem. Their names would have been adopted in Jerusalem by the Jews. This interloping dynasty had championed Israel. We're not going to get into the Book of Mormon here. The thing is that to celebrate the founding of this dynasty and to justify and sanctify it, he decided to restore the oldest temple in Egypt-the original temple, the one that was built to celebrate the founding of the first dynasty by Menes. It was the white temple at Memphis. He was to restore it and perform the same rites and ordinances (they didn't know about this at the time) that were performed when the kingdom of Egypt was originally found. The kingdom was "founded" by Menes in about 3100 B.C. But at that time it was very old. See, he was founding the order of God on earth, the celestial order on earth. He built the temple, and there they rehearsed in dramatic form the Council in Heaven, the creation of the earth, and the fall and redemption, and all these things in this document. Well, when Shabako wanted to renew the order of things and reestablish the ancient practices (which were very ancient at the time of Menes, Dynasty I), he decided to renovate the temple, and in the process in the cornerstone they discovered a document. It was a rolled-up scroll of leather. And he tells us about it here. It was the script of a play performed at the installation of the first king of Egypt. The dedication of the first temple that united Egypt to celebrate the beginning of the new rule, a new dynasty, a new nation, a new age of the world. And it was held, of course, at the new year.

Their play begins with the Council in Heaven and the controversy over the right to dominion with Horus winning over Seth-being recognized as the first born, the sole heir and the opener of the ways. More than a third of the text is missing. That's the middle part. That's where a column stood. It was found at Memphis, and this is the copy that was made at that time. But it was brought to the British Museum way back in 1805 or 1807. See, the French went into Egypt, and then the English came and took over, and they took this to the British Museum. It was very worn so they had difficulty reading it, and various attempts were made. Then new sophisticated methods of lighting and a much better knowledge of old Egyptian grammar (because it turns out that it was in the archaic form of the language), enabled Sethe to produce in 1929 the definitive text. So we have the text of it here, and we know what it says. It is a marvelous document which we will read the next time.

Lecture 7

The Council

It would seem that a lot more has survived than we supposed in Egypt from the Pearl of Great Price. (I have an honest cold now, not just that everlasting allergy. I've been kissing the baby too much.) We were talking about the Shabako Stone which is usually called the Memphite Theology. I try to avoid it, but I can't; it's such a very important writing. The latest study of it is by Zabkar who has said in the J.N.E.S., thirteenth volume, "One of the most important documents of the entire Egyptian literature, which originated at the very beginning of Egyptian history, is the document of Memphite Theology." We mentioned that the Shabako Stone was found by the French and brought to the British Museum way back in 1805. It has just been interpreted since the 1920s. But it is the most important document we have from all of English literature, as far as we can conceivably go back. "A supreme god and creator, in this case Ptah, created the whole universe and all it contains by conception of his mind and the commanding efference of his will. The impact of the Memphite Theology was so fundamental that its effect on the Egyptian religious thought remained constant until the end of Egyptian religion." This was the dominant text. If there was a Bible, this would be it. This was their Genesis, their Torah.

"Unparalleled in the history of the ancient Orient as far as its cosmogenic significance is concerned, it deals not only with the creation, but it deals with the cosmogony." And, of course, this is our Pearl of Great Price, especially the Book of Abraham and the Book of Moses. They are both very strong on cosmogony, as you know-Moses being a re-editing of Abraham, as we will see later. It's not cosmological explanation of the cosmos, but cosmogonic-how the cosmos came to be. "It traveled from century to century from one theological system to another. Its theme sounds from the first line of Genesis." We have it in our Genesis: Bereshit bara Adonai, etc.

Joseph had it right too because he said it was the council that met at the beginning. That's what we are talking about, the council here. "Ã‚Â…from the first line of Genesis, from there on through the Old Testament and the latest period of Hebrew literature. It reaches the pages of the New Testament witnessing to what extent this conception of creative power of the Word of God persisted in the ancient Orient, becoming a universal theological theme." It goes right on down into the New Testament. "While the Memphite Theology through the Old Testament prepared the consciousness of the religious world for John's logos doctrine about the manifestation and creative power of the Word of God, so the Amarna Theology sounded some themes which in the New Testament, especially in John's gospel, represent fundamental features of the relationship between the Father and the Son."

Now all of this is in the Shabako text. Remember we said that Shabako was the third king of the twenty-fifth dynasty. After Kashta comes Piankhi, and then Shabako is his younger brother.

His name means "wild cat." It's funny, in this dynasty the names very often refer to cats of various sorts. Shabako began to reign about 716 B.C. That comes very late, but remember when he wanted to legitimatize the dynasty, he decided to go through all the ordinances and renew the Temple of the White Wall (the oldest temple where the Egyptian kingdom was founded). Here was Memphis right at the base of the Delta near Cairo, and the Red Sea comes down here. This is the Sinai Peninsula-carries right on up into the Negev. And here's the Dead Sea up here. But this is where it all centered here-where the two lands reunited, the north land and the south land. There's a very interesting account of how the serpents were driven from the land. And great heaps of them were piled up and set afire. That was the only way they could get rid of them. They were a health hazard and there was the foul stench. Remember, it was swamp country. To occupy it, they had to overcome these serpents all the time. Now Elise Baumgartel, who made the main study on this particular subject, says that in the earliest time they preserved all the land northward as a refuge for game and for cattle grazing. It was not farmed; it was practically not populated. She's wrong there, but she gives us a picture with various documents as to how they spared one part of the land for farming (the very rich Nile valley) and the other part was for game and hunting. They loved to hunt. Their hunting was in the marshes; they didn't hunt in the back country. The kings of the eighteenth dynasty went into Asia and hunted lions and elephants. They loved that sort of thing. But all the great hunting through the early dynasties was done in the marsh lands.

And remember in the Book of Ether, King Lib did the same sort of thing. After he had driven out the serpents (it rained and the serpents went away) then he divided the land into two parts. He kept the land south as a refuge for game, and he kept all the land northward as a land under cultivation. That was the populated country. They used the same system that was used in pre-dynastic times in Egypt. So this is what Shabako wants to revive now. He is going to have the center here which is called the mekhat tawy, "the balance of the two lands." It's written with a balance because the two are going to be equal. This is a very important theme in this document here.

I notice Klaus Koch, writing recently, says the only priestly mythological texts in Egypt (there are only four of them) that are important are this one and the other version called "The Victory over Seth," which I have a complete copy of and which is very good too. This was an ordinance that had to be performed in all the temples every day. And it's just this same story. This lasts right down through to the end of Egyptian religion and was taken up by the Hermetics and taken to Italy with the mysteries; also in the Old Testament and the New Testament.

I was going to have a couple of slides ready to present this today. It might help to see the characters, etc., and explain what is what here. They were supposed to have them last Friday, but they were lost. That's the second time they have been lost. They should be ready this week, so next time I may be able to expose you to these. As I showed you what it looks like, this one is too faint, and this one they reduced in size in order to get it onto one piece. But this was recopied, and he tells us how it was done.

In the top there's an inscription telling us what king this is, how it was found, and under what circumstances it comes down to us. Whenever the name Shabako is mentioned, somebody has scrubbed it out-every time. Another dynasty-because remember this was a Nubian dynasty; they came up there-and they were succeeded by the twenty-sixth dynasty which was a brilliant revival. That was Semitic with Necho. The next dynasty is the time of the Book of Mormon. But it's interesting that the Book of Mormon proper names all come from this dynasty which was much more favorable to Israel. There are some "dillies" in the Book of Mormon. They couldn't possibly have been invented. They came out of this dynasty. So it begins.

"His majesty had this copied in stone." You see, he found it. If we had the sign here, we would show you why it meant that. Companies come back and repeat and write over again. It's really quite graphic. This is written phonetically, but never without a determinant to show what we are talking about, the general idea, which makes it possible to guess. Without that you would really be at a loss. "His majesty commanded this writing to be copied anew in the house of his father Ptah."

Now Ptah is the god of Memphis. That's where this is. Memphis is here; Cairo is here, and right across the river you can see the pyramid of Zoser there at Saqqarah. Everything gathers together there. But he found it in the house of his father Ptah. (We will see that Ptah is very important here. We are going to see a lot of him.) "Ã‚Â…who is the lord south of the wall." That expression "south of the wall." It is divided into two parts. Around the circuit of the wall, the southern part belonged to his temple and belonged to him. The white kingdom, the older kingdom, was the southern one. The northern kingdom, the red kingdom, was the other kingdom. And this dividing is a very important thing. You get it very interestingly in the patriarchal romances of the Old Testament. It comes out all over the place. We are going to find mention of that here; namely, the reference to these megalithic centers. This was an old megalithic center way back here. See, Heliopolis is right near here. That's just the airport right up here. They're all together here, just north of Cairo. In fact, it's a suburb of Cairo. And there Mariette found it long ago. It has been destroyed. They took all the rocks to use for ballast for railroad tracks. The British came in, so all that went. But it was a tremendous megalithic foundation. I mean a circle of 365 stones. It was one of those centers which you find all over the world, we mentioned the last time. Professor Thom has correlated these things; even Professor Edwards has come around to realizing the significance of that.

"Ã‚Â…whose house is the one south of the wall. When his majesty found it as it had been produced by the ancestors in the earliest period." By the people who came at the very beginning of everything. So it was ancient when it was written originally, and it was written to celebrate the founding of Egypt, the first dynasty by Menes. You remember Menes, the first king. He founded the dynasty, and to celebrate it he built this temple. And the whole purpose of this is to justify the legitimacy of the king's rule on earth. Does he have God's approval and the right to rule? How does it come that God set up his rule on earth? This is dramatized-the Council in Heaven, the creation, Satan's rebellion, the fall, and the plan of salvation.

It's all set forth there quite clearly. As I said, it has remained the basic document. Is Menes the same as Narmer of the pre-dynastic palette? Some people think it was. Some people, even H. R. Hall, thought he was the same as Minos of Crete because it was a sea empire as well. The Minotaur was his pet, and the Ipis bull was Menes' pet. It goes on here: "When he found it, he found that it was the record of the ancestors." You think of Lehi looking at the Brass Plates and discovering they went back to the beginning. He was quite thrilled by it. "Unfortunately, it had been eaten by worms so that it could not be read in the beginning parts and in the ending parts." And then we have a blank here because, as I said, a column stood on here for thousands of years, so that blotted out the middle part. But we do have the beginning and the conclusion here, which I guess are the most important anyway. If a play has a good beginning and a good ending, you can forget about the middle. No, the middle is very interesting. It's the Garden of Eden story very obviously, but we start out with the others. "Then his majesty had it copied anew, more beautiful than it was in the first place for the purpose that it should not pass away again." He had it done on hard stone. But the main purpose is that his name should endure. It's very important to have your name endure-not only the king's but everybody's. See the Egyptians are very different from the Orientals here. You don't go into a great sea of being after this. You don't wish to be non-identified. You don't wish to absorbed into the nothing. The Egyptian is always dead set on preserving his individuality.

Say, I have some statements by Brigham Young on that that are rather interesting (they might be in here). He gives the Egyptian point of view very well here. No, he's talking about another creation story. It talks about Adam in very much the same way. Well, this would be relevant when we get to it. This is Brigham talking about other worlds, which was a favorite subject of his.

Well, he goes on here "that his name might endure." But you wanted that for your name, not just the king's name. Very early the king had these rights. It was the king's secret-the secret of immortality, the ordinances of the temple. He received all the ordinances and the endowment, and that would make him a king and priest forever. That was the idea. But very early, members of the royal family got let in onto this secret and they too have the same rights and ordinances, even the same insignia as the king has. And before very long, already in the Middle Kingdom, everybody has it. Any man can claim all the rights of royalty and be a king and a priest forever in his tomb if he pays attention to the proper ordinances. So he says, "that his name might endure and that his monument might be firmly established in the house of his father at the south side of the wall for all the length of time and eternity, that he might remember his father [and here's good old Ptah again]. This is his name to whom life is given forever." They rubbed it out here, so you can't read it very well to say the least.

It's arranged like a play, and it is arranged quite graphically. It's set forth in this inscription in the rock. It starts out with the two lines giving the setting, the time and the place, the dramatis personae, etc. "This is Ptah whom you see sitting here who is acclaimed in his name of Ptah-Tenen."

Now first as to the beginning in general. Since we went to the trouble to look up in Bonnet, we might as well tell you what he has to say about it. Hans Bonnet collected all the information on the subject in a big lexicon, if you want to know where to go for it. Of course, now there's a bigger one-the new lexicon on Egyptology which is many volumes. It's still coming out. "At the beginning of the universe, the Egyptians do not attempt to explain the first ultimate beginning. They begin with a given quantity." The material of the universe is a given quantity to begin with. They don't argue about what must come before that because it is a waste of time, among other things. It's imponderable. Should we be here? This idea of ontology: Should we exist at all? Is it right? Well, we could argue about that until the cows come home. But why bother? We are here, you see. As we used to say to the little kids when we went somewhere, "You should have thought of that before you left home." It's too late now; we're here already. Let's begin with that as a given quantity. That's the thing that Professor Wormuth up at the University of Utah was very much impressed by. He was one of their most eminent professors up there. He died recently, unfortunately, but they considered themselves very lucky to have Professor Wormuth. He said that Mormonism is the only non-speculative religion. Every other religion just speculates on the beginning of things, should we be here, etc. But we take certain things as given-that we are here and the world is here. We go on from there. Well, they go on from there too, he says here. Now the process of creation is, therefore, one (since they accept material as there already) of organization, beginning with matter unorganized. So it isn't a matter of existence or creation out of nothing which, as we say, the Christian world believes. The scientists say that's what you must believe if you are a Christian or a Jew. The Egyptians didn't believe that for a minute, and neither did anyone else there. There was a very good statement here from somebody on that subject. I can't see it now, but the creation was one of organization.

One system begins with light, with the spark, which heaven and earth bring forth-the sun and the darkness. It's the light in the darkness. It begins with that, but the darkness still presses. The fact is that the light shouldn't be there. There's something wrong because darkness prevails. That's the law of entropy. That's your second law. Everything runs down. The lights go out. Everything becomes dark, and that's the end of everything. They talk about the heat death. We've read dramatic descriptions of how the sun will die, finally fizzle out and have a final explosion after going through all the necessary phases. Everything will cool out and become nothing but a klinker, nothing but a cinder, lifeless and dead forever. The thing that puzzles people today is that should have happened long ago. Why hasn't it happened? They still argue that. Do we have a fluctuating universe or just a one-way one? But anyway, as far as they're concerned, the darkness was there and had to be dealt with in the first place because everything would run down otherwise. So it's a state of inertia. It's a state of entropy that can't move itself. It's this inert matter that Abraham talks about. There was nothing but earth. And we're told about the children of the helpless one, who can't help themselves-the exhausted. Matter, a person, everything in time becomes exhausted, and when it is exhausted where can it go from there? It must be revived from some force outside itself. The Egyptians are very fond of using that term "exhausted." The tired, the weary one, has nothing left. What can be done to stir it to life again? Of course, their concern with resurrection, burial, etc. shows interest in that. They always put it in. How are you going to bring life to something that hasn't got life in it? It has to come from outside. In the state of inertia, of entropy, it cannot move itself. And they are called the children of the helpless ones. They are represented on earth by snakes. Entropy is a real force, and it is represented by the great serpent Apophis which in the end consumes everything. It reminds you of the Fenris wolf in Norse mythology, and they have a great serpent too. But in the end the Fenris wolf will eat everything up, the gods themselves. The powers of darkness are going to win. It's the "Gotterdammerung." It's the turning off of the lights. The gods themselves don't have a chance because nothing can resist this power of destruction. Of course, the answer to that is always why are we here at all then? It's a mistake. Something went wrong; something moved against it. The Egyptians go on and tell us that.

So what happens? Well, entropy is a real force, and Apophis has to be overcome so very often. You will find the struggle with Apophis, represented sometimes by a hippo, and he is overcome but not forever and ever. He always comes back for more trouble. He represents Seth here. After a long period of tension between the two, which would it be, the light or the dark, that prevailed? Then Apophis was overcome and the time of Re could be entered in. Re is the sun god, the god of light. This was the Golden Age, paradise on earth. That was Eden. Too bad we couldn't have stayed that way. There's another tradition which some think to be older. Remember, the priests in the temples of Egypt (and there were hundreds of them) had their different schools and interpretations. You're not going to leave them alone with this stuff without them arguing about it any more than you can expect an elders quorum not to touch the mysteries. They just can't leave them alone. And the Egyptians couldn't either, so you have a number of schools and explanations. They all come back to this basic one.

The other one is that the earth was covered with water in the beginning and darkness. This is in the Old Testament-darkness covered the deep and brooded upon the face of the water. It was dark and inert. It was, as Bonnet says, a form of chaos or disorganization. Chaos is matter, but matter unorganized. You're not going to have chaos in emptiness. You have to have substance. But it's disorganized; and when it's organized, it's not chaos anymore. But "chaos" means the same thing as our word "yawn." It's emptiness. We get the idea of gaseous confusion. But nothing is running there. Then the water was divided and the primal mound came. There was a division of the waters, and the earth appeared on which man could dwell. This was the beginning. This was the primal mound, the foundation of this cosmogony. These were all part of the same picture, and you remember Professor Edwards talking about the pyramid. It represents nothing else but that primal mound. But it's built over a grave mound too, so he shows us. You get a pyramid like this. He notices, though he is very conservative and doesn't want to go in for any crackpot stuff, this road to heaven. You have to have definite angles here. The thing is definitely naturally boxed to the compass. It is a cosmogonic structure, and the height is five to eight here. Anyway, under the pyramid, including the great pyramid, there is a natural mound.

That's what the pyramid is for. It's for going up to heaven. I noticed yesterday the common word for grave, ahat, is "to raise up a thing" and ma is "a place where something happens." Ma'ahat means "the place where you ascend." It doesn't mean a place where you go down and rest forever. It's not the tomb where "you lie in cold obstruction and rot." It is the blessed place of arising.

And it's interesting that Professor Edwards who spent sixty years studying pyramids has finally reached that conclusion that this is what it is. When Osiris is buried there, the whole purpose is to get him out of there, to rise up. And, of course, the pyramid texts tell you that all over the place. Well, it became a paradise and the primal mound. Well, at Hermopolis they elaborated that an egg came out of the mound, and out of the egg came the sun god, or else the lotus which you find universally because the lotus rises. Remember, Egypt was under water when it was discovered. As the Pearl of Great Price tells us, when the woman Egyptus discovered it, it was under water. And what appears in the shallow, muddy, swirling waters? Whether it's in India, China, or anywhere else, it's the lotus. It's miraculous how out of the foulest waters overnight this beautiful white flower bursts open. Professor Edwards showed a picture, and there's an object over at the museum here of the young Tutankhamen's head emerging from a lotus. The king is the sun, and he is rising from the lotus. But the lotus comes out of the darkness and the waters of the beginning. They go together. The waters of the flood or the waters of Nun.

Interestingly, as Bonnet says, the creation of man doesn't enter into the story. "We have no problem about the creation of man," he says, "because that is going on every day. If you want to know how people are produced, you ask your old man." But we know how that happens, so he says we don't have to have mysteries. They don't have mysteries and stories about that at all. Mary Carr, the great sage, said, "All persons are images of the creator, who have come out of his own members. Man is in his express image." So that didn't bother them. That wasn't part of the creation. In other words, it's not part of cosmogony. Man is there too, like the other stuff.

And then he becomes Ptah-Tenen, as I said. Here it's a very strong demonstrative Ptah pen pel. A person by the name of Stolk quite a few years ago wrote a book on Ptah. Among the other theories that have been very popular is that the name Ptah is the same as PTH. You will recognize what that is, you Hebrew students, patah, pateah. The Arabic is fataha. The opening line of the Koran is called the Fatihun, "the opener." Fataha means "to open," you see. A thing that opens is a mifte'ah, "a key." The Arabic word is miftah. It's cognate with Latin. We have patio, which means "open to expand." It's related to pando, "to expand, to open up," and Pandora. It has also been related to father too. But Patah, our "father," is probably related to the word feeder, food provider, something like that. But anyway, it has been suggested that it means "the beginning, the opener, the one who started everything going." That's why this name Ptah is given to him. It's a universal, international name. You're going to find it in lots of things like that.

He is always represented in human form. In all this creation story, you will find no idols, no animal gods, or anything like that at all. This is all strictly human.

And here Sander-Hansen says that Nun, the primal material, the urstoff from which things were made, existed before the creation was universally believed in the ancient world. Nobody believed in creation out of nothing. There was a primal substance that existed before the creation, and the creation was the organizing of that substance. That, he says, was the universal view of the Egyptians, especially of the early times.

We will see a lot about Ptah-Tenen. Notice, he is being acclaimed on the throne. He is the father. Ptah-Tenen is a rare form (oh, you run into that a lot), but Ptah-Tenen means Ptah's office translated down to earth. It means the exalted one. It refers to the hill, among other things, the exalted hill. It refers both to the first land that emerged after the flood and the first land that emerged in the beginning when waters and darkness covered the earth. There was the later flood, and these are the waters the Egyptians are referring to as you learn from the Pearl of Great Price. Remember, when she discovered the land, it was under water. She went there after the flood. But emerging from the flood is a very important thing. And Ptah-Tenen is the land that first emerged. But it is also the person who first occupied it and first lived in it. It means the exalted one, the exalted or uplifted land. This Ptah-Tenen always belongs to the earth. He is the earthly representative of the heavenly order. Everything about him is earthy, and so he is chthonian. Chthon is the Greek word for "like earth." The solar is the light, and the dark is the chthonian. A perfect example of that is in our Facsimile No 2. Notice all these deities are solar deities. They all have suns, and they are moving forth in glory. All these are chthonian, the cow, the female, and including the inscription which tells us this is the dark place where everything comes to a halt. But you have to have both if you are going to create. This is the womb; that's why the cow. That's Hathor, the mother cow-the womb from whom all life comes. And the four figures in front of her are, as Joseph Smith says, the four elements-the four regions of the earth from which physical bodies must be compounded.

So it tells us here this is Ptah on the throne of Ptah-Tenen. Wolf has written a good study on Ptah-Tenen. "He stirs the earth out of her lethargy, which is called Nen, a state of inertia, a heat death. That is to say out of her condition of chaos. It is Ptah-Tenen who visits the earth and organizes the earth here below." Out of its condition of chaos, he puts things into order. But he does not give it life or force. He depends on another for that. And he's not a vegetation god. Now, you would think the earth god would be the vegetation god and all that sort of thing, but he isn't. When the waters were divided, he joined the lands together and made the earth habitable; and he sets the pattern for the king, for Horus Ptah-Tenen. He is Ptah's earthly counterpart. He is almost a byword for Ptah. His name is almost a synonym for Ptah's name-Ptah above, Ptah-Tenen below. But he carries things out. He never loses his identity as the exalted one, and his form is purely human. He never takes any form but human. He wears the same crown as the two sun figures wear. It's being worn by Abraham in Facsimile 3.

It's the Atef-crown. He wears that crown which is the same as Amon wears. So we get Ptah-Tenen and Ptah. That's the people that begin the play, and Act One ends here.

Then the place, the setting, is the division between the north and the south. This is the northland; this is the southland. They are united here at this particular place where the two thrones are joined in one and the king of the North deseret and the king of the South sut (of the sedge) are united in a single glory. Deseret and the bee are the symbol of the North king. His name is bit nu, meaning "belonging to the bee" which is feminine. They knew that the queen-bee was a queen already. They say in a single glory, Kha, which is a picture of the sun rising over a mound which represents the king mounting the throne. The two thrones are placed side by side. The throne of the king of the South with the sedge; the other with the king of the North with the bee. The king is shown underneath wearing the white crown; the other king wearing the red crown. Everything is divided between the two, and this division is preserved throughout all of Egyptian life. This is a very important thing. In fact it is the main theme of this document, namely the importance of coexistence here. The two thrones are together, and they are as one. Now, you're never going to get rid of evil in the world. The Egyptians knew that because that was part of the plan. This is why Egypt has been the most stable of all civilizations. They didn't try to wipe out all opposition. They could put up with things. Later, they are less tolerant, however. Notice, they wiped out the name of Shabako here. The theme is eternal life by eternal generation. The Gospel of Philip tells us nothing is eternal except progeny. All worlds pass away, as we are told in the Pearl of Great Price. They come into existence and pass away. What doesn't come into existence and pass away? Well, it is the individual. I wish I could find that statement of Brigham Young. It's really a nice one.

We'll go on to the next line. So here they are on the single throne. Now, we come to the theme here. There is much discussion about these lines and what they mean. Who begot Atum in the beginning? It was Ptah. Atum is the big thing. At Heliopolis he is everything. It's very interesting. In the very old Coffin Texts, he is called Adamu, using the Canaanite ending u and also the soft d instead of t. In fact, Lefebure, the famous French Egyptologist went to quite a great length to show that this is the same as Adam, and he obviously is Adam here. So it says here, Atum who was begotten and who then became the father of the pesedj or the Great Council. The pesedj is always translated Ennead, but is it the Ennead? I notice how Sethe sums it up here. Ennead doesn't have to be nine or eight, or any particular number. It means the vast number. The numberless Council of the Gods is what it means. I wish I could see how he put ennead here. (Well, who cares?) But it's important though. There are the nine gods, but there are dozens of lists of them. Sometimes they will add one or take some off, or add five or six. Originally, half should be female and half male, with Ptah making the odd number, you see. It breaks down into couples such as Shu and Tefnut and Geb and Neith.

But first Atum. His name is a very important one. Rudolf Anthes has written several long articles and monographs about the name of Atum. It means "many," as we are told in Moses l:34. And, of course, it means "many" here. I notice that even Siegfried Lawrence, a very prejudiced character, says what it means is a gesammtheit-a gathering, a compound, or a complex. It includes everything in itself, and you will see why. The word is very productive in Egypt. It's a word that means almost anything. You just draw a sled, and this is tm. It's a regular verb meaning "to be," but it also means "not to be." It means "to complete, to complete a circle." Immediately, you think of the Hebrew word for perfect. Job was an upright man, yashar watamim, upright and perfect. Tamim means a perfect circle. It comes right back to the beginning where it started. Of course, tamam is the standard Arabic word for perfect or complete. Well, Atum is the one who is perfect and complete. Oh, there are all sorts of things. When we get to the Atum literature, we may talk about them. But in Egyptian it means "to be complete, to be everything, to be nothing, and not to be." The basic meaning they give is mes-tm, "one who begot all." Well, that's what it's written as here. The father of all. Mes is like Moses, etc. It means "to beget, to have a child." And Mes-tm is the one who begot all. And Anthes says the name of Adam represents all that has gone before himself and all that comes after himself. He is the great bottleneck. As to his own origin, we are told it is deliberately left unrevealed or unreported. There are various theories, of course. But in the Pyramid Texts, the oldest writings we have after this, he is the primal god who comes down to earth-the first one to set foot on the primal hill when the waters divide. When the land becomes habitable, Atum is the first one to occupy it. We are told in one text he needed no magic because he came first. He was a real fact. He doesn't need to create something. He has a companion, Amun's pallakida, his mate. That's a very interesting way of putting it, as Bonnet does. This was his companion, his helpmeet. This was Hathor, the woman who settled the land. The priestesses of the Atum temple were always called Hathors. That goes back, he thinks, to a tree cult because they have this tree of life business here.

In spite of any magic background, the creation was an act of mind. But at Heliopolis there was a real marriage we are told. Right where the rivers meet, this is Heliopolis. I said that's where the airport is today. It is right here. It's the On of the Bible, and Unell meaning the stone pillars. It's the old megalithic foundation. It's very important in the New Testament. That's where Moses lived, where Joseph married the daughter of the high priest, where Abraham taught and was taught. Everybody went to Heliopolis, and the earliest Christian accounts say that's where Jesus' parents took him when they went there. There were two temples, the temple at Jerusalem and the temple at Heliopolis. In the Bible they are in frequent contact with each other. We'll get to more of that.

So he brought forth the first pair, which bringing together became the source of all life on earth. They were the first flesh. But it is an interesting thing. He does not create, he begets. He is a father. He does not create; others do that. And then there's this idea "who begot himself." What is it talking about there? Diodorus tells us "the seed not the womb contains the life of the child. Therefore, Adam contained within himself the life as in the womb, and so he is both father and mother." Bonnet insists that there is nothing offensive about this.

No lust is involved. It was really a compromise with matriarchy. Whatever form the child may have in the seed, without the mother it cannot enter into this stage of existence. And that's what we're after. This may seem like a mystery, but remember in the Book of Mormon Christ says, "I am the Father and the Son." Well, I think President Joseph Fielding Smith explained that very well when he said, "Who made it possible for us to be resurrected and live in bodies hereafter? Jesus Christ. Without him it wouldn't have been possible. He is the father of our everlasting, eternal bodies-not the bodies we have here. That's something else. But when we are resurrected, he has made that possible. So he is the Father and the Son. Of course, anybody can be a father and a son at the same time; I've achieved that difficult feat.

Now, the conflict between the patriarchy and matriarchy about which a lot has been written. This is dissolved in the very simple question, namely with this. Which one is it, the father or mother, who makes birth possible? You leave one of them out, and birth is impossible. Well, it's a silly question. When Atum's son Shu separated the earth and heaven, male and female, it was really Atum himself who was doing it all. So Atum comes down here, and he is the father of the human race in every sense. He comes down and transfers it. But you say, "Oh, this is getting sort of complicated. What have we got here?" So Ptah begot Atum who was the father of the Council of the Gods.

And now the play begins. Iyatenef means to call a family or a gathering together. It's quite often in those verses in the Coffin Texts, 131-146. Marvelous passages about sealing one's family to one's self in the other world so that you can be united in the hereafter, exactly as we understand it, and as Egyptologist T. G. Allen at Chicago pointed out long ago. But Ptah called together the family of the gods to settle an affair between Horus and Seth. Now there are these two plans. Seth had his plan. Horus favored the gods' plan. Seth had a perfect right to object if he wanted to, but they were going to discuss it. You can never get rid of Seth; he is always there. He was cast out of heaven not because he disagreed but because he resorted to violence in heaven. As we are told in Moses 7:31, there is naught before thy throne but peace, justice and wisdom. To settle this affair between Horus and Seth, he began by forbidding all contention. "There shall be no contention among you." What was the first thing the Lord said to the Nephites when he came to them? There shall be no contention among you as there has been in the past. All contention is not of me but of the devil, for it is contention that stirs up the hearts of the children of men to bloodshed. You shall not contend. So he absolutely forbad that there should be any wrangling, any controversy between Horus and Seth. And by establishing Seth in the kingdom of the South in the place where he was initiated. Yes, that's a funny word it uses there. Later on it says in the place where he was born. It's funny, nobody accepts this initiation, but that's what is written. It says in the place where he was introduced into the mysteries and ordinances; in a place called saa.

It's now Geb who takes over. He's the earth god but never a vegetation god. He's very much like Ptah-Tenen, but Geb is much more intimate. He is in the family. He is the principle of patriarchal succession on earth.

He doesn't represent the powers of the earth which must be stimulated from above. Things grow out of him but never because of him. Geb and Nut are the two who begat Re. Remember Geb is the earth god and Nut is the female heaven stretched above. We see pictures of that out in the museum right now. So they begat Re, making him the father of the gods, and he represents the patriarchal, monarchical power on earth. He's a reflection of the heavenly order here below. He is the Rp't meaning the mouth of the race, Rp't on the Throne of Geb. Any king, if he wanted to send somebody out on a mission to represent him, could let him sit on his own throne by courtesy and then he was a Rp't, authorized to speak for the king. Of course, that's exactly what we have in Facsimile No.3: Abraham, by politeness of Pharaoh, being allowed to sit on his throne. People used to laugh at that and say it was absurd. Pharaoh wouldn't allow anyone else to sit on his throne. Any Rp't could sit on his throne. And recently there has been a long study on that called Rp't on the Throne of Geb.

Well, he established his son Osiris as well as Horus. They both sit on the throne of Geb. He makes the lords of earthly dynasties as heirs of Geb attested by the mekes which they hold. And, like all cosmic gods, he is always represented in human form. The goose associated with him is a phonetic way of writing his name. It's purely phonetic, but he is always a human being. All of these people so far have been people engaged in this particular drama. And he wears the Atef-crown. He wears the same crown that the kings wear there. He is the wetu, which means the next in the patriarchal line, the most immediate to the throne, the true successor. The archaic title is Wepwawet.

I see this is taking too much time, but this is amazing. We are still going on this. They say why did Joseph Smith get us into this? Well, there's lots more here than we realize. It isn't just ancient mumbo jumbo and superstition. Professor John Wilson called it "mumbo jumbo," but I didn't call it that. Well, we should get things stirring up. This is really quite an exciting story when it gets going.

Lecture 8

The Council According to the Shabako Stone

This Shabako Stone is now viewed by many as the most important single document in religious history. It's in the dramatic texts of Sethe, etc. We will refer to the person who discovered it later. But it has been in the British Museum since 1805. George Spencer gave it to the museum, and many attempts were made for one hundred years to interpret it. Some people did some pretty good guessing, but for one thing they didn't realize that it was written backwards; the characters were facing in the opposite direction from what they should have been facing. This is very archaic Egyptian; it's not in the later period. When it reached James Henry Breasted at the University of Chicago, he was able to make a very good study and translation of it. That started things going. He was the one that discovered what it was all about-discovered the clue or key, which was reading it in the right direction. In 1901 he first came across it, and he raved about it. He calls it "The First Philosopher." I've had four or five copies made, and it's on reserve now for this class. It has the translation of this document as given by him. But there have been many changes since.

People return to the attack again and again, and every time they not only confirm but realize that what Breasted said about it was an understatement. Every time its great value, great importance and age is mentioned; it gets older every time. Of course, every time they are able to understand it better. Usually, as you know, when someone gushes and gets all excited at some great discovery, they go wild about it; and then there's an anti-climax. Well, there's been no anti-climax here. The more it's studied (and everybody's on to it now) the more important it is considered. He thought it was in the Middle Kingdom, but it's much older than that. After he was able to present his version of it, everybody could do it. It became easier then.

Is there anyone here who doesn't know the story of Columbus and the egg? You don't know it. Good, I'll tell you the story of Columbus and the egg. The rest of you can ignore it. Columbus was at dinner with some dignitaries. It was after his first voyage to America, and somebody said, "It wasn't such a great thing after all. The ship did it easily enough; anybody could have done it." Columbus didn't say anything, but later on he said, "Can any of you here make an egg stand on end?" Well, everybody tried it and couldn't do it at all. Columbus took the egg, gave it a light tap, and stood it on its end. They said, "Well, of course, anybody can do it that way." He said, "Yes, and anybody can do it when somebody shows them how it is done." There was nothing said about the egg couldn't be cooked or the shell had to be unbroken, or anything like that. Just, could you make the egg stand up. But the point with Columbus was that after somebody has done it, it's the easiest thing in the world and no great show at all. This was the same way. They were absolutely helpless and floundered around until Professor Breasted did it.

Incidentally, he was very much interested in the Book of Mormon. I knew his wife very well. We used to bicycle together in Claremont. She lived next door, and in those days people didn't ride bikes much, and so we used to wheel around the place. That was after Breasted had died; he retired there. But it used to bug him. She said that he intended to get rid of the problem once and for all. He liked a lot of Mormons, and he said the best thing to do is just show in a half hour or twenty minutes where this is wrong and that's wrong. There would necessarily be some glaring contradictions in it. So he set to work, and never a peep. Nothing came of it. Well, anyway he did the great work, and after that everybody could set the egg on end. He gave it this rave notice, as I say, which you will read in The Monist, "The First Philosopher." And then Erman came along in 1909 and gave it the name Shabako. Shabako Stone comes from Erman at Berlin. He called it the Shabako text because it was by King Shabako. The great study of it is by Sethe in 1929, and others have come out since.

But this is basic; this is the one that first brought it out, and it's good. But he says that Shabako was just the man. He believed that Shabako was the founder of the dynasty. Shabako was an extremely religious man. He had come up from the South, and he wanted to establish his dynasty there. He was just the man to do this, a great archaizer. He goes right back to the beginning of things digging up all the records he can. Lots of pharaohs practiced that-were interested in the archaic documents and tried to restore things, especially in the twenty-second, twenty-fifth, and twenty-sixth dynasties. And this was the twenty-fifth dynasty when it really happened. Under Piankhi, under the Sheshonqides (the twenty-sixth dynasty) they went back to the earliest days and renewed everything. And they thought, well, this is a copy from that. No, it is a faithful copy, not a reproduction-not an attempt to imitate anything.

But Sethe believes that Shabako was just the man to give us this text. Every time they founded a new dynasty, they would do the same thing; renew the theology, give a new version of the creation story. It was done at Heliopolis; then Menes did it at Memphis-that's this text here. Then Ammenemes did it in the Middle Kingdom at Dahshur. Then Amenophis IV did it at El Amarna. Then the Moslems did it at Cairo. Always with a new center and a new theology, but they always follow along the same line. But I said he was going to renew it, so he went back to the old temple that was built to celebrate the founding of the first dynasty in Egypt at the beginning of civilization 3100-3200 B.C. It was very old then, we find out. Epigraphically, orthographically, vocabulary and syntax all put it before the Pyramid Texts which are the oldest books in the world. They are all before 2000 B.C. They are found under the pyramids written on the walls, beautifully carved. But they are very old; they go back to 2700 - 2900 B.C. And this is older than that-the grammar.

As I say, this is proved epigraphically. What do we mean by epigraphically? By the way it's carved on the rock-the order, the columns, the way they are divided, etc. This is the quaint, the old-fashioned way. They never did it later on. It was never used after say the eleventh dynasty, so it puts it very old.

And then orthographically, the way they spelled things. They used the old, archaic spellings, very quaint spellings. Well, those of you who are in English know the difference between Middle English, and West Saxon, and Northumbrian, and modern. But you recognize immediately what particular period it came from although they are using the same words. It was the same thing here. And the syntax (the order in which the words occur) is peculiar to the times. And everything puts it before the Pyramid Texts, so it is very, very old.

What we are showing here is the left side first. The left side is the drama, and the right side is the theological discourse-the temple drama. Incidentally, as we read that piece from Zabkar the last time, this carries right on, he says, into the Old Testament and the New Testament. You will find that everything is very familiar here, and this caused everybody to pause because, after all, what are these people doing with the Bible? Shaba, the first person to look at this, was stunned (well, in other texts especially) by close resemblances to the Bible. In the 1920s the Petosiris text is a classical example of that. It has long passages from the Psalms and the Proverbs in it. And you will find Old Testament and New Testament passages-in a work from 300-400 B.C. Well, this shouldn't be, you see. So they would always soft pedal it. And they always skipped around it. It's only within the last ten years that they have said, "All right, if it looks like an elephant, call it an elephant." This has been staring us in the face all these years; everyone was blushing and no one would dare mention it. Now all of a sudden within the last three or four years half a dozen articles (big studies) have come forth showing that the wisdom literature of the Egyptians, the Ammenemes, as Erman showed onceÃ‚Â…He was ashamed of it. He kept it covered up and never mentioned the fact that it was his greatest discovery. The Wisdom of Ammenemes is the wisdom of Psalms; it's the same thing (word-for-word passages). Well, this really stunned him, so Erman tried to cover it up. That would never do. That's not the evolutionary development of things, but today it is being recognized.

The story that the rabbis tell is that when the animals came to Adam, he was naming them. And the Lord said, "What are you going to call this one?" He said, "I'm going to call it an elephant." "Well, why an elephant." "Because it looks like an elephant," he said. So Professor Popper used to say, "If it looks like an elephant, call it an elephant." Sometimes you hesitate and say, "No, it can't possibly be that." Well, if that's what it looks like, try that first. Take a chance here.

Well, we have the theology, and the left side is the myths of Horus and Seth and the learned discourse in the drama. The oldest parts are the dialogues of the gods here. They are preserved in archaic form like a Greek chorus. Now, most of the Greek plays come from Athens. They are in the Ionic dialect; that's Athenian. But the choruses are in Doric. They are in the old Doric or Aeolic. But Doric is the older language, and the chorus will come on with this archaic Doric with the long "ahs," etc. because they are preserving the original text. It's the same way here. There are obviously two texts. There's a rather free commentary, which is much newer, though it's far older than anything else.

But then what they are commenting on is the urtext, the text that goes right back to the beginning. The rest was more up-to-date, but very late. The speeches of the godhead are the remains, Sethe says, of a prehistoric book. They were kept in a book, but the book, of course, was long lost. It was copied from this. Remember, he tells us here that he had it copied from a roll that was found in the cornerstone, like the cornerstone of the Nauvoo House. When they were renovating the White Temple, the first temple in United Egypt (the one that began the kingdom), they found this document. And he had it copied on stone. That's why we have it today. But one of the reasons we've been so long getting it out is that it was so hard to read. And the reason it was so hard to read was that it was in a dark corner of the British Museum, and they would never take it out so people could look at it. That's so absolutely typical. The great Richard Laurence who discovered the Enoch texts had to do all his work in the Bodleian Library. They wouldn't even let him take the text over to the window so he could read it better. He couldn't take it out of that damp, dark cell in the Bodleian Library, and that's why he had to work all of his life. Since then it has rotted away from age. They haven't taken any care of it at all.

Anyway, the speeches of the godhead show that we had a very old book here. They treat the sagas in it, the stories of Osiris, Horus, and Seth in dramatic form. It was to be presented on a very special occasion, of course, at the new year-at the founding of not only the empire, but the beginning of the world, the revival of things. In fact, Sethe says, it very closely parallels the mystery plays of Easter and Christmas that were put on in the Middle Ages. But much earlier than the Middle Ages, you will find them all over the Middle East, the same plays. The complex on the top of the temple at Dendereh, which is preserved, is what Daumas (who did more work than anyone else on it) says is an Easter complex. It was to celebrate the rebirth of the god at the spring equinox which is Easter. And then they did the other one which was the new year when the sun comes back, the birth of the sun. But it is to celebrate the cyclical death and rebirth of all nature and all the rest. We get our friend Geb in here. So it is for presentation on festive occasions. But it was secret; it was given to only elect people, only those who were qualified and had recommends could see it.

It's in verse, incidentally. It has been quite recently discovered that the Book of Dead (that's one thing people have been reading for a hundred years now) is in verse. That's how little we know about the vowels and the values here. And Sethe, who is the one who made the definitive edition of this, also edited the Ramesseum texts, from the Ramesseum on the other side of the Nile, over on the west side in Thebes. This is another prehistoric dramatic text, and you can match the two. This was a text which was dramatized with the king. He took the role of Atum, the lead. They went through the whole thing, and it toured the whole length of the Nile. The king and the court would dramatize this and were chosen for various parts. On different occasions they could shift the actors around. So the idea of traveling troupes of actors is very old. It goes right back to the beginning of time. You still find them traveling with a donkey and a shadow theater. I have an Arabic book on the subject which is very good-the traveling shadow theaters in the Middle East.

It's from way back pre-war times when things were fairly stable. But a troupe would travel around, and what they would put on was a shadow theater. The theme was Adam and Eve, the Garden of Eden, and the Fall. They would go through all that sort of thing. People would all gather for it, and it would be a big affair. They'd get some good pictures of it, as a matter of fact.

In this text the actio is the drama. The word drao means "to do, to carry out visibly." There is the action of the play, and then there is the spoken word which is the dialogue. The action is clearly marked by stage directions. They are very short and very specific, as with the Ramesseum drama. Erman thought the stage directions were commentary, but it turns out they are not. They're directions on what is supposed to be going on here. And these are directed by the keri-hep, who is the lectern priest. He directs the ordinances and takes charge. He's the man who is in charge of the operations. There are others there. Of course, people participate in it, but he directs the whole thing. And he or somebody else would read off the lines describing the events that could not be presented visually. There are some things, you know. Should we recite the introduction to Henry V at this point? We'd waste a lot of time. But why is the play the oldest thing? It's because it is the most amusing, entertaining, engrossing. It involves you more than anything else. And it make things clearer than anything else. It's the best way of abbreviating. You don't have to go into long words. A play can be kept short and tell you a great deal.

And let us, ciphers to this great acompt,

[Now, you have to make the play, you see]

[This many represents the army. You divide him into a thousand parts, and you make an imaginary military force.]

Turning the accomplishment of many years

Into an hour-glass: for which supply

Admit me Chorus to this history;

Well, that's the idea. You have to do it. You are involved, and you're committed to the play. And Shakespeare is great at getting us that far, isn't he? There's a play for Hamlet. Well, we won't get into that. This is getting off the track, isn't it. We need to finish this whole thing today. Here it is now. Well over five thousand years ago they are putting on a play, and it's a terrific play.

The first time I went to the Hopis, it was with Brother Virgil Bushman who was the great missionary to the Hopis. He was born down there. Just after the war, he insisted on taking me down there. There was just a dirt trail, terrible road at that time. They still were using ladders to get up to the mesas. We went up there, and it was the fifteenth of March when they have their first dance. It was bleak and windy, and you go up on the high mesa. It was at Hotevilla. There in the plaza of houses at dawn was this play going on. This tremendous drama was being played. Magnificent costumes they had. It was a cloud dance. The rule is that you can never use anything bought. You can never use anything old or dirty. It had to be neat, beautiful and made of native substance by yourself-really something. And here they were doing this. I started counting them, and there were 52 dancers that came in. There had to be 52 dancers in the oldest Greek chorus (52 weeks of the year). And then the protagonist and the antagonist came in, and they started their dialogue. First the priest went around with the sack of meal and laid out the course that the chorus was to follow. Then a Greek play took place there. After that was a comedy with the mudheads clowning around, etc. But it was the same motif. There was the altar in the center as they have to be in the Greek play. Nobody answers in that because that is the conistra, the dust place, where nothing lives. This is the moat between us and the other world because in the center is an altar. And in every Greek tragedy, the ghost appears out of the ground from there. Then they have the two little evergreen trees, like Christmas trees, on either side of the altar in every place where the Hopis have their festival, the twelve villages. And the spirit feathers tied on them in their prayers. This takes you to the spirit world, and the ghost appears (as the ghost of Darius appears in the Persians) out of the ground.

So this stuff is very old, but who told these people to do this? They're as poor as dirt. They were chased to the ends of the world and brutally exploited by the coal people who were strip mining the sacred places in an awful way. But the thing is here they were by themselves going to all this trouble. There were only a few old people and a couple of little kids on the houses because who wants to get up on a cold March morning at dawn and go through that? But they went through it with the greatest solemnity, everything accurate, and the priests reciting their lines to each other, etc. They are very particular about any taking notes or writing anything. I just jotted down one little thing, and they had it out of my hand in a second. I learned from then on, but I went to many of the dances after that. I went later down to Moenkopi. It was the time I took Brother Merlin Myers, the anthropologist. He had never been before. So I said, "Well, I go down all the time." We went down, and I took down that very nice reproduction (1912) of the Book of the Dead-all colors and everything. It's a beautiful reproduction that Budge got out. I took it out, and there was a meeting of the Hopi kids. They met across the road from Tuba City in the Hopi village, Moenkopi. They recognized everything. For example, the dancers had to have the two feathers; they had to have the copper bracelets; they had to have the tortoise shell; they had to have the coyote or fox skin with the tail, as every Egyptian priest had to. They looked just like them. I mean there was no difference at all, an amazing thing that goes on.

Well, how old this stuff is. What is going on here? What have we been missing, you see? The thing is that it's still carried on. They still persist against tremendous opposition. Peabody Corporation tried to get them all to move out to Los Angeles. They offered them mobile homes if they would do that-give up this thousands of years old culture. They resisted the Spaniards; they resisted the Catholics. They resist everybody. They all joined the church at once when the gospel was first introduced to them. Then in 1906 there was a big fuss in Congress, and the Mormons were forced to leave. And, moreover, the Mormons had their farms down there. At Danabata Wash and Moenkopi Wash is where the Mormon farms were. Up along the benches you can still see the poplar trees which mark the farms. But they were all forced to leave in 1906 because of the influence they had on the Indians. Well, that's another story, but it is very close to this one. What are these people up to? How do they know so much about these things?

Now, the oldest parts are these dialogues, and the actio has these directions. The things that can't be staged are described by this lecturn priest who conducts the play. He says this is practically identical with the ancient mysteries of the Easter and Christmas celebrations. It's always with the seasons. There is no dancing now. It won't be until March 15, the first full moon in March. It's Easter when they start out, and always that one. That will give you goose flesh. No artificial lights; it has to be by moonlight. There the Havawuhti comes out. That's really scary. The only sound (no drums or anything) is the flute. Like the Greeks. In the earliest Greek play, there was just the flute. The lyre and the string instruments are for the [p.8] solos and the melodramatic stuff; but for the old archaic stuff it was just the flute. And it's eerie by full moonlight. And then the Havawuhti starts walking down the street with these giants with their long rawhide whips, whipping on either side and telling the people to get their distance. Well, that's the Egyptian Nut. She emerges out of the kiva all in black, and she is holding a bow and two arrows. That's her sign, the two arrows. It's in here. You will see the Lady Neith with the same thing, the two arrows. She walks in a stately manner, and everybody really keeps their distance. It's the Havawuhti who is Neith, the woman who brought things first from the underworld.

The speeches are brief and few. Here's another thing. There is no effort to follow chronological order. If a past event must be brought up, they don't hesitate to use a flashback and show what happened in an earlier time. So don't try to construct it according to the rules of Aristotle here. The speeches are brief and few, but they are the main thing. They are the prehistoric parts. Quoting Sethe here, "It is an ancient, holy, piously guarded heritage, a secret ordinance like the Easter mysteries." I mentioned the last time Henry John Feasey's very interesting work on the English holy week ceremonies (very ancient of course). I recommend that you read Augustine, not St. Augustine, but Augustine who in 590 came as the first missionary to England. He was sent by Pope Gregory the Great. You know the famous story about angles and angels. He saw these little English children for sale in the market place in Rome, and he said, "Who are these?" They said, "Well, these are Angles." He said, "They are not angles; they are angels."
