So he decided to send a mission up there, so he sent Augustine up there. We have Augustine's correspondence with Gregory. He said, "Look, do you know what these people do? At Easter time they meet at these holy places and they have their races and their festivals. They build booths with green boughs, and they feast and hold celebrations along with the races. They elect their king and queen and all the rest of it." And Gregory said, "Well, that's exactly what we do in Rome at the same time of year. We set long tables in the streets and have a feast for everybody at Easter." It's the time of rebirth; Christ has risen from the dead. Everybody celebrates, so let them go. Let them do anything they want. Let them build their green booths." Remember in Israel for the festival they had the sukkot. For the festival you had to have a booth. For the Passover they had to go to Jerusalem, and nobody was allowed to celebrate the Passover within his gates. He had to make it as a pilgrim. So when they were there, they had to have some shelter. So we have the ordinances of the green booths, and we have them all over the world. Well Gregory said, "Let them do it; just sprinkle it with a little holy water and there's no difference. I don't see any difference at all." And there wasn't. They were ancient long before that, and that was at the end of the sixth century.

So they were doing their Easter business long before Christianity ever got there. And it's the same thing here, he says. We're dealing with the same type of thing. As we mentioned the last time, it goes right over into the Old Testament and the New Testament. And this is the way Breasted begins his article: "During his sojourn in the British Museum, it was the writer's good fortune to subject to close scrutiny an ancient stone, the importance of which has hitherto been entirely unsuspected." Well, everybody suspected it might be important, but they couldn't make heads or tails of it. That was the trouble. "It contains the oldest known philological explanation of the world written a thousand years before the first Greek philosopher was born." He was wrong there; it was written a thousand years before that at least. It was written three thousand years before the first philosopher was born. There were other people philosophizing too. See, the archaic world has disappeared completely, and the marvelous thing is that this has been able to survive. He tells how it happened to survive; remember making a copy, etc. So for the first time there is tangible support to the Greek tradition of the origin of their philosophy in the East. The Greeks said, "We get everything we know from the Egyptians." But nobody would ever credit the Greeks when they said that. Theodor Hopfner was the man who spent his whole life searching as a classical scholar the Egyptian religion as it is recorded by the Greek and Roman classical writers. But he always insisted there was no connection between them whatever. The Greeks insisted over and over again that, yes, they got that there. Plato studied in Heliopolis. Parmenides did, and Thales did. German scholars always said "no" to everything like that. They had no proof that it wasn't so, but it just didn't fit. It was too picturesque, too fantastic. We don't allow things like that; they're not dignified.

Well, now the stone itself is three feet by four, seven. It is black granite, and it's very heavy. But alas, alas, it has that square hole in the center of it because it was used as a nether millstone. Of course, being that shape it couldn't turn as an upper millstone. But it was used as a lower millstone. You can imagine how that would preserve an inscription to grind it and grind it for a few hundred years. It was very hard stone. Naturally, the part that was ground out you can't read. Just very faint forms there. It is sadly damaged and so faint, says Breasted, that either a squeeze or a photo was out of the question. They couldn't make an impression of it, a photo. The best thing to do would be maybe make a rubbing of that, but they wouldn't let them do that. And paleographically it's an exceedingly beautiful inscription. The signs are very much like those produced in modern hieroglyphic type. It just looks like a modern printed book, like Gardiner's book. They never changed the style. It is beautiful writing, way back there in the beginning, just as clear as ever. "But it would be a lot easier to read outside of the dark hall. In years to come they might be able to bring some light in here," he says. And they did, of course. They put light from various directions to bring out the contours, and then they could read it.

Well, let's get to the text now. Turn it on and see what we've got here on the beginning. Here are the stage directions. Ptah-Pen-Pel. This one you see here, Pel, refers to whatever we happen to be talking about. It always answers the question: who is this? Or especially, what are you talking about? So we introduce ourselves by saying Ptah, the opener, the beginner. This Ptah is the one we are talking about, Ptah-Pen-Pel. Much he is acclaimed; he is heralded in glory, in the great name of Ptah-Tenen.

And the abbreviation here shows how archaic this usage is. Ptah is in heaven now. It begins with Ptah in his glory, and he wishes to transmit the powers of heaven (the powers he possesses) to the earth, the same celestial order.

Incidentally, I'm going to suggest that we are following the Terpanderinomos. At the twenty-sixth Olympic games in 676 B.C., they introduced musical games, and Terpander won the prize. He won the prize then four times in succession at Delphi at the Pythian contest. Notice, these were at the holy centers. Delphi was the holiest of all. They were oracular centers, and Terpander introduced the musical element and what is called the Terpanderinomos or the rule of Terpander. You know who Terpsichore is, goddess of the dance. The Terpanderian rule divides the whole according to the ritual pattern. Namely into the arche, the rule, the beginning. This is the foundation. You start up with Ptah on his throne in the world above in timeless ages. And that's the arche or the beginning. The middle part is called the omphalos (same word as Latin umbilicus). That is the navel or the center of the thing-the main center around which everything is going to revolve. At Delphi, for example, you can still see out in front of the temple there's the omphalos shaped just like an umbilicus. This was the center of the world. This was the hierocentric point from which everything came. Every holy shrine had its umbilicus. A great deal has been written about the umbilicus. Then finally comes the sphragus, the seal that seals everything up.

The umbilicus here is where the upper millstone turned. It turned around here. And here was the square to pull this in place. So this is pretty well wiped out, but you notice the millstone only went to here, so this part was spared which gives us a little Garden of Eden romance in here. Then on the final one (this is the other half) it ends as it began. But now his glory has been established on earth, naturally in Memphis. The whole purpose of this is to show that the king at Memphis (Shabako in this case) holds the same authority that was held by God and was handed down to man through Atum and through the heavenly council to the spirits and brought down to earth. That's what he is going to tell us about here. So we now proceed here, and Ptah is the main thing. But it's now Ptah-Tenen which means the uprising, the lifting up, the exalted. The rising of the land, the rising of the earth, the first pyramid, the first burial mound, the rising of resurrection-all that is together along with the name Ptah-Tenen. His name is Ptah-Tenen, the first person to put life upon the earth. So this is Ptah coming down to earth (Ptah means earth, you see). But it's earthy. The problem is how are we going to transmit the powers and forces of the universe to this one little planet? This is the way we do it. Well, this is the land; this is the setting. This is the land of the Mekhy of the North and of the Resy of the South. And it is the joining place of the two glorious ones. These are thrones of glory. That was originally a sunburst coming out here. It's very finely, very beautifully carved, but they were already conventionalized. It was the hill with the sunrise over it. So you had this form like this and then like this. That's what this is, called the Kha, which means to arise in glory. It's a regular word used for coronation or crowning of the king. Enthronement was his rising in glory, of course, being compared with the sun.

Notice he is wearing the white crown of the South; and North, the red crown of the North. There were always two crowns, always two thrones, even two graves for the king. Everything was double. Every ordinance was carried out four times really, but there was the white and the red and they were always mixed. We see they are mixed here. "And he was who begot Atum." Now here is our friend Atum again who in turn is the father or the begetter of the pesedj. The writing on this pesedj here, that means the nine. You can count the nine, and everybody translates that as ennead, but it isn't ennead. Sethe says about this, "Perhaps we are dealing here with the ennead as no more consisting of nine members than at Karnak, the writing of which has no connection whatever anymore with the number nine." Anytime you want a large council, it's ennead. With the Egyptians whenever you want a thing to be taken literally (they use it much more later on, but we should have some examples of it here), you put a little stroke by it signifying one-one item, one thing. It's interesting how that's used later on. You have to use it with every word later on if it's taken literally. If you want it to be dual, you put two strokes by it. But three means any number after two. You're not going to go on adding four, five, six and seven after that. So you have a singular, a plural, a dual and a plural. Incidentally, the dual is the same as it is in all indo-european languages. It sounds the same way. And the plural is the same. You'd be surprised. You'd think it was Latin or Greek or something like that here; how much like it they are. Well, anyway he begot the ennead, the nine of them. But he says here it has no connection with the number nine whatever anymore. That would only be something left over from the old Heliopolitan ennead.

And then this means to gather your family. There's a very interesting Iyat there. We mentioned in the Coffin Texts (which are very old, 2000 B.C.) of the nobility, Num. 131 on, and 146 especially, have to do with the sealing of one's family to one's self in the other world-the gathering of the family. That's the word they use for gather the family and for the family too-a family gathering. This means to call together, to assemble. "He assembled the Council of the Gods for discussion." Another interesting word here is wep. You see that all over the place "between Horus and Seth." Notice every time the name Seth occurs, somebody has piously rubbed it out because Seth is the opposition. Seth is Satan, you see; we don't want him in the picture. So for superstitious reasons we wipe him out.

But wep is so interesting. Notice it is always written with the horns. It means "open" and "brow." What is the Latin word for brow? It's ops, optic-the eye etc. And it's the opening out. Open, wep, ops, optic and webben (same thing). It means to rise and shine and all the rest of it. But it is to divide and make distinction. The horns divide and go in opposite directions. It's a division. When you open, what do you do? You unfold thy portals everlasting. You unfold the portals; you open them up. That's why this wep means to make a distinction, to set them apart. Then you go up to the top here. Breasted thinks that means, "he explained, he laid out in particular." But Sethe says it means "to forbid, to put an end to." The three balls you see together there always have the meaning of exception, to rule out; either to explain in detail or to rule out-to say we are not discussing that.

To eliminate, to explain by elimination. We'll say, he forbad their quarreling. Remember, he forbad that there should be any contention among them and he placed Seth on the southern throne in the land in which he was initiated. Later on it says in which he was born. But initiated will do because that's what it says. "In the land in which he was born therein at the place of Saa, a prehistoric place."

And then the other one. We mentioned who Geb was. He is the principle of legitimate succession. It's an interesting thing here that it is Geb who puts Horus on the throne of the North-on the bee throne, the deseret throne. Deseret means red land, (it was red rock country) and it is the word for holy land. All these things are mixed up. But it is Geb who appoints Horus because Geb is the principle of legitimate partriarchal succession. I was puzzled the last time because then he should have put Seth in there too. But he doesn't, which is may be significant-the fact that Horus is the only one that is legitimate. Then in the northland (this is Mekhy). Paul Karge said that the word Mekhy, like the word Mexico, means northland. This Mekhy always means "northland," and Mexico in the Central American language meant north country too. Another parallel with Egypt; you get lots of them. "At the place where his father was drowned." Now his father goes into the water there, and he emerges from the water to come into a new life and to mount the throne. Well, obviously baptism. In fact, that's what Gardiner calls it. He wrote a long article in the JEA on "The Baptism of Pharaoh." The place where he was initiated; the place where his father was baptized.

At the division of the lands en pesh taw (same word as part, split, etc.). There's a very interesting conjunction here that you only find in very old Egyptian. Then Horus and Seth mounted. See, here is an aha which is a mast on a ship which you mount. It means mounted up, of course. They both mounted upon the two thrones of Horus and Seth. This is a very interesting word. I talked with Professor Edwards when he was here about this. This is what an iyat is. If you go through the Pyramid Texts, you will find every once in a while a person makes a pilgrimage to the most holy place, and he circumambulates the iyats. There are usually three of them-always written like this. It is a mound surrounded by standing stones. This is the iyat, and that's what it says here. Each one has his iyat or his ceremonial symbol, which means it goes back to megalithic times (Stone Age). It's very old stuff. Each one has his iyat because we know that's what Heliopolis was. Mariette discovered that it was such a founding. Each had his iyat here, and it's very interesting that Edwards himself has come to accept that idea as the origin of the pyramid now. Somebody has just written a monograph on the subject which justifies it. They never would hear of it before. At the division of the lands, the two lands were pacified and reconciled. This is our old friend Hetep. You always see hetep all over the place in everybody's name. They pacified and settled the two lands at Eon, which means the beautiful place (like Nauvoo). At the Eon which is the place where the lands are divided. Remember King Lib dividing the Land North and the Land South.

Now we come to this next one. And Geb now speaks to Seth. You can always tell Seth because his name has been blacked out.

Now this formula; there's no doubt what that means. These words must be spoken out loud. They must be recited exactly as they are written, and these are to be spoken by the person who is taking the part of Geb, the earth god. Now the goose is purely phonetic because all of these characters are human. Nowhere do you find them in any animal or bird form or anything like that. This is the oldest times; it was all human. And he speaks to him and says, "Go to the place where thou wert born." So it's born here. And here are stage instructions. Seth takes his place on the south side of the hall. So this is dramatized that way. Then he says to Horus, "You go to the place where your father was buried in the waters-where he was drowned in that place. Horus goes to the north side of the hall. Then he addresses the two of them together, Horus and Seth. He says, "I have divided you; I have set you apart" (notice there are the dividing horns). So they take their places north and south, and that's it.

It continues this way now. Geb then speaks to the entire assembly of the gods (there are the three signs of divinity), and he says these words, "I have commanded Horus saying you are the yinnep, which means successor to the throne. It can also be the wetu, which means the same thing. It's related to the word for wepwawet here. "You are the successor," he says to him. Geb speaks to the gods again, "Only Horus; he is the only heir." You notice the word for heir or inherit is a leg of beef, called suet or iyat here. You notice why it is a piece of meat. It goes back to early times. Who gets the biggest piece at the table? You know in Homer the quivering chines of beef and you compete about the meat of honor. Well, at the table who gets the biggest chunk? This is a quivering chine of beef, you see, and it means a payment, or often a reward. You put a t on the end and it means he is the heir. He alone receives the inheritance. Then he says to them again very emphatically, "This one is given the heir." (And it's the old old form) "He is the heir; he is the only heir." He is making this known to everybody, you see. "I have chosen him. I have chosen for the son of my son Horus to be the (notice here is Wepwawet).

You will notice on the Facsimile No. 2 that the top figure carries the staff with the dog on top (that's Wepwawet) who opens the way, begins the years. It is the hound of the Pharaoh, or whoever it is, that goes ahead of you in the hunt and smells out the way in front of you, scares out the game, and leads the way. So Wepwawet is the one who comes first, of course. It goes back to hunting culture. And he is the one who shall be the opener of the way. Here it is written out, the Wepwawet. He is the one who will open the way. But here he is the wetu. This means the principle of patriarchal succession. He repeats the same thing again here, "He is the opener of the womb." There's wep again, you see. The firstborn, in other words. It is the belly and udders of a cow. And wep-en-khat is the one who opens the womb, the first born. "He is my son; he is my first born," he says here. "Horus, who is Wepwawet, who opens the way. He will establish things on the earth and take over."

Then in the final one here, he speaks again to the Council of the Gods and says, "This is my son who is born, who is Horus, and who is born at the new year." So it celebrates the new year. It's the beginning of a new age. Everything starts out at once-a new life, a new age, a new kingdom and everything else. We are founding a new kingdom, a new dynasty. What better than to go back to the beginning of all times; the oldest, most stable kingdom in the world when it was founded, and that's this. So this is founded at the new year. This was noticed long ago, this 18 here. Everybody noticed that. This is the end of Act One. They have been given their appointments. Things are settled, etc. Now what's going to happen? The play has to move now. So we get to Act Two, and the trouble begins. But we won't have time for any more trouble than we have already had here. We should be able to finish it the next time, ha ha.

Lecture 9

The Shabako Stone, cont.

Now, let's review what we said before. Remember this is the oldest document in the world, oldest writing in the world, and there are many to back it up. They have found a Demotic copy of this quite recently, so it is confirmed by other sources. We were just able to get started with it. We have to go fast because you are going to find this on many other very old documents. We will refer to them perhaps a little later. This is all very close to our Pearl of Great Price. This is the situation: Ptah-Tenen is going to establish the heavenly order on a new world. The setting is this, and they are going to be united in the United Kingdom of Egypt. And so he begets Atum; Atum begets the Council of the Gods. They are all identical. Now there are two things that are mentioned. Remember that Zabkar said, and many others have said (everybody has noted this), including Breasted. (You may have read that article in The Monist by now.) They have noted that this is very close to the Gospel of John and Genesis for two reasons. It emphasizes two things that you will find all over in John and Genesis; namely, the work of identification. John chapters 14 through 17, "I am in the Father, and they are in me, and I am in them. Those that listen to us, they are in us." What does it mean by "in us"? And "I am out of the Father ek Theou." These are the expressions that are used here in the same sense. And the other, the theme of conflict of Genesis, is the theme of creation. Well, this is John 2. The logos, the mystery of the word, the doctrine of creation by the word, the Word of God. And in Genesis God spake, and it's the word. And it uses the very same word; the Old Testament uses sepet which means "lips." Here it is ushepet which is the very same thing, by his lips.

First the situation; now we can begin. The most high god who is acclaimed decides to establish Ptah-Tenen here, and this is the situation in Egypt. He is the father of Atum, and through Atum, and through himself also, the father of all the Council of the Gods. He calls the Council of the Gods and they discuss a division between Seth and Horus. Seth is mentioned first here. But when he settles the land, he settles Seth first. Seth is the plaintiff here. He has always been making trouble, and that's going to be a very important element here. Seth is our Satan, of course. But the land is divided between them, the kings of the North and the South, and they are equal. This ends here. He sends each one to his part of the hall and says, "Let there be no more argument between you." And each one takes his place. But then he does a strange thing. He decides to give the whole inheritance to Horus. Why is that? Why did he change his mind? For two reasons made very clear here. First of all, he was very much disturbed. Geb's heart was troubled at the fact that he had givenÃ‚Â…Now Ptah doesn't change his mind here. It's his plan, but Geb's business (remember Geb represents the earth and everything on the earth) is to implement the divine plan down here. And he saw that it would never work with this equal division here. Therefore, he is troubled that the division was equal between Horus and Seth.

So Geb gave his own entire portion to Horus, the son of his son who was his firstborn. Well, what the theme is here is priesthood and kingship as well as land. Now, the division of the land is no trouble. You notice in the Bible the land is divided among the sons of Noah-Shem, Ham, and Japheth. No controversy there. In the fourteenth chapter of Genesis it's divided among the nations, etc. And the children of Israel divide it again. Israel (Jacob) divides it among his twelve sons; and they get their shares and that's all right. But they are jealous of one who is Joseph. They were jealous of him because he got the garment of the priesthood. We won't go into the story of the garment now, but the garment of many colors; incidentally, the word "many" is added in the King James; it's not in the Bible at all. It says the garment that had marks in it. It's a long story about the garment. When it was brought to Jacob by the brothers to show him that the lion had killed Joseph, Jacob both wept and laughed (this is a story told in the Book of Mormon). He was both pleased and oppressed by the fact that the garment was soiled and bloody. But also it showed that Joseph was alive. When he felt the garment (this is a very old source), he says he knew it. Remember, he had gone blind from weeping we are told. When he felt the garment, he knew it was the true garment of Joseph because we are told there was no other garment in the world like that. It was the garment that he had received from his father, Abraham, and Abraham had received it from Adam, and Adam had received it in the garden. This was the garment of the priesthood of which there was only one. When he gave this to Joseph, of course his brethren were jealous. And remember in the dreams he had, he was the king and they all bowed down to him. You can only have one king. And where did they bow down to him? In Egypt. This is an Egyptian story too as far as he goes, remember? Joseph becomes the second in power. He becomes the king's right hand. With all this authority he becomes a Rp't in fact which allows him to sit on the throne. So the brethren were very jealous of him because he became king.

But the division of the land is not the question of who is to be priest and king, and there can only be one. And that's the trouble here. So Geb gives his whole inheritance to Horus because you're not going to have a divided inheritance for one thing. Notice, the son of his son. It's the law of partriarchal succession and primogeniture. It all has to go to one, because if you start dividing up the kingship there is going to be lots of trouble. And Seth is the one to make trouble. Remember, the reason they held this thing in the first place is that there was trouble between Horus and Seth. So this had been going on before. And he finally decides to give it to Horus. Well, what happens to Seth? Is he mentioned anymore here? We soon discover down here why Seth is in discredit-because he had made trouble before. He's the one who introduces all sorts of mischief into the picture, as we see here. We read this right down to here. He divided it equally, and they all had parts of the land. Well, let's go on then.

Then it starts out with another theme. All of a sudden the Osiris theme is introduced here. Isis and Nephthys are worrying; without delay they draw him from the water. Notice in the top figure here is a picture of mi, and here are the waves of water. You notice Osiris lying on his back, and the sign on it is mer, which is "pool."

Notice that in old Celtic and old English mere is a pool, as in windamere, etc. The Latin word for sea is mare or la mer. The Egyptians used exactly the same word that we use for a sea, a pool, or a lake. So he is baptized. Notice he is lying on his back, an unusual thing here. "He comes of the place of Osiris at the water that belonged to him where it was established anew" (well, it's all missing here). But then here it is again. This is the story where Osiris is overcome and either thrown into the water or drowned (Sethe says you have your choice there) by the wicked Seth. So Seth has been making trouble all along. The two main problems here, you might say, are kingship and priesthood (dominion upon earth) and will the show go on? I mean if it's only temporary, who cares? It's eternal life and resurrection. So the two themes: eternity, life, resurrection and power and authority are the whole theme of the Shabako Stone. Well, now let's see, do we have anything here that we haven't mentioned? The lands were divided, etc. Well, the king in Egypt is the high priest, and by definition, of course, there can be only one. And the purpose of the text is to explain that-how it's possible to have one. So this is important to know, how it becomes one. We didn't read this did we?

Then Horus rose up as lord of the land of the South, and he bound together Dep and Peh; and he was acclaimed then in his great name as Uniter of the Palace of the North and the South at the place of the Lord of eternity. And it grew upon his head the weret-hekau which means the one who had ultimate power. It's translated as the greatest magic or something. Hekau usually refers to a goddess-she who is most great and powerful in authority. But it refers to the two crowns. It says they grew together on his head and became one crown so that Horus arose and mounted the throne as the king of both the South and the North-the sedge and the bee-and the uniter of the two lands at the temple in the place where the two lands are united. Two columns were placed in front of the temple (it is always so). We have this in the Bible, the two columns which were borrowed from Egypt, incidentally. Solomon borrowed those two columns. Hiram of Tyre had them cast in bronze, and they were in the manner of Egyptian temples. These two trees were set at the door to represent the North and the South. There's a very famous Egyptian story from the Middle Kingdom, the story of the two brothers, about the two trees that stand before the temple here. They were placed at the gates of the temple (the house of Ptah). Horus and Seth they represented. So the two of them were reconciled (notice the dual there) and they joined together; and the good old Sensen papyrus (same as the word for brother). Sensen means to embrace or kiss. And they united together and became one, and there was no more quarreling between them. Well, that seems like a happy ending after he decided to give it all to Horus. But then we find the trouble here. We come to the drama of Osiris where he has to go through the waters of death, etc. This goes back to the Osiris story. Isis and Nephthys are speaking to Horus, and he is telling them, "Go and rescue him out of the water." They are supposed to raise him out of the water, and he is restored then because he doesn't die completely. It follows right down to the bottom line. And they caused him to come up onto the shore here (it goes back to here, but it is missing). Then here at the top. Now they build the temple here. This part has to do with the building of the temple (the lines are hard to read here; yes, here we go).

Isis and Nephthys speak to Horus, "Go thou and do so and so." And this is the house of the old man. It means the very old man, the Ancient of Days. Notice it's the old man leaning on a staff. And it's the house of the temple in something. And seqed means "build or construct." See there's a man building a wall, and the word seqed means "to build up." Build the house of the king. Sethe says it's the king's castle or house. But it is in so many other things, as in our Joseph Smith papyrus, the Hypocephalus. It's there always-the house of the ancient of days. So they build the temple and things are connected here. This isn't the creation story here. Now this middle part is this drama. We'll shift over to the other now, but most of it is on this side anyway. This, remember, is where the millstone ground and ground and wiped everything out completely. This was the thing that kept this part from slipping around to the other part. They had to hold it down fast. But this part, of course, was hollow, so some of it was spared. But this drama is going on, and three people are speaking: Isis, Horus and Seth. There's an argument, and Isis says something here, and then he says, "You will have joy and happiness hereafter." Then one of them says, (well, that's strange; there's something missing here) "Your life shall be sweet to you" is what he says here. That's interesting because on the others (I have a number of reproductions) there's a sign like this there which is nedjem or bener. It means sweet, agreeable. The Johannisbaum fruit. What do we call that? It looks like a castor bean. We have them around here. That's the one they use. It means sweet and agreeable. "Your life will be sweet and agreeable," somebody tells them. "And he will wipe away your tears." There are the tears coming down from the eye. So there is some sort of drama here. The three people are arguing-the man, the woman and Seth (Satan). And he says, "There will be peace to you, and your life will be sweet to you. Then he will wipe away your tears." But all the rest of it is missing.

So now we go to the important part of it which is the creation episode, the second half. Yes, that's the way it should be. Notice, here you see the house of the old man, the house of the Ancient of Days. Now we come to this, and this is the theology of the creation. These three are met together (this is why they couldn't read it because it is written backwards). It should read this way by the direction the figures are facing, but actually it's read this way, which Breasted discovered. These are the forms that Ptah bestowed upon all life-how Ptah extended himself and was able to put his life, his nature, and his stamp upon all that was created. The gods who came into existence in or through Ptah. And first of all there is Ptah upon the great throne. He's the first Ptah. These are different Ptahs, you see. Then there is Ptah-Nun, the heavenly Ptah (incidentally, he is standing in the shrine of the temple of the South here), who is the father of Atum. Then there is Ptah-Naunet who is the mother who bore Atum. And then there is the great Ptah who is the mind, the lips, and the tongue of the Pesedjet of all the gods-whose mind and heart are in all the gods. You notice you have a matter of degrees here. You have a transmission of power, and what is it? Identity? That's what you have in John. The nineteenth chapter of 3 Nephi is the same thing. He goes aside and prays that I may be in you and you, Father, in me; and those that follow me may be in me as you are in me, Father; and that those who follow them (their converts) may also be one as we are one.

This idea of being in others and being identified. That's what we have here, and it's one of the keys of Egyptian, this doctrine of identity. In what sense are they identical? Down here we see "the lord who begot the gods" and this was obviously Nefertem that is at the nose of Re everyday. That's resurrection. He smells the flower Nefertem which is the opening lotus and that revives life everyday-the nose of Re, the sun.

Now we go to the top here. Notice it starts dividing into the two, and this is very important because everything happens between these two. You will see in the Book of Moses we are going to get the very same thing. Great emphasis is laid on this in Moses 1:4 and 38-39 which we all know by heart. So it happened in the mind and heart (that's the word hat; same word as our word head, front, etc; and there's a picture of a heart), and upon the tongue that the image and likeness of Ptah was in Tem. See, through the mind and the heart of Ptah, he established his image in Atum. Great and mighty is Ptah. When he creates something, then everybody breaks out into the creation hymn, which is a very important hymn. Our word poetry comes from that. As Walter Otto showed, the Greek word poiema is "to make." It means creation. It's the creation hymn. It was first sung by the Muses to celebrate the creation. Remember, when the plan of creation was decided on and everything was passed, then the "the morning stars sang together and the sons of God shouted for joy." That's the creation hymn. We often find reference to it-to hail God for his greatness, and rejoicing, overwhelmed with joy and exaltation at the thought of the creation of a new world coming into existence. That's what happened. "His mind was imparted; his image is in Tem. Great and mighty is Ptah." They acclaim him because through Atum, it says, it came to all the spirits as well. Not only to Atum but to all other spirits as well did Ptah bestow being; in the heart and in the tongue. We say heart and mind because they are the same.

And then again it divides. Notice this is Kheper en. "And it came to pass that Horus and Djehuty-Thoth cooperated with him." There are three that are going to operate then. There is Ptah. You notice Geb waits down below to take things over. Atum is the one who transmits the seed. But at the creation these three made the main council: Ptah, Horus and Djehuty-Thoth. As you know, he is Hermes, the secretary of the gods, the spirit, the revealer, the one who keeps all the records, the one who has all knowledge. He is the god of knowledge, the god of wisdom, the god of counsel. He is always rendered in the Greek by Hermes, so that's where you get hermetism, the source of all wisdom, etc. So these three. And, of course, Horus is the son; he is always the son. Horus, the son, and Djehuty who is represented in the abstract (well, so is Horus for that matter) by the ibis. They came into existence; they were in Ptah, with Ptah. They cooperated with Ptah in all this. Sekhem is a very common word. You get it in the names of kings such as Sekhemwy out here in the museum. It means might, power, authority-usually rendered as "authority."

"And authority, might, and power of the heart and of the tongue were in what he did." This again is something that has been restored. This is way back; it was made in 1901. This is in the thirty-ninth volume of the Aegyptische Zeitschrift, and it is Breasted's. Since then they have made a couple of corrections, and this is one of them. "It is the doctrine, the teaching that Ptah enters into" Well, henet is to stand in front, to confront, to be joined with, to be as close as you possibly can. "Ã‚Â…that he is in every body (that's the physical body) and in every mouth of all gods, of all human beings (male and female), of all cattle (there's a cattle hook), of all creeping things (notice there's a serpent), and all other forms of life, according to the plan and according to the order." It was planned and it was ordained. It uses this throughout the Book of Abraham, you notice. It says they planned and then they saw that their commands would be obeyed. They would plan and then they would give their orders, and the orders would be followed. That's what this talks about. "According to what was planned, ordained and commanded, according to his good pleasure." It was all according to his pleasure.

So we have the heart and the tongue acting here, and this is very important because, remember, in Moses he talks about the creation. This is very important because this is the famous logos theology that baffles everybody, and there's no need to be baffled because it is wonderfully clear in Moses l:4. The logos is the word. Remember, the Book of John starts out, "en arche en ho logos, In the beginning was the logos (discussion, council, word, reason). It could mean all those things. And the Book of Genesis begins the same way. Genesis begins in the beginning, but that begins beresheit, which Joseph Smith says is in the Council of the Gods. In the beginning they call out the council, beresheit barau. He separated them. There was this council and the discussion.

Well, what is the mystery of the logos? He tells us here what it is. Notice, he says in Moses 1:4 here: "I will show thee the workmanship of mine hands; but not all, for my works are without end, and also my words, for they never cease." They are the works and the words; they always go together. And then he says here in Moses 1:38 : "And as one earth shall pass away, and the heavens thereof even so shall another come; and there is no end to my works, neither to my words." The works are never without the words. It's very interesting. And the next verse, the thirty-ninth, tells us why they must go together. "For behold, this is my work and my glory-to bring to pass the immortality and eternal life of man." It's to pass it on. The glory of God is intelligence, and it is shared intelligence. There's no glory in a vacuum. What is the purpose of the word? It's not mystical; it's to communicate. The word is to communicate. It's to pass on, to share it with others. That's what he is doing here. It was in his heart and he brings it up in the Council of the Gods, and they are able to share it. It's through the word that this happens, and it's literally true. This is going to tell us how that happens. Incidentally, the psychology of the word is going to be explained very clearly and vividly here in a passage quite close to the one in the Sefer Yetsirah.

Well, let's see what he says here now. Let's see if there is a continuation here. "Everything was done as it pleased him." Then we go down here. The Pesedjet, "His council was in his presence in as." Notice, the full council, the pesedj, is always drawn this way as a circle divided in two because this is important. It represents the two sides of the argument (it [p.7] represents "yin" and "yang"), and it's always represented this way. That's what this has here. It's on stone, but very faintly it has a line through the middle here (the pesedj). "And this council was in his presence as his heart and as his lips, as his teeth in his mouth [see, to make enunciation possible] and as his hands." There were two aspects, the begetting and the formation. Remember, as what's-his-name says of Atum: "er shafft nicht, er zeucht, he doesn't create but he begets." But both belong here. This is both creating and begetting. The word for teeth and the word for lips (same as in Hebrew), and here are the two hands. And Atum was the agent through which this was done. And through him came all the gods, and by his begetting (phallic symbol there) and by his fingers, by his shaping (there's a literal laboratory procedure going on here). It's written djaba and a picture of three fingers. It has to be plural because there are more than two fingers; en djaba is the same word that is used in Arabic or Hebrew, so that's convenient. "And the Council of the Gods (this becomes rather important here) they are as his own teeth and his own lips in his utterance. And as things were brought forth, he pronounced the name of everything as it came forth from Shu and Tefnut," They are the Egyptian Adam and Eve. They are the gods who come down to beget the human race. But you notice in Genesis a very important thing is that the animals are brought to Adam and he names them. Remember, he names everything. It tells us in Gen. 2, as in the Pearl of Great Price, that God brought these creatures before Adam so he could give them a name, so he could classify them. You may have read the psychologist Levi-Strauss who has revolutionized the study of primitive man in our time. He shows that what the primitives do better than anybody else is classify. They are marvelous classifiers. They know hundreds of types of plants and animals that the people who go to study them have no idea about. They know far more than the people who come to study them. And they are all named as they come forth for Adam and Eve. I should say Shu and Tefnut. They're our parents here. And then the council brought forth the seeing of the eyes (see the two eyes), the hearing of the two ears, the senef (notice our word snuff, sniffle, snort, snout, snot-all those nose words) Well, they are international and universal. They are similar in Hebrew and Arabic. "And the breathing of the fenet, which is the word for nose (there's a sail with air in it which signifies breath). This is the Opening of the Mouth ordinance which is always performed when one receives an Egyptian endowment-whether it's coronation, initiation, or going through the temple. The first thing you have to be washed and annointed. Then you go through the Opening of the Mouth, first with water and then with oil. They touch your eyes that you may see. It starts with the mouth because you must breathe and act first of all-the most primitive of all organs. Many primitive creatures are nothing but mouth. You don't need eyes and nose so much, but you must have the mouth to live.

Your eyes that you may see, your arms that you may smite evil and all this sort of thing, and they go through that. Eighty-eight cases of that were collected recently by Eberhard Otto-a great collection of texts to show what this Opening of the Mouth was. It was a very important part of the temple. And it's the same thing that is performed in the old Jewish funeral (big words like funeral). The rabbi strikes the corpse on the mouth because it can no longer speak, the eyes because they no longer see. We are told when Adam was cast out of the garden and was dying, he sent Seth and Eve back to the garden to get the oil of annointing to annoint his eyes, ears, nose, etc. so that he would not lose their function-so that he would not die. They were met on the way by an angel (this is very well established; you will find this in your R. H. Charles book) who told them they couldn't have it yet. Until the Son of Man came with redemption, they couldn't have this ordinance. And so they had to go back without the oil of annointing. But it was to reverse the order of death. But at the funeral the rabbi angrily strikes the ears because they won't hear anymore, the eyes because they won't see anymore. Well, these were the blows of death inflicted by Satan. Cain learned them from Satan. That's a long story, but these things are all related. You'd be surprised how they tie in together.

Now we come to this interesting psychology. You notice these are the sensory organs. These are the perceptors. We get our knowledge of the world from them supposedly. No, we don't. They just come in as electrical impulses. That's all they are-just a form of energy. We have to form them inside when we get them. They come through these senses, but you notice the eyes, the ears, the mouth, the nose do not give out. They do not project; they only receive. It comes in and it's locked in you. You have your private universe. What do you do? It's only the word by which you can communicate this with others. That's why the word is so very important. You do not radiate from your eyes (you do some, but not notably); you do not project sound from your ears; smell, taste and the other things. You cannot communicate them with others. The only way of communicating is through the word. "There is no end to my works or to my words." Words mean communication with everybody else. The Lord says his purpose is to share his glory: "My work and my glory is to bring about the immortality and eternal life of man." He wants to share it with everyone. If the glory of God is intelligence, it has to be shared. Glory is shared intelligence. And it is done through the word. So there is no mystery about the logos. They make such a big secret of it. Remember, in the second part of Faust he is reading the Gospel of John in the heiligen Original (the holy original), and he says, "That won't do. With the deed; that sounds much better." Well, this brings them both. Here is the deed (the fingers) and the word. They go together here. This is the tongue. So the word is very important to come out here (and what are we doing here now?).

Notice, they cause to mount up. Here is a pyramid like the oldest stone building in the world-the pyramid at Saqqarah, that magnificent pyramid. It's marvelous, one of the first stone buildings ever built. And the others around it were built at the same time. They are some of the most beautiful and perfectly preserved buildings in the world. They're the oldest stone buildings we have, nothing primitive about them. Well, anyway this is a double stairway leading up "so that they might cause to mount up." 'Alah is the universal Semitic word meaning "to mount up." You know El Al, the airline. "Caused them to mount up to the heart." Now these impressions that you get from the eyes, ears, nose, etc. are conveyed to the heart. And the heart in turn passes them on. They go forth to the ariq. This is a wonderful word. The original word is "bind." Notice the determinative here is a piece of rope or cloth because it [p.9] means to bind. Therefore, it means a "covenant"; it means "sacred word"; it means "comprehension"; it means the "last day of the month"; it means anything "binding"; it means the "ultimate declaration." Here it would be the final structuring of things. Arik is putting everything together. It's very interesting, the arik. In Arabic it's the end of the world. If you have been to the Dome of the Rock, you know the big mosque with the silver dome on it was the holy one. It's called the arqa. It's the end of the world. That's where you take off for the other world. They use the same word as the arik.

"And this will formulate everything for the tongue to repeat and set forth." Now the tongue can convey it, you see. First, you have to receive the impressions (this is good psychology); then it enters into the heart and mind of the thing and it receives a particular form. It has to be handed on, and then it has to be put into comprehensible structure, and then it conveyed by being repeated by the tongue. "What has been thought by the heart is repeated by the tongue."

So the gods were all begotten, and Atum was begotten, and he begot them too. This applies to both Atum and his creatures and all the gods. "And all this happened through the divine command of God which he conceived in his heart and which he commanded by his tongue. And thus it was he made the spirits (this is the name of the ruler). This means those who are preordained. Qenet means to preordain, to set apart, to have a purpose for. And this is the female of the same thing, the hes-mett spirits-those who were to beget children. These were special spirits. He set forth the spirits of these two classifications: the hemswet (Sethe has a long essay on what they were). These are the feminine. Of course that's the symbol of the Lady Nut, the first mother who came to Egypt with her two arrows. And this other one here metenu means those designated for life and all those designated to beget children.

Then this is the last step. He gets the spirits ready to come to earth. "Then he created abundance of supply on the earth for them." Djifa means all manner of food, all manner of substance and anything you need for life. The earth was richly provided. "Adam, we have made for you this earth and provided it with everything." This is food offerings; everything provided in general. This is more specific: hetep, food spread out, every type of it by this counsel (en medet tjen). Then an interesting thing happened. Notice, he has provided the spirits to come last of all. Then he has equipped the world to give them all they need to live on. Then he proceeds to give them a law. Now this is why the whole thing is being done. It must have a purpose here. "He gave this law; namely, whoever does merret (that which is lovable, that which is desirable, that which is of good reporta good definition of what is good: remember it is anything that is lovable) to him shall be given life with eternal happiness and salvation." Hetep means so many things. It means peace, it means salvation; they just used it for food up here. This will be granted to whoever does good. And then right along with it, you notice, this is the law that is given to everything showing that the purpose of the earth is probation. "To everyone that does mesedjetÃ‚Â…" It means that which is hateful, that which is evil. The word mesedjet is a very interesting one.

It's the word that is used in Coptic and the Greeks picked it up, meaning the devil who is the great hater. Well, it the same word as "master" in Master Mahan. That's a funny thing. We don't want to get involved in that now. I don't think that first word "master" has to do with the name master necessarily; it can. But mesedjet is the same one here. Mesedj M-aha means the great hater, the great destroyer-Apollyon, which is what Satan is, of course. But it's anyone who ever does that which is hateful.

That is very interesting; it leaves a subjective definition to good and evil. You can argue until the cows come home, make laws about it, and legislate, but you know what is good (what is lovable and desirable) and you feel in your bones what is evil and what is wrong. "Whoever does that, to him shall be given death in a condition of hebet" (to be held back in prison, to be punished). Hebet is the regular word for crime, criminal punishment. So this is the rule, and it divides it down the middle. A law has been given that whoever does good will receive eternal salvation and whoever does bad will receive the death and combined punishment here. So then he says, according to this law everything which is done-any kind of work at all, any kind of workmanship, art, science, or any more sophisticated operation, the doings of the two arms, the walking of the legs, the motion of the limbs. So all the acts of man, all their achievements, all their activities, everything they make, their arts, and everything they do is governed by this law which shall apply to all things. And this was according to what he commanded, what he had thought in his mind and uttered by his tongue from the middle." Here it is again; this is a very important saying. It's as if it were right out of Protagoras, isn't it? "This law shall be the measure of all things." Some say this shouldn't be on here. This is iyat in that case-the extent by which all things shall be measured. Or if it only has one side, then it means the imakh, the measure of excellence, the measure of judgment. Sethe says it is simply the well-known saying, "This is the measure of all things." So everything that comes to earth is to be measured by this law that who does good will be rewarded and who does evil will be punished. And that's why he set it up. Therefore, as Nephi said, "This life became a time of probation." And that's what he is making it here, a time of probation, and it applies to everything.

And then it happened that when these things had been said and done by Atum, and had been caused to come into existence by all the gods, it was all in the name of Ptah himself, who is Ptah-Tenen now. These things will be transferred to the earth where Geb will take over then, I suppose. He is now Ptah-Tenen, and there's our picture of Ptah-Tenen here. "Whom the gods caused to come forth all things from him [remember, the syntax isn't ours] by the way of food and nourishment, clothing, anything else you need [all life's necessities]." They like to repeat all the time here. "Everything which is beautiful and good. Then it was understood that all this was by the great might and power of Ptah through the council. But it was Ptah who did it all. And after he had completed this and looked upon it all, he saw that it was good." As Sethe points out, this is exactly what it says in Genesis: He looked upon it and saw that it was good. "When he saw all things produced by his word [notice, produced by his word but brought forth by the gods] then the gods proceeded to establish cities on earth."

As I said, this is to be translated down to earth. They've got to get civilization going here.

There's a picture of three cities. It seems that it's always plural, and it's the city symbol. Rome was Roma quadrata, divided into four parts. That was a part of the mystery. It all goes back to an early sacral ceremonial center when you had to be boxed to the compass. We still do in Utah-North Temple, South Temple-everything is gauged according to the temple. Brigham Young was very careful about this. Everything must be very carefully boxed to the temple because it is a scale model of the universe. Everything counts itself from the temple; it's an hierocentric society-the last on earth probably now. But that's what they have here. "And he created the city, and he caused to be constructed [notice the constructing is a picture of an adze chopping a board] the fields and farms." These were fields sepet or nomes. The Latin word septum is a division of things. These are districts on earth where people would settle, of course. "And then the time had come; he placed the gods in their shrines (temples, houses) so he could cause to flourish there the sacred offerings, the sacred food. And he built their shrines [there are three of them] and their statues of their bodies in forms which would please them. Then the gods entered into their images in all manner of wood and precious stone and metal, and all manner of stuff that flourishes upon the face of the earth." So they have established the temples now. "And then this all being done, he gathers them all together." Here again he summons the gods all together in the grand Council of the Spirits and the Gods all together. This is a mysterious name which means he was pleased the way things were gathering. That's the name in which they met at this time-the lord of the two worlds. "And he is the provider of corn for the land." This is a corn barn here. Ptah-Tenen supplies all the grain for the land. "And here is the great throne where all the gods are united within the temple of Ptah [there's Ptah's name written in the temple] and they are all there to hail life. And there he gave life to the two lands at that place. From this place life goes forth to the two lands."

These last three have to do with Osiris-the baptism and the resurrection theme and the final glorious consummation. We'll put that to the next time because that's rather important too. This is strange stuff, but oddly familiar. We are not the only ones that recognize it. Everybody, every Egyptologist recognizes the relationship to the Bible. There's something really going on here. It's very old, and the Pearl of Great Price is what ties it all together. We saw that, "No end to my works or my words."

Lecture 10

The Babylonian Creation Myth

Those last three lines of the Shabako Stone are rather important, so I've just got to get rid of them. It's a nuisance, but it's worth it because they are very important. They are what the whole thing is getting at-how after baptism he gets eternal salvation and exaltation, etc.

Here are some things I'm going to put on reserve. They'll be very useful, I think. Tha'labi says that in heaven there is a screen of mother of pearl like this, and there are beams like laser beams that write the affairs of various distant planets, including the earth, upon these screens, and they play out very skillfully everything that is done. The angels consult them to find out what is going on.

Incidentally, it is framed in red rubies because that is the principle of the laser, the ruby. This is an Arabic writer from the tenth century, but he gets it from the Jews. It's framed in red ruby, and it siphons these beams in, which are like lasers. It says that God consults it three times a day to see where he is supposed to send angels and to see what is going on. Gadgets everywhere; science fiction all over the place. It's very interesting and ingenius, though, that they should have connected these things like that-the mother of pearl screen and the like.

Speaking of science fiction, do you know who the first LDS science fiction writer was? Some people think that we take naturally to science fiction. It was W. W. Phelps. He wrote a story which is in this book at some length, several pages. It's a science fiction story explaining certain things, the creation etc., in terms of space travel. It's very interesting. I'm going to Xerox it because it's real science fiction by that character, W. W. Phelps.

We are just going to put on the tail end of this because, as I say, it is important that we put it on. That's right; that's the way it should be. Just the final things we want to take into consideration here, these last two lines. It starts out here in the palace of Ptah where life begins to the place where all things come together and all things are brought to a conclusion. Then it starts at the top, "And Osiris was submerged in his water [there is something particular about it] and the two ladies saw him and were supposed to rescue him. And Horus gave an order and cried out to them, 'Ladies, go rescue him and take him from the water and prevent his drowning.'"

They run around, and just in time they catch him and bring him up again upon the land. Being upon the land, he asserts himself. He now enters through the gate [it also means initiation] of the glory of the land of the holy ones, the lords of eternity." The final test is the baptism. He emerges from the waters of life. Remember, this is the theme you are going to get in all your year dramas. This is the de profundis formula from the Psalms, "out of the depths I call."

It's the king, you see; he calls on the Lord to rescue him from the depths. The same thing happens in the first chapter of Moses in our Pearl of Great Price where Moses is delivered from the waters and comes out. The Lord says, "I will give you power over many waters, and you shall be the king. They shall obey you as if you were God, and you shall lead my people." It's the old acclamation of the king, the same motif that goes on here. And he's brought from the water. You'll see that in this passage of Moses; it's quite relevant actually, in Moses 1:25 : "And calling upon the name of God, he beheld his glory again." He emerges again. Remember, he is overcome; he is overwhelmed by the depths. He calls out with his last breath of strength to be rescued, and then God saves him out of the depths (delivers him, you see). "He saw the bitterness of hell." That means he went down into the waters. He despaired; he gave up. It says here in verse 20 that he feared, "and as he began to fear, he saw the bitterness of hell." Then he received strength to call upon God and Satan was banished. Then he emerged again here, and calling upon the Lord he beheld his glory. And the voice of the Lord said you have won: "Blessed art thou, Moses, for I, the Almighty, have chosen thee, and thou shalt be made stronger than many waters [Moses was-both the Red Sea and the waters of Marah, and also the waters of death he is calling out from here], for they shall obey thy command as if thou wert God. [The king's representative on earth he is.] And lo, I am with thee, even unto the end of thy days; for thou shalt deliver my people from bondage (verses 20-26)."

It is God's people that Moses is delivering; the king only holds his authority this way. Well, in the same way he emerges and on doing so, this is what he does. He enters immediately in through the gate of the great holy ones of eternity. The word holy here is written very phonetically, desheureu; it's deseret, the word for "holy, set apart, sanctified." They use the word deseret a great deal. It's the red land, the holy land-very special. And, of course, the red crown of the North is called the deseret crown, and the deseret bee is the symbol. It all goes back to her anyway. "The lords of eternity to walk along the same paths of him who rises in glory on the horizon along the way." Metchenu is the word for "highway," and it's used all through as the way of life. At the tomb of Petosiris, for example, "I will teach you the way of life, the way of truth. God knows the way of the righteous, but the way of the wicked shall get lost in the desert and shall perish. So I will lead you on the wayÃ‚Â…"

This language all mixes in. It's very close to the Old Testament and to John and Genesis too. Well, on the way, on the highway. That's a picture of the way with the milestones along it. It's the word wat for way. Watling Street in England. Wat is to wait, to walk along, to stand along. It's cognate with our language all the way through here. "Ã‚Â…to the heavenly throne, to the great throne here. When you reach the great throne, there he shall be united in salvation and safety." This is Setep sa. It means the chosen and the saved or eternal. These are the knots that you wear on your shoulder here on the sacred garment. It represents security and safety because with a knot you tie a thing and make it safe. The knot is secure and tied. It's pronounced sah, and it's the name that was later given to the palace at Luxor, but it means the holy and safe place-the place where the gods assemble in brotherhood. Notice the two brother signs, sensen, with the nose there for sniff, "Ã‚Â…and they shall kiss one another and fall upon [p.3] each other's shoulders." This is like the ending of The Pearl, remember, when they all greet each other. "And he shall be entered into the brotherhood of the gods." Sensen is the two brothers. Our words twin and twain. Shenayim in Hebrew means "two," but in Arabic it's ethnayn (like twain) which means "two." It's perfectly good Old English. That's the word we have here.

"With his brethren the gods as at the place of good old Ptah-Tenen [Ptah has become the ruler upon the earth, but now he's up in heaven] where Ptah is in the heavens, who is the Lord of eternities, the Ancient of Days [literally the Lord of years] and then he shall become the Osiris, the one who enters his temple." See, he has now been sanctified. The idea is that Osiris doesn't stay in the grave. He comes and introduces the ordinances for redemption for the dead on earth. So he enters into his temple. Here is the house of the great old man. It is often rendered "the house of the Ancient of Days," in other words God's representative on earth. Notice, it is all representation here. As the Lord told Moses, "Ã‚Â…as if thou wert God." We say the Egyptians are always mixing these things up. You see it in the museum. Well, that's part of the scriptures. You do that. And Moses and Elijah and Elisha, etc., the garment of Moses. Then he approaches his son finally. "Then he and his son shall be brought together-his son Horus who is reigning on the two thrones in the embrace of his arms" (as Sethe shows). This is when the father and son embrace as the last act of his being initiated and set upon his throne. "In the presence of all the gods who were before an eternity and who are after an eternity." So this takes us back into the eternal scene again. We've gone through the cycle here.

I notice for the first time there's a good photograph of this thing in one of the books for sale over at the bookstore in the museum. The book is too expensive. I couldn't get it, but it's the first good photograph I've ever seen of it. You can actually read it from the photograph-the original Shabako Stone the way it looks today. Should I review this briefly now before I point out this particular theme? Remember, we said that Hermann Grapow was the author of the three million words of the five-volume Egyptian dictionary. He says this theme of the creation and the world before the creation is that of the Pyramid Texts. You will find it in the Book of the Dead. You'll find it in the Greek and Roman temple inscriptions. You'll find it in the Old Testament. You'll find it in the Babylonian literature. You'll find it in the Rig-Veda. You'll find it in the Vedas and the Sanskrit literature of India, of course. You'll find it in the Old High German. You'll find it in the Gothic Wessobrunner Gebet, and in the Voluspa of our Scandinavian ancestors. And we mentioned the passages from Hesiod's Theogony, the oldest Greek work that talks about how it all began. We also mentioned Mircea Eliade who has done the greatest comparative work of all, bringing the primitives together with the early religions and the modern. He says there is always this central myth which describes the beginnings of the world, including the origin of plants, animals and man-of marriage, family, death, etc. Taken all together these myths of origin constitute a fairly coherent history. You'll find them the same wherever you go. It isn't the sort of thing any primitive would make up, or that you'd make up to tell a child. Or you think that if you go back to the primitives throughout the world you [p.4] find a vast variety of strange exotic rites. You don't. You find the same story everywhere. That's what James Frazer showed in The Golden Bough. He looked all over the world and he found these things everywhere. His conclusion was, which was held until quite recently: well of course people do the same things. It represents a natural degree along the development of the human mind. It's evolutionary. At a certain period of evolution people think in these terms. Well, it's much too elaborate a story, and there are much too close parallels in it to be dismissed that way, so nobody accepts that anymore.

It was just in the 1930s that the Cambridge School started what they called patternism. Wherever you go in the ancient world-ancient, primitive, or modern-you will find the same pattern being followed. And does it go back to some pattern in the human mind in which we just do these things spontaneously? C. G. Jung, the famous German psychologist, still maintains (he's very old now) that there are certain primal images that we all have. But all the gaps have been filled in now, and it appears that they do have a common origin and do go back to a particular thing.

Well, let me turn now quickly to the Babylonian equivalent to this. What do you find in Mesopotamia? You find here the Enuma Elish story. Unfortunately, we can't put this on reserve because the Enuma Elish is on seven tablets. This one of Speiser only produces the last three, and the first four are the creation story. Those are the ones we want. I have made a summary here which I'm going to have Xeroxed today, from the text published by Labat here. I think we can use that. Not to slow us down, but just to show us what we are dealing with-the kind of thing we have on hand here. Speiser, who is the main student of this, says, "The struggle between cosmic order and chaos was to the ancient MesopotamiansÃ‚Â…" This includes the Babylonians, the Sumerians, the Amorites, the people of Mari; it goes clear over to Ebla. This is all the eastern area. Now, their language is close to the Bible. You notice this old Babylonian text is transliterated here. You students who have had Hebrew, Aramaic, and Arabic won't have much trouble. You'll recognize all the words. The grammar is a little different, but you get onto that pretty quickly. It's not bad, except the reading of the cuneiform. (When you get that in the mud, it's pretty terrible.) So you'll have to go to Brother Pack for that. He's very good, one of the best Babylonian men in the country, right here at BYU.

"The fateful drama was renewed at the turn of each new year." Just exactly as in Egypt, they dramatized the thing to celebrate the beginning of the world at each new year. "The epic which deals with these events was, therefore, [and this is important] the most significant expression of religious literature of Mesopotamia." Just as this is the most significant expression, the Memphite Theology is standard and fundamental right down to the end, right down to Genesis and John. In the same way in Mesopotamia, this remains the standard text and, as he says, the most significant expression of religious literature of Mesopotamia. The work consisting of seven tablets was known in Akkadian as Enuma Elish which means "as once above." It begins with the meeting of the gods above, the Great Council, well with the three on the great thrones. Enuma means "that which was happening on high" or "as it was once above, in the beginning." "In my first primeval childhood" as we read in The Pearl. Remember he starts out in heaven [p.5] the same way in his first primeval childhood. "It was recited with due solemnity on the fourth day of the new year festival." What's his name, a Babylonian scholar (I'll think of his name in a minute; I always say I'll think of his name in a minute, and I do too.)-he figured that this was what happened. At the new year (this is a Hopi rite, incidentally) the king had to go into the underworld. He went into a tomb and disappeared for three days. He had to show that he could overcome the powers of death. So he disappears into an underground vault for three days. While he is there he is humiliated. He's dressed in a false robe, he's given a reed to hold in his hand, and his face is slapped by the priest until the tears run. And he puts on a mock crown. Of course, immediately we think of Jesus. There's a book on this by no less a person than Robert Graves. That properly humiliates him and cuts him down to size. Meantime, he is in the grave. Does he have power over the resurrection; does he have the king's secret to be resurrected? On the third day he arises. He comes triumphantly through the Ishtar gate, and as he comes through, this is recited. This is the story that's recited reestablishing-just as it happened in Memphis there when King Shabako refounded the empire, after it was done by Menes. But Menes wasn't the first; it was very ancient. In other words this has a very old background and a worldwide background. Now the latest study by W. G. Lambert says the first major conclusion is that epic of creation is not the norm of Babylonian or Sumerian cosmology. It is a sectarian and aberrant combination. It has been handed down by numbers of groups. See, Babylon didn't have the united civilization that Egypt always had. They didn't have the one idea of the Pharaoh, the united land. It was always broken up into kingdoms and competing temples and religions and everything else. The sectarians all treat the same material, but they treat it in different ways. You get some of that in Egypt too, but not as much as you do in Babylon.

So he says, "This is a sectarian and aberrant combination of mythological threads woven into an unparalleled composition. In my opinion, this composition was not earlier than 1100 B.C." So it is at least a thousand years after the other. But you notice what they do. They have scrambled the motifs, as you could expect, but the motifs are still there. The evidence is all there, but it is scrambled. Just like when you have a stew. The stew has all been chopped up, the vegetables and things, but you can tell what went into it. You can tell there are carrots there, and you can put the carrot parts together, and the potatoes and the onions. You are not fooled at all. And even when they are badly scrambled up like this, you recognize the same familiar few motifs. But this is a most important text.

It will be familiar to you Hebrew students. You should be able to recognize it right from the beginning here, the introduction. "As it was once above." You notice this: What's a nabi? Of course, it's a prophet. Naba is to make an announcement. What's shamau? In Hebrew they follow the Egyptian idea and call it the two heavens, shamayim. They make it dual. God separated the two heavens. That first verse of Genesis actually says in the beginning God separated the two heavens. And that which was below. Its name shem, Ishmael, same as God. Zakhara means to name, to remember. Thakari in Arabic; Zachariah in Hebrew. It's the same one. So it goes along very familiar; it's the old Babylonian (1100 B.C.). Most scholars, however, don't agree with Lambert. They still would put it back to the beginning of the [p.6] Babylonian period. They would put it back around 1900 B.C. But still it was older than that.

This is the way it goes then: "As once above when the heavens had not yet received their name, received a frame from everlasting thou art God to endless years the same." Remember the old congregational hymn? We have it in our books. "Before the hills in order stood, earth received her frame, for everlasting thou art GodÃ‚Â…" It's the school song at Berkeley. It's very old. It goes back years in California. That used to be a congregational school when it started out. "As once above when the heavens had not yet received their name and the earth below was not yet named, the Holy of Holies, the most inner sacred sanctuary of the temple [see, the temple is the central point, the place where you go for the creation story and all the rest] had not yet been built." It's the gipara. There has been a lot written about this. It's the sacred holy of holies, the inner-most chamber of the temple. "Ã‚Â…when the gods were born from the bosom of Apsu and Tiamat." Notice, Apsu is another familiar word to all of us. The Greeks picked that up and used it a great deal in religion, etc. Tiamat is the Hebrew word. In the Old Testament it's tehom. Apsu is our word abyss. Remember in the creation in Milton, "Dovelike sat'st brooding on the vast abyss, And mad'st it pregnant." Well this is the vast abyss. This is the old Babylonian and the old Sumerian. It's very old, you see, the old word for the abyss, the emptiness, the void that wasn't there before.

Tiamat means disorganized, and in the seventy-fifth section of the Book of the Dead where it describes the Flood, because of the wickedness, God says, "I will cause a flood to come and things will return to the huhu and muhu which they were before the creation. That means the disorganized state of chaos. That's what Tiamat is. She is the woman who comes right down into Gnostic literature as Sophia from which things have to be organized-the Apsu and Tiamat. I left something out here because in the text it calls her Mummu, mother. Mummu is the same thing as Tiamat. You notice there is always this tension between the matriarchy and the patriarchy, especially in Babylon. In Egypt there's remarkable equality. They keep things very even there. But here you have this trouble anyway. "Ages passed and Anshar and Kishar of the nobler race of gods were born."

See, you have issues coming along. Things were going on long before this. This is not just the beginning. It's like when the creator, Ptah, calls a meeting to settle a long-standing affair between Horus and Seth that had been going on. It's the same thing. In this case it was between Anshar and Kishar, the nobler race. "The days passed, the years followed. Anshar begot Anu, the firstborn equal to himself." So Anu is the high god, the god of heaven. You don't have to remember these, but just notice we are following the same pattern here. Anshar and Anu, the father and the son. Again, you get the same thing in the other text. "He begot him in his own image." Notice tamish lashu mashala. How would you say it in Arabic? Like him mitlo. Mathala is to be like, to have an image. In Hebrew, of course, it's mashal. In Hebrew you say, lemashal, to give an example. Well, mashal is an image or likeness. It says here in old Babylonian, "according to his own likeness and image." And this was Nudimmud. That's the one who's to be on the earth and is exactly like our Ptah-Tenen. This is the title [p.7] given to Ea as the creator of mankind. It was he who was the creator we are told, he who was a vast intelligence. Remember the Pearl of Great Price, "I am more intelligent than they all." A vast intelligence, omniscient, omnipotent, mightier than the father of his father Anshar. So we have generations, patriarchal line of descent here. The women are bothering things here-Tiamat, because she is going to make trouble.

Now, we begin the story: "There was a great assembly among the brethren of the gods to resist the influence of Tiamat. [That's our tehom, the power of darkness, the underworld.] They began to take measures. There was much stirring in the heavenly mansions for Apsu [the abyss here] and Tiamat [the female who ruled over it]Ã‚Â…" They represent entropy. They want everything to return to a state of chaos and to a state of death and stillness. That's what things are doing; they are all running down. It's as if these people had an insight into the modern physics. These powers are trying to break it down, and we are trying to build it up against them. In the Old Testament it starts out tohu vabohu; it is still used in modern Hebrew. It means complete confusion, a mess, not organized. So it's the same thing here. "For Apsu and Tiamat plan to destroy the race of gods because of their rebellious independence." They did not want man to have his agency.
