Remember, Satan tried to destroy the agency at the beginning. So that is the issue. Are individuals to enjoy independence or not; or are they to revert to their primal state when everything is just lazy, gives up, collapses and goes down. Everything runs downhill. It's the heat death. It's the Second Law of Thermodynamics-everything running down, which is the normal course of things. And, of course, the problem arises-why hasn't it taken place long before-which is quite a mystery.

But this resentment of free agency is called in the poem "the beginning of evil" in line 44. "Their plan of destruction was reported to the gods. They heard about it, and when they heard of it they bestirred themselves. Their voices became silent and they sat perplexed. [This is a vivid scene; very Babylonian.] But the most intelligent, Ea the wiseÃ‚Â…" In the end it always goes up to Ea; he is your Ptah, depending on whether it's Ptah or Atum. Ea means the water. The temple is called the House of Ea. The word for temple is written with a Babylonian grating, like this. It represents the grating that was over the tehom in the temple, the lowest part when you went down. That was the underworld, which in the bottom of the Roman temple would be the mundis. There was a plug, the stone that was taken out once a year. This plugged the waters of the underworld. That's an important thing. Remember, when you go to the temple up here. What will you find in the lowest place? You will find the waters of the underworld where people are buried in baptism-buried in death to arise again, just as we saw Osiris having to go down to the lowest of the low and submit to the baptism, and then be dragged out again.

"Then the most intelligent Ea, the wise, the wonderful counselor who knows all things, perceived their problem. Accordingly, he traced out and determined a cosmic design. He devised against the evil one his pure, irresistible utterance. He proclaimed it and placed his dwelling above the gods." He made the plan of the temple-what the Egyptian would call the center. It's always quadrate. They always make it this way. This is the quadrata; this is basic.

The plan is universal throughout the world; the four parts. All our temples are boxed to the compass-should be. That one isn't, but they should be. It makes that one an endowment house rather than a temple because that's what temple means. Temple means a template boxed to the compass by which we take all our measurements.

To go on with this, "He proclaimed it and placed his dwelling above the waters." Remember we saw the waters of Nun from which all things emerge, the cosmic waters, the waters that surround us. One of the great discoveries of our time is that we are surrounded by water in space. Halley's Comet comes along, the well-known dirty snowball, and it's nearly all water. And you get clear out to Uranus there and see water-covered planets. "And after he had enchained the enemy [remember, Satan is bound in chains] and overcome, Ea celebrated his victory. He entered his chamber to rest and relax." He called his chamber Apsu (the abyss) because he had built it over the prison of his victim kept down there. "In this sacred place of destinies, the temple of divination, the god was begotten in the bosom of Apsu. Marduk was born, and he is the savior. This is the god who was worshipped at Babylon. Remember, this is a sectarian doctrine. Ea is not the principal god at Babylon, and Anu isn't either. He is the sky god, but it is the hero Marduk. "And he was perfect, endowed with double divine nature. His dimensions are incomprehensible, exquisite. He cannot be described. The sight has difficulty comprehending him. Tiamat conspired against the gods then, and sent forth the dragon serpent to fight them." Man and the dragon. It was the hydra, the red dragon.

These things all have association with the stars. These episodes are transferred to the heavens above; that's why we have them today. The longest and biggest constellation is Ophiucus which is the serpent that winds halfway around the heavens, a very important thing. And that's the hydra. It's not one of the houses of the Zodiac. But they come in here too. The red dragon, the ravening lion, the mad wolf-these are the creatures that tear down and destroy. In the Norse mythology in the end of the Ragnarok when all the gods shall come to an end and everything subside, everything collapse and pass away; then the Fenris wolf will take over, the mad wolf and the ravening lion, and the world will revert to the confusion and disorganization with which it began. It's a tragic ending. But this theme is the same one. But they are fighting against each other. This is opposition. "She exalted her son Kingu as the rival of Marduk." So we have the Horus and Seth rivalry here. There is going to be a ritual combat between Kingu and Marduk. The son of Tiamat, her favorite who was to be the champion, was Kingu. You notice there's a lot that's medieval about this. This whole thing was taken over in the chivalric order in the Middle Ages, and you get all this sort of hocus-pocus going on there. "She directed her hosts against the gods of heaven. She enthroned Kingu, gave him the tablets of destiny, and attached them to his breast." Now the twelve tablets of destiny. Remember, the high priest in Israel had to have twelve stones on a breastplate attached to his breast, and they represented the twelve months and the twelve tribes. But they also represent the destinies, each month of the year. This has to do with the Zodiac and all the rest. But in order to rule you must have the tablets of destiny. The Egyptians are the same way with the necessity of having the document. The king must have his mekes. There's a giant photograph of Ramses, and he's holding a folded document in his hands. That's the mekes, and you can see his name is sealed on it. It's his right to rule-his genealogy as a matter of fact. It gives him his right and his legitimacy. Here you have to have these tablets of destiny or you're not going to have anything.

"In heaven there was consternation and another council. Anshar remained alarmed. He gazed at the ground and groaned [it's a vivid picture here], he shook his head, he ground his teeth. He turned to Ea, shaking his locks." This is Zeus shaking his locks. Same thing as in the Aeneid with Poseidon, the god who shakes his locks. They are not called black. They are called purple, they are so black. He shakes his black locks when he is in a rage or distress. "Then they called an assembly of all the gods." Anunnaki means the gods in assembly. "They were embarrassed; they kept their lips closed. All sat in abashed silence. No god was willing to move against Tiamat [she was too mighty] for none could survive such an encounter. Who can survive the powers of destruction and despair?" See, when Moses feared, then he knew the bitterness of hell and he was finished if he hadn't been rescued at the last moment. He couldn't stand up to it, and it's the same thing here.

"Then the Lord Anshar, father of the gods, arose in majesty, and having thought deeply, he spoke. 'He who is mighty of power, the avenger of his father, Marduk, the hero, shall overcome the power of opposition.' [So they have chosen their hero to go and win the victory for them.] The Lord rejoices at the word of his father [this is Marduk] He received the assignment gladly and rejoiced at the word of his father. With radiant heart, he spake to his father thus, 'Lord of the gods, destiny of the great gods, if I am to be the avenger, call an assembly and render to me my all powerful calling and proclaim it in the great assembly where all may rejoice togetherÃ‚Â…" (I want the power openly and clearly appointed to me among all the gods. So they would know who it was. Of course, they were willing to let him do the dirty work.) "Ã‚Â…in the great assembly where all may rejoice together that my word may establish the destiny even as yours; that whatsoever I may create shall also be eternal, that the command of my lips may be firm and unchanging." Notice, "the command of my lips," shepet, is exactly as used in the Hebrew and in the Egyptian. Remember the Enuma Elish-the utterance of his mouth, the thought of his mind, the logos. It's by the creation of the word. Notice, "That my word may establish the destinies as yours; that the command of my lips may be firm and unchanging." That might just as well be an Egyptian statement; it might be from the Old Testament or anything like that. Remember, you must have the word in order to communicate. God doesn't work in a vacuum. We share a common universe of discourse, but how? As we said before, your sensory organs receive it, but they don't give it out. There's only one way you can tell how you have constructed the universe inside. We know perfectly well that the impulses that come to your senses are nothing but electrical impulses. They have no structure or anything else. You have to build it all up here. As it says in the Enuma Elish, then it goes to your heart and receives its form. Then it is repeated on the tongue; and by the word you convey it and share it with others. "There is no end to my works or my words," and this is my glory to share it with the rest of them. It can only be done through the word. So there's nothing particularly mysterious about the logos which is very important if you are going to [p.10] communicate and prove that there's more than one universe. How do I know that I'm not a solipsist, inventing it all myself-that you're my dream? You have dreams. I have dreams of teaching classes and being all confused like I am today, rather terrible dreams. But how do I know this isn't a dream? Because we can communicate; we can share and show your picture matches mine pretty well, etc. But the great evil of the deception by the word is that Satan was a liar from the beginning. He is the one who loves and makes a lie. If I start telling you what my universe is, you have no right to contradict me. If I tell you what I see, what I hear, and what I remember, and I'm lying to you, I've thrown everything away. There's no world between us; there's no universe. I've brought confusion upon the whole plan of raising God's children up-the plan by which he spreads his glory. Remember, "this is my work and my glory." So Satan is the old deceiver and the destroyer, but he's the one who loves and makes a lie. How easy it is to do, but what a great offense that is. A person takes it for granted you mean what you say, but he has no means of knowing whether you do or not because good liars are very clever. It takes such an important part in Shakespeare's plays, the deception that builds. And it leads to tragedy all the time. Somebody lies, and then you are in real trouble. So this is the same word he is talking about here.

As I said, it's surprising that they talk about the logos as a great mystery and nothing to be explained. But we are of the same universe of discourse as God; therefore, the word can exchange between us. He understands our language and we understand his; whereas the Christian world accepts right from the beginning, right from Alexandria, that God is the Totalitor el. He is totally other. We can have no communication. He is nothing like us in any sense whatsoever. So it doesn't go there.

Let's get along with our text here. Tablet number three: "At the assembly Marduk is proclaimed as the savior. 'Henceforth be thy command unalterable. Marduk, we have given unto thee the universal dominion over all things and make you lord over the earth and everything on the face of the earth. Take thy place in the assembly. Let thy word be sovereign there. Let thy destiny be the same as that of the gods that whatsoever thy word commands [notice the power of the word here] shall be created and destroyed as thou commandest. Speak and matter shall vanish. Speak again, and the same matter return intact.' Marduk spoke and behold material vanished [dissolved, vaporized, turned to another form]. He spoke again and material took substance. When the gods and his father saw that he had this power to create in his word, they said, 'He is qualified.' [He had proved it by this.] They rejoiced and they venerated him. 'Marduk is our king,' and they gave him the scepter and the throne and the crown."

Tablet number four: "Marduk confronts the powers of the evil charging Tiamat. [Of course, they have the stichomythia, the exchange of insults before the fight, which is part of epic literature.] 'Thou wouldst exalt Kingu's power to be equal to that of God Anu on high.' Having overcome Kingu in battle, he chained him and put him in the custody of the god Uggu who took from him the tablets of destiny to which he had no lawful right. Then Marduk progressed through the heavens inspecting their places of various worlds, and there he established a new world, an exact replica of the Apsu, the dwelling of Ea."

Remember "like unto the other worlds we have hitherto created." Same thing here-following the heavenly pattern. As President Joseph F. Smith said, this earth in its natural state is exactly like the world that came from the hand of God, and it's a duplicate of his own. "The Lord Marduk took its measurements. [They are extremely important here.] He built the famous Midok. He built his own palace, the Eshar, the temple of Eridu, the first temple on earth, representing the earth itself at creation [notice the name of Eridu, eretz, earth; we have had that before] after its pattern. The palace of Eshar which he built was thus the heaven itself. [The temple is a scale model of the universe.] He caused Anu, Enlil, and Ea [the three degrees of glory] to come and occupy the temple for its dedication."

Tablet six: "Marduk resolved upon a wondrous work. [He would make the human race and bring them down.] He opened his mouth and addressed his father Ea and told himÃ‚Â…" See, it's Christ that made it possible for the human race to come here; otherwise they wouldn't have. We will see that in the next one I read. "I told him of what he had conceived in his heart. [Notice, it was Ptah who conceived in his heart always.] I wished to bring blood and bone forth and put them together, to organize them into a human being. His name shall be "man," amelu. Let it be his duty to serve the gods and satisfy them." In order that there be satisfaction, Marduk proposed a plan to the gods. "Let one of their race be put to death that humanity might be; one be made an offering for others. Let one of the assembled gods be delivered up as a guilty one that they might subsist." Well, this is the plan of salvation, of course, the Lord making the supreme sacrifice. It is being suggested at this time to make the plan workable on earth when men should come down. "Kingu opposed the plan. [Well, we know that from our Pearl of Great Price.] It was he who made Tiamat rebel and caused the war. It wasn't because he opposed the plan but because of the war and the violence he introduced that Kingu was defeated and cast down, and the great assembly gave all power of heaven and earth to Anu for carrying out the execution of the plan. Then he established with great care the ways of the earth, assigning to the gods of heaven and earth the laws that should apply here [Shabako Stone again]. A city was built like unto another Babylon. They labored on it for a year. A second year was devoted to building the temple which was the replica of the heavenly halls above. After first directing the great tower over the Apsu, they built the three-fold house of Marduk, Enlil, and Ea, the temple all in its three degrees of glory." Remember, Peter said, "Let us build three shrines here-one to thee, one to Elijah, and one to God. "The Anunnaki, the assembly of gods themselves, designed these holy shrines at Esagil, which is the temple of Babylon. This is to celebrate the founding of the temple of Babylon, Esagil, just exactly as the Shabako Stone was to celebrate the temple, the white house, at Memphis-the temple of Ptah.

"All came together in the great assembly at the place to dedicate it. There Marduk ordered all the fathers and all his gods to be seated and addressed them thus, 'This is Babylon, the place to which you shall come hereafter. Come together to rejoice at this place at the season of the great festival year after year.' [It sounds like Israel.] Thus the rites were installed, the laws were fixed, and the destinies decreed. There were seven gods that fixed the destinies of the the human race. May God rule over them forever as their shepherd. [Remember, the Egyptian Pharaoh out there has the shepherd's crook and the flail that the shepherd uses.] "Let him rule over them as their shepherd when they gather here under his protection. Let them rehearse here throughout the ages to come at this spot what God has done that they may never forget it." When we go to the temple, we rehearse the creation all over again. That's what you are supposed to do, for this is the earthly image of that which was done in heaven. "Let the blackheads [that's the human race, the Babylonians] remember their prayers to the gods, knowing that for us by whatever name we call him we have only one God. He is our God. Truly, we have given him fifty names. It is he who nourishes and sustains us, whose mighty arm protects us against the elements and our enemies. [It sounds like the prophets in the Psalms, doesn't it?] Upon mankind he has created, he has placed the obligation of serving him that they be satisfied. Great planner, full of mercy and loving kindness, may he forgive their sins and deliver them by his grace, for Marduk is God, the creator of all things. Verily, he sustains the land and his people. It is he whom the nations acclaim Lord of Gods of heaven and earth." It sounds like the ending of the "Hallelujah Chorus" here because it is. They end in a grand chorus.

"He is the Lord who by his pure utterance, his incantations [shiptu shu is the word; the Hebrew word is shepet (lip), safat saftun in Arabic], his pure utterance raises the gods from the dead." Back to the resurrection motif. You never get away from that. "He is the pure God who purifies our ways. Let us praise him. [They have taken their places in the assembly to declare his name in the holy place.] Let all together proclaim his name." And then they all join in the hallelujah chorus at the end.

So this is quite a show for the principal religious doctrine of the Mesopotamians. We think of corrupt Babylon, etc. Of course, it became corrupt. They all became corrupt. But this was their basic doctrine. It's built around this after it has been shuffled around and scrambled, these motifs. But you recognize they are all there, and they hold together. This makes a theme which again gave great solidity to the empire and was the center of the political structure and everything else.

Lecture 11

The Human Condition

Well, let's see what we have been trying to convey here. I just wrote down on the typewriter a few things here. So far we have considered this. Remember, there are many precious things, very valuable things, that have been removed from the scriptures. We say, "Well the Pearl of Great Price has restored them." We talked about a number of doctrines. First, what the original teaching was (which is well documented), how it was received, and how it was wiped out by conventional Judaism, Christianity, and the rejection of the world in general. That's well documented. And also its restoration, including the inclination of some people to return to it today. First we talked about literalism-the idea, are these things real? "No," they claim, "religion is spiritual; we're not dealing with literal things here." That was very important, the literal basis.

Then we go from there to the doctrine of the preexistence, such as in the story of The Pearl. Well, they used to believe that. They don't believe it now; it's condemned. But many are moving in that direction. And then the cosmological element. You've got to bring the real universe into it, the cosmology into it. This leads us to the story of creation. Was it creation out of nothing? Is it ex nihilo? We said, "What did they always teach about that?" The preexistence is the Council in Heaven. They go together, you see-preexistence and creation. We were talking about the Council in Heaven and we mentioned the Memphite Theology, the Shabako Stone (the oldest in the world). We also talked briefly about Enuma Elish. We could add many to them because they all follow more or less the same pattern. Pattern emerges here, and there are now endless articles on borrowings, influences and origin. Who started what? Why are these all so different? There are all sorts of cults throughout the world, and yet they all have common themes. So who is borrowing from whom? How did it originate, etc.? This is a waste of time. But finally they are beginning to find out that things do fall into patterns.

Recently, there was a long study by a student, who was in this class for three semesters, in the Journal of History of Religion of UCLA on this subject. He lifted the whole thing, which is the idea, of course. No, he did an awful lot of work. He's done a great job here. But it's about the mystery symbolism and classic recognition drama. Remember, we talked about the Clementine Recognitions. He doesn't mention that here, but the recognition drama, the old mysteries, the old method of teaching this stuff was in a dramatic presentation which centered at all the temples. That's where you start out with the Shabako Stone, etc. You think, "Well, there are countless combinations and plots etc." That's the joke. You put them all together, and you just get one. They all tell the same story. It's a rather complex one. And this is very interesting. He ends his thesis with a quotation from Martin P. Nilsson, who was a professor at Berkeley, a Swede who wrote a vast two-volume work on Greek religion. But he wrote it in the 1920s, and we know a lot today that we didn't know about then. So he could write this [p.2] then about Orphism, the Orphic rites and mysteries which emerged in the seventh and eighth centuries B.C. It's the concluding sentiment here. Notice that Nilsson thinks that Orphism first invented all this about the seventh or eighth century. "Orphism is the combination and crown of all the restless and manifold religious movements of the Archaic Period." Not the Archaic Period at all; it's the last period after the great collapse, 1000 B.C., when the climate shifted and all the ancient sacral kingdoms collapsed at once. It was a terrible time everywhere. In the introductory chapter of a book called An Approach to the Book of Mormon , we deal with the conditions in the time of Lehi when the old sacral kingship had passed away. The question arose, who is in charge around here? So you have the rise of the tyrants like Polycrates, Dionysius, etc. The tyrant is a very capable man who is ruling and who rules well, but he does not have a right to rule either by family descent or by popular choice. He hasn't been chosen, and he doesn't inherit his office. He just took it. Usually, those men were very capable; they had to be. You had the age of tyrants and revolution and everything else. Well, Orphism rose out of that.

"The development of the cosmogony [you are bringing in the cosmos here] in a speculative direction with the addition of anthropogony [the creation of man] which laid the principal emphasis on the explanation of the mixture of good and evil in human nature [the problem of good and evil; you notice this was all in the Shabako Stone-it didn't originate in the seventh or eighth century], the legalism in the ritualism of life, the mysticism of cult and doctrine, the development of the other life into concrete visibility, the transformation of the lower world into a place of punishment and adaptation rather than eternal hell, retribution [the idea of repentance], and the idea that life is not just a repetition of the present over and over again [you are not trapped in the cycle], the belief in the happier lot of the purified and the initiated [you have the rites and the ordinances, and they are supposed to provide you with a sort of happiness and can be found]. For all of these things, parallels, or at least suggestions, can be found in other quarters." These things have been floated around the world forever, and we will soon see why they were. "The greatness of Orphism lies in having combined all this into a system and in the incontestable originality which made the individual in his relationship to guilt and retribution the center of its teaching."

Well, for one thing, we still know very little about Orphism, less than anything else. But the point is these have already been combined into a system. That's what we saw already in the Enuma Elish and in the Memphite Theology. And "the incontestable originality [does that make it original?] which makes it a problem of individual guilt and retribution." It keeps telling us "to everyone who does evil, so and so." But these decisions are forced on us. They are not original. The ideas have been going around all the time. Of course, they have been going around. That's all people could think of. We have been unable to think of anything else all these years because the problem never ceases to face us all the time. The pressure is on all the time-the hopeless disproportion, the hopeless imbalance between our capacity and our performance. See, I know I could do a lot better than this.

I know I wouldn't fumble and stumble, mix my words up, and stray off in all directions. I could do better than that. Why don't I? What we are and what we could be are so different. John Eccles tells us that we don't use one ten thousandth of our brain power. We have it, but we never use it. Think of the capacity we have for enjoyment. I lie on my bed and fret when I look up and see all the books on the wall there. There's an Arabic book I would just love to read, but I don't have time. I knock my head off trying to learn to read this stuff, and when I get to it I can't use it because I am doing something else. All this stuff with Egyptian on it. I don't have any time for that stuff. Well, it's pretty sad isn't it that you can't begin to use it. There must be a reason for this. The solution must be found in other times and places because the time is too short here. We can't play a game of baseball in three minutes or in a closet. We don't have time enough in three minutes, and the closet doesn't give us space enough. That's the situation we are in here. We are time bound. We're in a time box. We can't move in any direction without bumping into something, but we don't have time for that. Of course, we don't have space for that either because we can't think of everything at once. In this life we are condemned to concentrate always on one thing at a time. The rest, if you see it at all, makes just sort of a background, side issues, etc. You are aware of it, of course, but you concentrate on only one object and you concentrate, as Eccles also says, on the object you choose to. That makes this life a time of probation. That shows what the desire of your heart is. With the possibility of thinking of a billion things, you prefer to think of one particular thing. You have judged yourself then. This is what I wanted among all these things.

So we are trapped in this time box with a limited horizon. We have tunnel vision only. I can just see this; I can't see all those other places at once, and that's just too bad. So we feel cheated, and quiet desperation is our lot. The luckiest man who ever lived was Oedipus. Think of the two great plays that have survived from Oedipus. The final chorus of the Oedipus Rex is (got to go into a trance now) "Oh, all you people of Thebes, glorious, maternal, eternal Thebes. Look, here is your hero, Oedipus, the one man who knew the celebrated riddle of the Sphinx, which had gone throughout all the world." Incidentally, they have more archaic stuff in the museum at Thebes than anywhere else. You go to Thebes and what do you find? There are sphinxes on half the vases. The sphinx did come from Thebes. When you go to Thebes in Egypt, which has exactly the same name, what do you find? Sphinxes. Interesting connection. We won't go into it now, but it is very archaic.

"There wasn't a single man anywhere, fellow citizens or anyone else, who didn't look at his fabulous luck with envy, zelo. [Our words "zeal, envy, jealousy" come from that.] And to this tragedy he has come." You couldn't imagine a man in a worse condition. He is going around begging; he has blinded himself. He can't go back. He is under a curse. Nobody can take him in under their roof. Here was the greatest man in the world, and look at him now. "Any mortal looking at this conditionÃ‚Â…" That is the ahariff of the Old Testament. Never judge a thing until the ahariff, the final solution, the last day. Then comes the moral: "Don't judge any man fortunate until he has finally gone aroundÃ‚Â…" This is always translated wrongly as "reached the terminal of his life."

The word is terma, but the terma is the turning place in the race where you are supposed to be wrecked. If you get around that safely, you are all right. In the Roman races there were the three meta. At Olympia that was the tomb of Pylox, and the angry ghost always demands a sacrifice. So they would have the horse races, and the chariot was supposed to smash up at the terrictipus where the horses were terrified by the ghost. Then the human sacrifice had been provided. One of the chariots was supposed to smash up there. So until he has gone around the terma. The terma is the same as the trophe. That's the place where you turn back, and you bring a trophy back from where you have been. The last place you reach is the place where you turned, and you bring the trophy back to show that you have been there. "Ã‚Â…without having suffered any such horrible sickness and everything bad."

Well, that's the moral. Don't call any man blessed because this can happen to the luckiest person who ever lived. But then comes the second play, the great play. (Well, really, one play is missing.) The final play is Oedipus at Colonus, which is the greatest play of Sophocles. There is still a possibility; there is still hope. Surprise, surprise. And it goes back into the ancient ordinances, etc. It takes us through the initiation, the temple, and all that sort of thing. That play has a moral too: "Put an end to all mourning now. Forget all that. This has all been a misundertanding. All things are in the hands of God. Everything has been provided for. There is a plan. Your business is to find out what it is. But instead of mourning about it, try to find out because there must be a meaning to this," as he says.

Well, why not examine the implications of this strange situation. We're on the stage and we need the scenario. Oedipus returned to Colonus, the place of his birth. He went to the sacred place and they wouldn't let him in because he is cursed. But then Theseus comes from Athens and gives him the ordinances. His daughters perform the washings and anointings. Theseus says, "I can't tell you what happens after this. You come up to the veil, but be happy about this. There is hope." So that's Oedipus, the one who limps. We limp through life.

Well, there are three major plots, or rather eight major plots or possibilities, that confront us-past, present and future. Three lives to consider; is there a past, present and future? This gives us eight possibilities of the I Ching. If you read Fritjof Capra's book on the subject, you know something about that. This is what the plots are, then. This is the past (preexistence), this is the present (this life), and then we have the future life. We will say they are either dark or light. I'll use D for dark and L for light. The past (preexistence) is dark; we know nothing about it. It's mysterious and it's lost. Plot number one is the commonest plot actually. There are eight possibilities, and there are philosophies based on each one (important world philosophies and religions). There's a little fun in this life, so it's light. But what comes after is darkness. The story of the conversion of King Oswy, the first English king to accept Christian missionaries, is the story of that. They are having the banquet in the hall. It's light and bright, and they are celebrating. Either end of the great hall is open to let the smoke out.

A swallow comes in and is completely dazzled and frightened by the light. It flies around awhile, makes itself at home, and then flies out again. King Oswy sees it and he says, "Well, that is our life. We come out of this great darkness. We see a little moment of light, and then we fly out again." He says to the Christian missionaries, "If you can give me anything better than that, I'll listen to you." So he does because that is what Christianity promises, something better hereafter, but nothing before. So this is the standard scenario. It's really an evasion. We accept this life because we have to. We can't deny it. But for the rest, we just deny them. That's what most people do.

Then there's the most famous student song in the world, "Gaudeamus Igitur." This is the philosophy. It was forbidden by the church, and everybody sang it. "Well, let's enjoy ourselves while we can, while we are still young. After joyful youth, after miserable old age, the earth is going to have us. That's all there will be to it." This is an extension really of the Fifth Ode of Catullus, the most romantic and lyrical of all the Roman poets. The famous Lesbia, his lady friend. "Let's live, Lesbia, and love. All the rumors, all the traditions, all the possibilities-let's just forget them. Let's esteem them as worth one penny, perhaps a penny; but forget all that. Why? Because the sun goes down and the sun comes up; but once our brief light has gone out, what happens after that? There's nothing but one long perpetual night of sleep." Then he goes on. "And what's the solution? Give me a thousand kisses; then give me a hundred more." We'll just go wild in love; we'll just make sex and forget about everything else. That's the idea. Well, that's an escape from it because there's nothing else. Remember, as Alma [the book] says in the time of Korihor, "When a man dies, that's the end thereof;" therefore, the people raised their heads and decided they could do anything they wanted to do. But these are very important schools of philosophy. These were the teachings of Epicurus and the Epicureans. The man who started atomic physics (and it was a good sound theory he had) was Democritus. They called him the "laughing philosopher" because it's just atoms that make things and nothing else, so there's nothing to worry about. So you just laugh it off and enjoy yourself. Well, as Pascal said, it was a hollow laugh. It was the hollow laugh of the libertine. If you are an Epicurean you just enjoy what you can get here and don't worry about anything else. That is the famous "Nil Admirare Ode" of Horace. "Don't get involved in anything; just relax. You come to me and you will find me just a comfortable pig from the sty of Epicurus. Don't let these things worry you. Don't let anything worry you." Nil Admirare is a slogan. We have our light time here. Shakespeare uses a lot of this:

Our revels now are ended. These our actors,

As I foretold you, were all spirits and

Are melted into air, into thin air;

And, like the baseless fabric of this vision,

The cloud-capped towers, the gorgeous palaces,

The solemn temples, the great globe itself,

Yea, all which it inherit, shall dissolve;

And, like this insubstantial pageant faded,

Leave not a rack behind. We are such stuff

As dreams are made on, and our little life

Is rounded with a sleep.

So he says it was pleasant while the show lasted, but it was only a show. It came out of nothing It was just imagination, and it will fade into nothing. So enjoy it while you can, and that's all there is. See, at base it's quite sad, isn't it? All the poems of A. E. Housman are on that subject:

With rue my heart is laden

For golden friends I had,

For many a rose-lipped maiden

And many a lightfoot lad.

By brooks too broad for leaping

The lightfoot boys are laid.

The rose-lipped maids are sleeping

In fields where roses fade.

As I say, it's sad. So with Omar you say, "Take the cash, and let the credit go."

"How sweet is mortal Sovranty!" think some:

Others-"How blest the Paradise to come!"

Ah, take the Cash in hand and waive the Rest;

Oh, the brave Music of a distant Drum!

The bad time is coming; the drum's ahead. You are doomed; forget it. Don't pay any attention to it. If you want to survive, you'd better, but anyway you don't. Some long for the pleasures of this life, and some for the paradise to come. But you take what you can get now and let the credit go. And what you do is get lost in wine.

Come, fill the Cup, and in the Fire of Spring

The Winter Garment of Repentance fling: [forget about repentance]

The Bird of Time has but a little way

To fly-and Lo! the Bird is on the Wing.

And then there's that gloomy one: "The Stars are setting, and the Caravan

Starts for the dawn of Nothing-Oh, make haste!"

This is the dark-light-dark philosophy, and it's a common one. It's the seven ages. It's the quiet desperation. You seem to be all right and getting along. Remember "Richard Cory" in the Spoon River Anthology. One fine summer's night he went home and put a bullet through his head. Just like Oedipus, everybody thought he was the most successful man in town. Everybody envied him, but Richard Cory, with no explanation, went home and put a bullet [p.7] through his head. As we are told in Walden, we all live lives of quiet desperation. We can get away with it by distractions. We can distract ourselves from it, and this is the furious concern today with sports, competition, success, drugs. It's all escape; it's all distraction because we are unwilling to face this horrible thing. It's too much to look at-to look at nothing. Remember Claudio saying, "The most loathed earthly life that age, ache, penury and imprisonment can lay on nature is a paradise to what we fear of death." So we try to anticipate it with crusades and competitions and wars. We try to anticipate a terrible end to use Hitler's expression, "an intolerable existence with a terrible end." We go on in this condition until we have an answer. We're not satisfied. We've got to win or lose; let's get it over with. We get a lot of that today, you know. And Tao and Zen-those religions anticipate annihilation by saying, "Don't expect anything; don't take anything seriously, and then you won't be disappointed." Our scenario for today is, of course, the car and the gun that sum everything up, and the explosion. (The mandatory explosion, I would say.) And the Book of Mormon is the tract for our times. It deals with that thing exactly.

Coming up the hill, I was thinking you might paraphrase the prologue to Romeo and Juliet. "Two families, both alike in dignity, In fair Verona, where we lay our scene." You could paraphrase it to two nations, both alike. This is the theme of the Book of Mormon and the present world to which the Book of Mormon is addressed. Two nations both alike in dignity, on this fair planet where we lay our scene "from ancient grudge break to new mutiny, where civil blood makes civil hands unclean. From forth the fatal loins of these two foes, a pair of star-cross'd lovers take their life; [the new generation, you see] whose misadventur'd piteous overthrows do with their death bury their parents' strife." They have to pay for the parents' grudges, the wars and armaments, and all that sort of thing. "The fearful passage of their death-mark'd love, and the continuance of their parents' rage, which but their children's end naught could remove." They would only settle their scores after everybody had been killed. That's what happened in Romeo and Juliet. The younger generation was wiped out, and the elders are still left to build monuments to them and utter platitudes in the last scene there.

Now, we have another philosophy which is dark (that's the cheerful one). Is it dark all the way? Well, of course it is. We all know Macbeth's famous speech: "Out, out, brief candle! Life's but a walking shadow, a poor player that struts and frets his hour upon the stage, and then is heard no more; it is a tale told by an idiot, full of sound and fury, signifying nothing." He came out of hell; he's in hell, and he's going back to hell. That's Macbeth as far as he can see it. But Hamlet too. "How weary, stale, flat, and unprofitable seem to me all the uses of this world! Fie on't! O fie! 'tis an unweeded garden that grows to seed; things rank and gross in nature possess it merely." So he asked himself, well, why shouldn't I bump myself off? Look what you have to put up with in this world: "The oppressor's wrong, the proud man's contumely, the pangs of disprized love, the law's delay, the insolence of office, and the spurns that patient merit of the unworthy takes." Everything we have to put up with here is a lot of nonsense. It's horrible, and there's no escape from it. Unless, he says, we could make an end to it. Who would "grunt and sweat under a weary life" when he might his own quietus make? With the dagger, just end it all right there. But he says, "No, what comes after may be even worse. There is the respect that makes calamity of so long life." We are willing to hang on because it might be even worse. Who can tell after this? The famous line, "The undiscovered country from whose bourn no traveler returns.... To die, to sleep; to sleep: perchance to dream; ay, there's the rub; for in that sleep of death what dreams may come, when we have shuffled off this mortal coil, must give us pause." They could be pretty terrible, and so we put up with it. But it's all the dread of something after death that makes us pause. It may be even worse after. So that's not very consoling. But it is all bad, bad, bad as far as he's concerned.

Well, is this part of a religion? Yes, it's Stoicism. These are the Stoics, and they are proud of it. It's a religion of defiance, "Invictus."

In the fell clutch of circumstance,

I have not winced nor cried aloud;

Under the bludgeonings of chance

My head is bloody, but unbowed.

That is the famous "Invictus." If you want to enjoy that sort of thing, you are welcome. It's the heroic or epic position. Remember in the religion of the sagas, the Ragnarok and the Gotterdammerung; they are winding down to sure destruction. The gods themselves can only look forward to the monsters of disarray and the second law of thermodynamics-running down and being destroyed, the Fenris Wolf. This is what you get in Homer. "What happened to all these heroes? They were caught like rats in a trap. There was no way of escaping." They had been there ten years. As the great epic begins, what's happening? There is dysentery in the camp, and everybody is dying off. What a romantic war! There are funeral flames burning all over the camp all day long. The stink is terrible, etc. He says, "These heroes became a feast for the birds and bait for the dogs." So this is your heroic position, isn't it? The consolation that Achilles gives, the only consolation to a hero whom he is about to wipe out is: "But friend, I'm afraid you've got to be killed too. [That is a very real philosophy for the military.] But don't worry about it. What are you complaining about? Patroclus died, and he was a much better man than you, so what are you complaining about?" This was the comfort you would take in this sort of thing. So it's your heroic religion. And we all know about "Dover Beach." It's a great favorite with English departments, as you know.

Ah, love, let us be true

To one another! for the world, which seems

To lie before us like a land of dreams,

So various, so beautiful, so new,

Hath really neither joy, nor love, nor light,

Nor certitude, nor peace, nor help for pain;

And we are here as on

Swept with confused alarms of struggle and flight,

Where ignorant armies clash by night.

That's the world we live in, he says. They thought it was such a wonderful world, "the world which seems to lie before us like a land of dreams." Well, that's what the gospel offers us. This is the world that came from the hand of God. It's a great place. If it has no certitude, nor peace, nor help for pain, who's to blame for that? So that's not a very cheerful doctrine is it? But it was held by a large part of the human race who didn't want to give in to this. The Stoics felt it was unworthy of their pride to just play around, get drunk and enjoy yourself as much as you could.

Then we have the light-dark-light here. This is the most important of all religiously because this is the one that's behind all the cults, all the dramas. This is behind Pythagoras. This is the Hermetic doctrine. That means the doctrine that came down from Thoth, Hermes and the Egyptians in the beginning of the world. This has been handed down unbroken, and it is the Hermetic tradition, from Hermes. It is shared by Pythagoras (most important of all) and Plato. The Gnostics or Neo-Platonists wouldn't take it literally. They said, "Well, it's all just spiritual anyway." Then the apocalyptic, the mystic. The Pearl belongs to this. He comes out of the light. He's in this dark and corrupt world. Then he goes back to the light again. That's what you have in the Book of Moses. He's with the Father in the beginning. Then he comes down to be tempted. Then he goes back again. In the Book of Abraham he gives us an account of the Council in Heaven. "Abraham, thou wert one of them." Many great and noble ones and he was one of them. Then he comes down here and suffers the ten trials of Abraham. He lives his life in tribulation and doesn't even have a grave where he can be buried. He has to rent it from the Hittites. As the Lord told his Apostles, "In this world ye shall have tribulation. But be of good cheer; I have overcome the world (John 16:33 )." If these others are reliable, then don't worry about this one too much. There's a hymn in the Thanksgiving Hymns of the Dead Sea Scrolls that says if you knew what you were before or could see what you are to be hereafter, then you wouldn't worry about anything. Nothing could discourage you at all. In the Battle Scroll Milhama, it tells us, "When Israel is defeated in battle, don't be discouraged. Just remember what was before and what comes after. This won't last forever." So this is the one that carries on as we find in The Pearl and in the New Testament. We shall find tribulation. But why the tribulation here?

Well, let's have another one then. Now we get standard Christianity and Judaism. Dark-they don't believe in a preexistence. And dark, and light. You have eight possible combinations you will see here. These are the first four; these are the most important. Well, this is standard Christianity and Judaism. Dark-dark-light. The defective scenario, it is; it lacks in protology. That makes it a very unbalanced sort of scenario. The problem of evil becomes a mystery if you are suddenly out of nothing and you come into something. St. Augustine is your best guide here. That's what made him the kingpin of Christian theology of all sects. He gives us such things as, "We come into the world predestined." This became the thing with the Calvinists. When you come here, God has already decided whether you will be damned or blessed. You are predestined either for salvation or damnation. There is nothing you can do about it. God has taken care of that. That's because they left this particular scene out. I say it's defective. The problem of evil is insoluble. They never could solve it. That's why so many of them turned Gnostics. The Manichaeans belong in here. Augustine for the nine best years of his life was a Manichaean because they accepted this. He wouldn't accept this later on because the people at the school in Rome before he joined the church laughed at such things. A preexistence or postexistence was not philosophically or scientifically sound, so he wouldn't accept it. Augustine said this world is nothing but a festering mass of corruption; that's all it is. The Gnostics believed that very firmly. Well, what could you do about that? There's nothing you could do about that unless God has done it already for you, no plan or anything like that. He said, "You have made some of us to turn to you, and our hearts are restless until we return to you." That's a good doctrine too, as far as that goes. This takes up the Middle Ages. Dante is the same thing where Beatrice says that she can't be damned because she wasn't made that way. "Your suffering can't touch me; the flames of this hell can't touch me at all because God made me that way." That's all there is to it. You start right out of nothing. It's ex nihilo. It's a dark world for a lot of people, but you can't really call it testing because the test is already decided. It's St. Augustine's own word; it's predestined. What is the purpose of coming here? There are four questions that St. Augustine asks, and his biographers say he never succeeded in answering them. The problem of evil and the problem of damnation of infants, for example. He said, "Why should infants be damned just because they haven't heard the gospel and die before they have a chance to be baptized?" That doesn't seem fair to him at all, but he has a solution for it, a rhetorical solution. Remember, he is a great rhetorician. He says it is a light damnation. It reminds me of Bottom in A Midsummer Night's Dream. "I will roar as gently as any sucking dove." He'll be a roaring lion, but he won't frighten the ladies. He'll roar gently and softly. So St. Augustine actually says, "Yes, there will be a damnation, a real damnation, an eternal damnation, a hell damnation; but it will be a light and easy damnation." You figure that one out. As you see, they got themselves into these logical binds.

Now, what other things remain here? We have dark-light-light. That's what King Oswy wanted. Once the bird flies into the bright place, he doesn't fly out into the darkness. He flies out into the light. That's why he was willing to accept it. This is what the monastic movement intended to do. We have the writings of St. Antony who founded the movement; Antony and Pachomius who started the movement in the fourth century. The corruption of the world was so great. He lived in Alexandria, and the Christian world was so utterly vile. The only way to escape was to go to the desert and live by yourself, and then you could form communities. But it was to live the life of angels. So that's making this world a happy place. It's true you come out of the darkness, but by rejecting this world entirely, by contempt for this world (it's a Stoic sort of thing), you won't suffer from it. You get and live by yourself and spend all your time contemplating God. So you live in the light here looking forward to the light hereafter. This life of angels is a hard one, and they had a hard time making it work.

There are quite a few catches in it. We have more literature on that than anything else. At least half the Coptic literature is the trouble these people had living in the desert. The most interesting chapter in Gibbon is written about the monastic movement-how they started out. See, the world went so bad after it became completely Christian in the fourth century that they said, "This isn't the way the apostles lived. This isn't the way the old church lived. The only way I can do is to go out and live by myself." People do that around here all the time. There are all sorts of splinter groups that think they can go out and live united. But it doesn't work very well; it's sad. We have some marvelous accounts of this; the psychological troubles these people had. They immediately started having hallucinations, but they weren't happy hallucinations. One of the pictures you see, from the Flemish artists or anyone else, is St. Antony being tempted by the devils. He is surrounded by all sorts of fiends and evil spirits. He's out in the desert alone, and all he does is get temptation. These awful thoughts come to him all the time. He doesn't escape a thing. So you see the picture of St. Antony in his torments. That's no fun.

Then there is light-dark-dark. Do we have that? No we don't, but we might as well put that on here. That's an absurdity, of course. If you believe that you lived in a glorious life before you came here but this is a bad one and the next a bad one, that doesn't make sense at all. You know "Intimations of Immortality," the famous poem of Wordsworth, don't you?

Our birth is but a sleep and a forgetting:

The Soul that rises with us, our life's Star,

Hath had elsewhere its setting,

And cometh from afar:

Not in entire forgetfulness,

And not in utter nakedness,

But trailing clouds of glory do we come

From God, who is our home:

Heaven lies about us in our infancy!

Shades of the prison-house begin to close

Upon the growing Boy,

But he beholds the light, and whence it flows,

He sees it in his joy;

The Youth, who daily farther from the east

Must travel, still is Nature's priest,

And by the vision splendid

Is on his way attended;

At length the Man perceives it die away,

And fade into the light of common day.

Notice you have the four ages of man here. First the child with heaven lying about him. Then with the boy the light begins to fade already. The prison walls begin to close, but still he sees the light. Then the youth is going ever farther and farther away from the East, but he still has some light to go with him. "At length the Man perceives it die away and fade into the light of common day." And where do we go after that? That's the interesting thing. We don't go anywhere; that's the point. Wordsworth isn't really talking about the preexistence. The English Department has always been very insistent about that. That's true. He doesn't take it literally. He was a Romantic poet, and the Romantic poets were trying to escape from the evil world. They didn't like the Industrial Revolution. As Wordsworth himself said:

The world is too much with us; late and soon,

Getting and spending, we lay waste our powers:

Little we see in Nature that is ours;

We have given our hearts away, a sordid boon!

This Sea that bares her bosom to the moon;

The winds that will be howling at all hours,

And are up-gathered now like sleeping flowers;

For this, for everything, we are out of tune;

It moves us not.-Great God! I'd rather be

A Pagan suckled in a creed outworn;

Now, what is his alternative to this? "I'd rather be a pagan suckled in a creed outworn." That's the Romantic movement; go back to paganism. Well no, the Romantic period of a Greece that never really existed. It's like our own Currier and Ives, Rockwell American society that never was. I should remember; I was there. But they go back to that. Schiller in the Die GÃƒÂ¶tter Griechenlands, "Come back again, thou beautiful land-a glorious, gentle lovely age of nature." The Greeks lived in nature, and the Romantic poets try to do that. But Romanticism is escape. They don't accept it on a religious basis at all, and neither does Wordsworth here. So how far does he agree with these four ages of man?

What does the seven ages of man belong to? I think that belongs to this. Shakespeare makes mock of all seven, doesn't he? And he follows Solon who was probably a friend of Lehi. He had to leave Athens in 600 B.C. after giving them the first democratic constitution. He wrote a poem on the seven ages of man, and it's all downhill. The last scene of this eventful history is "second childishness and near oblivion, sans eyes, sans teeth, sans taste, sans everything." That's the end of the story. Well, it's not very happy.

You notice these are the great religious ones. Another one here is light-light-dark. But again, if you believed in the preexistence and this was a happy life, that's an absurdity. I don't think there's any religion that preaches that. Well, I don't know. Some of the Indians might preach that you came from a happy home. They enjoyed their life with the tribe, but now they have been utterly wiped out. Their chances have gone, but that was forced on them by circumstances here. But you wouldn't build a religion around that. That was the sun dance religion. They were trying to restore it, trying to turn the tide around 1906 when the Sioux Indians revived the sun dance. It electrified all the tribes everywhere.

Then we find the most optimistic of all which is light-light-light. We get that in the Book of Moses. It's the gospel, of course. You get it in Fourth Nephi, where they live after the manner of happiness. Nephi says, "We did live after the manner of happiness." Well, that's happy all the time, and you're supposed to be that. In our Pearl of Great Price is the best expression of it, in the Book of Moses 5:9-12 . Is it possible to have them all? Yes, they say so, in spite of their sorrows. He thought it was bad, and it turned out that it wasn't. "As thou hast fallen thou mayest be redeemed, and all mankind, even as many as will. And in that day Adam blessed God and was filled and began to prophesy concerning all the families of the earth, saying: Blessed be the name of God, for because of my transgression my eyes are opened, and in this life I shall have joy, and again in the flesh I shall see God. And Eve, his wife, heard all these things and was glad, saying: Were it not for our transgression we never should have had seed and never should have known good and evil, and the joy of our redemption, and the eternal life which God giveth unto all the obedient. And Adam and Eve blessed the name of God, and they made all things known unto their sons and their daughters." It turned out that they were to learn while they were still here that they were perfectly free to enjoy themselves. They had been happy before they came here; they enjoyed themselves, and they have nothing but happiness to look forward to-as many as will, if you are willing to be obedient. So we have the Gospel which is all light, the other extreme from this.

Lecture 12

The Plurality of Worlds

Let's pass these around. This is a talk Professor Baer is giving tomorrow night on who really robbed the Valley of the Kings. He has a very good sense of humor. You'll love Professor Baer.

Next time we reach the halfway point, and then we shift over to the text. But this introduction, on which we have hardly scratched the surface, is indispensable. I think you will find it so. The subject for today, for example. From now on almost everything I mention you will be able to find on reserve. You can get a lot of the stuff down at F.A.R.M.S. on the corner of University and Eighth North, on the top floor of the old Amanda Knight Hall. They have all this stuff copied for two and one-half cents a page.

Remember, we have been talking about literalism, cosmism, preexistence, and the Council in Heaven. All require the existence of other worlds. Of all the propositions that ever came forth in discussion of gods, the cosmos, etc, this has been the most obvious one, and the most inviting. It has also been the most hotly denounced and the most hotly resisted, especially in the seventeenth century. It was the big fight of the seventeenth century. Is there a plurality of worlds? If we go down the list here, we'll find the arguments for and against and the people for and against it. They are all very famous. They are all central to our whole civilization here. Nothing is more clearly set forth in the Pearl of Great Price than this teaching. I have all the passages listed here from Moses and Abraham. Let's read them after we've seen what the opinions were on these things. I'm going to keep score here. Who is for and against this plurality of worlds? (I've got to learn how to write better; I used to write carefully and very well.) In the ancient world from the earliest times in the old records, like the Egyptians, the absolute rule of the one is the idea. You have the one king, the one god on his throne. There cannot be two swords in a scabbard. There cannot be two suns in a sky. There could only be one ruler, an ideal king who sits on the throne. So there can only be one world, one heaven or anything else. But what gives him his glory is the hosts that surround him. He's in the midst of great variety and great numbers of the acclaiming hosts. What about the other worlds going around? The two go together. As soon as you require the god on the throne to have glory, he has to have something to rule over. This was a very definite concern. How can God have glory if there is nobody to acclaim him? Many thought that's why he created the world so that he would have somebody to cheer him and make him feel that he had done something wonderful. A foremost French theologian, Etienne Gilson, who recently died, wrote a book the title of which is God, and he said, "God is the self-thinking thinker who thinks only thought." He thinks only of himself because nothing else is worthy of his thought except himself, of course. He can't lower himself. Well, that's his ultimate definition for God. But he has to have more glory than that, so already you have the two sides here, the two ideals.

Even in the Pyramid Texts, the one becomes three.

Xenophanes, a philosopher of the sixth century B.C. and one of the Milesian School, said, "It is quite reasonable to suppose that there are boundless suns and moons and all of the same substance as this earth." So they were preaching that in the sixth century B.C. He was part of the school of the physical world teaching philosophy on the basis of physics which began in his generation in Miletus. And along with this goes the feeling of pothos. Pothos is an interesting word. Thucydides uses the term effectively. Pothos is the yearning for the beyond, the feeling that there is more there and the desire to get out there. It's funny, when the Russians gave up the idea of heaven, they became obsessed with this idea of pothos-of going out into outer space, the hope for realities beyond. It's natural and inevitably looks to dreams of other worlds. There are various articles and books on these subjects.

There are two well-defined approaches and traditions to the plurality of worlds. First of all we should mention that Xenophanes is for it (fifth-sixth century). Aristotle (Aristotelian doctrine) is against it (fourth century B.C.) What was his argument for that? Well, Professor Jaeger in his celebrated work on Aristotle says, "It is very important for Aristotle to prove that only one world is possible. [Of course, the Christian world is going to follow him.] Just as the mind may be focused on only one thing at a time, only one object is most worthy of our total contemplation, and that is God." You can't be distracted by anything else. There can only be one, and this applies to everything. Quoting a recent study by Munitz, "Aristotle's one-world theory with its foundation in the gross facts of familiar experience [After all, you can see there is only one world, and you can see the earth is the center of everything. Everything is drawn to the earth; everything falls to the earth; things that are heavy go down. So there can be no other. This is the center; man is the center. By your senses you can see the stars and planets revolving around the earth, around the place you stand on. This is the gross, obvious, observational evidence of the senses.] made reference to worlds beyond our own both unwarranted and unnecessary." We have enough here. We don't need any other worlds. They are not necessary, and it is unwarranted. This is obviously the center. You don't need to go any further and argue about it.

"Against this you have the intuitive genius of Democritus," says Munitz. Remember Democritus-the laughing philosopher, the inventor of atoms, etc. He is fifth century. Democritus is for it, and Aristotle is against it. We'll have to leave a place in the middle because with Plato you can go either way. "Against the intuitive genius of Democritus, there was no observational evidence whatever that there might be more than one world. [You can't observe it that way.] The observational range of astronomy at that time had no need for other worlds," he says. "Earlier, Plato's reason for looking upon the cosmos as unique, as only one cosmos, is essentially theological. There's only one perfection. That's it, and you can't have others." So Plato on one side was opposed to it [Aristotle's teacher was Plato], but on the other hand, his doctrine leads to the insistence on having more than one world. It was his idea of perfection that led to later Christian and Jewish involvement in pleniarism through possible infinity of worlds. If God made the best, he did the best of possible jobs. Why would he stop at one? There should be more. We demand more of a good thing. You can't get too much of a good thing, so why limit it to just one. It's to Plato they appealed both against and for it. Professor Shorey made Plato a high church conservative, and Professor Gifford made him a low church liberal. That was at Chicago. Professor Shorey wrote a book on what Plato meant, the absolute opposite of what Gifford and the others said about him.

So these are the two well defined traditions, the issue stated in the now familiar Clementine Recognitions. We are always running into that. Peter is arguing with Simon Magus who claims to be very scientific. Peter is on the side of reality, and Simon Magus is against it. This is early second century writing. Simon says that the heaven can only be seen in the mind and, therefore, cannot be corporeal. You can't have other worlds. Heaven is only something you see in the mind. Peter in reply says that he too sees only in his mind when he thinks of an island which he has never yet visited. But he says the island is real. It is there just the same. "I had a definite idea what Caesarea would be like before I ever came here," he says. "Though the real Caesarea when I got here was quite different. But it was real." Then Simon challenges him, "What do you think is beyond the heavens?" Peter replies, "I believe in a great source of light that is real and not a mere fantasy, nor the product of sophistic assertions, but it is testified by law and by nature." So Peter is being the scientific, down-to-earth, common sense one. He says, "Of course, there are other worlds, and they are going to be real-things beyond the sun." Simon Magus says, "No, this must all be spiritual." And Simon's viewpoint was the winner as far as the church was concerned after.

The Jewish theory is the same way. We have the Zohar. What does that say? "God creates worlds and destroys worlds, and you too will be able to create worlds and to destroy worlds." This was a doctrine common to both the Jews and the Mandaeans. We find it in Bin Gorion. So the Zohar is definitely for it. Not only that God creates and destroys worlds (as in the Pearl of Great Price), but you too can become a creator of worlds if you follow a certain line. This is an interesting theory. "Other worlds have disappeared," says the Zohar, "because God did not dwell in their midst regularly and constantly or [as the text reads] because God had not come down to them." They must be visited by God, so they just whithered and perished in time. There were unworthy worlds that passed away. That's an interesting thing because they do talk about unworthy races and pre-human races that weren't able to make the grade. Of course, we know that civilizations do pass away. Well, why shouldn't it happen with worlds too, if God hadn't visited them or if they rejected him? So the Jews worked on that.

There's a very famous work by the brethren Ahwan of Basra, edited by Dieterici. I have one of the few copies in existence of the text of this, and it's great. Back in the ninth century at Basra, where all the oil bombing is going on now, there was a society of the holy brethren. They were scientifically advanced. They knew all about microbes and molecules and everything else. They had a marvelous encyclopedia, and in it they say, "The business of the angels is to [p.4] coordinate the operations between the worlds, to keep them all functioning according to the same plan. There are many worlds. They are all coordinated, and they all work together in one big scheme." This is a very bold concept which, of course, we find in the Pearl of Great Price. So we will put the brethren of Basra here. It was a great school, a very important center at an early time. First Christian, then Moslem.

This was the doctrine of the Nazarenes, we read in the Migne collection here. "As the one-world school clusters around Aristotle, so the plurality of worlds developed with impressive logic and imagination by the Christian writers who pretend to teach the old Christian gospel of the Gnostics." The Gnostics picked it up because it was very popular, of course. So we will put the Gnostics over here. They knew they couldn't give it up. It was a teaching of the old church, yet they weren't inclined to it because they didn't believe in anything physical hereafter; no physical resurrection, no physical creation. God was not worthy of that. So some of the sects keep it, and there are many of them, because the Christian tradition demanded it. This is part of the teaching that went back to the early church as attested by a strict contradiction to their favorite doctrines.

Justin Martyr, the first Christian martyr, insists on it, and he's very important. Remember, he was the first philosopher to become a Christian. He was martyred for that. He never laid off his philosopher's robe, and he wrote apologies telling his philosophy friends in the school why he joined the church, justifying himself. But he insists that the Christians are not interested in mere abstractions. "They demand reality," he says. He promises the perspective of the Christian as Dame Virtue promised Heracles at the crossroads. He says here, "If you follow me, I can promise you unlimited worlds. The worlds go on without end." So we will put Justin Martyr down here. He's a very important one, very early too. As I say, the first Christian martyr, after Stephen. Remember, the first philosopher to join the church. Later, it was only the heretics that still clung to that old belief. Philastrius is one who collects a lot of arguments for and against it. We have him in the twelfth volume of the Patrologia. He's in the fourth century. As we have seen before, the philosophers of the fourth century rejected it. They didn't like any of this physical stuff at all. He says, "There are heretics who still cling to the old belief [you notice it is the old belief] that worlds are infinite and innumerable according to the silly opinions of some philosophers" to which he opposes Gen. 1:1, "In the beginning God created the heavens and the earth." This was usually the answer. He says, "That says the world is one and from one source, and you can't get around it. There can only be one." And this is followed closely by Methodius. Referring back to the cosmology of the Egyptians and Chaldeans, he argues that if the sun and the moon and other stars are divine and greater than men, then it is necessary that they also contain greater life. There must be worlds greater than our world. He says, "They would have greater peace, greater justice, greater virtue. Is that reasonable?" Well, why not. It follows all right, but they found it offensive. But he sort of remains on the fence, and a lot of them do. This is interesting in the early period. There's a good footnote in the Patrologia on that. Those who have the glory of God at heart [p.5] generally like this. It put Him at the head of the greatest possible show. They wanted God to have the biggest possible universe.

And our old friend Origen; where is he? He's stuck right in the middle and can't make up his mind. Remember, he knew what the old church taught and he was the best of theologians, but he also grew up on the campus of the University of Alexandria. He had to accept both. He tells us some interesting things here in his famous work on the first principles of the gospel. "Yet there may be other worlds, but what their nature and number may be I confess I do not know. If anyone could show me, I would gladly learn." That was the position of the church at the beginning of the third century. He was born in 185. "I don't know," he says. "There may be and I wish there was, but can you tell me about it?" Notice, there's no authority; the tradition is lost. The apostasy has taken place. They don't have anything. And, of course, St. Jerome. He said that Origen believed that the number of worlds was infinite, but he avoided being a follower of Epicurus by having them exist not all at once but in succession, one after another. So there would be lots of worlds, but just one at a time. Well, that explains a lot. Remember, the Pearl of Great Price says, "As one world passes away another comes into existence." But Origen is still carrying water on both shoulders here. Epicurus is one of the classic exponents of it. We mentioned him saying you should enjoy yourself, but the idea that there should be other worlds goes along with atomism and Democritus. Putting it on a physical basis, there is no reason why it shouldn't be repeated. Philastrius notes that in his day there was a wide disagreement in the church on the subject. Half the teachers took one side and half took the other.

Michael Scotus, the famous philosopher of the Middle Ages, got around this by supposing that God could create a plurality of worlds but that the nature of the world he did create made the existence of other worlds impossible. He said, "God could have done it. You can't deny him that."

"While some Christian tastes chided Aristotle for limiting God's control to a sphere of light within the circle of the moon [that makes it a very small show] preferring the broader scope of the schools in Babylonia, India and Egypt who taught that there were other worlds and whose teachings would be hard to refuteÃ‚Â…" That's a very interesting writing by a Palestinian writer in the Oriental Patrologia, also from the fourth century. The Aristotelian concept taken over by the doctors of the Christians and the Jews in the Middle Ages is akin to the doctrine of universal monarchy. All outside the monarchy are in the outer darkness and have no place at all. There can only be one kingdom. They went to work on that. The argument of perfection though works either way. "The Arab, the Jewish, and the Christian philosophers of the Middle Ages taught that the harmony of all nature requires that there be only one perfect universe." This was taken as the proof of only one God. So the strong arguments are against it. All of medieval philosophy now (Jewish, Christian and Arabic; the Arabs were leading at that time) says you have to have a perfect universe. The Book of the Mysteries of Heaven and Earth has Peter saying to the Lord, "' Nothing was created before heaven and earth,' and in spite of Matt. 25:34 nothing existed before heaven and earth except the three names of the Father, [p.6] Son and Holy Ghost. The Christian Hermius of the fourth century contrasts the Christian belief in one cosmos with Epicurus' idea of many and infinite worlds which he says would require many heavens, many ethers etc. and that would be absurd."

Augustine, of course, is our big one. Like Origen, he is on the fence. He's the most important, but he decides against it in the end. Early, he was a Manichaean and was very strongly for it. I was reading in The City of God just last hour. He says, "There is no time without mobility." That's a good point. Something has to move in order to measure time. "There's no mobility without creatures. There was no time after which God began to create the earth." He didn't begin at any time. Then he says, "It has to be understood that nothing at all existed before he created this world; therefore, there can be no other worlds. Since there was no time, from that we conclude that before this world was created nothing at all existed." Very interesting thing he says here, "The world was not created in time but with time. Time and the world were created together." The Pearl of Great Price says that Adam went into a new time system when he left the garden. Before then he was on another time system entirely, but it's still a time system. Well, there was nothing before this world because there was nothing to make time in this argument. This conclusion was after much wrangling and soul searching. They considered this a very important issue.

John Chrysostom, a very important writer of the fourth and fifth centuries, rejects it. We have many volumes of his writings, and he was the greatest orator of his time-a tremendous influence. He says, "The beauty and utility of the heavens are all exclusively for the benefit of man." This is the point of view of that time. Talk about anthropocentric. He rejected the possibility of many heavens. He says, "The stars were created first and foremost for the benefit of sailors. That's why He made stars, so there's no reason to go on creating them for anybody else because there is nobody else there." That was the argument, but it got feeble as it went along.

Then we get the Middle Ages (twelfth century) and Thomas Aquinas. He explains why Plato's idea of perfection makes it impossible that there should be more than one world. He says, "Duplicated worlds would be futile and foolish. Why make more?" So Aquinas is against this and he reads Plato that way. "Even though their totality might be perfect, still there would be imperfect worlds." That's his commentary on Aristotle on the Heavens, DeCaelo, lecture 19, D&C 14, etc. So in the Middle Ages they settled it this way.

Now in the Renaissance and Reformation this doctrine emerged again, and did they have a fight about it. This was the big thing, the main issue in the Renaissance. The Renaissance was the expansive period; they discovered this world and other worlds. When it emerged in the Renaissance and Reformation, it called for counter measures. It was the greatest crime of Giordano Bruno who was its great defender. He was burned at the stake in Rome for preaching this doctrine. He was a great genius in many ways. This was in 1600, right at the turn of the century. When they go into the seventeenth century, then it becomes more scientific. But it becomes the main argument of the seventeenth century. Whether it's Pascal or Descartes or the rest, they talk about that more than anything else. Bruno was teaching the "decentralized, infinite, and infinitely populous universe," and it cost him his life. He went everywhere, but they wouldn't accept him. They threw him out of Geneva. He went to Paris; he went to London. He was one of the great geniuses of his time. But this teaching that there is more than one world is what "cooked his goose." It rested not on the astronomy of Copernicus, as Lovejoy tells us, or Gallileo, but on the principle of plenitude. For a thing to be perfect, there must be the most possible of it. So there must be plentiful worlds.

Arthur Lovejoy's book The Great Chain of Being is very good on this subject. He takes it up here in the Renaissance and the Reformation.
