The other one is Alexander Koyre's book called From a Closed World to an Infinite Universe. It's a new one, and it treats the subject again very well. You can get these at the Bookstore quite cheaply. It begins with Nicholas of Cusanus. We'd better have him down here because he is very strong for it. He comes before Bruno. It sounds Italian, but he isn't. He's German. That's the town of Cusa in Germany. He's way back in the fifteenth century, 1401-1464. He became Cardinal Nicholas and traveled everywhere. He had great influence with the popes. He wrote a famous work called De Docta Ignorantia (On the Learned Ignorance). He was the most learned man of his time, and he said, "Learning consists entirely of knowing that you don't know." He takes as a famous example the squaring of the circle. That's following Socrates. You put a square around a circle and then you cut the corners off the square. Then you keep cutting those corners off. That can go on forever. You'll never get a square. He says that's the way with knowledge. You get nearer and nearer, but you never get it. And he picked up the saying from the Mandaeans, I think (which they applied to God), of the universe whose center is everywhere and circumference is nowhere. He says, "That's not God; that's the universe that we are describing." It certainly is Einstein's universe. The center is everywhere; you know, the exploding universe, the balloon and all that. Nicholas Cusanus said that the inhabitants of this world are inferior to inhabitants of other stars. That's one way to get into trouble. "They remain wholly unknown to us," he says.

Now I'm quoting Lovejoy here, "By the early sixteenth century, the theory of plurality of solar systems and of inhabited planets, of the infinite number of stars and the infinite extent of the universe in space were already common topics of discussion. [Was there an infinity of worlds and a plurality of solar systems?] What most attracted the seventeenth century was the idea of habitable celestial bodies with creatures more or less like ourselves." This was the big appeal. Lovejoy says, "This is a truly revolutionary thesis in cosmogony which was generally accepted before the end of the seventeenth century." Well, if they accepted it by the end of the seventeenth century, whatever happened to it? It comes in as quite a novelty in the Pearl of Great Price. In the middle of the century Burrell published his new discourse proving the plurality of worlds in which he called attention to the mountains and rivers on the moon and recalled that Pythagoras called the earth a moon. Pythagoras was the great one that was for it. He learned it in Egypt, of course. They learned everything in Egypt. In this century we have Campanella's famous work known as The City of the Sun in which he describes the sun as a populated planet. It was taken very seriously. Lots of people got all excited about it. He paid for it though. He died in 1639 after spending 27 years in prison for teaching it. Then finally Gregory VIII let him out again. But you had to pay through the nose if you taught these things in some parts of the world then. The Protestants were as strong against it as the Catholics. It was a big fight. He believed the sun inhabited by beings far superior in learning to ourselves.

Now Descartes, the father of modern thought, what position would he take? He was against it; that's a strange thing. The great Rene Descartes, who introduced so much of modern thinking, belongs over here. He died right in the middle of the century, 1650. He says, "The question of many worlds is silly and irrelevant since this world is infinite and we must never dispute about the infinite. There's nothing you can say about it." Well, I guess he's on the fence, isn't he. "This matter of the sky and the earth is one and the same and there cannot be a plurality of worlds since it is all a complete self-centered whole." If it's just one perfect whole, you don't divide it up. Well, it is a whole, but can't you have other worlds? This is the argument. But he was engaged with that idea. "Even if the worlds were infinite," he says, "it is impossible that they should not be constituted from one and the same matter."

Well, we believe that. Of course, we have always taught that. "Like unto other worlds"; exactly as has been done in other worlds. We keep repeating that formula over and over again. "As has been done in other worlds." This is not new. There's nothing new about it as far as substance and structure is concerned, but the combinations are different. There's no end of difference between the worlds. He says, "Even if the worlds were infinite, it is impossible they would not be constituted from one and the same matter; therefore, they cannot be many but only one" because they are the same matter. Well, in that sense we agree with him. His argument is that no matter how many there might be, all must belong to one and the same system, so after all it is only one cosmos. The one who treats him is Koyre. He goes into quite a bit of detail on him.

Blaise Pascal, the great inspired genius, came right after him. He died quite young. He invented adding machines and things like that, a mathematical genius. But he couldn't accept it either. Why not, a great "noodle" like his? He couldn't accept life on other worlds because of his attachment to the Jewish and Christian tradition. "He was very pious which long since disposed of his earlier competitors who viewed as absurd the assumption of the existence throughout infinite space of countless other races of rational and presumably sinful beings." He said, "The Christian and Jewish tradition won't allow it. It's absurd. You don't need to go on endlessly repeating things. There's no point to that." Henry More, who was a follower of his, follows the familiar orthodox medieval premise, "The center of each of several worlds is a sun with its planets, and these altogether one world, I concede, and under the inexhaustible God, and God is height whose simple goodness makes the highest diety." Well, he's going into ecstasies about the fact that there is an infinite number of worlds. Well, Henry More says that, the orthodox medieval premise. "Each several world has its suns and its planets. If Descartes rejects the infinity of inhabited worlds, he nonetheless refuses to suppose that the power of the Creator is so imperfect that no such stars could exist." He thinks it's absurd, but he says you're not going to limit the power of God; so God could make more if he wanted to. Descartes is sitting on the fence with that argument. "He could make them if he wanted to," he says.

Likewise with Leibniz, the great German philosopher of the time. Let's see which side he's on first. You'd expect him to be very much on it. Well, he is against it because he believed in the best of possible worlds (if you've read your Candide). He died about 1717, so we're now in the eighteenth century. He says, "No, because this is the best of all perfect worlds." Of course, Voltaire has no end of fun spoofing that. If you call this the best of all perfect worlds, what have you got? That's the whole theme of Candide. But he argues like a German philosopher. He's called Pangloss, the typical German philosopher. As Voltaire says, "He's a little philologian like all Germans."

Well, Leibniz says, "An infinite, immutable, and sempiternal God could not be conceived as limiting his creative action to a small stretch." Well, now wait a minute. He has to extend his actions out here, and where can he stop creating? This is the contradiction that you run into here all the time. Newton is very strongly for it. Newton takes us down to 1727. This is a very neat list we are making, isn't it? Remember, Newton was very religious. He interpreted the Bible literally and would not go along with any of the theology of his time. He was the greatest scientist probably who ever lived, but extremely devout to the end of his days. He speaks in terms strongly reminiscent of the Mandaean and Coptic brethren. He says, "For in God's house, which is the universe, are many mansions, and he governs them by agents which can pass through the heavens from one mansion to another. For if all places to which we have access are filled with living creatures, why should all these immense spaces of the heavens above the clouds be incapable of inhabitants?" He just turns around the argument-why bother to create all those things? He says, "Well, why not? Why let all that space and matter go to waste? Why not organize it?" The arguments work both ways. "Why should all these immense spaces of heavens above the clouds be incapable of inhabitants." By combining Democritus and Newton, the great Immanuel Kant (the father of heavy German philosophy, as you know) was remarkable-his anticipation of the present cosmology and this new sophisticated stuff. He preached about island galaxies and the formation of solar systems from spiral nebula and things like that (Laplace's theory). He went a long way in the study of the stars. Today, they have just barely caught up with him. Some books like Korte's pay great tribute to Kant for anticipating, not just by intuition but by reason, so much of what we teach today.

Incidentally, just before him is Athanasius Kircher. He was a Jesuit who was into everything. And he could get away with everything because he was the pope's pet. He made some good Coptic studies and thought he had deciphered Egyptian. He was not in trouble until he made this statement, and this practically destroyed him: "It would be in God's power to create similar worlds to ours at various distances from the earth." His idea of an iter ex staticum or travel to the planets produced, even among the scientific brotherhood, only an uneasy silence.

Even the scientists couldn't take that. So Kircher went off the deep end with that one. So he was naturally for it. He's a fascinating character. He never let anything stop him. He'd come out with wildest ideas. Some of them were good, and that's the point. He could get away with anything until he started saying that it would be in God's power to create similar worlds to this. Then we mentioned the great Isaac Newton who died in 1727. Then Kant concluded that "the cosmos must be infinite because of the infinite power of God." It works both ways. He carried it on to an infinite hierarchy of island universes of spiral nebula and all the rest. He had it all worked out, solar systems and the like.

Then this argument was used by the famous Richard Bentley who got into the argument with Dr. Arnold at Oxford, that celebrated feud that ran on for years. He was a classical scholar, but he had some pretty nutty ideas too. He says, "This is absurd. What good would an infinity of worlds do? What indeed could be the usefulness of these innumerable stars that are not even seen by us?" If you can't even see a star, what good is it? (as if God created all the stars for our particular benefit, never went any farther).

And then Leibniz again and this utilitarian doctrine. "What good would it do us if there were a lot of other worlds?" (Interesting question, isn't it?) "It's both a materialistic and atheistic argument, yet the doctrine of other worlds was opposed on the grounds that it was too materialistic; indeed it has been widely assumed among scientists that the discovery of life on other worlds would be an end to belief in God." This is a very important thing now. I've got some interesting things here. "Though Leibniz defined universe as one of a system of worlds that could exist but of which only a single one has been effectively realized." So God could have created other worlds, but he only created this one because there's only one perfect world possible and that would be it. So he said, "Well, of course, there could be other worlds and God could do it, but we can't figure it out. So leave it there."

"It may be hard for the present generation to believe that up to this very time it was the scientists who contemptuously rejected the idea of plurality of habitable worlds. While the old tradition lingered, the doctors had trouble making up their minds." (Now this is the official Roman Catholic position on the subject today, which is rather important. It's in a recent issue of the Revue Biblique.) "The doctors had trouble making up their minds in the church. They went both ways until the question was settled this way." The whole trouble, according to Grelot here, is that they couldn't get rid of false ideas of localization. There had to be such a thing as place. If there was no place, then there would be no problem. But localization is a physical thing. Forget locus, forget place, forget space and all that sort of thing. Take it all as spiritual and you can forget about this other worlds business. That's the present position here.

This is a very interesting statement by Whitehead. We put the Roman Catholic Church today on this side officially, and on this side we put Alfred North Whitehead, the famous English philosopher and mathematician. His deathbed statement is a very interesting one on this subject. He says, "The Bible excels in its suggestions of infinitude.

Here we are with our finite being and physical senses in the presence of a universe whose possibilities are infinite." He rises up from his bed, shakes his head and says, "And even though we may not apprehend them, those infinite possibilities are actualities. Don't reject anything. The Bible interpreters unfortunately have scaled and whittled down the sense of infinitude into finite and limited concepts." This is what they did in interpreting the Bible-the things we have been talking about. But we must leave the door open, and this is, of course, the great possibility.

An astronomer reports in 1955 (this is from a journal; notice how recent) a dramatic reversal in outlook on the subject of life outside the earth took place. Before then, in my day, because they had the pass-by or near-miss theory, it could not happen. The same reasons are used the same way today. According to the near-miss theory, a star came near enough to the sun to pull out so much substance and it figured out just right. In the 1930s that was the only one. That was accepted. But then it was junked, and now the new one is the other one. But now you have the 15 key coordinates that have to be fine tuned together. The possibility of coming up with another world by chance is infinitesimally small. So this must be the only one; there can't be any other world like this one. This was argued either way. It was the scientists that opposed it. Old Professor Larkin would throw you out of the room if you suggested it. If a science fiction nut suggested that there might be other worlds, it was absolutely not to be tolerated for a moment, which is surprising. It comes from the scientists here.

This astronomer, Luyten, says, "A writer like Descartes feels that discussion of the question represents the antithesis of real science. [Descartes said, 'This is not a scientific subject because we can't see those other worlds. We can't study them, so forget it.') This is not real science since the same scientists held to one theory of origin of the solar system." Well, as they did in the 1930s, and another theory in the 1950s. "The collision theory-the origin of the planetary system by a chance pass-by with just the right distance, just the right time, just the right place, temperature, speed, and all the rest-was such an extreme rarity that it was all but impossible that it could have been reproduced at all." I'm quoting from another journal Ein Codarschrift and certain digests. "In 1960 many astronomers will prove conclusively and to their own complete satisfaction that planetary systems are quite common." But before 1960 you couldn't say that safely. I have an interesting reference here. This is W. Sullivan, an astronomer, speaking in 1964 on the subject of a project designed to receive or send messages to other worlds (this was Carl Sagan's pet, you know). He says, "This is a subject which we would not have dared to discuss on this kind of platform even as recently as two years ago." So recently they gave up the idea. There may be contacts, etc., but they wouldn't have dared to discuss it before 1962.

The discoverer of radar, very largely, was Arthur Clarke. He wrote 2001 Space Odyssey. He's quite a scientist, as you know. He objected to some of the fancies that have inevitably grown from the acceptance of life's existence elsewhere. He doesn't question the possibility, but like Descartes, he questions whether it would have any significance that people have assumed for it.

He says, "Man will never 'conquer space.' It will never be possible to converse with anyone on another planet [because of the time lapse]. The whole concept of interstellar administration or culture is seen to be an absurdity. Any form of control or administration over other islands in space would be utterly impossible, and all parallels from our own history thus cease to have meaning."

Maybe he doesn't take some things into account. Last night, I was reading Nathan Ha-Babli, a medieval Hebrew traveler of the Middle Ages. The Great Khan was administering an empire effectively where you couldn't reach some of the cities and some of the forts without traveling three, four, or five months. And yet they kept the thing organized. Well, maybe you could get your space empire that way if you are willing to sit it out. Of course, you can go into "overwarp" and that takes care of all that.

Has he forgotten about the great empire spreading over most of Europe and Asia? The capital was often months or more than a year's journey from its agents and messages from the provincial centers of government. I was reading about the Khazar and the Hibar kingdom of the Jews in central India in the sixteenth century. They asked for help from Charles V. It was a matter of two or three years to reach it. Isn't that something? We'll finish this next time, and then we will read the passages from the Pearl of Great Price which are very striking on this same subject.

Lecture 13

The Pearl of Great Price on the Plurality of Worlds

This is the middle of the semester, and we switch over to our text at about this point. (We should have been on it all the time, you understand, but these things are all relative.) We were talking about plurality of worlds. How many have your Pearl of Great Price along? If you don't, you're lost. Go home. It's not that big a book. It can be carried in a fairly small briefcase. Let me see by a show of hands how many. Well, that's good. All right, let's look at Abr. 1:31 here. Now this is on this subject of plurality of worlds we were talking about. How does the Pearl of Great Price treat it? So we get to that vitally important first chapter of Abraham here. What does he say in verse 31? Notice, the gospel as far as he is concerned takes in a lot of things-the things we have been talking about. He has a record, and his father is the patriarch. These things have always been recorded and always been studied where the gospel is. "But the records of the fathers, even the patriarchs, concerning the right of the Priesthood, the Lord my God preserved in mine own hands [so he has the record; with his authority to carry on, he must have the record]; therefore, a knowledge of the beginning of the creation, and also of the planets, and of the stars [they are relevant; they come into the picture], as they were made known unto the fathers, have I kept even unto this day, and I shall endeavor to write some of these things upon this record, for the benefit of my posterity." Well this has always been part of the tradition in the gospel-to know where we are, to know what the setting is, to know what the plot is. What is the scenario? What are we supposed to be doing? This is the theme. The fathers were given this. So he is handing it on.

I should have gone first to Moses 1 which is very good here, as you know. The fourth and fifth verses here. "And, behold, thou art my son [notice, this knowledge and information is handed on to a legitimate heir]; wherefore look [he is going to be shown and really see it-not in the mind's eye, Horatio; he's going to see it; the Lord is showing it to him], and I will show thee the workmanship of mine hands; but not all, for my works are without end [that's going to be of extreme importance presently], and also my words, for they never cease. Wherefore, no man can behold all my works, except he behold all my glory; and no man can behold all my glory, and afterwards remain in the flesh on the earth."

So, here we have a big scene. We're only going to be shown a small part of it, but it's a real part. He says, Look, and I will show it to you, but I won't show it all to you because it is without end, and you can't see that now. So we're being sent to a much larger picture here. And then in verse 33 of this same chapter, it goes on, "And worlds without number have I created [that's a basic principle of the gospel]; and I also created them for mine own purpose." They don't concern us now, we'll see. Notice he says two verses above, "For mine own purpose have I made these things. Here is wisdom and it remaineth in me." When Moses wanted to know about the whole thing, the Lord said oh no, not this; you're just going to have to limit yourself to the small scope that you can comprehend.

Then he says in verse 35, "But only an account of this earth, and the inhabitants thereof, give I unto you." This is all you can handle. We are supposed to have it, but we must know its setting. To know what sort of a place Europe is to live in, you have to know about the other continents. It is by sailing across the ocean that they discover the nature of their own community and continent. "But only an account of this earth, and the inhabitants thereof, give I unto you. For behold, there are many worlds [an important thing to know] that have passed away by the word of my power. And there are many that now stand, and innumerable are they unto man [the ones that stand now, new findings on this have been absolutely staggering, haven't they!]; but all things are numbered unto me, for they are mine and I know them." Then in verse 37, "And the Lord God spake unto Moses, saying: The heavens, they are many, and they cannot be numbered unto man [heavens in the plural here in a galaxy far away]; but they are numbered unto me, for they are mine." Now, here's the process, the local systems. There are local systems and larger systems. I should have brought the map along, but I'll do it the next time: "And as one earth shall pass away, and the heavens thereof, even so shall another come; and there is no end to my works, neither to my words [verse 38]." And the whole thing is "to bring to pass the immortality and eternal life of man [verse 39]," and to involve and let us hack in on the system-to get in on this thing of the eternities. Of course, it's a grandiose conception. Can you do anything to prove it one way or the other?

Well, verse 36 is important; we skipped that one. Notice, Moses is going to to settle for this (we are going to have to settle for this), and Moses said, "Be merciful unto thy servant, O God, and tell me concerning this earth, and the inhabitants thereof, and also the heavens, and then thy servant will be content." Notice, the heavens are in the plural, used all the time in the plural. It's dealing with systems. We talk about galaxies and systems now. Again, you see, this is a complete refutation of the old idea that the earth was the center of the universe and the heaven was everything that revolved around it. There were the seven heavens. Heavens are something different this time. Every world belongs to its particular system and has its particular heavens, always mentioned together. And so he says, "Tell me concerning this earthÃ‚Â…and then thy servant shall be content." First lesson he had to learn. The twenty-seventh verse of this same chapter: "Ã‚Â…Moses cast his eyes and beheld the earth, yea even all of it." That, he could see, all of it, every particle. Notice, we are dealing with particles. Particle physics is very important today. Do we mean he counted every particle, every molecule? Well, we would say if he saw every particle, he saw every class; he saw every particle of which it was composed. He saw the quarks; he saw the protons; he saw the neutrons and all these things. If he knew all the particles, then he knew of what the earth was composed. I don't think he had to count all the protons and neutrons, especially since they are changing into each other constantly all the time. But he did see that there were particles, and he saw them all. And here in verse 27, we are told, "Ã‚Â…and there was not a particle of it which he did not behold, discerning it by the spirit of God." And in the next verse, "And he beheld also the inhabitants thereof, and there was not a soul which he beheld not; and he discerned them by the Spirit of God; and their numbers were great, even numberless as the sand upon the sea shore." Now, this is used constantly in Oriental writings. It's used many times. The Egyptians use it. I was just reading a text, and it talks about Joseph. He supplied the people with grain and it says this. In Genesis it says the same thing. In Gen. 37 it says he provided grain for the people as numerous as the sands of the seashore [see Gen. 41:49 ]. It could not be numbered, very great. It goes on using the same expression applied to grain or anything you can't count. Can you count the sands of the seashore? No. Well, that's how many there are if it's beyond counting. That is the standard expression for that. I know people who say if you count the sands of the seashore, every one of them, that last one represents the last world or the last person because that's exactly how many souls there are, as many as there are grains of sand, which is rather silly.

What does the next verse say, "And he beheld many lands; and each land was called earth, and there were inhabitants on the face thereof [verse 29]." Again, you see, the earth is divided into different lands. The word earth is eretz and the word land is eretz, so you are going to use the very same word for all of them because they are separate communities, separate worlds. When I was a little kid, it was darkest Africa; and most of Africa was unknown. It wasn't until the 1920s that central Asia and Africa were explored. Central Asia was unknown; Africa was unknown; the Arctic had never been discovered. We always used to get so excited about Perry who went to pole, and they had the big fight. Did Perry get to the pole first? The trouble with Shackleton and Scott and all the rest. Those were all in my day. Nobody had ever been to the poles. Many of the islands of the sea were still undiscovered. Most of Asia and Africa were unknown. Imagine that. Each was a separate land and separate community. For years and years they didn't know anything of each other. There's quite a collection of early Jewish writers, people like Nathan Ha-Babli. Way back in the ninth century, he was a great traveler. He was born in Abyssinia among the Jewish community there, and he traveled all over Asia locating and interviewing people who might be members of the Ten Tribes. He got Ten Tribe notices all over the place. There are many people like that. There's a very notable one who went to America for that purpose. He went much later, in 1640. That was Antonio de Montezinos who was a famous Jewish traveler. He said he found, not the Ten Tribes, but small Hebrew-speaking enclaves. They didn't speak it, he said, but they recalled it. They had phrases and uses, and they were Jewish in their practices-all along the Cordilleras in Central America, and he went into Equador. He was one of the first to go into many areas there. So there were lots of earths, lots of separate lands, if you're thinking of it that way. It's not the same anymore. It's one globe, one world, now.

Then Moses calls on God and says, Tell me why this all is. And God says, I won't tell you why it is. It's none of your business yet. "Tell me why these things are so, and by what thou madest them [verse 30]?" We used to talk about that as the epic question. When the epic situation is stated in the first few lines of every epic poem (The Iliad, The Odyssey, The Aeneid, etc.), there is the question: Why was all this? Tell me, Muse, why this was? In The Aeneid, "Tell me why a man of such noble piety would have to go through such labors"

There's the introduction, the first ten lines or so of an epic, and then the poet asks the question of the Muse, as Milton does in Paradise Lost. He says, Tell me how this first happened, how our first parents sinned, "whose bitter fruit brought death into the world, and all our woe. Tempted to that vile revolt." Why did it all happen? He's going to ask the Muse to tell him what is going on. This is the epic question, and Moses asks it here. When a great panorama is spread before your eyes, the first thing you say is what's it all about? Can you explain to me what I'm looking at? This is a noble prospect; tell me all about it. Summarize the gospel in two sentences. The person is always rebuked; you can't do it in that short a period. Then the epic usually runs to a thousand pages or so that follows, dealing with just an episode or two. But here he says, "Tell me why these things are so, and by what thou madest them?" How did you ever do it, and why? This is quite a question, and God said, I'm not going to answer that now.

"And behold the glory of the Lord was upon Moses, so that Moses stood in the presence of God and talked with him face to face. And the Lord God said unto Moses [a good question, but] for mine own purpose have I made these things. Here is wisdom and it remaineth in me [verse 31]." (You'll have to be content with your world. That's all you can handle now.) And then Moses says, Yes, thy servant will be content. Then in verse 40, "And now, Moses, my son, I will speak unto thee concerning this earth upon which thou standest; and thou shalt write the things which I shall speak." And then the first verse of the next chapter: "Ã‚Â…Behold, I reveal unto you concerning this heaven, and this earth [this system, I am confining you to a particular system]; write the words which I speak. I am the Beginning and the End,Ã‚Â…" So we have a multiplicity of worlds, and we are limited in our view. Turn to the seventh chapter now. That's a very important one here on the same subject, where Enoch is the enlightener. Verse 36: "Wherefore, I can stretch forth mine hands and hold all the creations which I have made; and mine eye can pierce them also, and among all the workmanship of mine hands [Notice the use of the word hands; he's not going to hold a red giant or a white dwarf even in his hand; nobody does that sort of thing; you know what that means.] there has not been so great wickedness as among thy brethren." Now, that is quite a recommendation for this world we live on. "Ã‚Â…Satan shall be their father, and misery shall be their doom, and the whole heavens shall weep over them [see, there are other heavens and other worlds, not just the earth], even all the workmanship of mine hands;Ã‚Â…" Again, this is another grandiose concept that you will find in modern times. First, in the Pearl of Great Price the idea is that all the worlds contribute when a new world is to be made. Everybody wants to make his contribution and have a share in it. It's of interest to everyone. Everyone is concerned. We are in a state of quarantine here, so we don't know about these things. Remember, the veil is dropped for us. But he tells us here that all the heavens, all the workmanship of his hands, it all weeps to see one world destroyed. We have some literature on that which I may mention. We will take at least one hour for the creation. That's a thing that must be accounted for. There are basic questions that have to be answered-questions of time, questions of scope. Those questions are not taken care of in the Bible or in a lot of theological writing.

When they see another world, they have all contributed, you see. So they don't like to see it have to pass away. It's a terrible thing, and it concerns everybody. It gets around. I was talking to a physicist yesterday who told me there are certain phenomena now that are being observed. No, it was on Sunday. He came down and we had a nice talk. He's up at the University of Utah. He says in particular physics now, to account for the action of certain particles, it must be assumed that there is a force that moves with eight times the speed of light. Until now C, the constant, has always been 186,300 miles a second. That sets everything; that sets the boundaries, the limits beyond which we can't go. Now, it seems there must be forces to account for reverses, especially in certain mirror effects and the "arrow of time" and the like, that must go much faster than light. Who knows? But he's talking about a lot of things going on out there that are not confined just to this earth. "Ã‚Â…Should not the heavens weep, seeing these shall suffer?" And a prison he says he has prepared for them. Then in verse 40 he says here, "Wherefore, for this shall the heavens weep, yea, and all the workmanship of mine hands." They not only exist without number, but they are all connected. They are all associated with each other. There are connections and understandings between them. We will mention that a little later. And, of course, the explanations to Facsimiles one, two, and five assumeÃ‚Â…(turn to your facsimile, which is opposite page 35). Here is the first creation nearest to the celestial. (That means there are other creations.) First in government, one day to a cubit. Notice, there are measurements of time. Things are measured, things are timed, things are spaced. Everything has structure here. And number two, next to Kolob. "Ã‚Â…the key of power also, pertaining to other planets; as revealed from God to AbrahamÃ‚Â…" The next grand governing creation near to the celestial (we're getting a hierarchy here). And then the fifth one is Egyptian, and one is borrowing from the other. Note, there are governing powers. What is happening is that energy is being swapped around here. This planet receives its power through the medium of others who transmit the power to others; and so it goes. What we have is a system here. We are not studying it now. We are just mentioning the fact that we do believe in the plurality of worlds and the Book of Abraham insists on it.

Notice in the third chapter verses three, four, five, six, seven, and nine, he repeats that you must always understand what you are seeing in terms of the earth on which thou standest, from where you view it. You don't see things from above or with the eye of God that embraces all things at once. You have a limited point of view, and that's going to restrict you. So throughout that third chapter, he says things like: "And I saw the stars, that they were very great, and that one of them was nearest unto the throne of God [there's a multitude of beings, verse 2 ]." Then he says here, "I have set this one to govern all those which belong to the same order as that upon which thou standest [verse 3 ]." So Abraham lives on a particular order that belongs to a particular star. We know that the sun is a particular kind of star of a particular order. This is the order to which Abraham belongs, where he stands at present. This is where he is now, and he says everything has to be seen from that point of view. In the next verse you notice, after his manner of reckoning, a thousand years, according to the time of your order, "unto that [order] whereon thou standest [verse 4 ]." In the next verse he says, "The planet which is the lesser light, lesser than that which is to rule the day, even the night, is above or greater than that upon which thou[standestÃ‚Â…[here is a planet greater than that upon which thou standest, verse 5]." Again this formula, "the earth upon which thou standest," is used twice here. So it used seven times in this one chapter. It always specifies that all your reckonings and all your views and pictures of the universe are only what can be seen from the place where you are standing. And this, of course, is a very important principle today. It's the anthropic principle. It has become absolutely basic in astronomy in the last ten years or so. It's new and it's extremely important. Seventh verse, "Ã‚Â…reckoning of the time of the earth upon which thou standest." Again, the ninth verse, "Ã‚Â…Kolob is set nigh unto the throne of God to govern all those planets which belong to the same order as that upon which thou standest." Notice, Kolob doesn't govern all the planets in the universe, only those of the same order that concern Abraham. So he is shown Kolob for that purpose, because it concerns him. Then in eleven and twelve, "Thus I, Abraham, talked with the Lord face to faceÃ‚Â…and he told me of the works which his hands had made, And he said unto me: My son, my son (and his hand was stretched out), behold I will show you all these. And he put his hand upon mine eyes, and I saw those things which his hands had made, which were many [the same hand that had made all these things] and they multipled before mine eyes, and I could not see the end thereof." We can make it very plain here what the situation is. Then in verse 13 it talks about Abraham's local system: This is Shinehah, which means in Egyptian, one eternal round. Shenha means to go around forever. This is the sun, and that is what the Egyptians call it. "And he said unto me: KokobÃ‚Â…" That's the Hebrew word for a star. It's a very interesting word, the same as the Babylonian word and the Arabic word also, Kakkabum and Kawakibu "And he said unto me: Kokaubeam, [that which is the plural of Hebrew], which signifies stars, or all the great lights, which were in the firmament of heaven." So here we are getting local systems and all the rest of it. Then we are told about the council in verses 21-28 of this same chapter where they get together and start making things. We are talking about plurality now, not about the creation.

I want to mention just a few things here. We hadn't come to the end of it quite. This is the point, and it's a very important thing namely and to wit: For some strange reason, which will be obvious, it has been commonly held that the mere idea that there are other worlds, or planets or people besides this, and that there are other worlds greater and that our sun is a small star-that would wipe out the whole foundation of Christianity and Judaism. In fact, it would wipe out the whole foundation of religion if we once discovered that there were other planets. That idea has been commonly held by scientists and churchmen alike for the obvious reason, to begin with, their definition of creation. Creation is just once for all, everything out of nothing. Well, there are a lot of things to be accounted for. Philosopher Baillet said in 1954, "He sees the plurality of worlds. What a mockery of human design. What an annihilation of human vanities." A plurality of worlds is a mockery of human design. What we do here is nothing. If you want eloquent passages on the subject, all you would have to do is go to early Christian literature. It tells us in a Coptic Christian writing that when you approach the throne of God, the sun is as small as a grain of flour. It is absolutely lost.

It disappears in the multitude of stars, and it looks about the size of a grain of flour when you get that far away. That's an early Christian writing. They were not cut down to nothing. The contemplation of the universe had already cut the early Christian down to size, and he felt it was a salutary experience.

Now here's a vision of Paul (the second century). It has survived both in Latin and in Syriac, the Visio Pauli. Paul says, "I saw all this earth, and it was like nothing in my sight. And I saw the son of man and the human race, and it was nothing." That's what Moses tells us here in our Book of Moses, "Now I know that man is nothing, which I never had supposed [Moses 1:10 ]." So the earliest Christians were quite aware of the fact that you are not to be upset if you find out that this world is "very small potatoes" in the universe. "The thought that this is the only world did not heighten man's pride, but it placed him at the lowest point of all, to which all things fell and to which he was confined. Which was the more pleasing? While the new cosmology [we're quoting Arthur Lovejoy, The Great Chain of Being] provided astronomical reason for humility [that's true; we are very small], this was destroyed by the same new cosmology which denied life on other worlds but at the same time provided a substitute for the glory lost in the doctrine of incalculable vastness." They always had this idea that the universe was very vast. The size has nothing to do with it. The fact is whether it was closed, it had limits to it. And this is the subject of Koyre's book From a Closed World to an Infinite Universe. It's always assumed that getting rid of the geocentric doctrine cost man his high place in the universe. The earth is the center; therefore, he is the most important. It's just the opposite, as Lovejoy says. He points out that the geocentric doctrine does not exalt man. It debases him actually. What if the earth is the center of all things, as Aristotle says, then (quoting Lovejoy) "for the center of the world was not a place of honor. It was rather the bottom of creation [everything sinks to the bottom, you see] to which its dregs and baser elements sank. The actual center was indeed [what's in the center of the earth?] hell, of all things. If you want to go to the center, you'll find hell." You get that in Dante. "In a spacial sense the medieval world was literally diabolic." The nearer you get to the center of things, the nearer you get to hell. After all, the Devil is the prince of this world. It's a wicked place, it's a nest of vipers, it's a veil of tears. "In this world ye shall have tribulation," the Lord tells the apostles. Being a denizen of such a place is nothing to be proud of, and the Christians really rubbed it in. You don't have to push people off the sidewalk because you have to live on a central world. That would put you in a worse position than ever.

"But the modern assumption is that the discovery of other worlds older than ours and greater than ours would necessarily be a shattering blow to our pride." This is Arthur Clarke. We mentioned him before. He was the man in British Intelligence during the war who discovered the principle of radar, which was revolutionary in spotting the approach of German planes. He has been writing a lot of science fiction. Of all places, he lives in Ceylon now, which is turning out to be not such an ideal place because revolutions are everywhere. Who was it who gave a speech here many years ago in the old Fieldhouse? (I'll think of his name.) He was a famous New York humanist. You probably wouldn't know of him at all because it was forty years ago.

It was just before World War II, and this friend of his had been in World War I, and he said, "I'm not going to get caught in that world mess again." He owned an apartment in New York City. He sold it, took all the money and bought a ranch on an island in the most remote, inaccessible place in the world. He thought the war would never catch up with him in that place. The place he bought it on was Iwo Jima. So you can't escape trouble to which we are born, as the sparks fly upward.

Living in Ceylon, Arthur Clarke would find out that he did not escape from all the troubles of the world. "The troubles of our proud and angry dust are from eternity and shall not fail," as the poet said. This is what he writes. (Remember Clarke is the one who wrote 2001 Space Odyssey) What he produced in a thing like 2001 Space Odyssey is a shattering blow to our pride. "Few of our current religions can be expected to survive it." It's so different from the conventional view of the creation of the world. "The assertion that God created man in his own image is ticking like a time bomb in the foundations of Christianity. We will have to face the chilling fact: if there are gods whose chief concern is man, they cannot be very important gods." He's saying we're not important. This proves that we are not important; therefore, our God who created us and the earth and all that's in it is not important. After all, St. Aquinas said, "What glory is there in being a god over a lot of spiders?" We count about as much as a lot of spiders would. Why should God flatter himself to be a god over us. Arthur Clarke is saying the same thing. But, you see, from our point of view we have already stopped the time bomb from ticking long ago here. If there was a time bomb there, Joseph anticipated that. So the Pearl of Great Price beat Mr. Clarke to it. Remember Moses says, "Now, for this cause I know that man is nothing, which thing I never had supposed [Moses 1:10 ]." At the same time, Moses recalls in that first chapter how he has just stood in the presence of God. He is in the image of the Only Begotten-that paradox. But by the principle of pleniarism (plenitude, plenty). There must be plenty, and how much must there be? The idea is that you can't get too much of a good thing. If God neglected to create a beautiful world, which he might have created somewhere, then his creations would be imperfect. They would be lacking something. Something would be missing. "It would leave an empty space against the sky," as the poet says. So it has to be the most things possible. Let's quote Lovejoy again, "The more essences (regardless of their rank in the scale) there are realized in the universe, the better it is. A universe in which there was no evil would not be so good as the actual universe in which there is evil because something would be missing." You have to have evil too. All that is necessary has to be. Sounds like Leibniz, doesn't it?

So we are led to what is called today, by Buttar, etc., the crisis of science fiction, for example. This has led us into interesting things. As the propositions become more demanding, they lead us into a sort of crisis. The science fiction writers have it too. The problem is that of the cosmos without God. The famous theologian Bauer writes on that in Der Himmel ohne Gott on the problem of cosmology and metaphysics, and Lamb in The Religious Implications of Extra—

terrestrial Life [these are titles of new works]. Will the idea of extra-terrestrial life destroy the image of the Bible, our ideas of creation and life?

It is a religious question first and last. As Karl says, "What are we to think of the plurality of inhabited worlds?" That is the Ecclesiastical Literary Journal of the Catholic Church published at Toulouse. And Torm writes on the same thing in the Zeitschrift fur die neutestamentliche Wissenschaft, "On the Plurality of Heavens, On the Plurality of Worlds." The clergy have taken this up because it is worrying them, of course. We're suggesting possibilities here. The surprising thing is that the scientists have been the ones to deny it longest. As I said, Professor Larkin would throw you out of the room if you suggested there might be another world. It would make you a science nut because of the impossibility of it happening. With that near-miss theory, the conditions are so rare and so far apart. So many things must be fine-tuned together that the possibility of it happening more than once is not likely. It happened once; that was a fluke. It could never happen again. That's what the scientists used to tell us right down until the 1960s.

Now we have this ancient idea of three l's. Remember, we talked about light-dark-light. We have the three periods. There is the past (preexistence), the present (present), the future (hereafter). Every religion takes all three into account, except most religions reject number one, the preexistence. The Christians do, which makes it faulty and defective. It seems that only our religion has light-light-light. Light before we came here, light here, and light hereafter. As Adam and Eve were told, you can enjoy yourself here if you follow instructions, and you can live forever in eternal happiness hereafter. That's a wonderful arrangement. Why don't you find it in more religions? It's an interesting thing. A simple word answers that; it's revelation. But the ancient idea of three levels is a plurality of worlds too-with the world above, the world between, and the world following. What's the Old English word for world? Midgard the middle earth. A gard is a gorod; it's a thing with a fence around it (garden, a garth is an enclosed place). The Russian word for city is gorod, novgorod, etc. It's anything with an enclosure. So we are living in the middle enclosure. There's a heavenly enclosure above, and there's hell down below. All three follow the same pattern. This is fixed in the Babylonian system. It was first pointed out in the hierocentric. It's the hierocentric order of the Babylonian temples in the Enuma Elish. A temple here is a duplicate of what is above, and what's below is a duplicate of what is here-the rule of the three levels. With the world above, below and between, they follow the same.

Father Louis Speleers, a famous Egyptologist and Belgian Jesuit, takes the Egyptians to task for accepting other worlds than this. He says, "That's absurd. That's a derangement of their brains. While the Egyptians think of a real life elsewhere in the universe and hereafter [this is the official Christian position that is taken by the Roman church], the Christians forget all of that. They seek only and exclusively for the vision, if not the possession of God, the mystic vision. [That's the beautific vision; that's all you can expect hereafter.] For the Christian there is only one creation. It is ex nihilo." This is the way he defines creation: "Creation is the incarnation, the materialization of God's idea of what does not exist." That's a good definition isn't it? Because only God has a right to exist, as the Moslems say. Anything else is shirk which means sharing, anything that shares.

Since only God has a right to exist, "thine existence, man, is a crime compared with which all other crimes are as virtues." To have the presumption; only God has a right to exist. What are you doing here then? This is the point. So that is the crime of shirk, of presuming to share the universe with God-that anything else besides God can exist. It can't possibly exist. But then you run into trouble here with the Moslems. You ask him what his credo is, and he says, "I believe in God, and the angels, and his prophets, and his messengers [apostles or missionaries], and in the holy books." Then you say to the Moslem, "Now, wait a minute. God does everything. He's everything that counts?" That's exactly what Irenaeus said to the Gnostics. He said, "You're just wasting your time talking about anything else but God because nothing else is there. Just say he did it all." Then immediately he has to explain why all this evil came in. So he gets himself right into the same trouble as the Gnostics. Then you ask the Moslem, "All right, you believe there is only one God. You accuse the Christians of complicating things unduly and getting things all messed up." Moslems say, "Why do you have to have a Father and a Son and all that? That's outrageous. He does not beget; he is not begotten. We've gotten rid of all that. Islam is much more majestic, much more simple." You ask, "Well, then what are you doing with all these angels? Why are they there? What about the messengers? What about the prophets and the apostles that he sends? What about the books? Why do you need them at all? Why don't you just have revelation if you'd have anything?" So they are in the same boat with all the rest. There's an awful lot you have to explain. You can say God did it all, but that doesn't answer everything as far as we're concerned.

The Egyptian is more realistic then actually, isn't he? So the church defines creation as "the incarnation, the materialization of God's idea of what does not exist." In Christianity no creature has an existence of its own. We are all just in God, of course. As Augustine says, "You have made us of you, and we are unquiet, restless and lost until we return to you and get reabsorbed." As St. Jerome says, "Until we all return to the nothing from which we came." Then we will finally be happy. So he says here, "The creature doesn't exist by its own right at all. God takes consciousness of what does not exist by the intermediary of his creatures." We exist simply to give God an idea of what does not exist because anything but God does not exist. (This is what theologians get into.) "The whole idea of plurality of existence hereafter is vague and banal," he says. "All of this is as literal and as concrete as the Christian metaphysic is abstract and spiritual." To talk about literal and concrete things (that's what we have been talking about, you know) is a poor way to do it, he claims. "The Christian metaphysic is abstract and spiritual. It doesn't touch material things at all," he says. "This literature [Egyptian literature] is created by people with a disease of the brain, brains which do not function like ours." They don't accept Aristotle in the excluded middle, for example. Well, this takes a Christian position to which Mormonism was a complete rebuff. There's more to the doctrine of difference than we suppose, but in the end it's on these doctrines that our faith rests. So we get to this light-light-light business, these three levels. You ask, "Why don't other churches have this? This is the happiest one you could have. Why didn't the early Christians have it? But, of course, they did. But we don't allow them this. We don't allow them any preexistence at all.

Some do allow preexistence, but it was a miserable one. It's dark to light to dark. But the reason they don't have this is all in one word. It's revelation. So it all comes down to, revelation.

Do the others believe in revelation? Well, here are some interesting quotes here. Revelation as a present reality: The first volume of the American Anthropologist which we have here has a scientific analysis of Mormonism. It says this, "Mormonism is a portentous danger sign, a monstrosity [this is a scientific journal] born of deceit and bred in falsehood, a monster of iniquity and deceit." Well, what's wrong with it? "Its precepts and teachings are not in themselves immoral [he reassures us]. There is nothing immoral in the Book of Mormon, but its adherents have discovered the most dangerous weapon against the moral world order in the doctrine of a continuous revelation, a portentous danger signal to enlightened civilization." It says elsewhere in the article, "There is the cloven hoof." Revelation, anyone who believes in revelation. We mentioned this before. Revelation, dispensation and restoration were three dirty words. You couldn't use them. Now all the churches and journals do. I have a chapter added to a book of mine called The World and the Prophets. That tells how they have come around. It's called "The Return of the Prophets?" All the priests, and the pope especially, are trying to sound like bold prophets now. You'd go to the stake for taking that position before, that revelation was possible. This was the position they all took: "Continuing revelation, a portentous danger signal to enlightened civilization."

In 1898 the League for Social Services included some of the most illustrious Americans. It had George Washington Choate and Jane Addams. It had the most distinguished intellectuals of America in that time. They all signed and published a declaration, "Ten reasons why Christians cannot fellowship the Mormon Church." The first three reasons are the most damning: 1. They teach that they have the only true gospel. (Well, if it's true it's not like anybody else's, so how can the others have it too? There's no resemblance; there's a terrific difference here. We try to gloss it over.) 2. Revelations are still possible. (We cannot forgive them that.) They say, "We would like to fellowship. They are wonderful people, etc, but as long as they teach that they have the only true gospelÃ‚Â…And yet that's why we had the Reformation, because one group and another each claimed that it had the only true gospel. They went to the point of making war on each other and suffering great persecutions for making that claim. They were willing to go to the stake, "Courage, Dr. Latimer, we have lit a fire this day in England." They were willing to go to the stake for the 39 Articles and things like that. When the Lutherans and the Calvinists began, they believed that they had the only true gospel, and the Roman Catholics didn't and neither did the other sects that were quarreling with them. 3. Joseph Smith was a prophet of God (that you could have a prophet). Now many people on TV freely throw out the word revelation. "It was revealed to me," somebody will say. They put themselves in the position of prophets. "The huge corpus of anti-Mormon literature confirms the shocked reaction of the nineteenth and twentieth century civilization at home and abroad to the story Joseph Smith tells of himself in the last book of the Pearl of Great Price." That is a very important one; that's the one we should begin with. This is to establish the proposition of revelation. If revelation is so, then we know where we stand on things like this. You can never prove this, you know, as long as you live. You could argue "until the cows came home" and evidence would never do it, but you rest on revelation. "The same principle of on-going revelation is not only central to every chapter in the Pearl of Great Price, but it is the only way in which each chapter is to be accounted for [according to us]. Yet it can be shown that the propositions named above apply here. That the early church had revelation is not disputed. It begins with an angel appearing to and conversing with Zacharias in the temple." Nobody had seen an angel for four hundred years (the first chapter of Luke). It's very vivid. Luke insists on giving everything a clinical explanation, making it very lucid. Where the angel stood, exactly over by the altar here. How Zacharias was absolutely flabbergasted, struck dumb. He hadn't seen an angel before. When he went out after the long preaching with the angel, he was completely shaken. The people saw that he couldn't speak. They knew that he had a terrible experience in there. He was gone a long time. It said the people waited and waited and marveled that he hadn't come out yet. The people are usually patient during religious ceremonies. When he came out, he was shocked. Then the same angel appeared to Mary. It tells us the house, the street, the town, how he appeared to her, what he told her. This makes it very literal and clinical, like appearing to Joseph Smith. But revelation was not a thing that could be controlled, and the church renounced it because it couldn't be controlled. St. Augustine wrote a famous work, Doctrina Christiana. He said, "After all, the trouble with revelation is that you can't control it. The spirit bloweth where it listeth, and there is nothing you can do about it. We must have revelation that can be turned off and on as a tap so you can have revelation when you want it and in any quantities you want it, and you don't have to have it when you don't want it." In other words, you will control it. Well, you don't control revelation. You know that. So he said, "You will find that in ceremony and authority. You can impose authority. A breath can make them, and a breath hath made." Anybody can be appointed to any office under the sun, but it's interesting to note that since the beginning of time no one ever became a scientist or an artist or a scholar by appointment. But you can hold all sorts of offices. You can become president, you can become a dean, you can become a king or a queen by appointment. But there are some things you can never become by appointment though you pretend there are such things. But St. Augustine says, "No, we have appointment and authority and then we have things that we can control, namely ceremonies and ordinances. That's what we do, so we don't need revelation anymore. It's very uncomfortable. What we want is something more available and something more secure and reliable-something the people can rely on. Revelation just comes to one man. We want something you can prove." The Catholic Church defines three sources of revelation: the scriptures, traditions of the church (interpreting the scriptures), and reason about it. When the Protestant Reformation came, they said, "the scriptures alone" and immediately began to fight about what the scriptures meant. So they bring reason in too, reason and the scriptures. But revelation is not only out, it's wicked to suggest it. It has been until very recently, at any rate. "Its unreliability is apparent in the Pastor of Hermas who insists that God aloneÃ‚Â…" This was discovered in the sixties, a very valuable writing. It's from the year 120, written in Rome, and is valuable and authentic. You have to study it in theological schools everywhere.

"Hermas tells us that God alone can determine the time and place of revelation, a great inconvenience on the church, an intolerable restraint which the church replaced by more manageable service and forms of observances," as Duchesne, a great Roman Catholic scholar points out. "We had to have something we could handle; we couldn't handle revelation."

And Danielou writes in his book on Origen (he was the best Catholic scholar, who died under strange circumstances recently), "Traces of that holy spirit who appeared in the form of a dove were still to be found among the Christians as late as Origen's day." Notice, it lingered in remnants. What did the rabbis have after God ceased to speak to the prophets? They had the bath-kol, which means the daughter of a voice. A bath-kol is an echo. It's just like a voice swept on the wind from a distance, but it's not direct revelation. It's the same thing here when Danielou says there were traces (just traces) of the holy spirit who appeared in the form of a dove still to be found among the Christians in Origen's day. Eusebius, the father of church history, reports many events illustrating the immense hunger for revelation in the church after it was withdrawn. When the church lost revelation, a terrible thing happened. I've written a number of articles on this in a book called When the Lights Went Out. There are three of them collected there on this very subject, so we won't go into this here. The people were willing to accept anything, so all sorts of quacks went around claiming to have revelation, and that undermined the whole thing.

Pachomius and St. Antony were the founders of the monastic movement. Pachomius, who died in 346, resisted the supplanting of visions by relics and miracles. He said, "We've either got to have the visions or not, but relics and miracles will not take their place. It's true they can be controlled, but that's not good enough." He insisted that his successor be a man who had had visions. He said, "You cannot succeed me unless you are a visionary man [like Lehi]," even though they must send far and wide to find such a one. "The man they did find finally was Petronius who through his purity had been allowed to see some visions, but at that time the heads of the monastic societies were expected to have revelations, as Crum tells us. Cyprian rests his case against certain heretics on the revelations of a pure little boy who in a state of ecstasy denounced them [the last person in the world to claim revelation]." But he had to have fits in order to give him the revelation. The best he can do for it is, as they use in the Roman Catholic formula, "God suggesting or the Lord suggesting." They say, "With the agreement of the Spirit." But the recent statements of the pope include the astonishing expression, "It has been revealed." Ha ha, he wouldn't say that, not very long ago. This is the last of these basic principles we have been talking about. Revelation, they all rest on this. This was a thing which was in the early church, which the Christian world denies, and which is now being restored. Now, we will get to the text the next time.

Lecture 14

Treasures of the Heavens

This is the midterm, and if we were giving an examination at this time, these are the questions we would ask: What are the terrible questions? Why are they terrible? Where can you look for answers? Have any been answered to your satisfaction? Well, not yet in this class certainly. I say "yet." What makes you so sure? Are the terrible questions really important? Can we evade them? So it all centers around that particular issue, and that's the sort of thing we will have an essay on for the final, something related to this. Have you found any answers yet? We will find them if we look for them, and you may have been satisfied with what you learned in Sunday School when you were ten years old. As Paul says, when I was a child, I thought as a child, but now I'm grown up, I think differently.

Here are just a couple of remarks on revelation which I think you will find very much to the point. They are so nice and concise here. This is Paul Tillich who is considered the most eminent Protestant theologian of our time and just died recently. "The loss of the prophetic tradition belongs to the tragedies of Christian history. It actually was lost as far as the official church is in command." Notice, the greatest tragedy was that they lost revelation.

You all know about Paul Tillich. He was giving a very highly funded annual lecture on resurrection. Years ago, somebody gave this huge fund for an annual lecture on the resurrection, and Tillich began his talk by saying, "Well, now, of course, we all know there's no such thing as a resurrection." Everybody laughed appreciatively, and then he went on from there. This is the position they take. But Tillich said, "The loss of the prophetic tradition belongs to the tragedies of Christian history." It was actually lost as far as the officials are concerned. So now we read in S. V. McCasland, who is a very eminent theologian, "The return to the ideas of inspiration and revelation may be put down as one of the remarkable trends of our biblical scholarship in the last decade."

That's in the 1950s. It wasn't until the 1950s that it started coming back. Then, as I said, everybody started having the idea. They would burn you at the stake before. That was Joseph Smith's great crime, as we saw in the American Anthropologist. But he says, "The return to the ideas of inspiration and revelation may be put down as one of the remarkable trends of our biblical scholarship in the last decade."

Here is W. J. Hegga writing on the Catholic Church. The title is "The Flexibility of the Catholic Church-Revelation and the Development of Dogma." At this time, in 1962, they were having ecumenical council and thought, how can we make changes in dogma if we don't get revelation, if we just do it with our brains? People have been noodling this around for ages and come to different conclusions. Are we any better than they? Well, without revelation we're not going to get anywhere. So he titles his article "Flexibility and the Development of Dogma."

He concludes that revelation might be possible after all. There's a concession for you. They held a big meeting of all Protestant ministers (H. A. Kelly, D. D. McIntire), and the problem was this: The silence of God-how is it to be explained? Why doesn't he reveal himself anymore? Why is God silent now? Well, anybody who mentioned that God wasn't silent anymore was in real trouble, you remember. The last revelation ceased with the Book of Revelation. And so it goes. Here's another one by Patuchovski, Revelation and the Modern Jew. They are coming back to it too. In the Bull of 1950 the pope declares that "the doctrine of Immaculate Conception of Mary is established by our supplication to God. We have continually prayed to God, and we have invoked the spirit of light and truth; and by the authority of our Lord Jesus Christ and of the holy apostles Peter and Paul and our own authority we now pronounce and declare as definitely revealed this doctrine that Mary was immaculately conceived." It wasn't until 1950 that he could say, "It has now been revealed." Such a thing would get you to the stake in a great hurry a hundred years ago. And so it goes.

So let's move on to something else. This is the paradox we just encountered in Moses where he tells usÃ‚Â…(I forgot my big text, but I've got the little one. Never be without a three-in-one, whatever happens. Be without a handkerchief, but never be without a three-in-one.) In Moses 1:35 he says, "But only an account of this earth, and the inhabitants thereof, give I unto you. For behold, there are many worldsÃ‚Â…and innumerable are they unto manÃ‚Â…" (I'm only going to tell you about this one, but there are many others.) Then in the next verse Moses says, "Ã‚Â…tell me concerning this earth,Ã‚Â…and then thy servant will be content. Then, "Ã‚Â…God spake unto Moses, saying: [how does he talk about this earth?] The heavens, they are many,Ã‚Â…And as one earth shall pass away, and the heavens thereof even so shall another comeÃ‚Â…" He begins by telling him there's not just one, but then says, You're going to leave that alone now. Leave the other worlds alone, but remember that they are there. Keep them in mind. Why? Well, as Aristotle says, "You can't understand anything unless you know the category it belongs to." There must be a genus and a species. That's why God is incomprehensible because there is nothing to compare him with. He doesn't belong to any class. Everything (houses, dogs, keys, floors) belongs to a class in which you find other things. You can classify it in that order and compare it with things most like it. That's taxonomy, the thing that the biologists argued about for so many generations. How do you define a genus and a species? They are still not defined, you know (what makes a genus and what makes a species), except they look alike. If it looks like an elephant, call it an elephant. So they tell us, for example, that the shrew, a little tiny animal, is closely related to the elephant. Other animals aren't, that look much more like elephants. But you have to keep them in mind. As Aristotle says, to understand anything, you must understand the category to which it belongs, what it is like and what it is not like. And that's why God is incomprehensible. Everything else has genus and species. If you understand this earth, you must understand it in terms of other planets. If it is unique like God, you are not going to get anywhere with it. The first thing to understand about this earth is that it's not the first world and not the last. So he says to Moses, Now I want you learn about this earth, but first of all remember it's like many others, "like unto the other worlds that we have hitherto created." An endless line of them. It belongs to a category, it belongs to a species, it belongs to a family, it belongs to other things, and you are in a much larger picture. Why is that so important? As in the case of God, it's the only way we can avoid the absolute, and absolute is absolutely crippling and meaningless. When you say absolute, you have taken care of everything. When you say, "How can such a thing happen?" All you have to say is, "God can do anything," and you've got your answer, which is no answer. The absolute God doesn't answer anything or get anywhere; neither will an absolute earth. And especially the problem with evil.

There is a great literature of ascension where the hero ascends to heaven, sees the throne of God, comes back and reports, and leaves a record of it. The ascension literature is a very important part of apocalyptic, and it is very large. I'm not going to talk about it now, but there is an important theme to it. You have, for example, the Ascension of Elijah, the Ascension of Paul (all recently discovered among the Jews). You have the Ascension of Rabbi Ishmael who took the place of Enoch. You have the Ascension of Enoch who was taken to heaven. You have the Ascension of Abraham, the Testament of Abraham, the Apocalypse of Abraham, especially the Apocalypse of Abraham. In fact, there's hardly a prophet or an apostle of which we don't now have an ancient record of an ascension, when he went to heaven. When he goes to heaven in the case of Enoch, for example, which is the most important (that's the classic one), the hero asks his guide (he always has a guide, the Angel Michael or someone else). He goes there and he sees a venerable man sitting on a throne between two gates. Sometimes the venerable man is described as Adam, sometimes as Abel, sometimes Elijah. The judge is weeping and then he laughs. Then he weeps and then he laughs. This is found in some ancient ascension stories. Why does he weep; why does he laugh? Well, he laughs because of the spirits he is able to send (as the judge, he tests them) on to heaven through this door. It's a very ancient theme.

Remember, in the dream Virgil writes about the ivory gate, There are two gates, the gate of ivory and the gate of horn. The gate of ivory leads to bliss and heaven. The gate of horn leads to other things. Xenophon tells the story of Heracles at the crossroads. You come to the crossroads of the way of light and the way of darkness. It's universal, the doctrine of the two ways. But the reason he is weeping is that some people get by and go halfway to heaven. Why is he weeping? Because a lot of them must go to hell. What is the proportion? Some say seven thousand, some say seventy thousand. For every person who makes it to heaven, there are seven thousand or seventy thousand who must be condemned to hell. That's the proportion in this world. So you wonder then if God created the world out of nothing and all of a sudden, and these creatures are his own, is that exactly just? Why shouldn't anyone be weeping that he has created only one person in seven or seventy thousand to go to heaven? All the rest are denied anything but eternal damnation. They came into this world, which is bad enough, and then that happens.

Question: Where do you get your figure? You ask them; that's the way it is given. It's some odds. You could say seven to one. But at any rate is it fair to send all these people who didn't exist before (you just made them what they are) to hell? Why did God make a doomed and suffering race out of nothing? This doesn't make sense that he's wholly responsible for what we are. You get the well-known verses from the Rubaiyat again:

Oh, Thou who did'st man of baser metal make

And who with Eden did'st create the snake [He made all these things]

For all the ill with which the face of man is blackened

Man's forgiveness give and take.

If God made us of baser metal, why shouldn't we sin? As he puts it in another verse:

Oh, Thou who did'st with pitfall and with gin

Beset the way I was to wander in

Wilt Thou then with predestination round enmesh me

And impute my fall to sin?

In the first place he made us of base metal and prone to sin. He must forgive us that way. We are not to blame for it, and we will forgive you for making us so miserable and having to go through all this. Then he says, "Why will you predestine us (following St. Augustine) to damnation before we are even created? This is what happens. These seven thousand are predestined to be damned according to Protestant and Catholic doctrine. There's nothing they can do about it. And "wilt Thou then with predestination round enmesh me?" He has set the traps for us. And then when you sin, He says, "Ha, you sinned." Here He sets a careful trap; He makes you a weak creature; He watches you walk into the trap, and He catches you and says, "You sinned." That doesn't seem fair, does it? This is a problem of evil, one of the problems that has never been solved. So our existence as a one-act play, in other words, makes no sense.

All the critics of Oedipus agree on that. Oedipus didn't have a chance. He didn't commit a real sin. He didn't know what he was doing. He shouldn't have gone through that. Well, Sophocles sees all that. They don't get the answer at all, which is in Oedipus at Colonus. That wasn't the only existence Oedipus had or will have. Euripides' Chorus is the same way. There is nothing you can do about this. In at least five of the last seventeen plays of Euripides, he ends with this same chorus. He says, "The gods take many forms and many unexpected things they bring to pass. And the things we'd always been taught to expect all our lives really don't go into fulfillment. And the thing you never would have expected, God finds a way for it to happen. So how are you going to explain this tragedy?" this chorus is saying at the end. "There's nothing you can say about it. That's just the way it is." Loosely translated, "that's the way the cookie crumbles." Life is unfair, as the big corporation says, when it really rips you off. "Well, we know it's unfair, but life is unfair." You're supposed to feel good about that. These aren't proper answers at all, so the one-act drama doesn't account for things. As far as we're concerned, it is not a one-act play. It has a terrific background. It's always been going on, and it falls naturally, as all the ancient plays do, into three parts. You can see [p.5] why-past, present and future. You begin with the epic which tells a story of a family, like the Oedipus story. The family of Labdacus begins with an ancient curse way back in the beginning. But there was something before that. Or the Prometheus takes you back. This is the second generation. There is going to be a third. Prometheus tells us what happened in the first play, what happened before in the Council in Heaven; next, what's happening here; and then what's going to happen hereafter. So you have first an epic. An epos is the broadest period of time. Then you present this particular slice of it, only a segment. You can only see a segment, but it's a great trilogy. It's three great plays. You break the trilogy down into three plays. That's what it means, three plays. Each play is divided into acts. There should be three. The past, present and future prescribe the general pattern of three. It doesn't have to be that, but this is what they nearly always follow. And each act breaks down into scenes. A scene, you may have noticed from Shakespeare, is whenever the scene changes or an actor leaves the stage or an actor comes on the stage. Then you have a new play; then you have a new situation, a new group of characters, a new dramatis personae. You have a new setting and a new scene. You go somewhere else and you have other people in it, so that's another play. Each of these are independent plays, yet they all belong together. You can't separate them. You come right down to a monologue, if you want, but they are all the same. You cannot understand a scene-it's not going to make much sense, unless you know the play it belongs to, unless you know the act it belongs to. You can't understand the act unless you know where it goes in the trilogy. And you can't understand the trilogy unless you know the big story, the epic. And the epic is on-going.
