It always goes back to the preexistence. It always takes you back to the courts on high. This is the thing that was corrupted by the Greek poets for fun, of course-the night life of the gods sort of thing. We don't know what happened back there, but it's always the on-going story. And, of course, this is what the Pearl of Great Price makes very clear to us.

There's also the physical reality we must consider. Now the pope has taken refuge in the "Big Bang" for example. In 1955 he did that. That's a singularity. That's not satisfactory. Remember, the singularity had to begin with the Big Bang. I was just reading in someone who said "the hateful word 'singularity' because you can't explain it." What was there before the Big Bang? All matter, all space was contained in one point that had no dimensions whatever. Well, that's absurd; to say the least it's singular. So they call it singularity, and it gives people a headache. They don't like it at all. But you begin with a singularity, and then comes the Big Bang. But the earth doesn't show up for a long time. It doesn't belong to that particular phase. You have to go through a photon phase when there's nothing but light. Then you go through a hydron phase and get hydrogen, helium. Then you're back to lepton phase again. Then things go together. You go through various phases. You go through a galactic phase, then a stellar phase, then supposedly a bio-phase some people suggest. But the thing is the earth didn't come along for a long time, and it's part of a very long process. There must have been something before the singularity. This is the thing that Henry Eyring, a great chemist up at the University of Utah, talked a good deal about. If all that stuff was compacted together, what jammed it together and where did it come from? We haven't answered a thing that way.

The pope wants singularity because it looks like creation out of nothing. Nothing there and then all of a sudden the big bang. You know how things happen there. But the earth isn't part of that show, nor the stars nor the galaxies. There may have been an incomprehensibly long and varied background, but there was a background. They used to put it at four and a half billion years. Within the last ten years they have changed it to fifteen and a half. Now they have more than doubled that because they can see farther out now with the new telescopes and techniques. So now they have it down to almost thirty billion years. They have almost doubled it, and it goes on doubling all the time.

We have been told about some of it in our scriptures, etc. Where do we find it? We have found it in the Doctrina Arcana, the ancient teachings handed down. What testifies to them? What kind of proof and support do you have to those ancient teachings handed down? Well, the best thing you have is using a comparative method and using a statistical method. How come Joseph Smith hits it on the head so often? Because he came just at the right time. In the middle of the nineteenth century everything took a new course. Science took over completely in everything-in humanities, geology, geography, astronomy and everything else. You get a different ball game. Joseph Smith came before that, and he gave us the whole thing before he could have drawn on any of that stuff. If he had come just twenty years later, you could have said again and again, "Ha, he lifted that from this, or he got that from this." Just as they say today that he got "it came to pass" from the Bible because you will find that in the Bible too. But of course. In fact every word in the Book of Mormon you will find in the dictionary. But that doesn't explain anything. But there was the council in the beginning and the plan, and that's what we have been talking about. We've been talking about the old documents. But there are scientific questions to be answered. We're not going to linger on them. If we get trapped in them, there are two disadvantages. One, I don't know anything about it. Two, it gets so interesting. You get involved and you never get off, and the time is limited. That's not our concern right now, but these we have to consider because we do talk about the creation. We have accepted the literal interpretation. We have accepted a multiplicity of worlds. We have accepted all the things that bind us to pay some attention to science. Now, granted all the answers of science are, as Popper tells us, so very clearly hypotheses, and they are all tentative leading to other tentative explanations. Nevertheless, we are prompted by data, and that data must be taken into consideration. What about it? You say, "Well now, we don't know how many other worlds, etc." We do know something basic. There are other things out there. So we have then the cosmological problem, the geological problem, the biological problem, and the historical problem. Those are the main ones that concern us in the creation business in which we need not linger. But they are all treated very nicely in the Pearl of Great Price. That's why we mention them.

Let's take the cosmological problem. The old record we were talking about. There's one thing that is on reserve that I would refer you to. It shows what a remarkable knowledge of things the ancients had, just taking it as science fiction. If they are speculating, they certainly have astonishing insights into things. So we're not going to talk about this, but we are very briefly here. This article "Treasures in the Heavens" [Nibley] you will find on reserve. Things have been going on all along. This is what the early Christians preached. They were very much taken up with this idea that it's part of the larger picture, that the place has been seething with action long before our story begins. "This preoccupation with locus assumes a plurality of worlds, and indeed in our 'treasure' texts we often find 'worlds,' 'earths,' 'cosmoses' in the plural in the early Christian writings." One thing about this, it has massive footnotes, so you find plenty of references. You won't be suffering for lack of references here. "It is only the Fallen Angels led by the blind Samael [who is Satan] who insists: 'We are alone and there is none beside us.' [In his vanity he won't allow other worlds.] To the Sons of Light, on the other hand, there is opened up the grandiose vision of worlds 'united in the common knowledge of Him who made them, exchanging joyful and affectionate messages as they keep faith with one anotherÃ‚Â…'"

Now this is pouring it on here. This is from the Mandaean books and the Odes of Solomon and the twelve questions, Mandaean and Coptic, and Dead Sea Scrolls. You will find these are quite frequently mentioned here. This is the way they talk. Here are these worlds "united in common knowledge of Him, exchanging joyful and affectionate messages as they keep faith with one another in the common Plan, and talk to each other and establish concord, each contributing something of its own to the common interest. The members of the vast complex are kept in perfect accord by the sustaining Word of God which reaches all alike, since it possesses through the power of the 'treasure' the capacity for traveling unlimited distances with inexpressible speed." So everything is coordinated and held together no matter how vast. "The Word is also the Son who has 'betaken himself to the numberless hidden worlds which have come to know him.'" We don't know them. There are numberless hidden worlds we know nothing about. As the Lord says, "They are known to me. Here is wisdom and it remaineth in me for my own purposes [you don't; they are hidden to you]." (Moses 1:31 ) "Ã‚Â…angels with special assignments and marvelous powers of getting around, who constantly go forth on their missions and return with their reports." That's a very common theme: "We will return and report."

Then the very old writings here: "With all its perfect unity and harmony, the system presents a scene not of monotonous[uniformityÃ‚Â…[as you might expect]." One star looks much like another. You have a hundred elements. Everything is made of the same elements no matter where you go, so isn't it very monotonous, just more of the same, more and more worlds? What satisfaction in that? Ah no, there's an easy way out of that. "Ã‚Â…presents a scene not of monotonous uniformity but rather of endless and delightful variety:Ã‚Â…' they are all different from each other, and He has not made one of them[superfluous[there's not one too many because it is like anybody else]; hence, each has good things to exchange with its neighbors.'" That same author, the inventor of the radar, Arthur Clarke, says, "If you had a million computers [this is playing a game of checkers] and each computer made a million moves a second, how long would it take to play all the combinations possible among your checkers, just sixteen checkers?"

In ten minutes you could do an awful of lot moves. It would take three hundred billion billion years for you to play all the possible games of checkers. You can figure it out; it comes out very quickly. Just use the factorials, and it comes right out. To play just a game of checkers. So don't worry about running out of combinations because given these elements they can do all sorts of things with them. Look at one that is used by Fred Hoyle, the eighty-eight keys on the keyboard. All your music comes from just eighty-eight notes. All the melodies come from just eight. So they never get tired.

"At a new creation there is a reshuffling of elements,Ã‚Â…" Incidentally, that's a quotation, the last one, and that's from Ben Sirach, one of the most old venerable and universally accepted of all writings. It belongs in the Bible. It's in the Odes of Solomon; it's in the Ginza; it's in the Mandaean Johannesbuch; it's in Berlin Manichaean; it's in the Pistis Sophia, the oldest and best of all the Coptic writings. So this is a common teaching here. "At a new creation there is a reshuffling of elements [that's what you would expect], like the rearranging of notes in the musical scale to make a new composition. It has even been suggested, as we have noted, that old worlds may be dismantled to supply stuff for the making of newer and better ones." That's the one where you get the sorm that's mixed up.

This is how it happens (quoting from "Treasures in the Heavens" by Nibley): "Beginning with a very old Egyptian idea, recently examined by E. A. E. Reymond, that the creation of the world was really a re-creation by 'transforming substances' that had already been used in the creation of other worlds, the Jewish and Christian apocryphal writers envisage a process by which the stuff of worlds is alternately organized into new stars and planets, and when these have served their time, scrapped, decontaminated, and re-used in yet more new worlds." As we know today, this world wouldn't exist if a star hadn't exploded. It had to explode after it had gone through its life cycle and done all the pretty things it should do. Bam, it was blown to smithereens. That alone produced the heavy elements that are necessary to make a world like this. So as one world completes its cycle, the whole thing has to be re-cycled again into a new one, and then goes through the same process again. Newer stuff is constantly re-cycled is the tohu wabohu (which the Egyptians call the hu) of the Jewish teachers who saw the ultimate forms of matter in fire and ice. It was either complete energy as it is in the photon period when there's nothing but light, nothing but energy. No particles have any weight, no mass whatsoever-nothing but fire or nothing but ice. Nothing moves at the completion of the cycle of entropy when everything dies down to a dead unit, nothing but a pile of ash. So it is either completely cold or it's completely hot.

According to Jewish teachings, these are the two ultimate forms of matter. "Likewise," according to the same authority, "the world holocaust of the Stoics was merely a necessary preparation for the making of new worlds from old materials. The whole thrust of Weiss' book is that until the early Christian Apologists [in the fourth century] we find no trace anywhere of a doctrine of creation out of nothing." Well, we've seen that before. So this is interesting.

"And so we have in the Pistis Sophia, continuing the Egyptian teachings, the picture of a constant remixing (kerasmos) [it uses the Greek word kerasmos, mixing up all the time] going on in the universe in which old, worn-out, contaminated substances, the refuge [or garbage] of worn-out worlds and kingdoms, is first thrown out on the scrap-heap and returned to chaos as dead matter [what do you do with it then?] then melted down in a dissolving fire for many years, by which all impurities are removed from it, and by which it is 'improved,' and is ready to be 'poured from one kind of body into another.' This whole process by which the souls as well as substances are 'thrown back into the mixing,' is under the supervision of Melchizedek, the great reprocessor, purifier, and preparer of worlds. He takes over the refuge of a defunct world or souls, and under his supervision the five great Archons [they are the five principles; they are always talking about the five principles; this is Egyptian too] process itÃ‚Â…each one specializing in particular elements, which they thus recombine in unique and original combinations, so that no new world or soul is exactly like any other."

Well, they get it going by going into something like a spiral nebula. "In this full blown pleniarism there is no waste and no shortage: 'If there were any superfluous or any lacking, the whole body would suffer, for the worlds counterpoise one another like elements of a single organism.' The worlds go on forever: 'They come and come and cease not, they ever increase and are multiplied, yet are not brought to an end nor do they decrease.'" Well they took that answer to the three questions: Is the universe expanding so slowly that gravity will finally take hold and draw it back together into another singularity? Is it growing so fast that it will go on forever? Or is it balanced in equalibrium so it will get so large and never get any smaller? So this says it is eternal expansion. The whole process is thrown back into the mixing. "It is essentially the Plan that all physical things should pass away." Well, that's the way it goes. The mere mechanics are quite astonishing.

We go on to a new world now. This is important too. "The mere mechanics of the creation process described in our 'treasure' texts display truly remarkable scientific insight. For the making of the world the first requirements, we are told, are a segment of empty space [for there is space there, first of all], pure and unencumbered, and a supply of primordial matter [matter unorganized] to work with." Those are the two things you need according to this doctrine; again, both of them well documented here. (We will go down for there is space there, and we will take of these substances and we will make a world; "yonder is matter unorganized.") "Mere empty space and inert matter are, however, forbidding and profitless things in themselves, disturbing and even dangerous for humans to be involved with-contemplating them, the mind is seized with vertigo until some foothold is found in the void [you go crazy]. The order and stability of a foundation are achieved through the operation of a 'Spark.' [The Spark is very important; it is what starts this inert matter into life again.] "The Spark is sometimes defined as 'a small idea' that comes forth from God and makes all the difference between what lives and what does notÃ‚Â…" Remember the thought of his heart in the Shabako text. It was conceived in the mind of God and then on the tongue of God, and that brought it about.

But first "it is this idea that makes all the difference between what lives and what does not."

The Vision of Kenaz is very interesting. It was the Prophet Zenos who lived between Moses and Isaiah, and he gave the prophecy about the vineyard. The Book of Jacob in the Book of Mormon tells the story of the vineyard at great length as it was accounted by the old prophet Zenos. Of course, Zenos wasn't discovered until 1906. So the Book of Mormon knows about Zenos too. But Zenos says that. "Compared with it all the worlds are but as a shadow, since it is the Spark whose light moves all (material) things.' It is the ultimate particle, the 'ennas, which came from the Father of those who are without beginning,' emanating from the Treasure-house of Light from which all life and power is ultimately derived. Thanks to the vivifying, organizing power of the Spark [we'll call it that; we don't know anything better to call it. It's a very good word because of the electrical analogy. What is the spark that jumps the gap; nobody knows-unsatisfied electron, I suppose], we find throughout the cosmos an infinity of dwelling places, topoi, either occupied or awaiting tenants. These are colonized by migrants from previously established 'toposes' [topos, a place in space] or worlds, all going back ultimately to a single original center. The colonizing process is called 'planting' [they always use plant; you will plant another world; you will take the seeds down and plant; you will plant Adam there], and those spirits which bring their treasures to a new world are called 'Plants,' or more rarely 'seeds,' of their Father or 'Planter' in another world. Every planting goes out from the Treasure-house, either as the essential material elements or as the colonizers themselves who come from a sort of mustering area called the 'Treasure-house of Souls.'" Always there's that in-between place which the early Christians called the refrigarium or the anapausus, the place where you stop and rest between worlds, a sort of pressure chamber to accustomize yourself. When you came into this world, there was the birth shock; and when you go out, you'll be left in a pleasant garden (a greenhouse) to rest and collect your senses and get ready for the next step-get over the shock. Back in the old times with the ritual dramas, you had the greenhouse for that purpose. You pass from one act, one phase, to another. The actor goes to the greenhouse where you rest, refresh yourself, and get ready for the next strenuous act that is to follow. These things run throughout everything.

"With its 'planting' completed, a new world is in business, a new Treasury has been established from which new Sparks may go forth in all directions to start the process anew in ever new spaces; God wants every man to 'plant a planting'.... This is a very interesting quotation, incidentally. It's from that second Gnostic work I mentioned before and Reymond and the Gospel of Philip (that's a good one, you know). He says, "God wants every man to 'plant a planting.' nay, he has promised that those who keep his Law may also become creators of worlds." Now that's a flat statement from the earliest gospel we know, even earlier than the Gospel of Philip , after the New Testament. That's the Gospel of Thomas. That's the Gospel of Philip. If you keep his law, you may also become creators of worlds. He wants you to be. "But keeping the Law requires following the divine pattern at every point; in taking the Treasure to a new world, the Sent One (who follows hard on the heels of the colonists) [He goes to teach them. The teacher is sent to teach Adam after he is in the world.] seeks nothing so much as [p.11] complete identity with the one who sent him; hence, from first to last one mind alone dominates the whole boundless complex." ("Call upon God in the name of the Son forever more.") But you call upon God, and God only, in the name of the Son. "Because each planting is completely dependent on its Treasure-house or home base, the system never breaks up into independent systems; in this patriarchal order all remains forever identified with the Father from whom all ultimately come forth."

Well, we here on earth are not aware of all this because we comprehend only what we are like. So that's quite a system they had. They didn't work it out. They had it going among the early Christian and Jewish societies. That's the cultural problem here. Now, the important thing to remember is that at all times we only see part of the picture. Of course, that's the weakness. Here's a very interesting quotation on that in this book. Some of you may have it now, The Dancing Wu Li Masters. It's required in Professor Garner's physics course. This is the situation, you see. "The general theory of relativity shows us that our minds follow different rules than the real world does. A rational mind, based on the impressions that it receives from its limited perspective, forms the structures which thereafter determine what it further will and will not accept freely." Everything that goes into your world, every structure, is determined by what you have already accepted of it, and you accepted that very early. That limits you very much, notice. "A rational mind [perfectly reasonable] based on the impressions that it receives from its limited perspective, forms structures [and you have to] which thereafter determine what it further will and will not accept freely. From that point on, regardless of how the real world actually operates [our thought has nothing to do with the real world from that point on; it's ours; this is the doctrine of anthropism] this rational mind, following its own self-imposed rules, tries to superimpose on the real world its own version of what must be." Every scientist, every artist, everybody does that. You impose upon the real world, whatever it is, your own version of what it must be in view of your experience of what has gone on. And your experience is very limited, so where does that get us? "The important thing is that we are very limited here in that regard," he says.

"The common denominator of all experience is the I that does the experiencing, you [this is physics he is talking about]. In short, what we experience is not external reality, but our interaction to it. This is a fundamental assumption of complimentarity." Then he quotes Niels Bohr, "The necessity of a final renunciation of the classical idea of causality and radical revision of our attitude toward the problem of physical realityÃ‚Â…" We must make a radical revision of our attitude toward physical reality here. What's happening then? "The special theory of relativity and quantum mechanics have propelled us into unimaginably expansive areas of reality, areas about which we literally had not one previous idea." It's all different from what we ever supposed before, so we must prepare ourselves for all sorts of shocks, and not resent it and dig in our heels because this is the structure we've already made. "Not one physicist, not even Max Planck himself [when he discovered the famous Planck constant which is so very important] wanted to accept the implications of Planck's discovery." And the great Heisenberg wrote about [p.12] this quantum revolution, "When new groups of phenomena compel changes in the pattern of thought, even the most eminent of physicists find immense difficulties [they don't want to change], for the demand for change in the thought pattern may engender the feeling that the ground is to be pulled out from under their feet. I believe the difficulties at this point can hardly be overestimated." You've got to pull yourself out into a wholly new situation. As Moses said, "Which thing I never had supposed." He starts out learning about reality he didn't know about before. "The powerful awareness lies dormant in these discoveries, an awareness of the hitherto unsuspected powers of the mind to mold reality rather than the other way around." Bacon said, You just observe and the phenomenon itself will tell you what to think and what to believe. The phenomenon itself will give you not only the theory, it will give you the facts. You just observe the facts and make no judgments. Keep an open mind, and you will see things as they are. That's the Baconian theory, and it sounds very quaint today. In this sense we still hear it from people who think they are detached, impersonal, cool, objective observers. They are not at all, but rather the other way around. "In this sense the philosophy of physics is becoming indistinguishable from the philosophy of Buddhism, which is the philosophy of enlightment. Even though they are space-like separated, the state of the particle in area B depends on what the observer in area A decides to observe [what you decide to observe], that which he orients, his magnetic field." What you decide to look at is what you will see. You decide what the universe is going to be, to look at. And so it goes on and on. "Thus one is led to the new notion of unbroken wholeness [our dealing with just one system] which denies the classical idea of analyzability of the world into separately and independently existent parts." Well, this gets rather mysterious when you start talking that way, doesn't it? And so we get onto these problems of the physical world. This takes us to the geological problem. We talked a lot about cosmology, and the main thing is the geological problem.

The whole thing in geology is that you never begin at the beginning. You always come in after the show has been going on a long time. That's what Teilhard de Chardin showed. This is the National Geographic from last August. It gives a graphic picture of what the situation is here-where we come in. You follow the earth back. It goes back, back, back-goes through all these phases. Then the interesting thing to notice these billions of years ago is a thing that had never been noticed before. All of a sudden comes a mass extinction 650 million years ago. Then again 230 million years ago comes a mass extinction of everything that was there. Then 65 million years ago. That was the beauty; that's the one we learn about in the temple and in the Pearl of Great Price-that one between the Cretaceous and the Tertiary, 65 million years ago. Some of you may have seen Alvarez at Berkeley give a documentary last week on this very thing. At that time at least 95 percent of all life forms on earth were completely wiped out and had to be replaced by totally new forms. You can see why. Then at the bottom it has a very interesting thing. Today it says, "Man-induced extinction." That's where we are now. We're going to get another one of those mass extinctions. But it makes this interesting remark in the beginning. "Far more dynamic than ever imagined, our mother planet constantly renews her surface and changes her face in vast movements hidden in time from our short-lived [p.13] species." The whole business of old-fashioned evolution was just one steady, constant, unchanging rate; that accounted for everything. "Now space-age tools help explore the massive advances of crustal plates [notice, space-age tools; we must go into the outer universe to discover what's happening on home base] and the creation of new landforms as we discover a 'new' planet under our feet." (I've created a new world like unto the other worlds that were hitherto before Adam.) So Adam does find himself on a new world, but that doesn't mean there was nothing there before. Just as we find ourselves today on a new world. Some things may have held over, like the coelacanthus, or some freaks that shouldn't be found there at all. Cousteau is always blundering on things like living gastropods that had no business living after all those billions of years. These things we consider briefly, but they are given quite a play in the books of Moses and Abraham, and that's why it is right to consider them. [p.1]

Lecture 15

The Geological Problem

I see they keep coming. Someday I might say something worth listening to. They want to be there to catch it. So far it's like fishing. They patiently wait with the line in the water. Someday, he'll say something.

Well now, we were talking about the new world, remember. The reason I bring National Geographic around is that they are light, easy to carry, and you can get copies of them. There are some better textbooks to be sure. We were talking about this particular fold and the scales on the back. It talks about various times in which there have been mass exterminations and comes down to this mass extermination, 65 million years ago. Here's one back here at 220, etc. They have taken place all along, and after a mass exterminationÃ‚Â…How mass are they? Oh, ninety percent, not a hundred percent. But the funny thing is that after each one takes place, new forms of life appear just as suddenly as the old ones disappeared, which is a very strange shift of things. And as it says here, what this amounts to, allowing for these mass exterminations, is that we have living creation of new landforms as we discover a new planet under our own feet. We're not living in the same planet the dinosaurs lived in at all, some of the same substratum or not.

We have a lot about that in the Pearl of Great Price, a good deal about it. This doesn't mean that it's a new earth, a new world. In Matt. 24, which is in the Pearl of Great Price, it gives us that proper version of it with the chronological order, etc., and the last verse of that says this. It has given us three times when the world comes to an end. It finally gives us the one in our own day, and it follows up by sayingÃ‚Â…(this is the one that follows immediately after the Book of Abraham, Writings of Joseph Smith, number one). The last verse says this, which is very significant, "And thus cometh the end of the wicked, according to the prophecy of Moses, saying: They shall be cut off from among the people; but the end of the earth is not yet, but by and by." He has three times said it is the end of the world, or the destruction of the wicked. Finally, we get to the last one in these last days, but that's not the end of the earth. It's the first time he uses that expression, end of the earth. The earth is something else. That will be, I'm not telling you when, it doesn't concern you now. "Ã‚Â…but the end of the earth is not yet, but by and by," it says. So when we say these are the latter days and the last days (which they are), the last days of what? Well, they are not the last days of the earth, but they are the last days of the world.

What changes take place here? Well, I'd better dig up Otto H. Schindewolf here, if I have him. In the 1950s German paleontologists began to notice certain patterns, very distinct sharply marked patterns of extinction (it better be here too; I had it all laid out). In 1963 there was an interesting summary. The interesting thing about this (it's called neocatastrophism) is that the earth has been destroyed by catastrophes. It's not the old catastrophism that Cuvier talked about, the Flood, etc. There have been various catastrophes, and neocatastrophe is here to stay. They mocked it at first (this article is from 1963), but now no longer. There's a great deal about it. In fact, you may see more about this in documentaries on KUED and KBYU than almost any other subject now, which is this neocatastrophism. This article I'm referring to particularly here is by Otto H. Schindewolf. He's not the one that invented it, but he made the first earthshaking publication on the subject. It had already been noticed in 1959 by Stopano, but this is Schindewolf. It came out in the Zeitschrift fur der deutschen geologischen Gesellschaft for 1963, volume 114. Now, this is the story he tells us: In the history of the world's organisms, especially in the animal kingdom, there have been drastic turning points from time to time. There has been on the one hand an almost total extinction of numerous species and at the same time the sudden emergence of totally difference species. That's a strange shift. This, of course, is in contradiction to Darwin's doctrine of gradual evolution, occuring by natural selection. Convinced neo—

Darwinists, like Newell and Simpson, have now reneged and come over and are accepting this.

When I first came to BYU, George Gaylord Simpson was the "little tin god." Everything he said went. He was the straight Darwinist. Everybody lived by Simpson around here. But remember at that time they knew nothing about plate tectonics. We knew nothing about any of this stuff. Of course, no probes had been sent into space or anything like that. The whole picture has changed now. Well, this is the way he gives it to us, and that it happened suddenly. This is the thing. This sudden extermination began almost instantly, almost overnight, the same way, and then the reappearance of forms. He gives us some charts here; we don't go into them. Here he gives us a breakdown. He lists about forty different life forms. You will recognize some of them. This is in the Archeozoic which is the Cambrian, the Ordovician and Silurian. Here they are lined up. They go along very nicely, and they continue the line. Then all of a sudden, most of them disappear. These are recognized. They are the foraminifera, the radiolaria, and the radiolarian shirk. I remember I went on a geology expedition to Oxnard, California, to study foraminifera and radiolaria years ago. And the annelida, and around here you get the brachiopods and the trilobites, and all those things we don't have with us anymore. They were very abundant, but they disappeared. Then this goes into the Carboniferous which is the Mississippian, Pennsylvanian, and then Permian. Then what happens after you get to the Permian after all that Carboniferous? They all disappear. All the Carboniferous life forms disappear. In their place all these others appear. On the one side he has a line here. Everything goes up there and stops abruptly. Some of the things bleed over just a little tiny bit here, but not very many. And some of these begin a little earlier, but only one. They all begin all of a sudden when this one ends and that one begins in the Triassic and Jurassic. Then they carry over, the Jurassic, into the Cretaceous. Then comes the big break, the important one for us; that's between the Jurassic and Tertiary. That's here when all these forms disappear. All of them go and all of these new ones appear. There's no carrying over-an astounding thing. Well, what's going on here? This is not the picture we had of gradual, steady, gentle, constant, continual evolution in response to natural environment and survival of the fittest, which you hear so much about.

Now, what could have caused it? Well, in the 1940s they discovered the tektites around Australia first. You know what tektites are; they are little bits of glass, not volcanic glass, but little chunks of glass, usually small. They are shaped often like dumbbells, sometimes like little droplets. They were found in the sea, first in Australia, and then various other places. Here's the radiolaria, and here are the tektites. These are magnified. When they fell from the sky, they were in a molten condition. Naturally, they cooled off, and as they cooled their molecules lined up with the magnetic pole of the earth, which was not where it is today. The magnetic pole shifts around, and so we are able to date them. So these tektites were near Australia, and they are dated about 700 million years B.C. So that's the Southeast Asia. Now what could have caused it? Then the next one was West Africa. The next one is very important, the Nordlingen Rift. Then finally the Texas. These are the only places in the world where these are found, and they are always showered out in a particular direction. The Southeast Asia dates 700 million years. The West African one dates almost an even million years. The Nordlingen Rift is very interesting because we see all about them, where they came from. They are still older. Here is a space view of Nordlingen which is a town in south-central Germany. It was very important in the conflicts of the Reformation. Here it is. That's the big hole you see. It's a big hole twelve miles across. The town of Nordlingen is in it. You wouldn't notice it if it wasn't from space. It's this big hole. All the tektites scatter out from it. They go clear over into Moravia and Czechoslovakia, so it's called the Moravian tektites. They fan out in a particular order. This is the hole a meteorite made and the direction from which it came. It was a big meteorite, and it hit 14,600,000 years ago. Finally, the Texas one hit. That was the longest ago, 34,000,000.

When these hit, what happened? They gave a very strong jar to the earth. I might as well read from something here. Just this week there have been two documentaries on PBS on this very subject. What happened here? Alvarez and his son, two famous geologists, were doing some work in Gubbio in Italy (that's the famous Iguvium). The oldest Roman writings we have are the Iguvine Tablets. They come from the Arval, the Arbor and the Salian Brethren. These are ancient rites performed long before the days of Rome. They are very ancient stuff. But this is much older because when Brother Alvarez was working in there, he discovered between the Cretaceous and the Tertiary just a one inch line of iridium. Everything under the iridium belongs to the Cretaceous. These are dinosaurs here. After this there are none of them. They are just swarming here, and there are none of them here. So what happened? Something in between wiped them out. Here are the Cretaceous and the Tertiary. This is our new forms of life. Notice, how he lists the Tertiary as it turns to carnivores and birds and all the stuff we know today. They come there. But iridium is an element that is very rare on the earth, but it occurs in meteorites commonly. So what happened? Well, maybe a big meteorite struck the earth and showered iridium all over, iridium dust, among other things. It's in far higher percentage than it should be. But then they went elsewhere and found the same thing in Iceland. They found exactly the same sort of deposits in the same order and the same color (sort of yellowish brown) between these two in Montana. Throughout the whole world then, there [p.4] is a layer of iridium under which all life is extinguished and above which new forms of life appear. Well, what could have happened there?

Richard Muller at Berkeley noticed another thing. These extinctions occur with clocklike regularity geologically almost every 26 million years. Now, what could possibly account for that regularity? Why every 26 million years? He got into a big argument with the Alvarezes about this. But everybody is coming around now. It's the theory of Nemesis and the Oort Cloud. This sounds like science fiction, doesn't it, the monsters? What happened is that the sun has a dark companion star which in its orbit comes within a critical distance from the sun at a particular time. Or it runs into the Oort Cloud at a particular time-its orbit being every 26 million years. Every 26 million years everything starts getting stirred up, and the earth gets hit with a lot more meteorites than ordinarily. There is a patch of busted up matter out there called the Oort Cloud. It's supposed to be a sort of nursery for comets. We're hearing a lot about comets today with Halley's, etc. Comets come regularly, and every time this sun hits this Oort Cloud, it stirs things up and sends comets scattering in all directions. Many more than usual come toward the earth. The earth gets a regular shower of them every 26 million years. There's a better chance of getting hit, and so these big things come along and whack everything. When they hit the earth, what happens? Well, they jar it to its foundations. Here's the earth, and it is protected by a magnetic field. That protects it from the cosmic rays from outside and from the solar wind from inside, both of which have radioactive particles. They're dangerous. So this is the solar wind here. There is a place way out in space where they meet and cause a turbulent zone around the whole solar system.

When this comes and hits, what does it do? It shatters and breaks down the magnetic shield. You can see why it doesn't hold up in the North. You can see these ionized particles in the northern lights up here. That's why they get through because the shield goes in this form. Out there are the Van Allen belts and other things to protect it. When this gets very solidly whacked, what happens? At the beginning you have the earth's crust, which is only twenty miles thick; you have its mantle, and then you have two cores in the center. There's a molten area between them so that they don't turn at the same rate. That differential acts as a dynamo and sets up the magnetic field that protects the earth. But when this gets badly jarred, the liquid all "scubzes" up. The thing doesn't turn at a regular rate. The shield breaks down, and the rays get through. Notice the double action here. We should be reading some of these things now. What happens when these hit the earth? Well, tremendous amounts of dust and smoke start fires throughout the earth. It covers the earth with a veil of dust. The breakdown lasts for about a thousand years, but meantime the earth is covered with dust. This iridium one is the one that happened between the dinosaurs and us, 65 million years ago. When it breaks down, the dust and the smoke, especially the dust (and the meteoric dust as well), covers the earth for a period of time and causes most life to disappear. They figure that at a minimum 95 percent of all life forms were destroyed. It's the equivalent of five thousand kilotons of atom bombs. We have fifty thousand already to shoot off. So here comes our atomic winter which is very real.

How come the new forms of life appear so suddenly then? Well, what happens when you get these radioactive particles? What do they do to genes? What do they do to DNA? They cause spots, mutations, freaks, all sorts of things to appear. So all of a sudden you are going to have strange creatures coming out, a great variety of them. Then, I suppose, the natural selection gets to work and certain ones survive while others don't. But here you're going to have a whole new story coming along. These creatures appear very suddenly; they call them sports for that reason. They appear because there is nothing to protect them from these dangerous radioactive particles that we are cooking up so much in our own society.

Comets have built up the earth. It's the comets that do this. There is nothing you can do about it if they are going to hit the earth. Let's go to this one, Hoimar von Ditfurth. This book is not in the library. It's a very good one. This man is sort of the NBC man for the continent. He's in charge of all their scientific documentaries, etc. I have copied the relevant passages and chapters and you can get them on reserve. There is some interesting stuff here, but you can get this in plenty of other books; this is only a very convenient one. He says here, talking about the moon, "Meteors do frequently crash into the moon, and the resultant explosions really do hurl large quantities of lunar matter into space. This process has been going on for billions of years, so that by now the surface of our planet must be almost buried in lunar matter. You can pick up moon rocks anywhere you go. There can be no doubt that at some time in our lives each of us has held a moon rock in his hand. Unfortunately, we cannot tell the difference between earth rock and moon rock." Then he goes to the others. Now, this is a thing that is being taken very seriously today. Alvarez and the other boys are into this. We're talking about comets. "Their orbits may be two or three light years away from the sun [enormous orbits. Remember, a comet has two orbits. It's an elliptical thing that goes way out there.] At these remote points the comets are already traveling in the border of regions neighboring the solar system [almost outside the solar system] where they sometimes cross paths with comets from neighboring systems [they intermeet there]. At such times two comets may actually change orbits-that is each comet begins to orbit a new sun-thus a constant exchange of matter takes place at the border regions of the neighboring solar systems. When a comet approaches the center of its system, it falls under the influence of various massive bodies in the region, including its own sun. How fascinating to realize that the surface of our planet must be strewn with matter from other solar systems." Well, the more recent report here is that our earth is almost totally made up of matter brought to it by comets. That's Parley P. Pratt's old theory. Remember, he used to defend that. It might be interesting to listen to later on, but we won't have time.

Now here, "Each galaxy began to attract great masses of interstellar dust and meteoric material from all the other galaxies in the group." We said the last time that all the worlds share with each other, each partake of each other. They make a common course-this being the old Christian and Jewish doctrine dealing with this same thing. "So the cosmic debris streams out from galaxies and begins to form a bridge between them [we actually have a photograph of that going on]. This matter [that is brought by comets] tears down and builds up all objects, providing new raw materials for new creation. This metabolism resembles the metabolic process of organic life, a new combination, a new creation." Oh, what a shocking thing to say! How unchristian. Haven't I heard the expression, "We have a new world, like unto the other worlds that were created." Every time something is said about delivering something to this world, a specification is made, like unto the other worlds. It's going to be the same matter and the same substance and follow the same pattern. This is nothing strange here. This is what we have been talking about all the time-this delivery among the worlds. This is made like unto other worlds, and as far as the matter is concerned, the same thing. This goes a long ways. "Astrospectographers who snapped a picture with illustrations 25 [it's in here] were not recording a unique event-the Milky Way containing matter of other Milky Ways. During the past few billion years, a certain quantity of extra-galactic matter [now we're going outside the galaxy] has continually filtered down to the surface of the earth from outside this galaxy." The picture he is talking about is this surprising picture showing exchanges between galaxies going on. It's really something. And remember, there are billions of stars counted, but there are more billions of galaxies. There are more galaxies than there are stars that have been counted-a hundred billion at the very least. Galaxies, mind you, and each one has an average of a hundred billion stars. So there is a lot of stuff out there. We have just recently almost doubled the expanse. We thought that was the end. It's not the end. But here they are swapping around with each other, and we are doing the same sort of thing. It's the same picture we get in the books of Abraham and Moses. Apparently, we are looking at an actual example of intergalactic exchange of matter, metabolism of the cosmos. Notice, "yonder is matter [the things you have to have]; let us organize it into a world like unto the other worlds." So we are following the pattern. "Yonder is matter, and there is space there."

Yesterday being a nice day (it's 70 degrees now, and everybody should be absent if they had any sense, including me) I went on a hike and I went up Indian Creek. Nobody knows about that. It's not far from here, a very short distance with a magnificent waterfall in it. Nobody knows about that either. I had been up late last October, and it was beautiful. I thought it would be nice to see the falls and see if they are really roaring now because they always run. They drain half the west side of Timp. What did I find? All around I saw irreducible rock-rock that's the same stuff as the planets, and it had been there a long time. But I hardly recognized the place. There were three avalanches. The first one covered the trail, but you could climb over it. It was snow. The second one was a "humdinger." It was huge boulders, huge rocks, trees all mashed up. It completely smashed and took out the trail, left nothing there. You have to climb over it. There was sort of a gorge. Then when you approach the falls, there is a perpetual trickle of water coming down. Just because of that little trickle, at the base of it was a huge snow slide. The water had caused it to come down. Well, what did we learn from that about the processes of the creation? The Lord has told us that first of all we start out with the waters, and they are very important-the darkness covering the waters. As the scripture says, "Darkness brooded over the face of the deep."

Is this sort of thing possible? Well, it certainly is. I hope I can find something here. As you know, we have been looking at the big satellites of other planets and seeing amazing things now. Here is the planet Io where seven active volcanoes have been seen in action. Nobody ever dreamed things would be like that out in dead space there. But the most interesting ones are the many planets that are covered with water. Here for example is Europa. The whole surface is covered with water, the same kind we have. And Saladis which is coated with ice and long crevasses, broad white marks. Antiona has water geysers that shoot up from it all the time (so much water around every place; it's an absolutely amazing thing). The biggest one of all is Ganymede here. We're talking about the moons of Saturn now. The interesting thing about Titan (there's an article here on Titan) is that it's raining there all the time. It never stops. It's covered first with a blue atmosphere, a blue sky. Under that it is raining methane. It's covered with methane all the time, and it's a sort of drizzle that sort of comes down. The gas on other planets can be ethane. I wouldn't want to light a match if I were there because that's the gas we use for house heating and all that sort of thing, the next step from methane. It is very organic. It's a hydrocarbon, C2 H4 . Ethane is very elementary gas; very necessary. We find all these organic elements on these planets. The interesting thing about some of them is that they're perpetually raining right now. They have clouds, and some of them used to. Looking at Mars, we saw that it once was completely covered with clouds and had torrential rains which produced those rills all over the place that are so much like rills we take from the earth and from other planets (there are a few on the moon).

Here's a big boy. This is Titan. The outermost layer is this bluish transparent haze of organic carbon-based compounds. Below that is a thick layer of smog (it's getting almost like the earth), again organic compounds but of larger particles, characteristically red. Then fine methane sleet falls out of perpetually cloudy sky. Methane is closely related and acts just like water. It says here, "Since methane likely plays the same role as water does on earth, it can be solid, liquid or gas [just like water]." But on most of the big satellites (and some of these are bigger than the planet Mercury) it's water that comes down and covers them completely. But this fine methane sleet falls out of perpetually cloudy sky. "Jagged methane escarpments rise from a methane lake under a weak, obscured sun where full daylight may be comparable to full moonlight on earth." Well, now look at the picture we get here on earth. Darkness covers the face of the earth in the beginning. But we are told in Abr. 4:2 that to begin with there was nothing but earth, and then the rest had to come. But it was just rock like the rest of them, just silicon dioxide, just like all our rocks and sand. So yesterday, I saw these mountains and hills, and I saw the signs of plate tectonics. Of course, we didn't know about that before. Along here we have the subduction, all along the Wasatch Front, which causes an upthrust to come up. It heats the ground. Near the coast where it is even more active, it gives us things like St. Helens and that whole chain of volcanoes like open sores along the Cascade Range. These things are taking place here, and so what forms first of all out of this are the mountains and the hills because this is basic. This is the motion of the earth's plates and the motion of its surface. Then what comes next?

Great rivers and small streams. Those are the things that do the work, and that is what I saw yesterday. There are no great rivers around, but these small streams were breaking down the mountain. That big snow slide was caused by that innocent little trickle that goes down all the time. You could see plain enough among the strata (I think its Pennsylvania stuff there) how the water was seeping through everywhere.

I wouldn't advise you to picnic up there for awhile because there are some very dangerous overhanging rocks there. They were rumbling down while I was there. It's still happening. If you go up and spend the night at Emerald Lake on Timp, it's lots of fun. All night long you hear rumble, rumble-just rocks rolling all night long. If you look at the snow down there, it's all covered with litter of rock and dust coming down all night and all day long, especially when it freezes at night. Then in the morning it thaws out and the stuff is loosened up. When it freezes, naturally it expands and widens the cracks. The water melts from the snow, gets down in the cracks, and freezes overnight. When it freezes, it opens cracks wider and wider and down the stuff comes. It comes down all the time. So we have mountains and hills, great rivers and small streams, and that gives the face of the earth the characteristics it has. Those are the two big things, as you know-mountain building and erosion. Why? Well, we are told to give variety and beauty to the scene. Was all of this being done for my benefit? Well I'm glad I was thoughtful enough to go there. Otherwise, it would have been wasted. Nobody else was up there. So the Lord is tearing down this mountain just so I can enjoy the variety and beauty of the scene. That's legitimate. If you happen to be there, you can enjoy it. But this is another thing we must remember here all the time. It's for the benefit of others too. This pluralism we must keep insisting on; namely, that the earth is meant for a multiple purpose. We are told that. "I have commanded every form of life to multiply in its respective sphere and element [it has its place] that every form of life may fulfill the measure of its creation and have joy therein." I'm not the only one that's supposed to enjoy this. Others are supposed to enjoy it too. This whole thing is to be enjoyed by lots of people and lots of things. But the variety and beauty is a perfectly legitimate thing to have there if we are there to enjoy it.

It's like the miracles. Miracles are nearly always a matter of timing. The Red Sea often withdrew when the wind was in the right direction, but especially at that time there were lots of earthquakes. It was a very active time. The sea withdrew and then it came back again like a tsunami. It didn't have to come back much if it was a strong wind. It was those sand flats by the bitter lakes, and the Israelites walked over them. Pharaoh came along with heavy chariots with narrow wheels. They would sink into that as soon as the water came back. A foot or two would be enough to stall them hopelessly. They couldn't get out of there at all. It just happened to be at the right time; the Lord can see things coming. They crossed the Jordan the same way, the way it is described in Joshua. The waters were just a trickle. They were held back, and the children of Israel got by. Then suddenly the waters broke loose, and there was a great rush of mud, timbers, rocks, and trees. There was a natural dam up there and it broke and came down. Well, it's the timing that makes it a miracle, if you just happen to be there.

Not that the Lord said, "I'll do this for your benefit now," but if you can foresee all things. When you time things, it is a miracle. Not that there aren't other types of miracles, that things don't happen according to rules you don't understand. Brigham Young always insisted there was no such thing as a miracle. But you see the timing and the coordination of things is so important, so many things going on and the like. It is to give variety and beauty to the scene for our benefit as long as we are here-the same as we are told about the moon. We are told the moon is given to us to serve as the light by night. It does serve as the light by night and has many services for the human race. It doesn't say that's all it does. We mentioned before the claims of certain church fathers. John Chrysostom said that the sole purpose of the stars was to guide sailors at sea. There's no other purpose; therefore, why should there be life on other worlds? The stars have fulfilled their purpose if they just guide sailors at sea. Well, they do guide sailors at sea, but is that their only purpose? This is the thing we have to keep in mind here. So never forget this pluralism, this multiple use of the earth. It's not made for us alone. We are allowed to share it with God's other creatures, and we are told in the seventy-seventh chapter of the Doctrine and Covenants that other creatures have just as much right to the earth as we have. Not only that, but they have spirits and will be resurrected in their own world in their own time. We mustn't interfere with them in their proper places. We try to build our houses up in canyons where they have no business being, spend two million bucks-and smash, down goes the house. Of course, it serves us right. We have no business being up there. We can visit, but to take it over and want to possess everything is not right. Remember he tells us it was set apart for various creatures, each in its proper sphere and proper element.

Now the question comes, where do we hack in here? Where do we get in this picture? What about the seven thousand years? That's relevant. Our history is only a seven-thousand-year one. That all-important interface between the Cretaceous and the Tertiary. Now that the earth is formed, divided, and beautified, it doesn't have plants growing. That's the trouble. "Go down and place seeds on the earth of all kinds." Here's another thing. It's strange we don't have it in the library here, but I have put Xerox copies on reserve and I think you can get it at the Bookstore. It's a series of essays by Loren Eiseley, the famous paleontologist and biologist, called The Star Thrower. Here's a very good one on page 67. It has the title "How Flowers Changed the World." This is what happens: "A little while ago-about one hundred million years, as the geologist estimates time [now it's down to 65 million]Ã‚Â…-flowers were not to be found anywhere on the five continents." They just appear suddenly overnight in this order: grass, flowers, shrubs, and trees, each bearing seed after its own kind, because their whole purpose is to bear seed. Before then you had spores. These are the first angiosperms, the first plants that have their seeds encapsulated. Angio means "little pot" in Greek. Inside is the seed which is very nourishing protein, so it gives rise to an entirely new breed of animals on the earth.

Well, let's hear what Brother Eiseley says about it, "Ã‚Â…flowers were not to be found anywhere on the five continents. Wherever one might have looked, from the poles to the equator, one would have seen only the cold dark monotonous green of a world whose plant life possessed no other color. Somewhere, just a short time before the close of the Age of the Reptiles [that's what we get here; that's our iridium layer. As I said, they are doing more of that all the time at Berkeley, Harvard and Princeton. People are working like mad on that.] there occurred a soundless, violent explosion. [Most of these things do not happen gradually and imperceptibly.] It lasted millions of years, but it was an explosion, nevertheless. It marked the emergence of the angiosperms, the flowering plants. Even the great evolutionist, Charles Darwin, called them 'an abominable mystery' because they appeared so suddenly and spread so fast."

And incidentally, the new time scales that are given are much shorter. Of course, it doesn't make any difference. If you are talking about a million years, you might as well say a hundred million years; you're outside of fundamentalism by that time. All you have to do is go beyond seven thousand and you are an abomination to the fundamentalists. But, of course, we preach that things were always going on. "There's no end to my works." Worlds have always been coming into existence, and as one world comes another passes away. "There is no end to my works or my words." So Darwin called these flowering plants "an abominable mystery" because they appeared so suddenly and spread so fast. Evolution does not mean revolution where things happen suddenly and violently. It's evolution where things unfold gradually, regularly, and almost imperceptibly. It doesn't go that way at all. It's a series of violent convulsions all along. So he called it an abominable mystery.

"Flowers changed the face of the planet. Without them, the world as we know-even man himself-would never have existed." So this part had to come next. You notice there is a succession and process in our story of the creation. We're told about the six main periods of creation. But things have to be done in a certain order. Now that the earth is formed and divided, it's time to put the plants on. But the animals aren't yet because they have to follow. "Francis Thompson, the English poet, once wrote that one could not pluck a flower without troubling a star. Intuitively, he had sensed like a naturalist the enormous interlinked complexity of life. Today we know that the appearance of flowers contained also the equally mystifying emergence of man. [Before that, under the layers we had the dinosaurs.] Tyrannosaurs, enormous bipedal caricatures of men, would stalk mindlessly across the sites of future cities [heartless and mindless]Ã‚Â…" On the other hand, there has been a new big study by Adrian Desmond that says that the dinosaurs were probably very intelligent and furry creatures. We don't have the skin of dinosaurs anywhere. We have the skin of giant sloths found in caves in Nevada, but he says they might have been quite clever creatures, quite lovable creatures, these dinosaurs. We don't know, in other words. But they're not with us anymore. It was their world. Eiseley says, "Ã‚Â…a cold-blooded world whose occupants were most active at noon day but torpid on chill nights,Ã‚Â…Creatures without a high metabolic rate are slaves to weather.Ã‚Â…A high metabolic rate, however, means a heavy intake of energyÃ‚Â…The agile brain of warm-blooded birds and mammals demands a high oxygen consumption and food in concentrated[formsÃ‚Â…[they could only get it when there were grass, flowers, shrubs and trees], or the creatures cannot long sustain themselves. It was the rise of flowering plants that provided that energy and changed the nature of the living world. Their appearance parallels in a quite surprising manner the rise of birds and mammals." They come after this has been provided, as you might expect.

Now the true flowers. "After a long period of hesitant evolutionary groping, they exploded upon the world with truly revolutionary violence. The event occurred in Cretaceous times at the close of the Age of the Reptiles." As we say, the end of the Cretaceous and the beginning of the Tertiary. That's exactly when it fits. This was published in 1978. Lots of things have been discovered since then, although it's only a short time ago. But he got the time right then. They hadn't discovered the iridium then. "Before the coming of the flowering plants, our own ancestral stock, the warm-blooded mammals, consisted of a few mousy little creaturesÃ‚Â…Neither the birds nor the mammals, however, were quite what they seemed. They were waiting for the Age of Flowers. They were waiting for what flowers, and with them the true encased seed, would bring." Before that "all is stiff, formal, upright and green, monotonously green. There is no grass as yet; there are no wide plains rolling in the[sun,Ã‚Â…[that has to come first] Nevertheless, the true flower, and the seed that it produced, was a profound innovation in the world of life.Ã‚Â…But the seed, unlike the developing spore, is already a fully equipped embryonic plant packed in a little enclosed box stuffed full of nutritious food. Moreover, by featherdown attachments, as in the dandelion or milkweed seed, it can be wafted upward on the gusts or ride the wind for miles; or with hooks it can cling to bear's or rabbit's hide; or like some berries, it can be covered with juicy, attractive fruit to lure birds [that's what the fruit is for, to lure the animals who will take it abroad] pass undigested through their intestinal tracts, and be voided miles away."

They had ingenious ways of spreading themselves. You say it would take a trillion years to work that out by accident, trial and error. The thing is it happened suddenly, almost instantly. It is surprising. "Plants traveled as they had never traveled before. They got into strange environments, etc.Ã‚Â…These fantastic little seeds, skipping and hopping and flying about the woods and valleys, brought with them amazing adaptability. They produced concentrated foods in a way the land had never seen before.Ã‚Â…That food came from three sources, all produced by the reproductive system of flowering plants." There were the tantalizing nectars and pollens which attracted the insects. You had to have flowers. You have the grasses which were eaten by the ruminants; you have the flowers which are taken by the insects (bees and others); and you have the fruits which are eaten by the big animals like bears. We eat the fruits. What makes an apple? It's a pod for an apple seed. So the main purpose in producing all of these is that each shall bear seed after its own kind. That is the purpose of bringing forth grass, flowers, shrubs and trees, in that order. So he says, "These were the tantalizing nectars and pollens intended to draw insects for pollenizing purposes.Ã‚Â…There were the juicy and enticing fruits to attract larger animals, and in which tough-coated seeds were concealed, as in the tomato, for example. Then, as if this were not enough, there was food in the actual seed itself, the food intended to nourish the embryo. All over the world, like hot corn in a popper, these incredible elaborations of the flowering plants kept exploding. In a movement that was almost instantaneous, geologically speaking, the angiosperms had taken over the world.Ã‚Â…The explosion was having its effect on animal life also.Ã‚Â…The flowers bloomed and bloomed in ever larger and more spectacular varieties."

And what would you expect? First of all the "grass has a high silica content and demands a new type of very tough and resistant tooth enamel, but the seeds taken incidentally in the cropping of the grass are highly nutritious." So there were the great grazing herds upon the earth. But the first to occur were the great herbivores, namely the elephant of all things, because that's the carry-over; remember like tyranopherms. You had great animals that crossed the barrier there, and the mammoths were the first, a great variety of elephant-related creatures. He says here, "A new world had opened out for the warm-blooded mammals. Great herbivores like the mammoths [so first you have the elephant] horses and bisons appeared. Skulking about them had arisen savage flesh-feeding carnivoresÃ‚Â…" What do you have when you have large grazing herds? Fierce carnivorous creatures, big cats (the lion and the tiger). Finally, you have the omnivorous bear who can eat anything. But this is the order they appear in: the mammoth, the lion, the tiger, the bear, the horse and all the other things. "Skulking about them had arisen savage flesh-feeding carnivores like the now extinct dire wolves and the saber-toothed tiger.Ã‚Â…Their fierce energy was being maintainedÃ‚Â…by the concentrated energy of the angiosperms. [That's supporting the grazing herds who are supporting the carnivores.] That energyÃ‚Â…was being accumulated and concentrated in the rich proteins and fats of the enormous game herds of the grasslands." And so it goes. Finally, we get to man, but he comes last of all. So this is an interesting thing. This is where we "hack in" then. It was necessary before the Tertiary beasts could occur to have these things happen in their proper order. And this is the order we get in our four accounts. There are traces of it in Genesis. It's very clear in Abraham and Moses, and most explicit in the temple.

Now it's time to go to the Pearl of Great Price specifically and to first Moses. Ah, what a book! We are going to talk about the great dramas, etc. The author of First Moses could claim to be the greatest literary genius of the modern world because he wrote the Book of Moses before they had discovered the Book of Enoch or any of it was known. This first chapter is the classic drama of every man. It's the fundamental drama. Moses isn't the first man to appear. We take Adam in the order he appears. When we get to Adam, where does he come in? Again, there are a lot of creatures running around that are going to look like men.

Oh, I must tell you about this first. Some while ago I gave a spiel called "Before Adam," which is on reserve. You can get this through F.A.R.M.S. at half price. Oh, it isn't half price anymore? Oh, the rascals. Well, F.A.R.M.S. has become corrupted like everybody else. Come up to 4012 in the library and you can take this and Xerox any particular pages that interest you. It's divided into separate sections.

This appearance of Adam and primitive man; that's another thing we have to take up. I see the time is up now. I guess we'd better talk about that the next time instead of going to First Moses.

What about primitive man? What about forms millions of years ago? What about the Olduvai Gorge? What about all those other creatures that were running around that looked so much like man? We begin, of course, with the Solo man. Whatever happened to the (you think of various ones) the Solutrean, the Aurignacian, the Magdalenian-all these cultures, all these people that lived long ago? They lived long before Adam. Well, what were they doing then? What is Adam's date? How is he related to them? These are questions we must face. After all, these things have to be accounted for. Does the scripture accommodate them? Does it leave room for them? Does it actually tell us about them if you only read it more carefully? Well, if you read it carefully, it tells us a lot more than we are willing to accept because nobody reads anything with an open mind. I already have a set and a prejudice when I read. Before you can fit anything into a pattern, you have to put it there. That's the wonderful work of Karl Popper on that subject that changed the thinking of so many scientists on that. You do not have facts here. We are not dealing with facts; we are dealing with hypotheses, and every one of them is tentative. Facts are few. The fossil I hold in my hand is a fact. The spectrum I see on the screen is a fact, but my interpretation of it is not a fact. That's just my interpretation unless you want to take it as a literary fact or something like that. We talk about facts. These rocks are facts; these bones are facts. They don't mean anything unless you interpret them, and you interpret them as you please. That's all there is to it. Two scientists had the same bones spread out on the table in front of them, and each used the same bones to prove the exact opposite. "These bones absolutely prove what I say." "No, these bones absolutely prove what I say." Well, the bones were real. They were there all right, but who are you to follow? No, it's something because it's this time schedule business. People worry about time. And then they are finding a lot about primitive man now. He walks around and all this sort of thing, millions of years ago. What happened to him? What happened to the Heidelberg man and the Windmill Hill and all that sort of thing? I guess we might as well consider that because there's a place in the scripture for that too.

Lecture 16

The Mountain of the Lord's House

So today is something I've been waiting for eagerly, the first chapter of Moses, the first chapter of the first book. You will notice first of all the significance of that date, June 1830. What does 1830 signify to us? The Church was less than three months old at this time. And what does June 1830 mean? The month the Book of Mormon was published. This is as old as the Book of Mormon. That's a very significant thing. If you think the Reverend Solomon Spaulding wrote the Book of Mormon, who wrote the Book of Moses in that case? The Book of Mormon, we say, would take a good deal of revelation to produce. They say, "Oh, well, anybody could do that with a little help." At the same time he produced a very different type of book, which is the Book of Moses-equally marvelous, equally wonderful, equally impossible.

We are told here that it is a series of visions. Notice, the "Visions of Moses" (that's the title) as revealed to the Prophet Joseph in June 1830. And notice the title comes in here. "The words of God, which he spake unto Moses." What a very interesting beginning to have here. This is exactly the Egyptian word for scripture, "the words of God." Here's the symbol for God. It comes first, you see. It's pronounced medu-netcher. That's a staff. Then you have to have a little chickey here to show it's in the plural, give it an "oo" sound. Then you have to have a man with his hand in his mouth to show that he is speaking. Then you have to have three strokes to show that it is plural. You put the word God here, netcher, first for honorific transposition. That indicates God always comes first. Actually, it's read this way, and it means "the words of God." Every sacred Egyptian writing which is in black is in theory the words of God. The red part (the rubric), is the commentary of the scribe, the writer, the copyist-any explanation you want to put in, any stage directions or things like that. You will find this on the first page of Gardiner's Grammar if you want to see how it is to be rendered; namely, the words of God. Singular, not the words of the gods or something, but these are the words of God. They are inspired and revealed, the Egyptians believed.

And then the other part comes in. As we start out here, these aren't "the words of God, which he spake unto Moses at a time when Moses was caught up into an exceedingly high mountain." That's an explanation. The words of God begin, you notice, in the third verse. "Behold, I am the Lord God Almighty." All the rest would simply be in red ink. The Egyptians have this very easily solved. They write in stereo. You see the human element, and you see the divine element and the commentary so that you don't mix them up. It's very convenient to know, and it's very important because it shows us, among other things, that they considered writing a divine gift, a gift from heaven. It's purpose was to preserve the words which were given to the fathers in the beginning by revelation. So that's what we have here, the words of God, and we start reading the red print first. In this first verse we come to the high mountain.

Well, I decided this morning to play (I should have been doing something else) "Trivial Pursuit" with a mountain motif, and it turns out to be not so trivial (a very important thing). First of all, why are mountains important and significant? They have a great significance as we mentioned last time-upthrust of the earth's crust, things that begin. The mountain, of course, is near heaven, but it is also what? Literally near heaven? Well, it's up in the sky. You could say, "Well, that would be superstition." But what can you say that is literally true about a mountain. If you wanted to make contact with holier things, why would the top of an exceedingly high mountain be an advantage? Remember the brother of Jared is caught up into an exceedingly high mountain which is called Shelem because of its exceeding height. But what would be the advantage of being on an exceedingly high mountain? It's isolated and set apart. It's a pure place; it's not frequented often. It's removed, private, inaccessible, undefiled. If any place can be kept holy and undefiled, it would be that, because people don't visit it. They may go up there and leave their beer cans and their lunch remains. But if it's a very high mountain, they don't. They leave their names and flag or something like that to sort of sanctify the place. It's a holy sort of thing.

This is an advantage to it, but then it also has other particular functions. Looking at Timp right now, we find that it beckons to us. It commands attention as a reality. Remember what Sir Hillary and others have said. Why do you climb the mountains? "Because they are there." They are there and they are (to use the much overworked word) a challenge. But they combine these paradoxical elements. The mountain is the most visible and conspicuous thing around and yet it's the most inaccessible. You can see it from a longer distance and from more directions. The mountain is there. It dominates the scene, and yet it is inaccessible. This is a surprising thing.

You know the famous German poem, the dialog about the Jungfrau. The Jungfrau was only climbed quite recently, just in this century. There it was aloof and majestic, and yet everybody could see it. "Have you been climbed yet?" The Eiger and the Jungfrau carry on a conversation. It is most inaccessible yet most visible, so it invites us and calls our attention. You must realize there are such things as a disquieting phenomena. It's unsettling, yet it is thrilling and invites an attempt.

Now Petrarch (1304-1374) was the first great poet of the Renaissance. The Renaissance begins with Petrarch. To show the type of the Renaissance mind (that he was the typical mind, that he had a new point of view on things) he lived near the foot of the Alps and he couldn't resist going up to them. He started climbing them and looking around, and he started being thrilled by the sight. He suddenly realized that he shouldn't do that. To be delighted with worldly things, with nature, was a pagan thing. He checked himself, he says. He had to take out his copy of St. Augustine's Confessions and start reading on the spot lest he be carried away by the beauty of the view. Here is Renaissance man or modern man. He aspires beyond the "letter that killeth," and St. Augustine is supposed to rescue him from that.

But then in the Restoration poetry in England, you had the English garden, and everything had to be controlled, gentle, and civilized-the lawn and all this. Mountains were regarded as savage, hateful, and an abomination. The nature poet Shenstone made himself immortal by calling mountains "earth's dishonor and encumbering load." The idea was that the earth was smooth like a lawn and well-behaved until man sinned. Then because of the sin, the mountains were inflicted on the earth as great boils and bumps and unsightly things. "Earth's dishonor and encumbering load" to punish the earth for the sin when man fell. So they didn't think of it that way.
