It's an interesting thing here, as against the everlasting hills; they didn't believe they were everlasting either. But the mountain is a place of pilgrimage. Remember Isa. 2:2, "And it shall come to pass in the last days, that the mountain of the Lord's house shall be established in the top of the mountains, and shall be exalted above the hills; and all nations shall flow unto it." It's a place of pilgrimage and a place of attraction too. And the temple in Jerusalem was on the temple mountain, on Mt. Moriah. We find all the sacred places are the mountains, and they are very important here as the place of pilgrimage.

The first Christian pilgrims (and pagan for that matter) to go to Palestine to seek the Holy Land were mostly in the fourth century. It started out with a Bordeaux pilgrim, and then a lady called Etheria or Sylvia. She left us a minute account of her travels there. She was a rich Gallic woman, one of these dynamic ones. She was hell on wheels, a terrific old gal. She would sweep through, visit all the holy places, and terrify everybody by her magisterial authority. But the place they went to visit was the top of the Mount of the Ascension. They weren't going to the Holy Sepulcher. The goal of the pilgrim was to climb to the point (some thought it was the Mount of Olives) where the Lord had last left the earth, and there was the Lord's last footprint. There the pilgrim would go and look up to heaven with yearning for the time when the Lord was to come back. It tells us in Acts, Why stand ye looking up into heaven? He'll return in the same way he went up. So they went up and expected him to return. They climbed the mountain to see where the Lord had last been in contact with the human race and where he would make it again. And they did it with great yearning.

This idea of footprints on the mountains is very common. I should have put down here, but I forgot. Remember Adam's mountain in Ceylon (Sri Lanka)? In the center of the island is this vast sort of Monadnock with a footprint on it, the place where Adam landed when he came to this earth. It's the oldest pilgrimage there. Pilgrims come from all over. They cross Adam's bridge, which was formerly a land bridge from India to Ceylon. Then the idea is to climb and pull yourself up the mountain, and they do it. You can still see pictures in the National Geographic with chains, ropes and everything else. Originally, there was a hand rail that rotted away. They pulled themselves up.

There is an account of the iron rod from Jerusalem that led to the temple. That's grasping the iron rod. We're told that there used to be. Remember, before the top of the mountain was leveled off for the temple, it was much higher, and it was risky. When it was rainy and stormy (it was spring when people would have to go), it was hard to get up there, to follow the sacred trail up.

They still do that in Athens, going up to the Acropolis the same way-the sacred trail going up to the top of the mountain. So they had the handrail going up to help people. It was iron, but it rusted away. It was supplanted by wood, brass, and other things. But they always used the expression, for old or weak people or when it was dangerous and slippery, to get to the temple you had to hang onto the railing, "grasp the iron rod." This emerges in the Book of Mormon in a context which Joseph Smith couldn't have discovered or anybody in his time. We have these interesting things. Anyway, we have the mountain of Ceylon and Adam's mountain where Adam came to earth and Adam fell. It's quite a story. The Koran tells us the story when Adam fell. We won't go into that.

What about the guru on his mountain? We see in the cartoons in the New Yorker, etc., a person who climbs a high mountain to get to the guru. There are some studies on that. What would make a guru most at home on a high mountain, very uncomfortable and by himself? As you say, he's alone. He's a holy man in his own place. You have to make an effort to reach him. For one thing, we are talking about real mountains where gurus are. In the Himalayas the oxygen becomes very thin at the ten-thousand-foot elevation. The rule was in the Air Force that you put on your oxygen mask at ten thousand feet. Well, that isn't considered very high anymore. But your breath comes short on Timp before you get to the top there. The idea is that this thin oxygen, fasting, and altitude sickness (that's a very real thing) and exhaustion and cold produce hallucinations. Of course, they do. You get an unworldly feeling. You get carried away by the wind. I know lots of people who do. When we used to have the big hikes up Timp, people would get mountain sickness. They would get giddy and turn pale and have to sit down and pant. This is the best way to have visions, revelations, hallucinations, etc. So the guru sits up there breathing this thin oxygen which isn't enough to keep you going.

Then there's the story of the brother of Jared. This is a very important point in it. Remember, he said he talked with the Lord on an exceedingly high mountain. It was called Shelem because of its exceeding height. The original word of Shelem, Shalom, means "peace," but it originally meant "safe" (safety, security) because it was a high place. The Shelem was a high place. It's still the word for ladder: silma, selma, a sullam in Arabic. It's a very high elevation. The whole third chapter of the Book of Ether is the Lord speaking with Enoch on this high mountain called Shelem because of its exceeding height. He goes up there and moltens out the stones and gets them all ready, but they won't shine. He knows from the story of Noah that they should shine because Noah had the Zohar in his ark. They were two stones that went in the ark of Noah that told him when it was day and night because they would shine. They shine by their own light. The story of the Zohar is a great one. There's a long story about that. We are told in the Book of Ether that they were built after the manner of Noah's ark. So the brother of Jared asks the Lord, What should I do to bring light in the arks because we are going to be dashed under the waves? There were terrible storms from the Ice Age storms, the great torrential vast winds. For 340 days the wind did never cease to blow. Anyway, what would they do? Well, he knew what to do. The Lord said to him, "Well, what would you do? You go figure it out for yourself." So without any further instructions, he went and made these [p.5] molten stones because we are told that Noah had two fine crystals just like that, clear as glass, and they shined in the dark. He was going to do the same thing, but there was one check. The stones wouldn't shine. And so he was humiliated. And then those marvelous passages, the most beautiful description of humility and repentance you can find anywhere in literature, is the way the brother of Jared addresses the Lord. He is absolutely flat. He is on his face. He is completely humiliated, and the things he says there are quite thrilling. But the thing is he climbs the mountain with these stones. He goes back to the top of the mountain, gets as far as he can to the top, then he holds up the stones and says this is where I get off; I can't go any further. The moral is that you do everything you can; then you ask the Lord to take over. That's what he does. It says he went to work. He worked exceedingly hard getting the stones out, melting them, etc. Then he toils up the mountain and gets to the top. Now, Lord, this is as far as I go. You must take over. He holds the stones up, and then the Lord comes down and touches them. It knocks him flat on his face again. All this is very dramatic, remarkable stuff. Joseph Smith at the age of twenty-four was cooking all this up out of nothing. It's quite a thing.

Then we have the Mount of Transfiguration in Matt. 17:2 and Mark 9:2, where the Lord is transfigured on an exceeding high mountain. And again remember, Peter was out of his head (let's build three temples), and he knew not what he was saying. Then we are told in Mark that they didn't know what was happening until they came to themselves. The Lord was shaking their shoulders and saying wake up, it's all over now. Then they woke up. We won't go into that, but again you see it was on the Mountain of the Transfiguration where Moses and Elijah came, and the Father came behind a cloud. Remember, it says he was sheltered by a cloud. Well, a high mountain is a place where you would find a cloud anyway.

But there are other mountains now. That's the one sense of a mountain, that place of contact, the holy place. But more significant even is the jabal, the oreion, etc. The Arabic word for mountain is jabal, and the plural is jibal which means range of mountains. It means borders. The very same thing happens in Greek. To oros is the mountain. Ho oros is the boundary or chain of mountains or range of mountains. It is the natural boundary, the range of mountains. And it's the same thing we have. Remember, Achilles says, I have no quarrel with the Trojans. I didn't want to come on this crazy expedition with you guys because many ranges of shadowy mountains lie between my land and theirs. They're in another world as far as I'm concerned. So we get the idea for the oros. If it's masculine ho oros, then it's a boundary. It's a limit, a boundary line, any kind of a fence or boundary. If it's to oros in the neuter, with a plural oreia, then it's a high mountain. But it's the same word, you notice, as the Hebrew word har. That's the word for mountain. In the Book of Mormon, the judge Pahoran means the "mountain man," an Egyptian name. The Russian word for mountain is gora. It means a range, but it is the limit of your visibility. In Egyptian this is very important because it is the aakhut, the boundary between the two worlds. It is extremely common. The names of so many Pharaohs use it. Akhenaton, the famous reformer, means the "solar disc on the mountain." You will see it all over when you go to the Ramses exhibit.

You will see the aakhut which means "the sun in the horizon." There are the two mountains, and then the sun is rising between them. And it is very important here. I'm going to read you something about it. Paul tells us that faith is "the substance of things hoped for, the evidence of things not seen." Well, this is the justification of faith. In the horizon you have the evidence of things not seen. Solid, tangible evidence (it's really there) of things that really exist that you can't see, and may never see. In fact, you can never see them because they are always beyond the horizon. But they are always there, and they are always real. There's a section here in this book. I don't know whether I should take up time with it, but I think it's quite to the point here. Why the Egyptians stuck to this crazy stuff (it seems to us) is that it was actually very real. These are real experiences with the nomad peoples. I know with the Hopis it's a very important thing to stand up at the second mesa. There are the cliffs of the second mesa just above the corn rocks in the cemetery there. It used to be back in the 1940s and '50s. They don't do it anymore; it's too bad. Things are getting lost after thousands of years. All the men and boys of the village would line up along the edge of the cliff and face the sun to see the first point of sunlight on the horizon. And they would pray to it.

This is from the Joseph Smith papyrus, "That he might enter the horizon along with his father Re (the sun)." "If the essence of cult and festival is transition, the rite of passage, succession of[livesÃ‚Â…[that's quoting a famous work by Mowinckel]." See, the rite de passage, you pass from one state of existence to another." That's what we are interested in more than anything else-transition, the rite of passage, and succession of lives. "Now the great exponent of such meaningful motion is the sun which rises and sets everyday." We quoted Catullus on that. "The sun sets and rises everyday, and it keeps up, and that's an example. But we, unfortunately, can't follow it," he says. The disc is directly observable under normal conditions only on the horizon. That's when you can see it clearly. The Book of the Dead says, "Thou passest over the sky. Every faith watches thee in thy course. Thou thyself are hidden from their gatesÃ‚Â…" except when you show yourself at dawn and at eventide. Then they can look at your disc, and they can see it. It turns red, and you can view the sun. Otherwise, you'd better look through smoked glasses.

This is from Erik Hornung, an Egyptologist and very good authority on this business: "The gate of heaven or the underworld between the earthly and the other-worldly spirits was from the earliest times localized in the aakhut." That is the seam or juncture, the Nahtstelle, the interface between the other world and this world. The sun has been invisible. It has been in the dark until now. And now it's coming into our particular sphere. Then you bid it goodbye. It must go somewhere else, and there must be somewhere else for it to go. So why should we think we have seen everything there is? This is the moral. "Ã‚Â…in the aakhut in the East and West where heaven, earth, and underworld came into contact with each other and where intercourse between the spheres is possible." That's what happens. That's where the touching comes. "In passing the horizon the sun god and his ship both change their natures.Ã‚Â…Thus one begins his journey through the hours of the night by standing on the threshold, the bank or the horizon, which is the door of his house [the middle chamber, the three main rooms, etc.].

The word aakhut can designate either that particular spot on the skyline at which the first or last brilliant point of the sun's surface is visible, these being movable points [everyday they move along, you know] represented by the ancients as a series of gates or windows [you find more about that in the old Book of Enoch than anywhere else] or else it can be the region just below the horizon whose effulgent glory can be seen reflected in our own upper sky at dawn and twilight." Before the sun rises, you can see there's a lot of light. It must be in some place that's very brilliant down there. And after it sets, it goes into the warm, bright chambers of the West. Where has it gone? There must be something very pretty back there. So you start thinking about that.

According to Siegfried Schott in the Pyramid Texts, the inhabitants of the aakhut lived beyond the mountains of the eastern and western horizons, but this side of the underworld itself. They belong to the place of transition. "For those who travel, especially in the desert, the horizon is constant and visible proof of realities that may escape the city dweller and the farmer. The horizon at dusk and dawn is not a fade-out place. It's not a dim inter-region. It's as sharp as a razor." Here you can see it against the mountains. It's not something vague, something that fades out. But it's there and very real. So you want to go up to it and see what's on the other side. Well, there is something there after all. You were right in supposing there was something there, but you've still got a horizon in front of you. You haven't settled it. You haven't seen everything yet. The fact that it moves doesn't mean that it's not real. "It's a hard black line as real and intensely visible as anything could be. But when you pick a mark on the horizon and walk up to it, there is more beyond, another horizon. We discover that the horizon, the absolute limit of our vision (it's a real limit; you can see 14 or 15 miles) beyond which you cannot see, beyond which for us nothing exists, is only a relative thing after all. The absolute limit of our present visible world turns out to be only the threshold of another world beyond. Farmers and city dwellers unaware and suspicious of what lies beyond the safety of the oasis and the city walls are prone to existentialist philosophy and take a skeptical and gloomy[view.Ã‚Â…[There's nothing beyond, in other words. What lies beyond the present life?] But the nomad of the desert [who is always a pilgrim] can always believe there is something beyond because for him there always is something beyond.

One of the peculiar traits of Egyptian culture and belief is surprisingly enough an obsession with the idea of eternal progression. [Another nomad heritage, people that are always wandering.] They have always been drawn to distant horizons and spurned this present world as altogether too confining. The passage from world to world and from horizon to horizon is dramatized in the ordinances of the temple, which itself is called the horizon." It is the place where you experiment with these things. So we get this horizon idea. Incidentally, what if the sun isn't there? This is another interesting thing. The word for mountain without the sun is dju which means "danger, unknown territory, stay away, look out for it." The sun isn't there. The mountain shuts off a land beyond which might not be a very safe land. It might not be very healthy for you to visit. It means a treacherous, threatening, dangerous unknown. It's the common word for evil, dju, a threatening thing.

Now, you all know about pyramids and ziggurats. They are artificial mountains, and mountain rites take place there. They are specifically places of contact with the worlds above and below. The king every New Year's Eve would ascend to the top of the ziggurat in Babylon, and there he would cast the dice with 360 possibilities, one for each day of the year. If he was a Persian, he would draw the baresma. He would tell the fortunes, make the divinations for the year. There was a couch where the king would spend the night with the gods, just that one night, dealing with the matters of the eternities, finding out what the future of his people would be. In fact, there's a good book by G. Widengren on this doctrine among the Jews and the Christians called The Sent One and the Heavenly Book. The king goes up to the top of the mountain, or the chamber at the top of the temple, or the top of the pyramid and comes down with the heavenly book to show that he has been there. So the mountain of the Lord's house is the temple mountain. And the mountain of all is Sinai. That's a very dangerous mountain, as you know. It was divided off by a boundary. They set up a stone wall along the bottom of it, and any ordinary Israelite who stepped one inch beyond that would be struck dead. There was a high wall, and priesthood could go in there. Beyond that, others could go with Moses. He went up with Joshua. But to the top only Moses could go, and there he talked with the Lord. The mountain was covered with flame by night and with smoke by day, obviously a volcano, and it shook violently when Moses went up it (very dangerous). Fire came out, rocks fell down and smashed people. It was an active volcano. It is very clear from the Exodus description of the mountain. But they are also steps to heaven. There are various degrees of purity as you go up. A person who is so pure may go so far. Another may go so far. There are four different steps there. Finally, Moses can reach to the top and talk to the Lord. In this Moses is withdrawn. We are told here that this particular mountain is a sacred one and you're not supposed to know about it. Notice, the last verse of our first chapter here, "unto Moses in the mount the name of which shall not be known among the children of men." This is Moses and the Lord alone.

Then we have Ps. 121, "I will lift up my eyes to the mountains, [we say "the hills;" it's the same word, har, heharim] from whence cometh my help." The Lord is my help," etc. This is an interesting thing. Why would the help come from the mountains? That is explained very well in those wonderful verses of Isaiah which the Prophet Abinadi uses so effectively, Isa. 52:7 . Why does Abinadi repeat it five times in Mosiah 12:21 , 15:15-18? "How beautiful upon the mountains are the feet of them that bear good tidings." Why does Abinadi keep throwing that at the corrupt priests of King Noah? This verse in Isaiah is interesting because it is the only time the word navu (Nauvoo) occurs, ma-navu. That's where we get Manavu Ward. It's the only time it uses the word Nauvoo for beautiful. "How beautiful upon the mountainside are the feet of him who brings good tidings." Literally, are the legs of the runner who brings good news and who causes us to hear that there is peace. And who is a bringer of what is good. It's doubled. "Who brings good tidings of good,Ã‚Â…" it says in our King James Version. It seems redundant, but he wants that to be "brings good tidings of good news and causes us to hear about our salvation, our Savior." Same word as Jesus, Yeshua, (Josiah or Joshua). You can see what the point is here. Abinadi is announcing that from the other side of the mountain, [p.9] from the other world, comes the good news. This is revelation. It's the welcome messenger from the other world, the angel who brings us the gospel-who brings us the message of salvation, who brings us the message of peace which you don't find on this side of the mountain. So that's why he repeats, How welcome are the feet of the person who brings these. How different are the feet of Apollo rushing down to bring vengeance to a wicked people. The famous line from Homer: "And his coming was like the coming of night. He smote them because of their wickedness." But this is why; he's talking about angels coming from the other side. That's what this is, the feet of those on the mountain who bring good tidings. They come across the mountains, and you wait to see if they will come.

The first chapter of Moses is a primeval drama. It's an everyman drama. Another primeval drama that I've never mentioned in this connection before, but certainly should have, is The Prometheus of Aeschylus. It's the same type. This introduces us into a most remarkable setting and remarkable world. You know who Aeschylus is? He's the earliest, but we forget that the great Greek tragedians, along with Aristophanes, all lived at the same time. Everybody lived in one little bunch there. Just one generation produced them, everything we have been living on ever since. Aeschylus lived from 525 (the year the Persians took Egypt) to 456 B.C. He wrote Prometheus Bound, and you notice we have a famous poem in English, Shelley's Prometheus Unbound. The word means "the prophet." Theo means to see ahead of time. He could foresee all things.

The Prometheus Bound is a marvelous drama. It is one of the seven by Aeschylus that survived. It doesn't take place on earth. It has nothing to do with cities or human affairs. There are no human characters in it at all, only one. It's a primeval drama. We find the hero in the beginning, as we find Moses here, on a high mountain. Remember, when the Lord talks to Moses, it's on the high mountain here. We find Prometheus on a high mountain, and he is being crucified because he is the savior of the human race. It's a long story. He tells us here what's going on. This is the third dispensation that he recalls. Way back in the beginning, it started out with Uranus and Gaea, the primal parents, and the starry heavens. There was a Council in Heaven among the Titans. Cronus consulted with his five brother Titans. He decided to overthrow his father and take the rule because he thought he was an important person. So there was the rebellion of Cronus against Uranus. You will notice strange echoes of things in here. They are all mixed up, and they are supposed to be. As a result of that, he is punished. They bind him in Tartarus. But one of the brother Titans is a very clever man, and that's Zeus. With the council and advice of Zeus, he is freed. He is liberated again and takes the throne. He's jealous and doesn't want anyone to succeed him. He eats up all his male children so he won't have any successor on the throne. But his wife Rhea plays a trick on him. He thinks he's eating three of his children, but she has given him three big rocks which he swallows. Thereby, she saves the lives of Zeus, Poseidon, and Pluto. Zeus is saved and Cronus is overthrown, so Zeus now becomes the king. Another succession, another overthrow, another Council in Heaven. How did Zeus do that? He did it with the help of Prometheus, the wise. Prometheus was one of the councilors in the Council in Heaven, and he told him how he could overthrow Cronus and rule.

Zeus overthrew Prometheus. Then they decided on the creation of the world. Zeus had contempt for the human race. They are weak. They are unable to survive. They are unfit to survive. They are poor, miserable creatures who don't deserve to survive. He planned to let them just die out, but Prometheus would have none of that. The great Prometheus made possible their survival by giving them fire so they were able to survive. This infuriated Zeus. In the opening scene Prometheus is being nailed to the rock. Either the chains or he is, but the torture goes along with it because of the famous eagle that preys on his liver as he is tied up there, which is not a comfortable situation. He's going through this torture, and in this position the play opens. It's on the highest and remotest mountain in the world in the top of the Caucasus. It sounds like our Book of Moses, only a very different sort of thing here.

We've been talking about earlier dispensations. The Prometheus drama, you will notice here just from what I've said, is full of scrambled motifs, especially suggesting the Book of Enoch. Where did Aeschylus get all these ideas? For one thing, his gross insult to Zeus? Prometheus says, "I've already seen two tyrants fall (Uranus and Cronus). The third dispensation is going to be the shortest and the evilest. It's going to fall the worst." He predicts that Zeus is going to fall. This is Zeus, the god of the high heavens, the most high god. How can you get away with this in a religious celebration in a sacred drama and put Zeus in that light? This wouldn't have been tolerated if it wasn't a bonafide tradition, of course. He says he has seen two destructions of the wicked and he prophesies a third coming up. This is the least glorious and the most calamitous of all the dispensations so far, and he says it is going to fall too. What's his position going to be? He predicts that a savior is going to come and save the human race. We go into all these motifs. They are all sort of mixed up here.

Aeschylus, who was a very pious man, takes liberties in telling the story. Of course, he does. Yet he needs to convey a true account of man's condition (this is what he is talking about) because traditions have become tangled up and broken up. We recognize them all over the place here. He must necessarily recompose or restructure, put together the elements in a way that makes the best sense to him. He is at perfect liberty to do so. There have been some interesting writings recently on the way the Egyptians did the very same thing. Anything that will convey the story in its most clear way. You get together the scraps and take your turn presenting them. All the great dramatists show great liberty, and yet they deal with absolute established and fixed themes. Everything is prescribed, but if you stay pious and want to explain things, you are justified in putting it in different lights. In other words what we have here is that he recomposes the elements in the most meaningful way to teach us the lesson, and this is what we call a myth. Is a myth bad? Is the Book of Mormon a myth? No it is not. It is history. Are the parables of Jesus myths? Well, these things really could take place. When he is talking about a certain man who did so and so, it's perfectly plausible. People do that all the time. For example, the lost penny. The wife lost a penny and looked desperately all over the house to find it. And the lost sheep. Well, this happens hundreds of times. There's no doubt that such things happen. I'm talking about a particular thing.

When we get to the Book of Mormon or the Pearl of Great Price, it's different from that because it names names and sets dates. It's a different sort of thing. It could have been myth and been quite healthy too. A few years ago they used to talk a lot about mythopoeic thinking. My old Professor Wilson used to talk about that. This was a bright idea in the 40s and 50s-that is thinking that makes up myths to explain the world and all that's in it. Henry Frankfort called it mythopoeic thinking. John Wilson calls it mythmaking mentality. Today no one takes that seriously anymore. As Horning and others have shown us, mythopoeic is not a way of thinking, but simply a way of telling the story. You don't think that way; you tell the story. It's an interesting thing, in the Middle Ages (speaking about these pilgrims) among the relics they went to see were the various things told about in the parables. On display was the original penny that the woman was looking for. They would have all these things like that. There was the inn where the man stayed, and they would show you his bandages and all the rest. They made it very real. It could have been, but it wasn't a way of thinking. It was a way of telling a story. So the mythmaker says, "This is the way I tell the story to make it as clear as possible."

In the Book of Moses we find an astonishing wealth of these mythological motifs, a tremendous wealth. You'd be surprised. I think you can list well over a hundred major myths in there, the details that turned up in the oldest traditions of the race. They are scattered all through there. We find them scattered here and there in various plots and combinations in the mythology of all nations. We have the collection over in the library. It's on reference. You can see it anytime. There are all kinds of myths, and they all deal with the same sort of thing. This is the thing that came out with the patternism of the Cambridge School beginning with The Golden Bough. Sir James Frazer was able to show that you do not find an infinite number of myths and legends and practices throughout the world. You just find a few standard ones. By a few, I mean not an infinite number, but they are always the same. You always run into the same things everywhere else. You always keep saying, "Well, this is where I came in." But in the Book of Moses we find it neatly and consecutively arranged and put together. Here they tell a straight story. This is as near as you will ever get to the original story. There was an original. We remember, as I said before in the case of parables, that all history deals with characteristic and repeated events. All history is a parable. Just as poetry deals with a very small number of set themes. As Dr. Johnson showed, there are only so many plots you can have to a story. There's a limited number of plots possible. Or if you get the new comedy of Plautus and Menander or Menander, Plautus, and Terrence (the Romans take it over from the Greeks) with a set number of characters, you have the number of characters prescribed. With those particular characters, only certain things can take place. You have only a limited number of prescribed plots which have to be followed out. This is the way it is in this life.

But Albright pointed out in The Christian Century, one of the last things he wrote, that Mormonism is the only historical religion in the world today of our time. Originally, he points out, Christianity and Judaism were historical. When they talk about Moses and Abraham and tell the stories of the wandering of Israel, or whatever it might be; when they tell the stories [p.12] of the Lord and the apostles in the Acts, these are histories. These took place in times and events. In the first chapter of Luke, the angel came in the temple on a certain day, stood in a particular place, said certain things to a man. It tells us who his wife was, who his family was, the people waiting outside. He goes and visits Mary. It tells us where she lived-the street, the city, the room. There she was. It's historical. But as we have mentioned again and again so far (wasted the whole semester trying to make that one thing clear), the Christian world, the Jewish world, and the rest of them reject that. Literalism they don't accept. We have to have that here. That's what's so marvelous about the Book of Moses. It's going to be really here. These things really happen. The whole Book of Moses comes across as a message from the archaic world. Some of the chapters, especially six and seven, are written in a totally different language. It's a poetry you will not find in the Book of Mormon or Doctrine and Covenants or anywhere else. It's nature poetry, some gorgeously beautiful passages, but it's very characteristic of the archaic world. This peculiar idiom, quite different from that of the Book of Mormon, came from the press in the same month as the Book of Mormon. These passages are rich in metaphor. Here you are going to have metaphors. "Why do the heavens weep and send forth their tears as rain upon the mountains?" There's a nice balance to them, beautiful passages there. "The voice of the beasts was heard out of the wilderness. The rivers were turned from their coursesÃ‚Â…." There is an extravagance about it, a lushness about it, that goes back to days that now we know correspond to days of geological extravagance. In the early days when I went to school, things didn't happen that way. They happened slowly. Nothing was picturesque; nothing could happen fast. Nothing could happen dramatically. Well, if there is anything I learned about the war, it's that it was unbelievably dramatic and picturesque. Hollywood can't even begin to approach what really happened, so it's an astonishing thing.

The first chapter is structured as a drama, and a good one. This is the simplest, directest way of carrying us back in time and placing us in a situation, a strange and distant situation, which would be very hard to grasp if it weren't placed in this most elementary and simple form. It is given to us as if it were a play. Moses' commentaries on the words of God together make a perfect presentation of the primary theme of the drama. It's the primary theme of drama, theology, philosophy, and science; namely, man's condition in the world. What is the human condition? And that is what we have here. There are other plays like it; the most famous are mystery plays or the old English play Everyman. The time is up now. We will begin with the first chapter of Moses next time, unless I think of something else to interrupt. This goes on indefinitely, doesn't it? But there are many things here. That's why Sunday School lessons are so maddening-22, 25, 34 chapters to a 40-minute lesson! [p.1]

Lecture 17

The Heavenly Prologue

Well, now the subject is the first chapter of Moses, verses 2-8. And that is the Prologue in Heaven. Very important. Now here we have a surprising phenomenon. You notice in Moses 2 and 3 what it is. Moses is in the presence of God and he is being given his assignment. And we read in the first verse, however, that he had been caught up. So this is an ascension. There is a great branch of ancient literature that is called the ascension literature that has emerged recently. And we have lots of ascensions here. Some of the most interesting ascensions are in the Book of Mormon. Of course, 1 Ne. 1:11 and following. There is a very important ascension there, where he tells us. What is it? Is it heaven or earth or what? It is an exceeding high mountain and all the rest. It's right in the first chapter, right at the beginning of Nephi, because in the beginning we have a Prologue in Heaven, but in verse 11, theyÃ‚Â…I've got the wrong one here. Well let's look at 2 Ne. 4:25 . That's another ascension. He says the same thing there so it's all right, I guess. I'm just making too many mistakes these days (these days indeed).

"And upon the wings of his spirit hath my body been carried away upon exceeding high mountains. And mine eyes have beheld great things, even too great for man; therefore I was bidden that I should not write them." (Oh, I see what I did. I put the dots in the wrong place.) It's 11:1, not 1:11 because the first chapter does have a real Prologue in Heaven, but if you look at the eleventh chapter of 1 Nephi in the first verse. "For it came to pass after I had desired to know these things that my father had seen [he had been carried away, too], and believing that the Lord was able to make them known to me, as I sat pondering in my heart I was caught away [using the same expression here used for Moses] in the Spirit of the Lord, yea, into an exceeding high mountain [well, did he imagine he saw it? Or did the spirit actually carry him? Notice in 2 Nephi, he said his body was carried up] which I had never before seen, and upon which I never had before set my foot. And the Spirit said unto me: Behold, what desirest thou?" (verse 2 ). So here is another ascension. It's not a transformation; it's an ascension. We talked about the mountain the last time.

Well, there are some other Prologues in Heaven, and they are important ones. The most famous one, of course, is in Job. I should have brought an Old Testament along. Job was first to be recognized as a drama. The first chapter of Moses is a drama here, and Job was recognized long ago as a drama. The use of the waw-conversive gives it away absolutely as a drama because of the stage directions in it. But the theme is Everyman. It starts out, "There was a man in the land of Uz, and his name was Job." It doesn't use a waw-conversive, very interesting. This is the narrative part; we haven't gotten to the drama part. This is just the introduction. "He was perfect and upright, tam wayashar. He feared the gods, and he kept away from sin." But notice that he is the man. He is the archetypal man. It starts, "There was a man in the land of Uz, and he was the perfect, tam, man." He was representative. "He was upright. He walked straight in all things, fearing God and avoiding evil." So he's our character, and he has to go through all this suffering, as we all know from Job. So with this is archetypal man, beginning, in the sixth verse of the first chapter, already you have the Prologue in Heaven, where all the heavenly hosts are come together, and Satan comes among them with his challenge to God and says, "Have you seen my friend Job?" You know what the situation is. Satan comes before the Lord and he challenges him. The Lord says, Have you seen anyone like my servant Job? And Satan says, Yes, but he hasn't been really tried. You put him to a real trial and he'll turn against you. He'll denounce you (or renounce you). And the Lord says, No, all right, we'll give it a try. "We will allow Satan, our common enemy, to try man and to tempt him." So the Lord says, I will allow you to try and tempt him all the way, just so you spare his life, that's all.

Well, then you know what Job has to go through. And his friends come and comfort him in dramatic speeches. Incidentally, notice this is not a Bible text. Job is not an Israelite; he is a man of the East. And the three friends that come to him are Eliphaz the Temanite, Bildad the Shuhite, and Zophar the Naamathite. Eliphaz is very interesting because the name is significant; it means "a soft liver." The Temanite means "of the south country, the pleasant country." It's used in the Songs of Solomon about the gentle winds that blow from the Teman. Bildad (balada, an Arabic root) means "far away, the man from a distance." Shuhite means "dwells in the lowlands, in the low valleys, in the low plains." And then there is Zophar, the Naamathite. Zophar means "vain, twittering, superficial." See, they all come to advise him and tell him what he should do. They are safe; they have it easy. They're easy livers, and notice these are tribes not mentioned in the Bible anywhere. They come from distant places. We're dealing with another culture now that is very old. And the Naamathite, naam, is "pleasant and beautiful, easy, soft going," and all that. So he has his easy-living friends, you might say, come to comfort him, and he gives them these various speeches. But they come from a different cycle, and this is explained later in the Book of Moses where these things come from.

The most famous of all of these dramas, however, is Faust, the most famous drama of modern times. From the discovery of manuscripts recently, we learn that Goethe started to write it before 1775. Nobody knows when. He was born in '49, right in the middle of the century. So he was quite young when he began it, and he didn't finish the second Faust until 1831. The second part was to be published after he was dead. How many years was that after the Church was founded? So Joseph Smith couldn't very well have used it, could he? Because it's so very close to this passage of Moses, I call attention to it here. It begins with the Prologue in Heaven. (I forgot the text. At least I remembered some of it; I'm glad of that. That would have slowed us down anyway. We have to get along here because of the time.) It begins with the Prologue in Heaven and the music of the spheres. It begins with a multiplicity of worlds, with the plurality of worlds here. It starts out: The endless old-age cycling of the spheres in which the sun has just completed a cycle. (Notice, it is the beginning of all things. It takes us back to the [p.3] cosmos; its cosmic.) In immemorial fashion the sun again sounds as it reaches a certain point. (It happens to be Easter. It happens to be the spring equinox in the play.) In cooperative rivalry with the brother spheres, it revolves with all the other spheres of the universe, and in this course it reaches its beginning. In its predescribed cycle, it comes back again to where it started and completes it with a roar of thunder. Then when it comes there, it brings renewed strength to everything. The sight of it brings strength to the angels, to all the forces. (Though it is unfathomable what it is, what the force and power is.) The incomprehensibly exalted creations are glorious as they were on the day of creation. We're right back to the new year again. So here's your old cosmic cycle which you're going to get throughout the literature. But it's not just that. You go back to the oldest monuments of the human race. It's an absolute obsession with the Egyptians. And this is a new development in Egyptian studies, this idea of the constant return as an earnest of our own resurrection-because all nature is renewed. So this is the opening chorus. And there is a Council in Heaven. And in reverse order, in order of their importance, the angels speak. First Raphael, then Gabriel, and then Michael. They all speak, each praising the glories of nature and the creation and the Lord. And then the Lord speaks. It's a hymn of creation. There's the angel chorus. They resume again. Then they all three sing together with a slight variation. The angels sing again the chorus of creation. Walter Otto was the great collector and coordinator of Classic literature. At the end of all his work, he wrote a book called The Muses, (which I think we have here) in which he shows what the word "poem" comes from. Poem is the creation song; it's the song that was sung on the day of creation. Poiema means "creation," really. Poieo is "to make," and poiema is the act of creating. When the creation was completed, then what happened? Well we get that from Job, again. The angels sang together, and all the sons of God shouted for joy at the sight of the creation of the new world that was created and was going forth. So here we have the creation song, and it's being sung here in Goethe's play. To start out, it is being sung by the angels here. "It has returned." And this poem, of course, is a chorus. The Greek play has a chorus. Well what does chorus mean? It's a ring dance; it's a circle. Same as our word curve; Latin: curvus; going around. The chorus sings, and the chorus of the muses sings the poiema, the creation song. Remember, the blind muses. Each one is in charge of describing and studying one department of the creation. So they all get together. When they sing together, it's the poiema, the song of the creation. It's a glorious thing. It's a round dance like the Egyptian maypole. And it's the music of the spheres and those things we have heard about in literature.

Well, then what happens? Immediately Satan enters in this one here, as in Job. His name is Mephistopheles. This indicates his role and assignment. Later when he appears to Faust in the chamber, Faust asks him what his assignment is. He says his only task is to destroy, to tear down. He must react. He can't act; he can only react. His business is entirely negative. And somebody has to have that role. It's an assignment, he says. He gives it as that because the word Mephistopheles, mephis, means "spite, evil, rancor." He wants to do as badly as he can. He's out to make mischief, but opheles, opheleo means "to do good, to help somebody, to help the plan along, to assist in things." So he plays a necessary role. Without him they are not [p.4] going to have any play or anything else. So Mephistopheles is the one, as he says, who always wants to do evil and only succeeds in doing good. Of course, it drives him wild. He can't win. I always compare him to the Road Runner and the Coyote. He has these fiendish, clever devices that always backfire. And so it is with Satan. We get this marvelous passage from the fourth chapter of Moses. It says, "Satan sought to destroy the world for he knew not the mind of God [Moses 4:6 ]." That, I think, is the most encouraging verse in all the scriptures. Satan seems to be getting the upper hand all the time, but he doesn't know the mind of God. There are a lot of things he doesn't know. That's reassuring because he seems to ruling things, like that verse by Clarence Day. "Might and right are always fighting, In our youth it seems exciting, Right is always nearly winning, Might can hardly keep from grinning." Because might always wins in the end on this earth.

But Mephistopheles comes to report since God has been asking for reports. The angels come and report. He doesn't think so much of the way things are going on the earth. He says, "As in olden times, we used to be friends." This refers to the time when he was Lucifer. He says they met under friendlier circumstances, "but here I am again to report anyway, whether you want it or not." He's rather defiant here. He cannot go out and visit other worlds. He has been cast down and confined to this world where "little man is the little god, and thinks he rules things." He says man would be better off if he didn't have a memory of his divine origin because it despoils him. He starts pushing people off the sidewalks; he thinks he has it made, etc. "Man is rational," says Goethe, "but he acts like an animal." So this is the challenge to God: Is the creation a success? Is the plan for the human race working out at all, or is it a terrible bungle? What do you say to this? With Goethe the main purpose of the play is to banish religion from the scene. He doesn't want that in it at all. He was a marvelous man. When he was very young, he saw through philosophy, wouldn't have anything to do with that. He wanted to get down to the most solid information he could. He went into all branches of the sciences. He was a universal mind, one of the great thinkers of all time. But he sums it up in this play that he worked on for so many years. He had worked on this play so much. This brings his thought together. During his middle years when he was with Herder, it was a very literary period. Then Herder acquainted him with Schiller and they became friends. And then the Weimar period when he came so close to Shakespeare and to Homer and the Greeks, etc. He brings all these elements in. He is aware of all these things, and he sees that they fit in somewhere.

The Lord says to Satan here, "You're always the accuser, aren't you? Can't you ever say anything good about anybody? You never do." Satan is the accuser, and what is the Greek word for accuser? It's diabolos, "devil." Diaboleo is "to accuse somebody." As the Talmud says, he never accuses falsely. When he accuses, he accuses rightly; but that doesn't make him saintly. People do wrong, but you have no business being the accuser. Remember, Satan is the accuser of his fellow man. Four things we must never do. One of them is accuse. Never accuse because that will block you off from the ways of repentance which you need. Diabolos is, of course, our word for the devil. You from the Bay region know about Mount Diablo.

The diabolos is the devil, the accuser. Remember in the scriptures he is called "the accuser of his brethren." And Adam in replying to him says, "The Lord judge thee. I will not bring a ringing accusation against thee." As Brigham Young says, I do not accuse Satan. He is doing his job manfully. He's filling his mission as he is supposed to do. I'm not going to accuse him. I'm going to accuse myself when I fall short. He is there as the accuser. His business is to foul things up. He says to the Lord, things aren't going well on earth. The human race is not doing too well. And he challenges God to a wager that he can tempt man and make him fall, just as in Job. The Lord says, All right, he's in your hands again. "We will allow our common enemy to tempt man." And then the test begins and the opening scene. We'll have to get back to Moses to see how closely this follows now. But first we might as well see where this leads us.

That's the prologue. This is in heaven. It's brilliant; its light. Then the curtain rises (I hope you've read the first chapter) and it's dark. The whole first act is called Nacht; it's night. He finds himself in a dark chamber, in a dark tower at midnight. Nothing could be darker, and his spirit is absolutely black. He has reached the end of his rope. He says there's no joy in it. The whole thing is a farce. He says, "I've studied things. I've learned everything, and where does it get me? I'm no more clever than I ever was, no sharper than I ever was. After all that study, where has it got me? [Then he reflects on his way of life.] You call that living. No dog would live longer like this. I'm not going to put up with it anymore." And he sees nothing but hard times ahead. He's completely disillusioned. Also he's very poor, and he's ignored though he's the most famous man in the world, Dr. Faustus. There was a real Dr. Faustus in the sixteenth century. Some attribute the invention of printing to him. Only a magician could have done that.

There's Marlowe's Dr. Faustus with the same theme of redemption and the like. If you've read Marlowe's Faustus, "See how Christ's blood streams through the firmament; one drop of it will save me. Oh my Christ." He goes through the same thing that this Faust goes through. He turns to his hermetic books. He's going to apply to magic just to satisfy himself. He sees no future in anything. The world doesn't interest him. He's lost all that: I don't have to savor sour sweat, things that I don't know; that I might see all truth and the sources of all things, and not just fool around with terminology for the rest of my life the way they do in the schools. That's what happened to the young Clement in the schools. He said he went to all the doctors, and all they did was talk about words and argue with each other in terms of terminology. So that's the world, and it's all so silly. From a hermetic book he summons up a spirit. In Marlowe's Faust, it isn't Satan that guides him through. It's a familiar spirit with whom Satan puts him in touch. The same sort of thing happens here, but he decides on suicide. He's going to end it. He uses the hermetic book to call up the spirit, but "no dice." It's all the more discouraging, and he decides on suicide. Just as he puts the poison to his lips, the dawn comes and he hears an Easter chorus. It's Easter, the spring equinox. It's the return of the sun as the opening chorus said. The sun has come back again, and it's new life. He says, "I'm back on earth again." He's going to give it another try. It seems that the Easter chorus sings (wouldn't you know it) of the [p.6] resurrection. But again that's one thing he can't take. That's one thing the doctors of the church couldn't take. St. Augustine said an afterlife, yes; but resurrection, that's absurd (St. Augustine mind you).

"Resurrection," he says, "for those who pass the test." Then in that famous line he says, "Well, I get the message. It's marvelous, this marvelous Easter message." In Europe, especially in Germany, Easter is the big thing. It's the great festival of the year. "I hear the message, but I just can't believe it [that's all that's wrong with it]." So Faust takes an Easter walk and is followed home by a black poodle which follows him into the room and becomes a rather sinister object. This may go back to Andrew of Constantinople. They used to use poodles for divination. Andrew was famous in the tenth century for having a talking dog. And that electrified the world then, Andrew's talking dog.

Then he tries more conjuring, and Satan appears. He tells him his name and his office-that he is the destroyer. He does not act but he reacts and he is always against something. Then Faust says, "What's the deal?" And Mephisto says, "I am going to make a deal with you. I serve you here. You serve me there." Now that's the famous pact with the devil. That comes later on in our Book of Moses. It's very vividly set forth, better than anywhere else. Faust accepts. Moses does not accept, as you know. So we are back to the Prologue, and we get to Moses' calling here. And again, let me refer you to another great Prologue that begins a story; namely, the opening episode of the Book of Mormon is the Prologue in Heaven. We sometimes overlook that. In the first chapter we begin with the eighth verse. The first part is the Colophon, Nephi summing up his situation and the situation of the world at that time. Then he gets to his story and his own family, about his father. In verse seven he returns to his own house at Jerusalem and things begin. Now things start moving. This is the revelation to Lehi's dispensation. "And being thus overcome with the Spirit, he was carried away in a vision [he gets carried away; here's an ascension again] even that he saw the heavens open, [he sees the heavens open, and this is the ascension to heaven] and he thought he saw God sitting upon his throneÃ‚Â…." There is the theme. The heavenly hosts are assembled around God on his throne. But you notice that it's put as a vision (he thought he saw God). It was as if he experienced it because he is going way back in time. This is the Council in Heaven at the foundation of the earth you are beginning to see.

Let's not forget the Shabako Stone there. Let's not forget the Enuma Elish, but especially the Memphite Theology where the oldest record of the human race tells us about this Council in Heaven. And here we have it again. "Ã‚Â…he thought he saw God sitting upon his throne surrounded with numerous concourses of angelsÃ‚Â…" A concourse is a circle. Of course "numerous concourses" means circles within circles and reminds you of dancing. And what were they doing? Surrounded means "all around." It's the angel chorus "Ã‚Â…in the attitude of singing and praising their God." It was a choral dance. "And it came to pass that he saw one descending out of the midst of heaven, and he beheld that his luster was above that of the sun at noon-day."

Now he saw the various spirits assigned to their work on earth, and they are descending on their various missions. He sees the Twelve come down and the Lord. Now this was all planned at that time. This hadn't happened yet and this is what makes Nephi rejoice. He sees that it is going to be all right. He sees the Plan of Salvation. "And he also saw twelve others following him, and their brightness did exceed that of the stars in the firmament."

Notice the cosmic element here, too: the stars of the firmament and all of the rest of it. "And they came down and went forth upon the face of the earth; and the first came and stood before my father and gave unto him a book,Ã‚Â…" As in John, The Revelation of John. The giving of the book, the bitter book that you eat; the eating of the book, the book that tastes like honey or the book that tastes like wormwood. The eating of the book is a very important element. In fact, G. Widengren, the Swede, has written a book on that subject (a book about a book). This book is important. "Ã‚Â…and bade him that he should read. And it came to pass that he did read and was filled with the Spirit of the Lord." Notice, what is this imagery? What world are we in? First he told him to eat the book, and didn't eat it but he read it. But he is filled with the spirit of the Lord. "And he read, saying: Wo, wo, unto Jerusalem, for I have seen thine abominations!Ã‚Â…that it should be destroyed and the inhabitants thereof;Ã‚Â…when my father had read and seen many great and marvelous things, he did exclaim many things unto the Lord; such as: [typical Egyptian phrase: such as to say the following] Great and marvelous are thy works, [Now this sounds like the Creation Hymn, doesn't it? "Thy incomprehensible, exalted works are glorious as on the day of creation." He says it here.] O Lord, God Almighty! Thy throne is high in the heavens and thy power and goodness and mercy are over all the inhabitants of the earth; and because thou art merciful, thou wilt not suffer those who come unto thee to perish! And after this manner was the language of my father in the praising of his God;Ã‚Â…" Notice, he says I'm just giving you my own version: "after this manner." This is not a direct quote. This is the sort of thing. We're dealing with types and images, and you can shift them around. This is formulaic literature, which is perfectly fair because these things will lead no one astray. "Ã‚Â…for his soul did rejoice, and his whole heart was filled, because of the things which he had seen, yea, which the Lord had shown unto him. And now I, Nephi, do not make a full account of the things which my father hath writtenÃ‚Â…" So here we have the Prologue in Heaven again beginning the great Book of Mormon. And we fit it in the plan. We have mentioned the third chapter of Abraham before. That is the Council in Heaven very clearly, from verse 22 to verse 28 (a pretty long account of it). Abr. 3:22 "Now the Lord had shown unto me, Abraham, the intelligences that were organized before the world was; and among all these there were many of the noble and great ones."

Oh, I remember another important one. In the Odyssey there is a marvelous one. The Odyssey begins with the Council in Heaven. It gives a brief account. As Job begins, "This is the upright and perfect man," the Odyssey begins, "Tell me, muse, about the man." The first word is "the man" who had to suffer so much and go through so much seeking to save his soul and to bring his fellowmen through as best he could, because he was a magnanimous spirit.

But why couldn't he do it and what went wrong? Well, I tell you. So it begins already at about the 16th or 18th line (right at the beginning of the Odyssey), where it says that the most high God, the Father of gods and men, called an assembly of all the gods in heaven to meet in the palace at Olympus in the high halls above. And they all came, except Poseidon. He takes the role of Satan. He holds back. Incidentally, the word Shaytan (Satan) means "the one who holds back, the one who lies in ambush." Shatana means "to hold back;" Shaytan is a person who lies in ambush, who holds back, who will not cooperate. And Poseidon didn't come.

Another famous one, right out of Faust, the most famous epic of the Middle Ages, was Reynard the Fox: And it was at the spring equinox, when everything was green. The bush and the trees were covered with the first green leaves. It was a marvelous time. Noble, the king, had sent his criers out to summon all the animals to his presence (to celebrate the creation) that they would come at his command. All the animals great and small, came, except Reynard the Fox. He had done so many wicked things that he didn't dare appear before the king at court (but he does appear).

And then we have others. There is the marvelous one in the Lokasenna. That's one of the great eddas where the courts all meet and Loki comes. Loki is Satan. He comes and defies God and all the rest of the gods with his refusal to cooperate. So it's a very common image that you will get throughout the world. Notice that Reynard the Fox is both sly and curious, and yet he's very intelligent and very clever. And like Loki, he is dangerous. And so we have this in the Odyssey. The opening speech is that of Zeus himself. God, the father of men, spoke, "Oh how men blame the gods for all their misfortunes and all that comes upon them because it's only their own foolishness, their own pride and their own stubbornness and refusal to repent that brings all their troubles on them. They suffer more than they need to suffer." Down on earth meantime Odysseus is being held back in the cave by a very charming enchantress, Calypso. That is a marvelous story about his being held back there. Well, anyway this is a universal theme.

Question: Is there an etymological connection between Shaytan and Poseidon? Poteidon is a very interesting word that has been analyzed by Curtius and others. That's the old way with a t instead of an s (just like in Attic you put a t, where in the other Greek dialects you put an s). So it's Poteidon, the potos of the underworld. He's the gaieokhon, the earthshaker. It means the possessor of the underworld, the earthshaker. And he comes in here. When Satan departs from Moses, he lets out a great cry and the earth shakes. Well, Poseidon's regular epithet in Homer is gaieokhos, which means the "possessor of the earth-he who rules this earth." But it is nearly always translated (probably wrongly) as earthshaker. He's both the earth shaker and the earth holder. If he holds it, he shakes it. So it goes back to the common thing, that he is of this earth, under the earth. And, of course, he is Plutos and he is wealth, too. This comes in.

Let's see this marvelous section here. We're leaving the good part out. D&C 1-9 is the Prologue, and it is most wonderful, just like the Council in Heaven. This is where we fit in.

This is the plan. Abraham: 3:23 "God saw these souls that they were good, and he stood in the midst of them [He's surrounded on all sides] and he said: These I will make my rulers; for he stood among those that were spirits, and he saw that they were good; and he said unto me: Abraham thou art one of them; thou wast chosen before thou wast born. And there stood one among them that was like unto God, and he said unto those who were with him: We will go down,Ã‚Â…" They are discussing the plan. And what happens? Satan steps up and makes his challenge here. "And the Lord said: Whom shall I send? And one answered like unto the Son of Man: Here am I, send me. And another answered and said: Here am I send me. And the Lord said: I will send the first. And the second was angry, and kept not his first estate, and, at that day, many followed after him." So here we have the revolt again, just as we had Horus and Seth in the presence of Ptah in the beginning of the Memphis Shabako text. So here we go. The Lord speaks to Moses and he gives him his name, which is Endless. All we know God by is epithets, things describing him. The Koran says there are 95 names of God. They are not names; they are epithets. Every name is an epithet. It's something that describes a character. If you are Rufus, it means you are red headed. If you are Curtis, it means you are short, etc. There's nobody here whose name doesn't actually mean something. We should go down the roll.

In Moses 1:3 God says, "My name is Endless." Notice it is capitalized in our books, and it should be. If you ask the rabbis what is the ultimate name you can call Him by, it's still an epithet, but it is En Sof. It means "who has no end." The Sof is the limit, the boundary. But there is no boundary, he tells us. Either in space or in time he is unlimited, so is that not endless? So he is the En Sof. "Endless is my name." Again, the importance of the name; we saw that. "There is no end to my works or my words." The works and words come out in all of these things. They must go together because the works mean nothing unless they are communicated, unless they are shared. "Ã‚Â…for I am without beginning of days or end of years;Ã‚Â…" Notice, that is time. Right across in verse six it says: "Ã‚Â…there is no God beside me and all things are present with me,Ã‚Â…" That means present in place and present in time. If I can be there, it's present; if it's right now, it's present. Remember we use the very same terms for time as we do for space. We don't use absent the same way, but the present is whatever is here. You can never make the statement "I am not here." And you can never make the statement "It is not now." Both statements are always false because you are always here and you are always now. Nobody else is here, and no other time is now when you perceive it. Strange paradoxes, aren't they? He says, "Ã‚Â…I am the Lord God Almighty, and Endless is my name, for I am without beginning of days or end of years; and is not this endless?" Meaning no limits. Now here comes the second one. He has introduced himself. In ancient literature, when a god describes himself, it's called an aretology. And this is a typical aretology. When someone else describes him and praises him, that's an aclamatio. That's an acclamation or a laus. Laudis is praises. Arete is "virtue, quality." The high qualities and virtues of a person are arete. Aretology is proclaiming your particular office, calling, function, etc. The most famous of them all is the "I am Isis" from the Egyptians, which the Greeks took over. "I am Isis," and then she describes herself.

This is an aretology. He says, "Ã‚Â…I am the Lord God Almighty, and Endless is my name; for I am without beginning of days or end of years;Ã‚Â…" And then he tells Moses how he is related to Him (why I am bothering to tell you this, why I am bothering to talk to you?). Remember, the brother of Jared says, "He talked to me in all humility as one man to another." Now that's what humility is. It is not bowing the knee in the presence of overwhelming superiority, power or glory. Anybody can do that. But it is recognizing that other creatures, small, very unimportant creatures, are just as good as you are and are on an equal basis with you. That's what humility is. You are listening and you put yourself on the level with them. So the brother of Jared, says, "He talked with me as one man to another in all humility." It was humility for the Lord, and it was humility for the brother of Jared, too. You notice how he was smitten down when he held up the rocks.

Then the Lord says to Moses, "And behold, thou art my son;Ã‚Â…" After the aretology, He says, this is where you come in: you are my son. In the Gospel of Philip it says an astonishing thing. It says, do you want to know what God is? A horse begets a horse, a dog begets a dog, a bull begets a bull. What does a god beget, if a god has a son? What can it possibly be except a god? This is the problem here. If you are the son of such a being, there is a lot of explaining to do here. And, therefore, you're qualified and I'm going to show you a few things. "Ã‚Â…look and I will show thee the workmanship of mine hands; but not all, for my works are without end, [There is your En Sof again. His works are without end. His presence is without end. His knowledge is without end. Everything about him is without end.] and also my words. [Remember that the works and the words always go together. The words are to communicate and the works are what's done, but it doesn't happen in a vacuum.] for they never cease [they go on and on-spaceless, timeless]. Wherefore no man can behold all my works, except he behold all my glory;Ã‚Â…" So his works are his glory to behold. Remember that he says, "This is my work and my glory." My work is my glory. That is what it is: to share this with others to bring about the immortality of man. "Wherefore no man can behold all my works, except he behold all my glory; and no man can behold all my glory, and afterwards remain in the flesh on the earth." Then: Why am I telling you this? Because I have work for you to do. I am going to give you an assignment. The Prophet Joseph says every one of us received an assignment before he came here. You'd better find out what it is and do it. "And I have a work for thee, Moses, my son; and thou art in the similitude of mine Only Begotten; [Now not only is he His son, but in the similitude and the likeness as the Son is in the similitude of the Father. He is in the likeness of the Only Begotten.] and mine Only Begotten is and shall be the Savior for he is full of grace and truth. [That is very interesting because He refers to the Book of Ether 3:8-9 . We won't go into that.] but there is no God beside me, Ã‚Â…" (verse 6). It tells us later on in the Book of Moses that he is commanded to "call upon God in the name of the Son forevermore." He says to call upon God and God only, but always in the name of the Son. He is full of grace and truth, and of course those are the qualities. How do you define grace? That's charis; that's everything good. Charis is your attitude toward others, toward everything else. It is an attitude of complete love. It is related to our word cheer and the Latin, gratia which means "thanks, grace," etc. We just described Mephistopheles as all negative, all willing [p.11] to destroy. This is the absolute opposite of that. There is no shadow of that in it at all. Full of grace and truth-nothing false, nothing deceiving, nothing phony. Whereas Satan, on the other hand, is the one who loves and makes a lie. He was a liar in the beginning. He is the deceiver as well as Apollyon, "the Destroyer." He works by trickery and by lies. He is called "the father of lies." And this is the opposite. The Lord is full of grace and truth. So to what point do we repent in this life, or any other life? Repent until you are full of grace and truth. So I think we all need some repentance. Don't call upon anyone else to repent until you have repented. That will be when you are full of grace and truth. That's a long ways off for some people. We can't even conceive of what that would be like, but that is the object. That's what we are after.

I'm going to limit you to this one thing because I have one assignment, the Lord says. We focus on the thing we are supposed to do. "Ã‚Â…for thou art in the world, and now I show it unto thee." I will show you the world you are in. We mentioned this before, the anthropic principle. Where you are determines the way everything will appear to you and be to you because you have to act from that point. You don't see everything. You don't move in all directions, and you don't know what's behind you at this very moment. "And it came to pass that Moses looked, and beheld the world upon which he was created, and Moses beheld the world and the ends thereof, and all the children of men which are, and which were created; of the same he greatly marveled and wondered" (verse 8). That's the end of the Prologue. Then the lights go down and the stage is darkened. As in Faust and as in Job we see our hero. In the Odyssey he is in a cave. Odysseus is imprisoned in a crystal cave under the ground by Calypso. Her name means the one who hides you, the hidden one, the dark one, the witch. So we are getting these heroes all in the same position. As soon as we leave the Prologue in Heaven, we find them in the dark. The presence of God withdrew from Moses, and his glory was not on Moses. The lights go down. As he was left unto himself, he fell to the earth. Remember, Joseph Smith says, "I found myself lying on my back. It was some time before I could regain my strength." It's the same thing here. "And it came to pass that it was for the space of many hours before Moses did again receive his natural strength like unto man;Ã‚Â…" So as the play opens we have Moses lying there in the dark and dreary world, all alone and out cold, the picture of helplessness; he has reached the bottom. You can't go lower than this. Well, this is a fine way to begin your career on earth, a fine way to launch. How auspicious a beginning is this? As he begins to receive his natural strength, he pulls himself together and he says to himself this great truth, "Now, for this cause I know that man is nothing, which thing I never had supposed." "Oh, human race how I esteem you as nothing," as the Greek chorus says. "How I estimate you as equal to exactly nothing," it says (I'll have to look it up. I can't remember the play). The Greek tragedy is man's condition on earth, what's going on, how he gets himself into it, and how he gets out of it. But Moses says this, "Now, for this cause I know that man is nothing, which thing I never had supposed" (verse 10). He has seen what is up there, and he has seen what is down here. Man is on earth where he was created.

Incidentally, in Oedipus Rex you get that. When Tiresius, the seer, comes on the stage, the first thing he does, he looks. He is blind but he faces the Athenian audience and says, "All of you [p.12] know nothing, so don't fool yourself." Nobody will understand Tiresius since he is a seer and a prophet.

But here is the contrast. If he is nothing, then how about being so close to God. "But now mine own eyes have beheld God; but not my natural, but my spiritual eyes, for my natural eyes could not have beheld;Ã‚Â…" Are we trying to escape the issue here by calling it spiritual? No, the spiritual packs a greater energy than the physical. Spirit is a form of energy, just as faith, love and joy are forms of energy. It's a real and substantial thing. The spirit is one of the real forces. We'll say a form of energy and let it go at that. "Ã‚Â…for I should have withered and died in his presence; but his glory was upon me; and I beheld his face, for I was transfigured before him." He was carried up and he was in the presence of God. He was on another level, in another energy zone, and he could do it. As he says here, "Ã‚Â…not my natural, but my spiritual eyes, for my natural eyes could not have beheld;Ã‚Â…I was transfigured before him" (verse 11). That's the transfiguration on the mountain.

That's the end of that act. A new scene is when a new character enters. Now, the play begins because you have to have an antagonist and a protagonist in a play. Now Satan enters the scene. Notice, when the hero is at his lowest, when he is the most helpless, that is the time that Satan strikes (when he gets him at the greatest possible disadvantage). Satan does not play fair. He going to make all the disruption possible. He can unbalance you. He can destabilize governments and things like that. He likes to take advantage and destroy and knock down. Nothing is to be gained by that anywhere, even though it's the wicked you are fighting. But here, "Ã‚Â…when Moses had said these words, Satan came tempting him saying: Moses, son of man, worship me."

Here he takes advantage of him in his weakness. This happens. Faust is in the dark ready to commit suicide, and Satan appears to him there. It's the same thing with Job. The tempters, his companions, come to him then. But remember, it is Satan who is exerting his power over Job. The Lord said to Satan in the beginning, I've put him in your hand, but spare his life. So Satan is doing it. This is a stock drama. Since it starts out by ish and andra, (aner), "the man," that means everybody. We are to think of ourselves respectively as that. The man is Adam; ish and enosh too. Ben enosh is a man. The word Enoch and the word enosh are the same. It's all mixed up together, but we are identified with these things. See how these flow together and this language is trying to convey something to us. But there's quite a difference in the gospel the way it is given to us by the Prophet. He is going to show us finally what the reality is behind this after the human race has dabbled around for thousands of years. And Goethe worked at it. He sweat blood trying to find out would it really make sense, and ended up on a rather sour and negative note, which we don't need to. When was the first Faust first performed? He started in 1775, and 1808 was when he finished it. So I doubt that Joseph Smith could have used it for this. I doubt if he did use it. The second Faust didn't come until 1831, and this was published in 1830. [p.1]

Lecture 18

The Combat

We have to go very fast and just touch high points, but you will notice that every verse we come to in the Pearl of Great Price raises questions, legitimate questions which would take some time to answer, mostly. Now we have got to Moses 1:12 . We find Moses flat and helpless. This is the time we see that Satan chooses to attack him. The result is a stichomythia. Now we are going to get a combat. We know now throughout the entire world in ancient times from the very beginning at the year rite of the renewal of the world (we will see this is a renewal of the world) there was a combat with the powers of darkness. The king overcame and was crowned for another cycle, proving by his victory that he was worthy to rule. He had overcome the powers of death and darkness. Well, there's a great deal on that, more on that than anything else, I guess. But here we come to the combat, and it is between Moses and Satan. Satan came tempting him, saying, "Moses, worship me." Moses must be tempted. Remember, that was the understanding here. So from twelve on we have the stichomythia. Stichos is the word for "verse," and mythia is the throwing back and forth of rival speeches and verses. Before a duel between the heroes of the kings, there is a long preliminary exchange of insults that goes on. This is a regular part of heroic literature. It's part of the epic milieu that belongs to barbaric ages, surprising as it is. A typical example I just remembered this morning.

When I was ten years old, I remember I memorized a stichomythia from Walter Scott. Terrible poetry, so we will skip it. But the amazing thing is that the stichomythia has been revived in our own day. You see it, and it is barbaric. It is savage, and it is depraved. Namely, in the wrestling business. Half the battle are the insults they sling at each other. You recount why you are fighting, how badly you were injured, how you were cheated on the last bout, and what you are going to do to the guy this time. Well, that's stichomythia. They take up half the time swapping these insults and challenges and grinding their teeth. They do it in an artificial phony voice, as you know. Everybody has to growl most savagely. That's a stichomythia, and it's a very important part of ancient drama. There's a lot of it in Shakespeare's king plays. The first part of Henry IV opens with a stichomythia.

So here we are. Notice Satan speaks first. "Moses, son of man, worship me." He's going to be the king. It's a contest for who shall be king, and it turns out that Moses is to be king. This thing is between Moses and Satan here, not between God and Satan. God never fights Satan at any time. All He has to do is snap His fingers. That takes care of Satan.
