As long as you are here, it's not too late to repent. The door is open to everyone here. We mustn't judge people and divide them into the good guys and the bad guys. As Ezekiel says in chapter eighteen, it's never too late. However wicked the bad guys have been all their days, they can still repent and become the righteous. And however righteous the good guys have been all their days, they can still fall and become the wicked. The door is open right to the end. Never, never claim that you are saved. No one is saved here (beyond sinning). And again, no one is damned. You are not damned on this earth. You are damned in the judgment in the hereafter. And you are not saved on this earth. You are in between and you are being tested. Therefore, this life became, as Nephi says, a time of probation. It's still not too late, so what did Cain do? The Lord is protesting kindly, giving him the situation very clearly, and Cain turns on his heel and stomps out in wrath. He's a character, you see. Pride was the first thing. Cain is wroth, and says he isn't going to listen to any more. It's getting through to him, and he won't stand it. It's his choice, so he turns around and stalks out of it. "And Cain was wroth, and listened not any more to the voice of the Lord, [imagine walking out on God himself; that would be something] neither to Abel, his brother, who walked in holiness before the Lord." Obviously, Abel protested, and they talked together a lot and discussed the gospel. He wouldn't listen to Abel, and he wouldn't listen to the Lord himself. What do you do when your children act that way? Well, all Adam and his wife could do was to mourn before the Lord because of Cain and his brethren. He was followed by the others. Remember, Eve said, Now we will have someone who will turn the tide. He will be obedient. "We've gotten a man of the Lord." But he didn't. The wicked continued wicked. He walked out with his brethren, and all they could do was mourn about it. Adam didn't really know what was going on. Cain is very careful to make sure he doesn't find out what's going on.

Question: What made her think that Cain was of the Lord when he was born? Answer: He was a glorious person, like Satan himself. Why would Cain be special among all the others? We don't know, but the name is very interesting. He was very special and Abel was very special. We get these periods, these acts, these scenes. This opens a new scene. Cain and Abel, two new people that weren't there before. Cain is a very rich and significant name. Abel is usually taken to mean "mourning," the lost opportunity. Hevel means "a mourning." Of course, he was the one who was sacrificed. Now, Cain gets busy. He gets married, and the wives and brothers form their coalitions, their cliques. They all think alike in this. He takes one of his brothers' daughters to wife. It was not Abel's daughter. We say, Well, it must have been Abel's daughter or one of his sisters. But brothers' is correctly plural genitive-possessive here. He had more than one brother. He took one of his many brothers' daughters to wife, and they loved Satan more than God.

Now, Satan is going to organize this thing and get it going. He's going to put it under control and make it effective. He is going to take over the world. He has been imitating everything in the gospel all the way through, and perverting it all the way through. Everything is done in the name of God and salvation. His original plan was to save them all. Now, it is the mockery of the ordinances of the endowment, which he takes him through. It's mockery and it's reverse. "Ã‚Â…Swear unto me by thy throat, and if thou tell it thou shalt die; and swear thy brethren by their heads, and by the living God, [Notice again, who do they swear by? By the living God. They don't swear by anybody else.] that they tell it not [the main thing here is secrecy]; for if they tell it, they shall surely die; and this that thy father may not know it;Ã‚Â…" He doesn't want Adam to know about this because Adam is the head of the Church. Adam is the one in charge of the ordinances and all the rest. He doesn't want Adam to know what he has been faking and what he has been doing here. He must not know that ordinances have been perverted, or he would put a stop to it. "Ã‚Â…and this that thy father may not know it; [you do this] and this day I will deliver thy brother Abel into thine hands."

So the arrangements are all made here. Notice, we are told back here in verse 23, "thou shalt rule over him." Cain rules over Satan for this life, not the other way around. And sure enough, in verse 30 here, "And Satan sware unto Cain that he would do according to his commandsÃ‚Â…." So Satan swears to obey Cain here, to do anything Cain wants. Do anything you want: That's the famous pact with the devil. You can have it all, and I will see that you get it. And I'll tell you how to get now, he says. "And all these things were done in secret." Well, you can be sure of that. "And Cain said: Truly I am Mahan, the master of this great secret,Ã‚Â…" This is a very interesting etymology here. The word secret is sirra in Arabic; the eighth form of the verb, mustirra, means "to hold a secret, to keep a secret." It's the same as the Greek word sathra for secret. The Egyptian word is seshet; meseshet is "to hold a secret." Sether is the Hebrew word for keeping a secret (the master of the secret). So this word master may not be our word master at all, but master means "keeper of secret," and Mahan means "great." In any language maha means "great." In Arabic, English, Greek, Sanscrit, or anything maha means "great." Words like magnus, mighty, might, many, maharaja; anything that's big is ma. So this could mean Master Mahan, the "great secret keeper." (It could be; this is just a suggestion here.) So he got the master's degree, the master of this great secret. This was the secret: converting life into property. You can do it. All great acquisitions of property and gain are based on the taking of life, human and other life. You can't escape it. "Wherefore Cain was called Master Mahan, and he gloried in his wickedness" (verse 31). As I say, all great enterprises are going to put life at risk and take it.

He goes into the field and talks to his brother Abel. They had talked often about this. "And it came to pass that while they were in the field, Cain rose up against Abel his brother, and slew him." The usual story is that they were a couple of adolescents, and one in a fit of pique bashed the other over the head (that's the conventional story). They debate among the fathers of the church. Some say they were something like 13 and 15 years old. Others say 20 and 25. Here they were not only grown men, but Cain knew exactly what he was doing. He had been plotting and scheming all along. We are told in verse 50 of this chapter that Cain slew his brother Abel for the sake of getting gain. In the Doctrine and Covenants we are told that he slew him by a conspiracy. He had been conspiring and planning it for a long time. It was deception. He overwhelmed him here. And he was not ashamed at all. "And Cain gloried in that which he had done, saying: I am free; surely the flocks of my brother falleth into my hands."

Satan promised Cain what he had promised Adam before: You can have anything in this world for money. In those days they didn't have money. Well, money actually goes back to geese here. The words for money we have here are, remember, "Ã‚Â…surely his flocks fall into my hands." So we have flocks, fee, Old English feoh, and German Vieh, meaning cattle or flocks of all kinds.

The Arabic word for flocks is ghanam, which is our word gain. That is where we get our word gain from. It is flocks of sheep. Ghaniy is "to be rich." The land Ghana means "rich land." A ghaniuun is a rich person (plural aghniyau). That's the way you measured it. They still do in Africa. It devastates the country we are told now.

What he was after was his wealth. He says, "Ã‚Â…surely the flocks of my brother falleth into my hands. And the Lord said unto Cain: Where is Abel, thy brother? And he said: I know not. Am I my brother's keeper?" (verse 34). We are told again here that he had it all planned out. There was a conspiracy which was worked out, and he got what Satan had promised him, power and gain. He's on the way to success in this world. Cain then becomes a real epic and tragic figure of real stature, the big show. Now I have to rush like a crazy man.

Question: What does the name Cain mean? Answer: It means a traveling smith or metal worker.

Lecture 20

The Heritage of Cain

Even though the time is limited, we must linger on this theme for a little while because it takes a huge bite out of history. It covers more ground than anything else. We've got to know it. If it was missing, as it is from other religions, the gospel story would be far from complete, a very serious defect. So we were on Moses 5:35 . "And the Lord said: What hast thou done? The voice of thy brother cries unto me from the ground." So the purpose of the earth is what? It's to sustain and support life. That's its purpose during our test here, during our time on earth. Every creature is supposed to have its time, but the purpose of the earth is to sustain and support life. Anything that destroys life prematurely is against the purposes for which it was constructed, and the earth is offended. That negates its whole purpose.

"Ã‚Â…The voice of thy brother's blood cries unto me from the ground. And now thou shalt be cursed from the earth which hath opened her mouth to receive thy brother's blood from thy hand." He still has a right to life. He has not yet finished his course. Yet the earth has a responsibility. What Cain has done by murder is why it's such a terrible crime to curtail life. So the earth has "opened her mouth to receive thy brother's blood." As a result the earth is not going to give you anything. You have taken life from it; now it will not return life to you. The earth will not yield her strength when you till it. Remember, he was a tiller of the earth. "When thou tillest the ground it shall not henceforth yield unto thee her strengthÃ‚Â…." So he is going to become a nomad and a wanderer. He is not agrarian anymore. He is going to become nomadic and he does. He goes to the land of the nomads because he can't farm anymore. There are such people in the earth, as you know. All civilizations that last are rooted in the soil. But as you read in "The Deserted Village," "Ill fares the land, to hastening ills a prey, where wealth accumulates and men decay." This is the deserted village when people leave the land. This is going to happen, and Cain represents another civilization entirely, you notice. He goes off and founds a city. They go into the metal work business. He is a wanderer, and he becomes a type. "And Cain said unto the Lord [now, as you might suppose, Cain is the one who has been injured]: Satan tempted me because of my brother's flocksÃ‚Â…."

The thing to do is not to resist temptation but to avoid it. That's the best way of dealing with it But you notice it was because of the flocks-he was greedy. He coveted his property as we were told before. He murdered his brother Abel, it tells us in Moses 5:50, for the sake of getting gain. Cain slew his brother Abel for the sake of getting gain. He couldn't resist. That's what Satan had promised him-that you can murder and get gain. That's the big theme, that you can convert life into property. So he couldn't resist. He knew the tricks. He thought he could get away with it. (Also, I was mad because you favored him.) Well, you know why that was. This had all been planned by Satan. "Ã‚Â…And I was wroth also; for his offering thou didst accept and not mine; my punishment is greater than I can bear."

The Lord still has compassion. He puts a mark on Cain so that he will be saved. "Behold thou hast driven me out this day from the face of the Lord, and from thy face shall I be hidÃ‚Â…." He now becomes an outlaw. The worst penalty you can possibly have is to be driven from the face of the Lord. You will no longer be facing in that direction. You will no longer have much to look forward to if you are driven from the face of the Lord. God will be an alien to him. He has alienated himself. He has cut himself off. Remember that verse where he turns on his heel and walks out. "And Cain was wroth, and listened not any more to the voice of the[Lord,Ã‚Â…[I am through. All right, if that's the way you want it.]" He carries that all the way through. "Ã‚Â…and I shall be a fugitive and a vagabond in the earth;Ã‚Â…" He has become an outlaw now. He has taken life, and he can't claim the law's protection because he not only has broken it, but he has taken it into his own hands and taken life on his own. The first rule the Lord gives, as it says in the Book of Ether, is that "God will not that man should shed blood, but has in all things forbidden it since the beginning of man." That's a general order that applies at all times. He has in all things forbidden it since the beginning of man. Cain has broken the rule now. "Ã‚Â…and I shall be a fugitive and a vagabond in the earth; and it shall come to pass, that he that findeth me will slay me,Ã‚Â…" That's the status of the outlaw in ancient and medieval societies. A person who was outlawed had no law to protect him, so anyone who found him was perfectly free to kill him. He had no protection. To whom could he flee? Who was his protector? He had no lord to champion him. He couldn't go to the courts because he was outlawed. He had not the protection of the king's highway or the three days' protection. He had nothing. So he said, anyone that finds me will be perfectly free to kill me because of my iniquities. He has a bad reputation. His name is known for evil now. Anywhere he goes he is going to be at great risk. What can he do? It's a terrible thing. "Ã‚Â…for these things are not hid from the Lord."

But the Lord says to him, the fact that one person kills doesn't justify you in killing him. You do not correct the taking of life by the taking of life. This doesn't give anyone else the right to kill. So anyone who kills Cain will be avenged seven fold. Otherwise, people will start the rule of vendetta, and that never ends. Then you'll get northern Ireland, and you'll get the Philippines, or Lebanon. It's the Arabic rule of the raid, of the revenge. You get the Jukes and the Kallikacks, the feuding of Kentucky. And the famous border ballads in Percy's relics, the Douglases and the Percys. They feud for generations and generations, and when does it end? When they are both wiped out. That's your Book of Mormon theme. It ends the same way with the Jaredites and the Nephites, with extermination. This is the old Asiatic theme. It's a very old and very well established theme. So the Lord says this is not going to happen. You don't have a right to do that. "Vengeance is mine saith the Lord; I will repay," he says in the Book of Mormon. It was because the armies of the Nephites went up against the Lamanites that they began to be smitten. The Lord had strictly forbidden it no matter how badly they had been offended. Once you play that game there is no end. As soon as there is a murder committed on both sides, then there is no stopping because each side says, "Ah, you did something to us. Isn't that terrible." This goes on forever. Cain was an outlaw and a vicious person, but the Lord said, "Ã‚Â…Whosoever slayeth thee, vengeance shall be taken on him sevenfold. And I the Lord set a mark upon Cain, lest any finding him should kill him." We say it was a curse. No, it was so he wouldn't be killed, to mark him. Now we come to this very interesting thing. "And Cain was shut out from the presence of the Lord, and with his wife and many of his brethren [a large troupe of migration; they go forth now. These are the Cainites] dwelt in the land of Nod, on the east of Eden." Nod means to move back and forth. Nud is the same word in Hebrew. What do you do when you move back and forth? Well, you migrate. You are a people that have no settled position. The migrants and nomads of central Asia tour and follow the grass with the seasons. They are always on the march. Or the Arabs with their camels. These nomadic peoples live in the land of Nod, which means migrating or nomadism. The basic meaning is not to stay in one position, but to go this way, then this way, and then this way. In summer you go up to the summer pasture. In winter you go down to the winter pasture. So he went to the land of Nod east of Eden, and that's where they are going to live for the rest of their days.

There is a great tradition about the mark on Cain. In Peter Matthiessen's book on the Himalayas, he writes an interesting thing. There's also a massive work on this by Robert Eisler. Unfortunately, I've loaned it to somebody here on the campus and wasn't able to bring it with me today. It's just as well because it would use up too much time. But it's these Cainites that wander. Cain, Qayin, is the wandering smith in Arabic, Hebrew, and Aramaic. A qayin is a blacksmith. He blackens his face professionally because he works at the forge. This is a mark of his profession, the blackened face. It advertises his profession, and he wanders. You find these, and they are great metal workers, as we will see Cain's descendants are. Their rites are secret, and they intermarry. You think of the Gypsies, of course. The Gypsies belong to that particular class of people. They are always wandering. We don't see Gypsies as much as we used to. There used to be a lot of them. When I grew up in the Northwest, we had Gypsies with us all the time. They would park in the back lot, and they were remarkable people. They were the Romany. Gypsy is related to Egyptian, but they spoke a language very close to Sanskrit. Maybe you have read George H. Borrow's Lavengro. That used to be required reading from everybody. It is about the boy who grew up among the Gypsies and learned the Gypsy language and secrets. They have Gypsy princes. For us they are rather a sinister people. They live to themselves, and they don't hesitate to steal or anything like that because that's legitimate. They can't make a living. The earth will not yield. They cannot plant because they say it won't grow if they do. They tell fortunes and have all sorts of insights. They can really tell them too. I've had some beautiful fortunes told, and they hit it "right on the button."

Peter Matthiessen writes here, "The new porters are dirt-colored men in dirty rags and small black caps carrying the curved Ghurka hatchet-knife called Kukry. They are not interested in the telescope. Most are the Kammy people." We say Gypsy means Egypt, but Kammy is the word for Egypt too, and it means "black." That's why we usually say that the name for Egypt (Kemi written with a crocodile's tail) comes from the "black land." Whether it does or not, it comes from the black people too. It is also written with a thunderbolt where it is read not Kemi [p.4] but Kham, the people of Ham. So these are called the black people of the blacksmith cast. "Ã‚Â…Soot-faced familiars of the smelting fire and of the iron stolen from the rock, feared and despised as black magicians by primitive people throughout Eurasia and Africa since the beginning of this dark age of iron." These people have always been wandering there. You will find them, and they are very interesting people. Nobody knows the source of their wealth. As I say, they take what they want.

Once we were held up for half a day. It was in 1964 when they were having lots of trouble between the Arabs and Jews, and Nasser was stirring up a great deal of trouble in Palestine. We were stopped on the border between Jordan and Syria. They had a little checking station and would check the people that passed by. Along would come a poor old camel driver or somebody with a donkey. He would be all bedraggled, with long whiskers and rags, dirty, etc. This being customs, the soldiers would insist that he unroll the bundles he had on his camels and show what he had. You would gasp to see what came out of those bundles. There were the most elegant silks and satins and jewelry, gold plate and silver, all sorts of things. You'd wonder where they picked up this loot. These were rich men, but you would never guess if you looked at them. They looked utterly dismal. Well, this is the tribe of Cain, of Kemet, and they wander around like this.

Now we get to the Cainites, and they become a regular people. It's a way of life. There's going to be another line too, and we are given the line here of the Cainites. "And Cain knew his wife, and she conceived and bare Enoch,Ã‚Â…" (I thought Enoch was a holy man.) Well, this was another Enoch who went and built a city. The first city builder was Enoch. Then to him was born Irad (not Jared). I think the best thing to do is to follow the fourth chapter of Genesis here. Let's do that. We get the Lamech story there. First we have Adam, and then Cain, and then Enoch. He bore Irad. Now Irad is not the same as Jared. It looks like it, but it is spelled quite differently. It means the "shy person," the person who holds back and is withdrawn, who is unsure of himself. These people don't have complimentary names. It's a very interesting thing. Then Irad begot Mehujael. Mahijael and Mehujael both mean the same thing. They mean a person who has been smitten by God with a disease of some sort. The basic root meha means to have a chronic disease. Usually, Mehujael means smitten by God. That means you have had divine smiting. You're paralyzed. It's a word for paralytic. Irad is the shy withdrawn person, a person who can't circulate in society. Mehujael is a person who has been smitten with palsy, polio, or something like that. Uncomplimentary names. The next person was Methusael. It sounds like Methuselah, but it isn't. The reason I'm putting this on is that the Sethites (the sons of Seth) run in seven lines with almost the same names. But they are read differently as if you were punning on them, like twin names. This is a typical trick. The Egyptians do it all the time. So Mehujael begat Methusael which means "the one who has been prayed for." This is a more complimentary name. It means one who has been prayed for or one who prays, one who supplicates. Then he begets Lamech. All this is building up to the story of Lamech, which is a very sad name. So here are the seven. Lamech means destruction, loss, perdition. His name shall be perdition. So these aren't very flattering names in this particular family.

Lamech took two wives. Now we will get to the story that I was so interested in, but first I must give you an introduction. It's very important. Here is a book that just came out. It's a later edition. Marija Gimbutas's The Gods and Goddesses of Old Europe. It deals with the old European civilization. We have been talking about the Egyptians and the Greeks and all that sort of thing. This is much older. This is where it all comes from, as a matter of fact. You can trace one from the other. Here are maps showing where the old civilization is. Notice, it begins in the Balkans up here. It goes down into the Aegean later on and across into Egypt. The interesting thing is that it says the gods and goddesses of Europe from 6500 to 3500 B.C. That was the time when they flourished. They were much older than that. There are over 30,000 artifacts from this particular civilization, but 6500 to 3500 was when it flourished. Around 6500 was its peak. That's 3,000 years older than the Egyptians. It is 3400, according to Emery, when the Egyptians suddenly move in and you get a new civilization. They start full blast with the writing and everything else. These people have writing; they have everything at 6500 B.C. This is really knocking things over. Moreover it can be traced back. There are some pictures of temples and shrines and pottery. (It's very well illustrated.) The grotesque masked figures are made that way on purpose. There is some sculpture which shows that they were quite skillful. When they wanted to, they could make perfectly good figurines. But these things are deliberately shown as grotesque and masked. They are dancers and they play various roles in a drama. Here is the mother goddess in her various forms and how she compares with the Aegean. That's the one on the cover on the front. This is much older than anything we ever heard of. You say, what about your primitive people and primitive civilization? Well, this fits into the picture. The concluding chapter will show us what we are talking about here just briefly. What are these figures? They had all sorts of ornaments fixed on them. Here is a mating scene (male and female) called the Juwelnitsa lovers. It's possibly the portrayal of the ritual connected with the sacred marriage. That's what we are getting here in Lamech. We are going to get this marriage business and what goes on there.

To get the general picture we have to see this institution which is so very important. We'll call it the panegyris here. Pan means "everybody," and gyris means "a gyroscope," around in a circle. This is when everybody meets in the big circle, the cosmic circle. "In ancient times at holy shrines, each believed to mark the exact center of the universe [this is the situation], represented as the point at which the four quarters of the earth converge [the navel of the earth, always written with the quadrata like this when it is represented; Indians and everybody have that sign] one might have seen assembled at the new year [always at the new year; we've had this motif before] the moment of creation, the beginning and ending of time, vast concourses of people would come together." That's what you have at Stonehenge and Avebury and well over a thousand ancient megalithic centers scattered all over Europe. They are also in Asia. There are lots of them in Palestine. "Ã‚Â…vast concourses of people, each thought to represent the entire human race in the presence of all its ancestors and gods. A visitor to any of these festivals would have found a market or fair in progress. Why? The natural outcome of bringing people together from wide areas in large numbers. The temple of the place functions as an [p.6] exchange or bank. He could have witnessed ritual contests-foot and horse and wagon races, and odd kinds of wrestling."

They still have the glima here. Spanish Fork is the oldest Icelandic settlement in America. It's older even than Winnipeg. There were five wards there in which they still spoke Icelandic when I came to Provo. They hold their annual festival here, and they have the glima. That's a ritual form of wrestling. It's belt wrestling. We find this in the earliest glyphs. We have lots of pictures of this ritual glima wrestling from Egypt, from the Old Kingdom and the Middle Kingdom. It's very common, and sometimes it took a pretty deadly form. In the K'sen we have it mentioned that way. You would get hold of the other person's belt and throw him over. But the Icelandic custom (they didn't do it this way here) is to grab the other person's belt, and they had short knives. You couldn't let go of the belt, and you tried to do as much damage as you could with the knife. It was a pretty gory affair. There were races because they had to pick the winner. And there were choral competitions and the famous Troy game, beauty contests, etc. The kallisteia because they had to pick the queen as well.

"Ã‚Â…He would note that all came to the celebration as pilgrims, often traversing immense distances." Pilgrim roads are the oldest marks we have. We have pilgrim roads in England going back to 4500 B.C. Flying over them, you can still see them from the air because they are marked by standing stones along there. They all converge together on Avebury which was built about 4500 B.C. I used to fly over that twice a week at least. We could see where all the roads would converge from vast distances in all directions on the Kennet mounds where the king was buried. There is this vast processional way with these huge stones and then this enormous circle. They have made it a national park. It is a tremendous layout. "Ã‚Â…They often traversed immense distances over prehistoric sacred roads and dwelt during the festival in booths of green boughs. [All this was done in Israel too, you will notice in the Bible.] What would most command the visitors' attention to the great assembly would be the main event. [This is what we are getting at now, the famous ritual year drama for the glorification of the king.] In most versions of the year drama, the king wages combat with his dark adversary of the underworld, emerging victorious after a temporary defeat. [We already had that with Moses and Faust and the rest. When you begin, your hero is overcome. He is beaten.] He emerges finally after temporary defeat in his duel with death to be acclaimed in a single mighty chorus." Remember, Moses is acclaimed. When he is victor, the Lord says, You have won. I will make you stronger than many waters. You will lead my people. So he is to be king. "Ã‚Â…mighty chorus as worthy and recognized ruler of the new age. The new year was the birthday of the human race, and its rites dramatized the creation of the world. All who would be found in the Book of Life opened at the creation of the world [the book is very important] must necessarily attend." If you didn't, you were banished for three years from the kingdom. You became an outlaw then if you didn't come to the presence of the king when you were summoned by the royal herÃƒÂ¶r. There's a famous saga called the Orvar-Odds Saga. You must be summoned and if you don't come, then you will be banished from the kingdom. You have no rights whatever for three years. "There were coronation and royal marriage rites accompanied by a ritual [p.7] representation of the sowing or begetting of the human race, and the whole celebration wound up in a mighty feast in which the king as the lord of abundance gave earnest of his capacity to supply his children with all the good things of the earth. The stuff for the feast was supplied by the feasters themselves for no one came to worship the king without bringing his tithes and first fruits."

This is well documented in footnotes here, but I have information on well over a hundred such places. It lists all the things they did, comparing and matching them. This is the pattern that emerges. That's patternism. That's what we have here. If they left this out, there would be a huge gap. The Bible gives us just two verses on it, but the Book of Moses gives us a beautiful rundown on what was going on and what the situation was. So let's see what the Bible tells us. There are just these two verses here in the Lamech story, Gen. 4:19-24 . Lamech means "lamentation, destruction, sadness," etc. He took two women to wife. One of them was called Adah. Sometimes they end it with h, and sometimes they don't. The other one was Zillah. Adah means "the beautiful, the adored one." It means she is "all got up." It means both masked and adorned, completely glorified. The words zinnah and Zion are the same thing. They mean adorned, beautiful-not only beautiful herself but heavily made up for the whole event. Adah is the gorgeous one. Zillah has the double meaning in all Semitic languages. Zil means an image, a counter image or a shade, or dark haired (zillun, a shadow; nazlun, shadowing; a likeness, an image, mazalla, zalla, a reflection). So she is the other one. There is going to be a competition here. We are going to have an antiphonal chorus. They have these chorals to choose the king. With the antiphonal chorus, one chorus sings to the other one. We have that in the Book of Samuel and in Chronicles. They represent the competition of the maidens. This is very important too. You get this in the corn dances of the Hopis. They divide them up the same way. This is good old Adah and Zillah. (We might as well get through with this part of it.) Adah bore two sons and one was Jabal. We have that Jabal, and it should be Yabal, which is quite different. Jabal would be a different thing entirely. But in Hebrew it is ya here, so it's Yabal. Our word jubilee comes from it. It means "to sing, to rejoice, to jubilate" and the like. He was the father of all those who practice tofesh, which means "to strike an instrument." The kinnor is any stringed instrument.

One thing about the nomads is that they practiced the nomadic arts which are portable. Not architecture or sculpture or painting or even pottery, but what is it? It's weaving, singing, and musical instruments. They all come out of Asia, and they are the instruments of nomads. It's the same thing here. Way back from the Middle Kingdom there is the famous Jabesh who is shown coming into Egypt from Palestine on a donkey with his little kids and his wives. They are all dragging along. One is playing a harp, and they are singing. These nomads walk along and play a harp because you can carry a harp with you. You notice the bards were wandering singers. They would go from court to court. They didn't settle down. So music is one of the most portable of arts. Tofesh is to strike an instrument. The words kinnor, zither, and guitar are related. The ugav is the bagpipe, the oldest of all wind instruments. You find bagpipes in Crete in ancient times. Of course, you find them in Scotland; you find them in Ireland. The Greeks had them.

Bagpipes are extremely ancient instruments. They were common in England earlier. Ugav also means accompanying for a dance, dance instrument. So here we have the culture. So the traveling rock bands are part of Cain's people. They are a very important part. They are mentioned quite a lot. The kind of music they have is not contemplative, but it's wild, frenzied and exciting.

But how about Zillah? She bore Tubal-cain. That's that word cain again. Tubal is a derived form of Yubal. There are two interpretations. Qayin means a "spear, a sharp instrument, a sword or a dagger." Cain is also a smith. Nobody knows what the Tubal means, but it is related to Yubal. Tubal-cain was the great smith it says in Macaulay's famous rhyme on the subject.

Tubal-cain, the man of might,

In days when the earth was young;

The fierce sparks sprang from his anvil bright,

And the strokes of his hammer rung;

The mighty Tubal-cain.

He was the mighty smith, and it says he was that. He was the teacher of all those who kharesh, which means "to smelt" or "to carve." The basic meaning is "to carve or engrave." It means to form bronze and iron. It comes at a very early time. And his sister was called Naamah, which means "delightful, beautiful, lovely." That's a good name finally.

Now we come to series of quatrains here because there is a drama. There are two choruses, site opposite. The earlier versions of the Old Testament didn't put it that way, but that's the way it should be because the lines match each other. You have these two choruses singing together. This chorus business is rather an important one. There's the chorus of the light and the dark, the antiphonal here. The earliest lyric poet of the Greeks was Alcman who left the maiden songs. It was in the days when Sparta was the cultural center and not Athens, way back in the eighth century. For the maiden songs, they divide up into two choruses and sing to choose the queen. It's at the new year, but it has to be at dawn. In the maiden songs, the two choruses are the colts and the doves. They are rivals to each other. They run a foot race at dawn. The winner of the foot race is chosen, and then they sing the choruses about each other. One is Hagesichora whose hair is like spun gold, and the other is a dark one who is equally beautiful. This rivalry goes between. There is always the light one and dark one here. You get that a great deal. You get it in the Canticles of King Solomon. "I am black but comely." You have that in Canticles or The Song of Solomon. It's the love song. You have the two choruses singing for the king, and the one says, "I am black but comely." You get that one all the time.

You also have Miriam and the Ethiopian. When Moses went up on the mount, the Hebrews started to celebrate this in his absence. He rebuked them for it. They set up the golden calf and divided into choruses, and they made fun of Moses' wife Zipporah because she was an Ethiopian. They say she was black. That is why, we are told, when Moses came back in a rage Miriam was smitten. (So you make fun of blackness; we'll make you white.) She looked and she had become white as a leper, white as snow. The Lord healed her later on, but as a rebuke for having made fun of the dark one she is made whiter than she ever wanted to be. Then we get Sarah and Hagar. Remember Hagar was a dark Egyptian woman. We are told in many traditions that Sarah was a raving blond. There was that rivalry between them. There's Abuzaid and the rest. A very famous story from the high priests of Memphis, Si-Osiris, tells about the rivalry with the two choruses (the black woman of the South). It's the Si-Osiris and the son of the Ethiopian. They are rivals, and their mothers put them onto each other. You will find this all over the place, and that's what we are finding here, the antiphonal chorus.

Then we get this contest now. This is what they sing in the contest. We are going to follow Gen. 4:23 . "And Lamech said unto his wives, Adah and Zillah,Ã‚Â…" Now he recites this, and notice each line is a parallelism; he repeats the same thing twice each time. He says, "Hear you [two of them] my voice, oh wives of Lamech." In the second half of the verse, he says, "And give ear to what I say." Well, listen to me and give ear are the same thing. These next two lines are all we are told of the story. We are told a lot more that is very important in the Book of Moses. This is the key. "I have sacrificed a man for wounding me." It's a ritual contest. The King James has it all cockeyed because they have the subjective genesis as the objective genesis. This is a major crux in Semitic grammar, and the Arabs never stop arguing about it. The killing of Zide. Does that mean Zide did the killing or Zide was killed? Which is it? It could be either one. There's no difference. Gardiner goes to considerable length to show that the same thing happens in Egyptian. When he says, "I have killed a man to my wounding and a young man to my hurt," he just repeats himself. "I have slain a man for trying to kill me, and the same man for trying to hurt me." He won in other words. He won the contest. It just leaves it there. We don't know what is going on until we look at the Book of Moses.

If we look up these words that are used here for killing and slaying, we will find what they mean. This patza for "wounding me"; it doesn't mean wound. It means "to place a cut or mark upon, to put a ritual mark." The other, khabura, we are told, is the mark or stroke of a wound on the skin. There's a conflict in which ritual wounds are inflicted. We are told that Satan showed Cain the blows of death. This is a very important thing. He taught them to him before he could have his showdown with Abel. Does this mean that Lamech, like Cain, was marked as a murderer? What's happening? You are going to find out very soon what this is about. It says, "Since [not if] Cain is avenged seven times, Lamech will be avenged seventy times seven." Remember, whoever killed Cain would be avenged seven fold. Lamech has done the same sort of thing, and he goes out. But it's a ten to one here, seventy times seven. And here is the ritual contest, what the women sing back and forth.

When David and Saul were competing, Saul was murderously envious of David and tried to kill him. When David came back from war, the women divided into two choruses and sang. One would sing, "Saul has slain his thousands. But David has slain his ten thousands," would be replied. It's ten to one preference for David. The same thing is Cain versus Lamech.

And the Hebrew tradition is that it was Lamech who slew Cain in the contest. He was the successor because we are told he was also Master Mahan. He took charge of the society we are told here. Right after this seventy times seven is the end of it. Then it says that Adam knew his wife and had another son, and it was Seth. That's the whole story. That's all it tells us in the Old Testament. But the Book of Moses tells us a good deal more here.

It was the same thing in Rome. When the emperor came back, the only way he could be recognized was for the girls to divide into antiphonal choruses. The Arval brethren do the same and the Salian brethren. They would say, "A thousand, a thousand, a thousand we have beheaded." (In order to become emperor, you had to have slain 5,000 in the field.) In the early days, the only way you could become emperor was to be acclaimed this way by the chorus of the maidens when they met at the triumphal arch. They would sing this mille, mille. Our word triumph comes from the Latin triumpha song, and also tramp. It's very heavy. It's exactly the way the Hopis dance all day long. The triumph song was one of these. All these things come together, but the point is that this is very old stuff. You are going to run into it wherever you go. I thought I had some references that might be some good ones. But you can think of plenty of them here, the antiphonal chorus and Adah and Zillah. And among the Celtic people there's Era and Ethna led by the white maid and the red maid. In Egypt there's the same sort of thing. For heaven's sake, I've participated in this thing which, of course, was at the May time, the Maypole. When I went to school in the early days in a rural school, we had the Maypole. We went around in opposite directions, the red streamers and the white streamers. We had to choose a king and queen of the May. The queen of the May was Vivian Johnson, I remember. I was eight years old then. The king of the May was a boy called King which was very appropriate because he was the biggest boy in the school. I had this terrible crush on Vivian Johnson which did me no good at all because they were the king and queen. We would have to bring our lunch, and we would all go out in the meadows and sit down among meadowlarks and eat. It was May Day. This was to celebrate the new year, the begetting of the race and all this. In England this has been going on for thousands of years. We have broken all those ties. We don't have those anymore. We still have a commercialized Christmas. In a common wall design in Egyptian palaces, you will find the tall Maypole, the red throne and the white throne. You will find the priests going in opposite directions. It's the Troy game that was done with horses. That's what a carousel is. The horses move in opposite directions. But again it was the red and the white. In fact, I wrote my doctoral dissertation on that very thing. Nobody bothered to read it, but it's a very interesting thing. I handed it in one minute before the deadline. Nobody had any time to read it, so it got by. (It was published later.)

Anyway this thing is going on here. Now let's look at the account in Moses. What was this all about? Lamech was the candidate. He became Cain's successor (verse 49). "For Lamech, having entered into a covenant with Satan after the manner of Cain, wherefore he became Master Mahan,Ã‚Â…" He is now "the big cheese," and Cain is his rival. So we have the normal story. The common Jewish legend is that Lamech, who was jealous and wanted to become successor to Cain, lay in wait for him. He was blind, so he took a boy along with him to tell him where Cain was and to direct his arrow. So he directed the arrow, and Lamech killed Cain. Because of that, the boy was held responsible. That's the story they made up to explain this garbled thing in which we only have two lines in the whole Old Testament to explain what's going on. But here we get a pretty good picture. (Oh, I didn't read you the conclusion here, but that's exactly what they are doing back there in 6500 B.C.) And Lamech became "master of that great secret which was administered unto Cain by Satan; and Irad [the sick one] the son of Enoch, having known their secret, began to reveal it unto the sons of Adam." Ah, somebody's giving the secret away; and, of course, they had sworn by their heads that they wouldn't do that. Since Lamech was the head of the society, it was up to him to see that the proper punishment was administered. "Wherefore Lamech, being angry, slew him [because he gave away the secret; remember, they swore by their heads that they wouldn't and they did, so that's what happened] not like unto Cain his brother Abel, for the sake of getting gain, but he slew him for the oath's sake [for his ritual obligation]." He had taken it and Irad had taken it, so he had to do this, not to get gain. Then it says, "For, from the days of Cain [he started it], there was a secret combination [it was done this way], and their works were in the dark, [but it was a society in which they had their marks and signs] and they knew every man his brother." This is the sinister element that you get with the Gypsies and with the Cainites. The world is full of that now. For example, you find among the Hopis the two groups, the two-feathered and the one-feathered, that act as sort of policemen. The two-feathered are good. They go around helping people, and they only move at night. They have secret societies, meet in their own kiva, etc. The one-feathered people are dangerous. They think they can commit murder with impunity (anything they want). They are a savage and dangerous lot. Stay away when the one-feathered people are running around. The same thing with the Zunis. They have these secret societies, and because they are secret, few people know about them. But they do exist. They are very widespread, and they have been with us all the time. This is the Gadiantons. It's a very important theme in the Book of Mormon. It pops up all over the place. But we have a lot of that going on all the time, secret combinations for power and gain.

Notice what happened. "Wherefore the Lord cursed Lamech, and his house, and all them that had covenanted with Satan, for they kept not the commandments of God, and it displeased God,Ã‚Â…" Now here's the point. This is the pattern. What happens? It spreads everywhere in the world. That's why you're going to find this everywhere you go in the ancient world. "Ã‚Â…and their works were abominations, and began to spread among all the sons of men." Beginning with the Cambridge School in 1930, this was called patternism because it follows the same pattern wherever you go. The people at Cambridge discovered it. At first, people sort of laughed at it, everybody doing the same thing, but now it is very well established. No one doubts it. "And it was among the sons of man, and among the daughters of men these things were not spoken, because that Lamech had spoken [now we come to a very important thing] the secret unto his wives, and they rebelled against him, and declared these things abroad, and had not compassion." This is the dame fatale, namely the woman who gives away the secrets. You know about Pandora, but this goes all the way down the line. This is like Lilith.

The wife gives it away, and it becomes a very important pattern. We shouldn't linger on these things, except for the fact that it is a very big chunk of world history. These things have only been dropped in the last generation. People were doing these things and they still do among tribes, etc., more than you think. Among the Chinese, wherever you go, you are going to find these secret societies. They are a very important element, as you know, and have always been in Chinese society and in Hindu society and throughout the whole world. But if this wasn't part of the Book of Moses, we would have a very serious defect. There would be something lacking in this story. Not only do we have evil coming into the world, we see the forms it takes here. We need to study them.

Lecture 21

The Eve Theme; The Book of Enoch

T. G. H. James is talking this hour, and he is the director of the Egyptian section of the British Museum. He took a group through last night over at the Ramses exhibit. Maybe we should be over there, but I don't think so. Unfortunately, our time is limited. I'd love to go over there. But he will give us only the most introductory, common-place things. He's an interesting man. He'll be around a while. (A fresh sneeze. Ah, such self-control. Tio; I like the Greek word for "sneeze.") This is very important. We are going to talk about the Book of Enoch now. Remember, we ended the last time with certain fatal ladies, and there is a long list of them. We all know about Pandora's box, but these legends all go back to the very beginning of time. Pandora was the first woman. She was Eve. She was the wife of Epimetheus, who was Adam. Prometheus was his brother, and he advised him not to marry Pandora because the many gifts she brought were fatal, the dame fatale. It's the old rivalry of matriarchy and patriarchy here. It runs right through, and it's very old. The oldest story we have in Egypt is "Re and the Sun's Eye." This is the theme. After the flood, Isis wanted her sons to rule the world through the matriarchal line, which they did in Egypt. To do that, she had to get the secret name. She had to get the true name from Re. He had left the earth and decided to blight it with a flood because of the wickedness of men. She wanted her son Horus to have the rule, but to do that she had to have the right name. She had to have his name, so she wheedled it out of him. She makes a serpent out of clay and puts it in his path. When he walks in the garden, it bites him. He is poisoned, and the suffering gets worse and worse. Only she can cure it. She will cure it if he tells her the secret name. So the secret name is a theme that runs right through. This is the first couple we are talking about. This is prehistoric, of course, in Egypt. Then we get the Greek legend of Prometheus. We mentioned him. He is practically a twin of Enoch. That's the primal drama we had in Moses 1. The same story is told in the story of Moses and Zipporah (Ex. 4:24-25 ). It's inserted there; it doesn't belong there at all. It's a very strange story about the conflict between Moses and his wife Zipporah who was an Ethiopian woman. Then there is Lohengrin, the swan knight who is not supposed to tell his name. He will have to leave the earth. He can't stay here if his secret is given away. His name is wheedled out of him. He says, "My name is Lohengrin." Then Nachste Schwan, that famous joke.

You know the story of Delilah. "What is the secret of your strength?" she says to Samson. She wheedles the secret out of him. And Tomyris ends up with Cyrus the Great who was completely bamboozled by her. She got his secret, his rule, his kingdom and everything else. And there's King Solomon and Bilqis, the Queen of Sheba, who was the Shulamite. She was responsible for Solomon's fall. We are told, even in the Bible, that Solomon was righteous up to a particular point. Then this woman got hold of him and led him astray.

There were others such as Calypso and Candace, who was Alexander the Great's undoing in Egypt. In all the early Christian Gnosticism it's Sophia who does this. Well, this fatal dame; who is she? In our story the wives of Lamech knew no compassion; they gave him away and he was an outcast. Among the sons and daughters of men these things were not known. His wives rebelled against him and declared these things abroad and had not compassion. This is an interesting thing. This story is not in the Bible, but it does come in the earliest accounts we have of these things outside of the Bible. The Lord cursed Lamech and all them that covenanted with Satan. Lamech is the successor to Cain. They covenanted with Satan and kept not the commandments of God, we are told in Moses 5:52 . God ministered not unto them. Their works were an abomination, and they spread among all men. So this gets out.

There is this book and I wonder if you can still get it (it's only $3.95). Because of its sensational title, it was just sort of a pot boiler. They got a lot of things together, but the things they got together include some of the best apocalyptic writings there are, some of the oldest and best. The editors don't know anything about it. They just picked them up and threw them together, but they are good. They go back to early translations, all of these. The Infancy Gospels, Nicodemus, and Paul and Seneca are not good. They get them all mixed up. But First Clement and Barnabus are absolutely priceless. These are the Apostolic Fathers. The letters to the Ephesians, the Trallians and Romans. That's Ignatius of Antioch, the seven letters to the Smyrnaians and the Philippians. Then there's the Pastor of Hermas which was discovered in the 1860s. That's genuine and invaluable. These are all the seven earliest Christian writings we have. They're in here (the Hermas is priceless). Then there are the Adam and Eve Books which are interesting. They are only a small section, but they include some very interesting ones here. For example, it tells you here how Lamech was betrayed by his two wives. He became Adam's [Cain's ?] successor. He was Adam's son, according to this. They were going to have this duel and this showdown, but Lamech was blind. They were out in the field, and Cain was raging through the world full of vengeance because he had fallen. He has a terrible state of mind. He's not only suffering, but he's wrathful and determined to destroy anything he comes across. So he came to the wives of Lamech, and they betrayed him. It says "they had not compassion." They told Cain where Lamech was out in the field. Cain went out there, but Lamech got the best of him because the young man who was with him directed his arrow. It was in the dark, and he thought a wild animal had come. He said, "My lord, you need to shoot in this direction." So he shot, and who should he hit but Cain. That was the end of that. But the thing is that Lamech was betrayed by his wives. This thing gets out. These are all themes in which the hero was betrayed by his wife or his wives. Quite often it's his wives. There's a very good two-volume work on this theme by Ludwig Laistner called Das Ratsel der Sphinx. They have a copy at Berkeley. It has the best summary of these. It was written in the 1890s by a German scholar who went through it very thoroughly.

Now we come to the Cainite line. We mentioned that before. We are going to have two lines, the line of Cain and the line of Seth. The Cainite line is in chapter 5, verse 42 and following. Notice it's Cain and Enoch (which seems to be the same sort of name) and Irad and Mahujael and Methusael and Lamech. The two wives are Adah and Zillah. Then there's Tubal-cain and the others. These are the ones that covenanted with Satan. On the other hand, we get the separation. A separation took place between them. There are many accounts. I have over 500 pages of Xeroxed very early Adam sources. I wouldn't dare bring them along or we'd get all mixed up with them. The best way if we are going to cover any ground at all is just to tell you the story as it is. So this is what happened. They separated, of course, when Cain had to leave because the land wouldn't yield for him, and he became a nomad in the land of Nod. They wandered, and he went east. Since they couldn't cultivate, they went into city building and banking and things like that. That's what they did. There are good descriptions of how they started. He invented the money, etc. Tubal-cain and Yubal invent the instruments and have real rock music and they rock and dance all the time. They are in heavy business. They go into jewelry and investments of all sorts.

Enoch establishes the first walled city and the first army. And down the line we go. It's the two civilizations. The pastorals are up in the highlands, out in the mountains. Those are the Sethians. It says they live in the holy places. Their main center is called the Mountain of the Cave of Treasures, being the Hill Cumorah where they keep all their records and also their sacred things. When Adam died, he was buried in the cave. When they had to leave, they took him with them. They separated then, and the holy ones were left on the mountain and lived after the manner of the righteous with their flocks. They gathered fruit it says in "the manner of paradise." They still gathered any fruit of the garden they chose to take, and they didn't toil to acquire.

Incidentally, yesterday I spent most of the day reading a new book by Timothy Champion (it's by four authors actually). It's a collection of the latest of all available material on prehistoric Europe. How has the picture changed in the last twenty years? It brings it right up to date, and it's very interesting. One thing we use for developing is absolutely basic-the hand ax, the coup de poing, the faustkeil. This was the basic thing because you find this at all periods. The stone work was supposed to become more refined, but this says it doesn't. It shows no sign of evolution over the entire period. Just as one big break comes in the geologic history between the Tertiary and the Cretaceous. This big break comes at 35,000 B.C. when you go over to a new stone technique and everything is different. It seems like a new race comes and inhabits the earth, new people. But their racial distribution, their villages, their economy, etc. are very mysterious. Sometimes they are very advanced. Sometimes everything suddenly disappears. Around 3,000 B.C. the great fortified centers all over Europe just disappear. In their place you get undefended villages and general migrations of small groups. It's a completely different picture from what they ever had before. So what's going on back here? Are these our people? Do they concern us? What about the chronology, etc.? Well, things do fall into a pattern. You know that book by Marija Gimbutas I got the last time on old Europe. Her conclusions are from all these thirty thousand figurines, idols, etc. Remember, they had this marvelous metal work, the Varna culture in Yugoslavia, around 4500 B.C. It begins around 5500 actually. There are deep mines there, very rich. There's gorgeous metal works, as good as we can find, a thousand [p.4] years before anybody came to Egypt. So it's a different picture from anything you ever saw. That's the center there.

But we get this picture of the Sethians who are living righteously in the highlands, and the people of the plain with their licentious, easy ways. This, of course, is the pattern of Babylon. Remember, Mesopotamia is the flat steamy plain-very lush like Sodom and Gomorrah when you get to Abraham. So these two lines separate, and the holy ones are left alone on the holy mountain. Things go bad, you will notice (secret works; every man knew his brother). And what are they going to do about it? We get the Sethian line here in chapter six. This is the way it goes. It's Adam and Seth and Enos and Cainan. First Adam, which means man. Remember, Abel was killed and Seth becomes his son then. Seth means "second or substitute or equal." Shetaa means the same thing as two, double, twin because it tells us in the scriptures that he will take the place of Abel (this is to take the place of my son, Abel). The line of Adam from now on doesn't run through Adam, Cain, etc. It runs through Adam and Seth, the Sethian line. This is a parallel line. They have names that sound alike but aren't alike. So we have Adam and then Cain; then Enoch, of all people. You wouldn't expect an Enoch to be there. Then you have Adam and Seth, which means the second Adam. The next one, Enos, means exactly the same thing as Adam. It means human being or man. In Hebrew today if you call a person a human being, you can either say ben Adam, son of Adam, or ben enosh, which is the same as Enoch. We are just getting human race, human race, human race all along here. So then we get Enos and then Cainan. Notice the two Cainans are spelled very differently here.

The eighth verse of chapter six is very important here because the fifth chapter of Genesis begins like this, and it should be corrected. "And this was the Book of the Generations of Adam." That should be capitalized because that's the title. It says at the beginning of the fifth chapter, "This is the Book of the Generations of Adam." And the generations of Adam are Adam, Seth, Enos, etc.; not Adam, Cain, Abel. That's not the line. So when we talk about bene Adam, this is what we are talking about. God made him in his own image. And then here again in Genesis, he set them apart both male and female, specific of his own body. Then he blessed them, and gave them a new name. He gave them the name Adam now. This starts the new line. Then the books were opened at that time, it tells us. This is the book of the generations of Adam. He begat a son in his own likeness, just as Adam was begotten in the likeness of God. But Seth is the double of Adam. Notice, "in his own likeness, after his own image, and called his name Seth." It means "looks just like me." It means "mirror image or reflection." Seth means "the second person." Sata in Egyptian, as well as Hebrew, means "to take the place of, to replace." After he begot Seth, then we go down the line. In the verse 13 Seth begat Enos, and prophesied in all his days, and taught Enos in the ways of God; "wherefore Enos prophesied also." The children of men are numerous, and Satan starts his big counter attack now.

This is the story they tell between the Sethians and the Cainites. They go on, the descendants of Seth and the descendants of Cain. Their cultures have different traditions, but the Cainites win hands down. There's no contest here at all. It happened over many years, however. They tell us how it happened. The plains had great attraction for the more simple-living people of the high places. They naturally were enticed. Satan took charge down below. He made a covenant. They talk a lot about the covenant, how he directed them and gave them the jewelry and the beautiful clothes and loud instruments. He specialized in brass bands. This would attract the attention of the young people up on the heights. They'd come and look down with some envy at all the fun that was going on down there in the plain-all the lights, the dancing, the carousing, etc. Then we come down the list here. After Cainan and Mahalaleel, then comes Jared. His name means "going down" because that was the day when they went down from the mountain; they left it. Jared told his sons, if you ever go down there, you'll never get back. At first they didn't know the way down, so Satan had to teach them. Satan actually came with a delegation of very important-looking people. He came up and invited Jared, "Come down, Brother Jared, and visit us in the city down below. You'll see what a nice society we have and you will love to stay with us. You'll see what it's like." He was curious, so he went down with them. They dressed up like angels, and they fooled him. He actually was fooled until he got back home, and the angel came and told him, Look, this is terrible. You've been led astray. Don't let your people do this. Satan showed them a way down. There was a gully where a stream went down, and you could get down that way. They started drifting down and joining in the fun. Incidentally, when Jared went there, he was introduced into all the delights of the place. Immediately, they started giving him a real show. It was a session-a real orgy with obscene goings on, etc. He was horrified because the men who came to get him had convinced him.

And oftentimes, to win us to our harm,

The instruments of darkness tell us truths,

Win us with honest trifles, to betray 's

In deepest consequence. (Shakespeare)

They won Jared over, but he saw where it was going. Well, his kids started going down and joining in the fun. They couldn't find their way back, and they didn't want to come back either. But they were warned ("if you do"). Pretty soon there was only a small community left up on the hill, the righteous and holy ones. As I say, this is all in these very old traditions. These were discovered in the nineteenth century in several places. There are about a hundred different sources actually. But they go back to Mandaean sources, and they go back to Johannite Christians of Central Asia, the same place where they found The Pearl. Then down in Basra at the mouth of the two rivers in Mesopotamia. That's where about two thousand of the Mandaeans still survive and live in these reed (practically) palaces, huge things. You may have seen them in National Geographic. But they still preserve these stories on lead plates because they couldn't stand the water, of course, and gold was rather expensive. Elizabeth Drower was able to collect some back in the 1950s and '60s and get some of these records. We have more now.

But anyway, there was a comeback. It just left a small community. What were they to do to keep up? They removed far off. Actually, it's the Cainites who migrate then. We find this in the time of Jared. Notice, this matches the Book of Ether (it's very interesting). We could go through these cultural details. These little things are so close to the Book of Ether in the Book of Mormon which happens at the tower, as you know. So the holy ones are left. In our Book of Enoch they ask Enoch on the high places who he is. They say, "There is a strange thing in the land; a wild man has come among us." They say, "Who are you?" and he says, "I come from the land of my fathers, which is a land of holiness to this day." The tradition is that those who remained on the high places kept themselves holy, and it was still called the land of holiness when Enoch went forth on his mission. All this is in these sources. You won't find it in the Bible or any Jewish sources either for that matter. This is very old stuff. This is Mandaean, and it's in the old Chaldean dialect that the Jews can't read at all. It wasn't read until the middle of the nineteenth century when they began to decide what it was. The great orientalist De Sacy, in the first part of the nineteenth century, said it was just a lot of nonsense. So we get to this.

So Enoch is sent on a mission to go and see. Here's this crash program at the end of chapter five. When the people went bad, notice on the one hand, abominations began to spread among all the people. On the other in verse 58 "the Gospel began to be preached, from the beginning, being declared by holy angels sent forth from the presence of God,Ã‚Â…" There are direct messengers, His own voice, and the gift of the Holy Ghost. So it's a crash program by revelation and other ways, and the gospel is handed down as Mormon tells us in the seventh chapter of Moroni how that's done. Remember, we don't get to see angels or have visitations. We're not supposed to. This is Moro. 7:29 "And because he hath done this, my beloved brethren, have miracles ceased? Behold I say unto you, Nay; neither have angels ceased to minister unto the children of men." So the Lord sends angels down. An angel means a messenger. And he says (verse 30), "For behold, they are subject unto him to minister according to the word of his command [all they do is represent Him. You don't have to see God face to face; an angel will do] showing themselves [not unto everybody] unto them of strong faith and firm mind in every form of godliness." You receive no witness until after a trial of your faith. And also firm mind. This is not for hysterical people that are liable to see visions and take drugs and all sorts of things. It's not people of that type that you can't trust, over-imaginative and excitable. No, people of firm mind and strong faith are the only ones an angel can come to. Otherwise, it will set you off your rocker. Remember, Brigham Young said, "Pray that you will never see an angel," because almost everyone who has seen one has apostatized. It's more than they can take. That's an interesting psychological aspect. Let me go on here. "And the office of their ministry [these are the men of firm mind and godliness] is to call men unto repentance, and to fulfill and to do the work of the covenants of the Father,Ã‚Â…" They call men to repentance and carry out, enter, and fulfill the covenants unto the children of men "to prepare the way among the children of men, by declaring the word of Christ unto the chosen vessels of the Lord [they who have seen the angels declare this unto chosen vessels; this is another step; the chosen vessels are the priesthood authorities, general and otherwise, who preach these things; they are the chosen vessels], that they may bear testimony of him. And by so doing, the Lord God prepareth the way that the residue of men may have faith in Christ, that the Holy Ghost may have place in their hearts,Ã‚Â…" They receive the same witness that the person receives who is visited by an angel. It isn't because these people told them. They don't get their testimony from these people. They are introduced to it "that the Holy Ghost may have a place in their hearts, according to the power thereof; and after this manner bringeth to pass the Father, the covenants which he hath made unto the children of men." So it's through the Father and the men. It's not necessarily a hierarchy, but anyone who has the spirit. Remember, when they asked Joseph Smith, "Are you a prophet of God?" He said, "Yes, and so is any other person who can bear a testimony of Jesus Christ." This can only be done by the spirit of prophecy.

So they send out this crash program to try to save the world which is in a very bad way. Thus all things were confirmed unto Adam by holy ordinances accompanied by the covenants that go down. Of course, the great missionary who is going to preach it is going to be Enoch. So he is sent on his mission. We should mention the Enoch sources here because the Book of Enoch is one of the most remarkable and strongest evidences we have for Joseph Smith. Nothing was known about it in his day. It had been found, but it was held in utter contempt. We will mention the Enochs then quickly (what the sources are). I had this series of articles running through from October 1975 to August 1977 in the Ensign. That's a lot of them. You can find all this Enoch stuff in there. A lot of it has been brought together, not all of it. You can get it at F.A.R.M.S. down here. The only thing is, it will set you back $3.50. I figure that's about two cents a page though, which isn't bad for Xeroxing. They've gone to the trouble to pull all the articles out from the pages of the Ensign and put them all together. So it's quite a convenience if you want to part with that much "dough." But we will put it on reserve so you won't have to worry.

We start out talking about, among other things, the fact that the Book of Enoch was considered by far the most important of all non-biblical writings by the Jews and the Christians. It's quoted 128 times in the New Testament. Nobody knew that until we had the document and could see what they were quoting. They didn't realize that Enoch was everything. Why had they gotten rid of it? We talked about that before. The same reason they got rid of all the other stuff. The same reason they got rid of the preexistence, the Council in Heaven, and the plurality of worlds. All these things which are greatly emphasized in Enoch were banned in the fourth century. R. H. Charles has done more work with this than anyone else. We have three books of his. You will find the Enoch book in R. H. Charles. (You'll notice that big R. H. Charles book on reserve. You'll find it there. Both the 1 Enoch and the 2 Enoch are there.) He says the main fathers of the church declared war on it, including such great names as Hilary, etc. It was so completely forgotten that no one even knew it existed until Joseph Justus Scaliger at the end of the sixteenth century suspected. He discovered a passage from Syncellus, a Byzantine writer, which he said looks as if it came from a Book of Enoch. There had always been a tradition about the book, but the Jews and the Christians lost it entirely. Yet there were thousands of [p.8] copies around if they only knew it. So then they began the discovery which begins with 1 Enoch. That is the Ethiopian Enoch in 1773. Sir James Bruce, in the process of discovering the sources of the Blue and the White Nile, picked up a lot of manuscripts in Abyssinia, especially three beautiful manuscripts which he brought back and was very proud of. They were books of Enoch, in Abyssinia in Ethiopia where they had been preserved. They go back no earlier than the sixteenth and seventeenth centuries (they are not very old). He brought them back to Europe.

Incidentally, scholars like Peiresc in the Renaissance were very much interested in Enoch too and claimed to have found him. Peiresc bought a beautiful manuscript in Italy, which they say had come from Ethiopia. But the great scholar (he wasn't a great scholar, but he thought he was, and everybody believed him), Job Ludolf of Berlin, came to Paris in 1683 to examine the manuscript. He said, Oh, it's trash; it's terrible; never look at this. So for over ninety years nobody paid any attention to Enoch. They had a text of Enoch there, a good one. Actually, this is late and it's long. It contains everything, but it's badly copied. It's not the best possible source, but it was the first known. Because it is long, all the chapters are named after it. Whenever you look up an Enoch source, whether it's first, second, third, fourth, fifth Enoch (no matter what Enoch it is), it always quotes the same chapters and verses; namely, those that were established in the 1 Enoch which is the one that's edited by R. H. Charles. So this Ethiopian Enoch comes along, and no one would believe it. In fact, no one would believe that James Bruce had discovered the sources of the Blue Nile. It made him mad, so he retired to his estate in Scotland and didn't come out again. He took one manuscript with him and gave one to Oxford, where it rotted, and another to the Paris library. So he had three Enoch texts.

Richard Laurence was the bishop of Cashel in Ireland. He was a great man and did everything he could to put an end to the endless feuds between the Catholics and Protestants in northern Ireland. He got out the first translation of it in 1821. If you see a copy of that, it's very different from what came out later in 1838 and 1840 (not only unsatisfactory). Undoubtedly, it's the same book, but this is important because it's the only text that would have been available to Joseph Smith. In 1821 it was Richard Laurence. They wouldn't let him take a copy out. They wouldn't even let him take a light. He had to practically ruin his eyesight by reading it in the dark, damp cellar at Oxford in the Bodleian Library. He had to read it that way and make a copy of it. This translation is a very interesting one because "ah ha, that's where Joseph Smith got it from." That's an interesting thing if you hold them to that. Somebody (I think it was at Northwestern University) wrote a dissertation to show that Joseph Smith must have got his Book of Enoch from this because there are so many parallels. Well, there are a lot of parallels, very close, very striking ones. This is the point. But they are nothing like the parallels that come out in the later discoveries, which show he is really on target. Of course, if this came from an original source, all of these are going to sound very much alike.

Second Enoch is a funny one. That was way back as early as-well, it wasn't even recognized as a Book of Enoch until 1892. They didn't realize that it was different from the other Enoch.

They thought it was just the same one, but it's the Old Slavonic one. It was in 1896 that Morfill first made a translation of it. That's when it first came out. It was Charles himself who first suspected in 1892 it might be the Book of Enoch. The manuscript was discovered in 1857 among manuscripts never connected with Enoch in Petersburg. So this is in Old Slavonic. Well, what's Old Slavonic got to do with it? This is actually the oldest and best Enoch until we discovered the Hebrew ones. This is one of the Palaia texts. That means the old texts. This is ninth or tenth century. Well, what happened in the ninth, tenth and eleventh centuries? Most of the pilgrimages to Jerusalem came from Slavic lands. By Old Slavonic we mean southern Yugoslavia (Macedonian, that's what it is). It reads very much like Russian. We have texts of it here. I have a Xerox of it in various manuscripts published by Vaillant. They would take their pilgrimages, and pious monks would go from monasteries in Slavic speaking lands to the Holy Land and spend some time there. In the monasteries they would make copies of these texts. They translated them into Slavonic down there. They didn't bring the ancient copies back. They stayed there sometimes for years and translated them and brought these translations home. But the documents they had access to were very old. They were older than any of the others. That's why the Old Slavonic is very reliable. Matthew Black said, "Yes, but the trouble with Joseph Smith; of course, he has these, but he has too much of the Old Slavonic there." Just a couple of weeks after that came some articles out showing the Old Slavonic was by far the oldest and best text. But that was before something else happened. So this is the Old Slavonic. This is in that R.H. Charles Old Testament Apocrypha and Pseudepigrapha. It's translated there by Morfill who made the first and only translation in 1896.

When Richard Laurence's came out, as I say, nobody paid any attention to it. The 1838 edition, which was eight years after our Book of Enoch, was the only one that got any attention. The 1840 edition (it was quite different; it had much more to it and read different) was finally read by lots of people in this country. Michael Stuart, who was the head of the Andover Seminary (the big shot) said, Yes, it's fascinating; it's a great work, but it's trash. All Christians should be warned away from it. The ministry wouldn't pay any attention. So nobody paid any attention to it at all, except one person. It got a very good review in the Millenial Star by Parley P. Pratt in 1840. He was the first one to recognize the Book of Enoch as a valuable work. As far as he was concerned, he didn't recognize it as shedding any light on Joseph Smith's Enoch at all. He recognized it as confirming the Book of Mormon. There were so many things in it parallel to the Book of Mormon. But there it was recognized in the same year. Michael Stuart wrote his review in 1840, very much impressed but very much against. Parley P. Pratt wrote his review in the Millenial Star in England in 1840, very much impressed and very much for it. So we have 2 Enoch.

Third Enoch is the Greek Enoch. As I say, way back in the seventeenth century, Scaliger found a Byzantine copy of it. But it wasn't until 1886-87 that the Gizeh fragment was discovered (I have a copy of that). The French excavating at Gizeh (the great pyramid) discovered a Greek text which they thought went back to the eighth century. Now it's known it goes back to the sixth century. So now we are getting older and older. This contains things [p.10] just as they are there, but also a lot more. It's more specific, more convincing. It doesn't have so much nonsense in it.
