These texts as they get older and older get better and better. The Greek text is very good, the Gizeh one. Then in 1930, Chester Beatty. He's an important person because he was an Irishman who made all his money in Bingham copper over here. He used it for a great museum. The greatest collection of documents, really parchments, in the world are in this special museum in Dublin. He built the museum in Dublin with his money from Utah copper. Among these documents is a very valuable text of Enoch which went back to the fourth century. Then in the next year, in 1931, Sir Frederick Kenyon was visiting the University of Michigan where they have a big Coptic collection (the only big one in the United States at that time). In 1931 he discovered the other half of it, so they put them together; and they have a pretty good Book of Enoch here going back to the fourth century (the Kenyon and Chester Beatty papyri). We have a very good copy of it reproduced with perfect photographic verity (same size, color, everything). You might just as well have the documents in front of you. We have the big Chester Beatty collection here, and it's a good thing to have.

So now we're back to the fourth century. Then the important one, which isn't numbered. A long Hebrew text by Odeberg got out in 1927. R. H. Charles permitted Odeberg to call this 3 Enoch, which is a Hebrew Enoch. But it was back in the 50s, in 1853, Adolf Jellinek, who was a Belgian Jewish scholar, suspected that the original Enoch was Hebrew, was a Jewish Enoch. He thought they could find the original, and it would probably be much older when they found it. He looked and looked for it. He edited that great work, the Bet Ha-Midrash, collecting all these old stories. He kept searching, and then in 1859 he found a big chunk of it. He found what had been going under the name of the Book of the Hechalot which means the chambers or rooms of the temple which represent the various degrees of progress that one made in going through the ordinances. Hechal is the temple or shrine. Hechalot is the plural of it. So we have the Hebrew Enoch. He began to discover it, and then by 1879 he discovered the whole thing. Incidentally, the dates of these Hebrew ones are from the eighteenth century on down. But the big discovery came later. Let's see, what other Hebrew versions do we have? I think 1927 is the big one. It's funny, most of the scholars who studied all these things never heard of the great manuscript that Hugo Odeberg got out in 1927. Third Enoch; it's a marvelous work, and it's all in Hebrew. But then comes the big one in 1952 with the Dead Sea Scrolls in the caves. In the first cave there was an Enoch. There was a 1Q Enoch, which came out at an early time, and then the big one, the 4Q Enoch, and 4Q Enoch Astr. That means the Enoch text from the first cave. This is the Enoch text from the fourth cave, and this is the Enoch astronomical text from the fourth cave. They were all handed over to Father J. T. Milik (who lives in Jerusalem) by the Rockefeller Foundation. When these were found, they were to be studied. They were divided up among various schools and committees, etc. He got the Enoch book. He kept it for 27 years without letting anyone look at it. It finally came out in 1976. For example, when I was writing this series, it was absolutely unavailable.

In 1976 Father Joseph Fitzmyer was here and gave a course during the summer school in Aramaic. This writing is in Aramaic, and of course, his fellow Jesuit, Father Milik, would gladly let him see it. "Oh no," he said. Anytime he asked for it, he would hide it under his coat. He wouldn't let anybody see it. For 27 years it was kept out of circulation. Then after I had finished this series, the last day they finally published it. In 1977 it was published by Oxford, and the English was done by Matthew Black. Father Milik and Matthew Black worked together on it. Matthew Black came here the week it came out. He sent me a letter before he came and said, "Is there anything you would like me to discuss about Enoch when I get there." I said, "Yes, the story of Mahujah and Mahijah."

Oh, incidentally, we've got to talk about this thing, what it is. This dates from the third century B.C. Now, we are really back there. We're not in the fourth century A.D. anymore. We are in the third century B.C., and it's in Aramaic. It's the oldest and the best and has all sorts of stories. Well, now this is really something. The one that interested me here was this one about Mahujah and Mahijah. How come Joseph Smith knew the story about Mahujah and Mahijah? That's a story you find in none of the others. None of them mention it. But it is told here, and here it is told by Joseph Smith. Let's just mention it briefly here from the last of the series (August 1977). Father Milik, the Enoch Text from Cave Four. All scholars working on it have eagerly waited during the last quarter century to see what new information would be added, what theories might be toppled, what hypotheses, etc. Now here's what they finally show, but this didn't come out until the last of this series. They cut the series short in righteousness. It was really going to get good, but certain people didn't like it. They got a new person in there that knew not Israel.

Here is the story. What has always impressed me as the oddest detail of the Joseph Smith account of Enoch was the appearance out of the blue of the name of the only non-biblical individual named in the whole book, and that's Mahijah (Moses 6:40 ). Mahijah was the one who asked Enoch the searching questions. Well, this is what happens. (We are near to it anyway. It's just an example.) There came a man to him whose name; see, everybody is scared to death. Let's go before it, Moses 6:36 . This is a marvelous picture we get of Enoch. He's a mysterious character, and in all the Enoch literature this is the way he comes across. "Ã‚Â…from thenceforth came the saying abroad in the land: A seer hath the Lord raised up unto his people. And it came to pass that Enoch went forthÃ‚Â…standing upon the hills and the high places [remember the righteous are always associated with the high places; they have to come up to see him, and they do] and cried with a loud voice, testifying against their works; and all men were offended because of him. And they came forth to hear him upon the high places, saying unto the tent-keepers: [you stay down here, and we will go up and listen to him] Tarry ye here and keep the tents, while we go yonder to behold the seer, for he prophesieth, and there is a strange thing in the land; a wild man hath come among us." Now here's the story we get from here, and it is never mentioned in any of these. "Ã‚Â…for fear came upon all them that heard him; for he walked with God. And there came a man unto him whose name was Mahijah, and he said unto him: Tell us plainly who thou art and from whence thou comest. And he said unto them: I came from the land of Cainan, the land of my fathers, a land of righteousness unto this day [they had retained their righteous condition unto this day]. And my father taught me in all the ways of God. And it came to pass as I journeyed from the land of Cainan by the sea east, I beheld a vision;Ã‚Â…" He goes on, and then he talks about the book and says "why counsel ye yourselves and deny the God of heaven?Ã‚Â…And death hath come upon our fathers; nevertheless, we know them and cannot deny, and even the first of all we know [because of this book], even Adam. For a book of remembrance we have written among us, according to the pattern given by the finger of God;Ã‚Â…" Then he read to them from the book and they were ashamed. They couldn't face him when he read to them from the book and couldn't stand in his presence.

Well, I just had time for this concluding article, which they would allow only two pages for, to match these up. From Moses 6:39 : "And it came to pass when they heard him, no man laid hands on him; for fear came on all them that heard him; for he walked with God." From 4QEnoch: "Thereupon all the giants and the nephilim took fright [when they heard about Enoch]." Joseph Smith: "And there came a man unto him whose name was Mahijah, and said unto him: Tell us plainly who thou art and from whence thou comest?" 4QEnoch: "And they summoned Mahujah [Mahijah; it's written both ways] and he came to them. And the giants asked him and sent him to Enoch, saying to him: Go then and under pain of death you must listen to his voice; and tell him that he is to explain to you and to interpret the dreams. [Tell them exactly who you are. He comes to them, and he said unto them, Tell us plainly who comest.]." They are scared; they don't know who Enoch is, so they force Mahijah to go and ask who they really are. He's scared and he doesn't want to do that. The principal giant is Ohyah; he's the head of them. Ohyah said to Mahijah: "And I do not tremble. Who showed you all these things? Why do you tell us this?" And Mahijah said, "Baraq'el, my father, was with me." That Baraq'el is interesting too because Baraq'el is supposed to have been the father of Enoch. That's the name Enoch goes by in the Doctrine and Covenants. Joseph Smith is called Enoch or Baraq'el. A professor in Hebrew at the University of Utah said, "Well, Joseph Smith didn't understand the word barak meaning 'to bless.'" But Baraq'el means the "lightning of God." That was one of the names that Enoch bore. The Doctrine and Covenants is right on target in that, and this confirms it, that he is called Baraq'el here.

Anyway, he said to them (Moses 6:41), "I came out of the land Cainan, the land of my fathers, a land of righteousness unto this day." In 4QEnoch here Ohyah comes and following him Mahujah comes and reports to the people that sent him and says, "My accusers, they dwell in the heavens; they live in holy places, and they are more powerful than we." So again Enoch says, I come from a land of righteousness to this day. And his answer is, They dwell in holy places, and there is nothing much we can do about them. And then Enoch tells the story: And as I journeyed by the sea east, I beheld a vision: and lo, the heavens I saw. As I was journeying, I went up on the mount and beheld the heavens open. 4QEnoch: "Mahujah rose up in the air like a whirlwind, and he flew and crossed Solitude, the great desert. And he caught sight of Enoch, and he called to him and said to him: An oracle * [it breaks off]." And Enoch said, "We cannot denyÃ‚Â…for a book of remembrance we have written among us, according to the pattern given by the finger of God and it is given in our own language."

4QEnoch: "Unto you, Mahijah the two tablets * and the second has never been read up until now. It is the sacred book of *. The copy of the second tablet of the Epistle written by Enoch, the distinguished scribe, with his own hands." Remember, written by the finger of God. It says according to the pattern given by the finger of God. The finger of God didn't write it. Enoch said he wrote it according to the pattern given by the finger of God in our own language . And here it says, "Written by Enoch, the distinguished scribe, in his own hand, and the Holy One, to Shemihazah and all his companions." The record is sent from the Holy One (that's from God) to Shemihazah. Then it goes on, "And as Enoch spake forth the words of God, the people trembled, and could not stand in his presence." This one says: "Ohyah said to Hahyah, his brother * [the two leading giants] they were struck with fear and prostrated themselves and began to weep before Enoch." Here Enoch says, "Loosen yourselves from your bonds of sin. Repent from the sin that ties you up and begin to pray." Then the giants come to battle against Enoch.

The Joseph Smith version says, "Enoch led the people of God, and their enemies came to battle against them; and he spake the word of the Lord, and the earth trembled,Ã‚Â…" And this one says, "(Ohyah the enemy of Enoch): 'Ã‚Â…by the strength of my power, I had attacked all flesh and I have made war with themÃ‚Â…" This is the leader of the hosts saying, "They live in holy abodes, and they are more powerful than I." Then the next verse is interesting: "Ã‚Â…and the roar of lions was heard out of the wilderness; and all nations feared greatly,Ã‚Â…" 4QEnoch says, "And the roaring of wild beasts came, and the multitude of wild animals began to cry out. And Ohyah spokeÃ‚Â…' My dream has overwhelmed me and sleep has left my eyes.'" They even have the animals howling out of the wilderness here. "The roar of lions was heard out of the wilderness." It sounds so incongruous, so odd, there. Incidentally, this isn't my translation I've been following. I have been using Milik's translation.

As I said, Matthew Black was coming. He had just got this work out. I said, "How about this? Joseph Smith has this story, and nobody else has it. Where did he get it from?" He wouldn't talk about it (absolutely nothing). When he came from the airport, he had it in his pocket. He said, "Here's your letter here."

I said, "All right, how about Mahijah and Mahujah?" Nothing. I had one four-hour conversation with him, and he never let out a peep about it. That's when we went to a concert together. But he did let this out. Walking along, he said, "Well, someday we will find out the source that Joseph Smith used. Someday we'll find it; we'll find it, don't worry."

Well, just what are the chances of Joseph Smith (living in Kirtland, Ohio, in 1830) getting hold of any of these sources or anything else? Of course, none of this was there. But when you get things like this, they are awfully hard to explain. It is really quite remarkable.

Lecture 22

Enoch

We're just going to go through without drawing the comparisons with the documents. We mentioned them before. Almost every point we mention is recalled in the newly discovered documents. So we will begin with Moses 6:22 where we learn that visions and visitations are rare. Adam conversed with God himself, but he was rare. Those who followed him, no; they were preachers of righteousness. So the visitations don't happen to everybody. Joseph Smith, but since then nobody has had anything like that, and nobody is supposed to. You notice in the next verse, there were preachers of righteousness, and they called upon all everywhere to repent and have faith. And what do they teach here? Faith, repentance and baptism-the same old gospel way back in the days of Enoch. And Enoch is one of them. (He's the prize as a matter of fact.) He goes about, like the rest, preaching. "And it came to pass that Enoch journeyed in the land,Ã‚Â…" When he was 65 years old, he begat Methuselah. He journeyed in the land among the people, and while he was journeying, he received his call. He's going to have a special call. He is going to be the number one missionary. He heard the voice from heaven saying, Tell these people to repent, "my fierce anger is kindled against them;Ã‚Â…" Now, this is a typical prophetic calling too, isn't it. It reads like any of the major or minor prophets. "I am angry with this people, and my fierce anger is kindled against them; for their hearts have waxed hard, and their ears are dull of hearing, and their eyes cannot see afar off." The theme is apocalyptic in all of these.

Do you realize that up until the 1930s the word eschatology wasn't even found in the dictionary? They didn't believe there was any eschatology in the Bible. Up until 1960 they denied that there was any apocalyptic writing at all in the Bible (amazing). Then suddenly they discovered that the whole thing is apocalyptic. All our scriptures here are apocalyptic. That means they are brought forth by times of pressure and crisis. They always come on the eve of great destructions. That's why they are given to us. Read the first section of the Doctrine and Covenants: Hearken ye people of the earth. Therefore, ye people, fear and tremble. That's the theme. In the verse 7 of the first chapter, "Wherefore, fear and tremble, O ye people,Ã‚Â…" This is the message because these things always come when they are badly needed, when the world has been going the wrong way for a long time, and it is time to set things right again. But it is always accompanied by violent upheavals of nature and world upheavals (political, international, everything else). We are going to get a lot of that here. We see it in the days of Adam, and now we see it in the days of Enoch. This gets quite lively. You notice here, the fierce anger is kindled.

From 27 following, I just noted some things here to be able to do it more rapidly. The difference is that people are following their system. He says, they are not following the system I gave them. I gave them a plan. I gave them instructions. They pay no attention to it. They think they know better, and they are doing it their way. This is the constant theme in all this Enoch literature we put on the board the last time. That's one of the great crimes. The classic theme that comes in here (it usually comes a little later) is the Watchers. They had this crash program that begins here when Satan had great dominion and the gospel began to be preached. Holy angels were sent forth from the presence of God, and by his own voice and the gift of the Holy Ghost and his prophets. In this crash program they sent down angels to preach and put the people right. Now this is a tradition that you will find spread everywhere, including in non-Jewish sources, at a very early time. These were the Watchers, the agregoroi. They came down to observe and then went back to report as to how things were going. They came down to instruct the human race and put them back on the plan of the gospel again, but many of them (200 of them according to tradition) fell away, were tempted by the daughters of men, joined with them, and sinned terribly (these were the terrible Watchers). They knew too much, and that made them very dangerous. The Watchers are responsible for a lot of trouble here. You are going to run into them all the time. We are told that they came down on the top of Mt. Hermon, the highest mountain in Palestine, which means the "mountain of the oath." Two hundred of them came down there and took their oaths and covenants. They gave away the rites of the temple, and they perverted them. The whole thing is a rite of perversion. They had all the same things, but they perverted them and claimed that they were being holy, that they had the priesthood, that they had the covenants. This was their great crime, of course, that they perverted the real thing. Fortunately, we are told they didn't have enough knowledge. Their knowledge was limited, so they weren't able to destroy everything. "They knew not the mind of God," as we are told in this book. So their system was not the system God gave Adam.

This is typical. You will find this in many verses in this old Enoch stuff we mentioned. This verse 28 here: They have "sought their own counsels in the dark; and in their own abominations have they devised murder, and have not kept the commandments, which I gave unto their father, Adam." Cain did all this and kept it secret so that Adam wouldn't find out that he was copying and corrupting the whole thing. So here it comes out again. They did not keep the commandments I gave Adam, but they put their own version, their own counsel, and devised their own abominations and murders. "Wherefore, they have foresworn themselves, [they have the oaths] and, by their oaths,Ã‚Â…" In Moses 5:29 we read about the oaths they made to each other. "And Satan said unto Cain: Swear unto me by thy throat, and if thou tell it thou shalt die;Ã‚Â…" By their oaths they have foresworn themselves. They were false oaths. "Ã‚Â…they have brought upon themselves death; and a hell I have prepared for them, if they repent not [and this is a decree which has gone out of my own mouth]."

Enoch is called to go out and announce this gloomy message, and he doesn't want to. He's scared stiff. He says, I am slow of speech, I am but a lad, and all the people hate me. He's not a very good candidate for the mission field, you see. Why does he call himself a lad? This is a striking thing. It says when he was 65 years old he begot Methuselah. This was the time he got his call. This is a very interesting thing. In these early documents he's always called the na'ar, which means the "lad." The doctors of the Jews speculated way back in the early [p.3] time in the Mishna. Was it because he was younger than other people he went to preach to? Or in terms of age of his time (after all if he begot Methuselah who was supposed to have lived 963 years), then he was indeed a lad at the age of 65. There are various explanations, but nobody knows why. The interesting thing is that Joseph Smith has caught it here. It has only emerged not long ago that the stock name that was given to Enoch anciently (his mystery name) was "the lad" (the na'ar). Why, we don't know. That's not in the Bible. You won't find it there, or in any normal Jewish writings. So there is the lad here, and what is he going to do? The Lord will say, Well, I'll give you exactly what you are supposed to say. Filling his mouth; that is a common theme too. He actually puts the statement in his mouth. We have some striking parallels here which we are not going into. Enoch is to place the two ways before the people, of course. In verse 32 here: "Ã‚Â…I have commanded thee, and no man shall pierce[thee.Ã‚Â…[he thought he wouldn't last very long, and he wouldn't if the Lord didn't protect him]. Open thy mouth, and it shall be filled, and I will give thee utterance,Ã‚Â…I will do as seemeth me good [I will be in charge and I will take over the whole thing. Just trust me and do what you are told]. Say unto this people: Choose ye this day, to serve the Lord God who made you."

He places the two ways before them, and he does this again. Remember, we have two ways. This is the doctrine of the early church. After the third century we don't have the doctrine. The two ways are the ways of the dark and light, the way of righteousness and wickedness; namely, our party versus your party. That's all it is. If you belong to a particular faction, then you are in the way of light. If you belong to the other one, then you are in the way of darkness. Well, of course, that isn't the way it is at all. Every day, every moment of the day, everyone has to make that choice. This is why we are here being tested. The choice you make will determine what you will be hereafter. But no matter how far you have gone, no matter how many wrong choices you have made, there's always the best choice. There's always the better choice. So this is the doctrine of the two ways, and it is very ancient. And you can't compromise on them. This is the interesting thing. We try to do that today. But as Heraclitus, the greatest of the Greek philosophers, said long ago, "The up road and the down road are the same road." The only difference is you're facing the opposite direction. That's why you can't compromise. It's the same road. It depends on which direction you are facing. That's the road you are on. We are all on the same road, and you are going either up or down. I think we mentioned that before.

Nature behaves here as in the exodus. I mentioned before that all the great scriptures come in these times of crisis, and the natural upheavals go along with it. There's a long period of bad times (and there have been such bad times). It's a very important thing to note. I could recommend something like Claude Schaeffer, a Frenchman who got together a huge manual (thousands of pages) in which he compares all the archaeological work in the Near East and Europe up until around 1947. Those periods still stand. There's a brand new work out by Herbert Schutz just covering the European area, and the same things emerge. At a particular time all hell breaks loose, and the whole world is uprooted. Everybody gets hit. There's no security anywhere for anybody. That happened around 3,000 and 2,200 B.C. It happened again in 1,700 B.C. It happened in the seventh and eighth centuries B.C. It happened with the barbarian invasion and the fall of the Roman Empire in the fifth century A.D. And so it goes. In the year 1,000 A.D., that terrible time. We mentioned that meteorites may be responsible for it (there's the iridium layer). There's that break at 35,000 B.C. when you have a totally new humanity and everything else changes. But these things do take place. That's what I want to point out. It isn't a steady, gradual evolution. That was the Victorian obsession that everything had to be slow, safe, comfortable, easy, by minute variations; imperceptibly the race irresistibly improved. That's not the way it has been at all. Sometimes with these crashes they collapsed completely and had to start climbing all over again. This has happened numbers of times, when they lost far greater civilizations than they were able to get back again for at least a thousand years. But Enoch is going to be active in this. These are miracles. "Ã‚Â…wherefore all thy words will I justify; and the mountains shall flee before you, and the rivers shall turn from their course [as they did for Moses];Ã‚Â…" Again, it's a matter of timing. The Lord knows those things are going to happen, and the situation is properly set up. Of course, it happens that way. There's nothing unnatural about it. "And the Lord spake unto Enoch,Ã‚Â…" Now we see that he is a seer. He saw out of this.

We are in a closed, confined situation and system. We don't see very much. We are closed on all sides. We are very much aware of that. We're getting less aware of that. There are a lot of documentaries on TV showing breakouts in all directions now where people have discovered (to use Moses's language) "which thing I never had supposed." I mean these visits to the satellites of various planets (absolutely staggering). Nobody dreamed there would be anything discovered in Uranus. They discovered more with that unmanned spacecraft on the same day as the tragic blowup of the Challenger than all the other 25 manned space flights put together had ever discovered. What do they do? They just fly around the earth; that's all. They took good pictures of the earth, but you can do that with an unmanned satellite. Russians go up and dig samples from the moon and bring them back, dig samples from Mars and bring them back. You can do that. Well, that is nothing to do with this. But the point is he looks out and sees something very different. It isn't the way we think it is at all. Moses 6:35 "Ã‚Â…Anoint thine eyes with clay, and wash them, and thou shalt see. And he did so. And he beheld the spirits that God had created; and he beheld also things which were not visible to the natural eye; and from thenceforth came the saying abroad in the land: A seer hath the Lord raised up unto his people." A seer is a piqeakh, a visionary man who sees things that other people don't see, which are real. That was Lehi. His sons made fun of him, remember. They said, "Our father is a visionary man," because he saw things they couldn't see. Well, that's what a piqeakh is, and we got those in the Old Testament very rarely. Elisha and Elijah are examples.

So he goes out and preaches, and all men are offended because he doesn't bring good news. Remember what the people say to Samuel the Lamanite, Tell us what's right with Zarahemla; don't tell us what's wrong with Zarahemla. Samuel the Lamanite said, When a person comes and tells you how wonderful you are, you clothe him in fine apparel; you carry him on your shoulders and say he is a true prophet. If he tells you your sins, you immediately cry out, kill him; he's a false prophet. This is the situation here. Nobody likes him at all. Notice: "Ã‚Â…standing upon the hills and the high places, and cried with a loud voice, testifying against their works; and all men were offended because of him." Nobody liked his very negative record. Why? Because he testified against their works. He didn't support what they were doing. He testified against them (just too negative for words). And so they tried to pass him off as a nut here in verse 38. "And they came forth to hear him, upon high places saying unto the tent-keepers, [this is something; you've got to see this] Tarry ye here and keep the tents, [notice, they are living in the plain, and they go up always to the higher ground where Enoch is moving and preaching] while we go yonder to behold the seer, for he prophesieth, and there is a strange thing in the land; a wild man hath come among us." They are not going to take him very seriously. But when they listened (in verse 39), they stopped laughing. "Ã‚Â…no man laid hands on him; [they didn't dare] for fear came on all them that heard him; for he walked with God." That's the passage in Gen. 5:21-24 . We are only given three or four verses in the Bible about Enoch, and that's all. We don't have any Book of Enoch in the Bible. This is one of them: "He walked with God." But fear came on them, and they didn't touch him. Now this is the story we talked about last time, the Mahujah-Mahijah story of 4QEnoch from the Dead Sea Scroll text discovered in 1952 (we won't repeat that now). But notice in verse 41 he says, "I came out from the land of Cainan [my fathers are still righteous there. That's where I came from, and I'm coming to preach to you]."

Then he tells them how he happened to get his calling. He told us before that as he was going through the land, he received a call. Here he gives his testimony and tells the people how it happened. Moses 6:42 "And it came to pass, as I journeyed from the land of Cainan, by the sea east, I beheld a vision;Ã‚Â…" We have three or four early Enoch texts (the Gizeh text is the best), and he says, "As I journeyed by the sea east, I was going upon the hills. I fell asleep, and I received a vision." The same story that he tells here. They even bother to mention that it was by the sea east. One of them says by the sea west, so they say, "Well, that's a lot of nonsense." But remember, if these things were word for word perfect, you could be very suspicious of them. These documents were handed around for hundreds and hundreds of years. They are copied and are full of mistakes. The fact that so much has held together is really quite remarkable. They certainly confirm Joseph Smith here. This from the Gizeh fragment: "By the sea east I beheld a vision." He saw the heavens opened. He saw the Lord, and the Lord spoke to him and commanded him. "That's why I am preaching to you now," he says. This is the call referred to just before. Then Enoch continued his speech, Why don't you listen to God? Why do you counsel among yourselves? he says here. Now this is it. As the scripture says, "How can they believe who receive honor from one another?" If we form a committee; if we get our strength from each other; if we support each other and form a society (a very impressive order or group) then we think we are getting something accomplished and doing something simply by counseling among ourselves. No, you get your counsel directly from the Lord. You do not have to go through channels. This is the important thing. "Therefore, thou shalt do all that thou doest in the name of the Son; and thou shalt repent and call upon God in the name of the Son forever more." Here he says, Why do you counsel yourselves?

A great deal is made in these other writings about the secret societies they formed, how they worked together, how one group would fight another. There were various factions, all very secret, all making exalted claims. This sort of thing spread, as it said, among all the children of men from the days of Lamech. Then he says here, Past generations have disappeared, it's true; but you are still responsible for the message because we have that book. Moses 6:45 : "We cannot deny and even the first of all we know, even Adam. For a book of remembrance we have written among us, [remember the book that is open in Gen. 5:1 ; not written by the finger of God, but] according to the pattern given by the finger of God;Ã‚Â…" God taught men how to write. That's a very interesting thing now. We get something like the Tartaria alphabet which goes back to 7500 B.C. maybe. They were writing beautiful alphabetic writing then, according to some people. It is very interesting.

There is a brand new book out by Alexander Thom and his wife. He is the man who has made more study of the prehistoric, megalithic monuments of Brittany and England than anybody else. He's a mathematician and an engineer. He found out a lot of very interesting things which are now widely accepted. He is absolutely certain they must have had writing. They conveyed ideas in writing. He's finding signs, but nobody can read it or make it out. They had writing given to them. (We have this book written "according to the pattern given by the finger of God." You have it in your own language, so there is no excuse here.) When he read the book to them (now this is the thing we read in the Dead Sea Scrolls version, in the Qumran Enoch) they were ashamed and couldn't raise their eyes or look at him. They couldn't raise their heads up or look at Enoch because they were so overcome by the power of Enoch when he read to them from the book. Here it says, "And as Enoch spake forth the words of God [he had just mentioned the book, and he said, you can't deny it; here it is written], the people trembled, and could not stand in his presence." This matches the Qumran Enoch, the third century B.C. Aramaic Enoch, where it says, "They couldn't raise their heads or look at him when he read the book to them." And then he begins to tell them what the situation is. Here is the big problem in Moses 6:48 . Here's the situation, he says. We are here and we are going nowhere, and we are faced with death, which means misery and woe for all of us.

We look before and after, (Shelley, "To a Skylark")

And pine for what is not,

Our sincerest laughter

With some pain is fraught;

Our sweetest songs are those that tell of saddest thought.

We can never be really happy. There is always that pain, always the thorn there because we are never sure where we stand, and it looks pretty bad. We know what we all have to look forward to. So we live in misery and woe, he says. If that's our nature, we are finished.

I've got to get the Book of Mormon out here because, as Nephi tells us, this is a passage very much worth paying attention to. It's the Second Law of Thermodynamics, entropy. It goes on. That's the one that's really working, as we are told in 2 Nephi. If nothing was interfering with that, it would be the course of nature. Second 1 Ne. 9:7 : "Wherefore, it must needs be an infinite atonement [something that can overcome all opposition]-save it should be an infinite atonement [if there was any way of breaking that down, if this wasn't all powerful, if we didn't have the key to the whole thing, then what would happen?] this corruption could not put on incorruptionÃ‚Â…." As we know, the second law is a one-way passage. The heat death: heat always moves from a warmer to a colder body, never never in the opposite direction. So things are always all running down to an indiscriminate heap of ash in the end. Everything is going to run down, and that is entropy. Everything is just winding down and stopping. That's the law we cannot escape. He says, that's the normal law. Of course, the great mystery today is why we are here at all if that's the case. It has been going on a long time. But "this corruption could not put on incorruption. Wherefore, the first judgment which came upon man must needs have remained to an endless duration. And if so, this flesh must have laid down to rot [this is the normal course of things] and to crumble to its mother earth, to rise no more." See, by the course of nature, by the laws and everything else, that is the way things should happen. It is that. We would lie down, rot, crumble to earth and rise no more. To rise again takes a bit of doing, but he says, that's been done; that's been taken care of. That's where the infinite Atonement comes in. Something is working against entropy, and this is the thing that a lot of people point out today. It's a very puzzling thing. As it is, we had a nice chance here. Adam fell, and we are made partakers of misery and woe. Satan came and plunged us into this sewer of misery. Until we get out of here, we are in trouble. All our chances went with that. Moses 6:49 : "Behold Satan hath come among the children of men, and tempteth them to worship him, and men have become carnal, sensual, and devilish, and are shut out from the presence of God." That is the ultimate exclusion. We are finished if this hadn't happened. Now comes the good news, the gospel. We had that earlier when it was preached to Adam and Eve. God will take you back, if.

Moses 6:51-68 is the Book of Adam. And we say we have discovered a lot of Enoch literature. We have discovered even more Adam literature. Remember, Adam was out entirely because his sins brought death into the world and all our woes. They hated Adam because of his terrible blunders and the like. He was the great sinner, but it has all changed now. People were willing (since they didn't believe anything anymore) to start taking a look at the old Adam literature. It's as rich as the Enoch literature. It's even richer. There are marvelous things. I'm reading it now just for fun because I got looking into it the other day. I've already read it. They used to read Tolkien's series because that was romantic, exciting and far away. This is much better, much more exciting, much more ingenius, much more original. You should read this stuff; it's great. But anyway this Adam literature has a lot of controls in it. This verse 52 here points out the immense gulf between those who have arrived on the other side, either one side of the gulf or the other. It's the unbridgable gulf between them. The road goes in opposite directions. You cannot compromise on it. Notice, this verse 52 gives you an idea of what is there. If you will repent and be baptized "in the name of mine Only Begotten Son, who is full of grace and truthÃ‚Â…ye shall receive the gift of the Holy Ghost, asking all things in his name, and whatsoever ye shall ask, it shall be given you." This is a state of existence we can't even conceive of now. He says you're not going to get to it yet. It makes a very interesting remark on that. Then Adam asks the rational question, What's baptism got to do with it? Why do we have to be baptized with water? The answer is: I've forgiven the cause. Now, you have to get rid of the effect. The cause was the Fall. It made you dirty, but you have to wash off now. You have to take advantage in good faith of the sacrifice that has been made for you. Here's the chance. It's a very simple thing to do, but you have to do it. Why is it that man must repent and be baptized? Not because you are damned but because I have forgiven you, he says. I have forgiven you. I have taken care of the transgression in the Garden of Eden. That's the Erbsunde, the primal sin. To think we are not responsible for that and, therefore, we are not to blame for our sins is ridiculous. That sin has been forgiven, so if you want to go on what you do then is wash off and get started again. He says here, "Ã‚Â…The Son of God hath atoned for original guilt, wherein the sins of the parents cannot be answered upon the heads of the children, for they are whole from the foundation of the world." But "Ã‚Â…when they begin to grow up, sin conceiveth in their hearts,Ã‚Â…"

None of us avoid it, so as a result the baptism is a very important thing here. Until you repent, you are continuing in sin. You are not out of it if you don't repent. Obviously, you haven't repented; you haven't changed, so you are still sinning. This is the appeal of verse 53 to accept the Atonement. Deny not the gifts; this is the theme the Book of Mormon ends on. It's all given to you. Deny not the gifts. Don't refuse the gifts of God whatever you do. That's the most foolish thing you can do, and we do it. So accept the Atonement and deny not the gifts. You can't escape it. In verse 55 here, sin has a bitter aftertaste. Then a strange thing in this verse: How does sin teach you to prize the good? Does sickness teach you to prize health? Well, it certainly does. The angels recognize what is good, but they don't really know how to prize it, what it's really worth, because they have never had a chance. They have never been in sin. As Tertullian says, "The angels envy man his ability to repent because they don't have to." This is really something to have that. They recognize the good. Of course, they know it; they live in it. But they don't prize it. They don't realize its full value. You don't know what it's really worth unless you have sinned and been redeemed. But it isn't for that reason. As John Chrysostom says, you are under obligation to sin as much as you possibly can to give God the greatest possible chance to forgive you. If you don't sin all the way, you have denied God his divine office of forgiveness, and you have curtailed and frustrated his desire to forgive you. So do everything you can, and give him a chance to forgive you, he says. But this says, no, that isn't the way you have to do it at all. It's not necessary to plumb the depths. Notice verse 56: "And it is given unto them to know good from evil," on sight (you know that). And as 2 Nephi in the Book of Mormon tells us, with a perfect knowledge you know what is good and bad. You have that reaction. Remember, "I will place enmity between thee and the serpent (that gut reaction when something is wrong)." You know what it is. You can't excuse yourself. Therefore, men are without excuse. He says, They know with a perfect knowledge as night from day; all of them do. You don't have to be a member of the Church or anything else to know that.

And again the gap in verse 57: All men must repent for no unclean thing can dwell there. An unclean thing is completely out. Remember, one defect (the slightest defect) in a structure that's to last for an infinite length of time will destroy it. No matter how trifling it is, if it is to last for thousands and millions of years, that will be a fatal defect. So you have to be completely cleaned up if you are going back to the presence of the Father. There can't be any sin or defilement in you. No unclean thing can stand in his presence. Well, you can understand that. In the process of time, that would foul up everything because it is all infectious. Notice, this is the separation. He says, none of that, "for no unclean thing can dwell there or dwell in his presence; for, in the language of Adam, Man of Holiness is his name, and the name of his Only Begotten is the Son of Man, even Jesus Christ, a righteous Judge, who shall come in the meridian of time." He is holy; no unclean thing can possibly enter his presence. This is the gap again. What advantage do we have in this then? Teach these things to your children. Then in verse 59 there's a very interesting statement. The advantage in this world. What do you enjoy in this world in that case? As long as we are here, the way is open to repentance, and we need to repent. Nobody is safe, even on the other side. Remember, "He putteth no trust in his holy ones; his angels he chargeth with folly," as you read in Job. Notice the bottom of verse 59 here: "Ã‚Â…that ye might be sanctified from all sin, and enjoy the words of eternal life in this world,Ã‚Â…" You don't have eternal life here, but you enjoy the words of eternal life. "There's no end to my works or my words." And the words put you into the picture. They give us great satisfaction. That's the assurance; that's what we have to work on-the words of eternal life. And hereafter, "eternal life [itself] in the world to come, even immortal glory." You will get that when you get there, but you're not going to get it here. So don't expect it. But we do have the words of eternal life here. They have been given to us.

Then there's this statement about the blood and the water which has a rather mystical sound to us. The water is an easy act of obedience. We mentioned that before. By the water you keep the commandment. "I know not save the Lord has commanded me." That's your sacrifice. So you get baptized as an act of obedience. Then by the Spirit you are justified. That's the Holy Ghost. That's your state of mind. If you just go through the motions as obedience, that's the first necessary step here. The Spirit gives you the state of mind. Naturally, you enter into it-the understanding, the agreement without which any act would be utterly meaningless. You are not just being baptized as a bag of sand. You've got to be baptized physically, but then it goes beyond that to the spirit where you understand and are aware of what's going on. The Holy Ghost does that. He brings all things to your mind and all things to your remembrance. Then the last thing is "Ã‚Â…and by the blood ye are sanctified." You can't sanctify yourself but by completely giving up life in this world, which means suffering death, which means the shedding of blood. This is the end of earthly life, and people avoid and dread that more than anything else. That's why we find substitutes and the like. That's why we find proxies for the sacrifice. All through the Old Testament is that. So the shedding of blood is your final declaration that you are willing to give up this life for the other, and it is an act of faith. Now verse 61 tells you that it is the Holy Ghost that puts you into the scene, giving you complete awareness of what is going on. It's the Comforter, the peaceable things, and all these things it describes. There's a very important statement here in verse 63. Science today emphasizes the interconnectedness of all things. This is the principle that made Egyptian hard to understand until we realized that these things are all interchangeable. There's a wonderful passage in Santillana on this. The ancients believed we live in the midst of a great manifold in which everything reflects everything else. This is a beautiful expression of it.

It's very important to understand the vehicles in which these things are conveyed and the peculiar language. You notice that in these books of Enoch; remember, chapters six and seven are the Book of Enoch. But he is quoting the Book of Adam now. This is part of the Book of Adam. It is mainly this, that all things have their likeness. You can represent everything by everything else. "Ã‚Â…and all things are created and made to bear record of me, both things which are temporal, and things which are spiritual [you can describe temporal things in spiritual terms and vice versa]; things which are in the heavens above, and things which are on the earth,Ã‚Â…" The earth is a reflection of heaven, and heaven a reflection of the earth. We use the language of one to describe what's going on in the other time and again. We regard the temple here, as the ancients always did, as reflecting the heavenly pattern. As Murnane said in his recent book on the Egyptian temple, "The universe writ small." The temple is an observatory in which you take your bearings on the universe. These things are all related, all connected together. "Ã‚Â…things which are in the heavens above, and things which are on the earth, and things which are in the earth, and things which are under the earth, both above and beneath: all things bear record of me." So don't be led astray by particular terms of language that are used and what they are relating to. It all connects up. It all ties up, and it all points back to this one thing.

Then in verse 64 we get the baptism of Adam. The main theme in all these Adam books is the baptism. It's strange. They have long stories to tell about Adam. They go on and on, but the big thing is always the baptism of Adam. Why was Adam baptized? Did you know Adam was baptized? Well, that's what this Adam literature tells us about. That's the main thing. You will find it in the R.H. Charles, the big volume, Books of Adam and Eve. How Adam and Eve get baptized; that's a very important thing. And that's what this is all coming to. Adam says, Why do I have to be baptized? The first step is to be baptized, and here is Adam's baptism in verse 64. He is caught up by the Spirit and laid down in the water. "And thus he was baptized, and the Spirit of God descended upon him, and thus he was born of the Spirit, and became quickened in the inner man. And he heard a voice out of heaven, saying: Thou art baptized with fireÃ‚Â…after the order of him who was without beginning of days or end of yearsÃ‚Â…thou art one in me, a son of God; and thus may all become my sones. Amen."

So we begin the next chapter with the theory that as many as believed have become sons of God. In the first verse of the next chapter, all who follow Adam's example are sons of God. That's more than a complimentary title. We saw Moses starting out saying, "I am a son of God; I am in the similitude of the Only Begotten." And what's the alternative to being sons of God? Again, lives of quiet desperation. You're going nowhere.

Then Enoch goes onto Mount Simeon. That's interesting; why should it be called Mount Simeon? (I'm sure Joseph Smith wouldn't know.) Because Simeon means "conversation." It means an "exchange of ideas." It's shema. It means "an audition, a hearing, attention, a place of preaching" (therefore, a place of conversation). He goes up onto the mountain. Remember, he is doing all this preaching on high places, and people go up to hear him. Here he is on Mount Simeon, which means the place of preaching or the place of exchange. He talks with God on this mountain face to face as Moses did on the other mountain. "Turn ye, and get ye upon the mount Simeon." I often wondered why bother about Mount Simeon? Why give it a name at all? It's obvious what's going to happen on Mount Simeon because that's where I'm going to meet you. And he went up and stood on the mount and beheld the heavens opened when he got there, and was clothed upon with glory in the same way that Moses was so he could stand in the presence of God. He sees him face to face. He said, "Look, and I will show unto thee the world for the space of many generations." He is now shown his mission field exactly as Moses was shown. This is ascension literature. A large section of the Pyramid Texts, for example, is taken up with ascension literature. There's a book by Widengren on the subject. Before the king can take over his throne, the king must go to heaven and see the field of his labors, which is shown him on a map, and receive his assignment. So here's Enoch again doing this. He describes the people of Shum and the people of Canaan.

Again, you will notice a very interesting archaic pattern here. You could ask the question, Now, wait a minute; he showed him for the space of many generations. When does this happen between Shum and Canaan? Is it past, present or future? It makes no difference. It's following a pattern. You can't figure out which one it is, but it makes no difference here. This is the vision of Shum and Canaan. Shum lives in the valley, we are told. And Canaan is a nomad who lives in tents. In the verse 7, the nomad is over in the valley in the immemorial fashion. That's the basis of Haushofer's Geopolitik, which was taken from Alfred MacKinder's Geopolitics. The basis of Geopolitics is the heartland. The nomads live on a marginal subsistance with the grass, and in the bad years they have to migrate. They go off in all directions to the peripheral civilizations. All the civilizations are peripheral-the Chinese, the Hindu, the Egyptian, the European, etc. They are all on the outside of this great shield which Alfred MacKinder, the Scottish geographer, called the "heartland." From that heartland come the periodic invasions that overrun and destroy the civilizations on the outside. Well, that's a long story. That's a whole course there; we don't go into that. But this is the pattern that's laid down here. The people of Shum live in the valleys. The nomads in their tents overrun them and destroy them utterly. Then in verse 7 what do the nomads do after that? What nomads always do.

They split up and start fighting each other. And only they, we are told, can survive in these fruitless lands because they live a marginal existence with their flocks and move on. There was a terrible situation. Much heat and barrenness in the land forever.

"Ã‚Â…There was a blackness came upon all the children of Canaan, that they were despised among all people." That's interesting again. The Arabs divide all societies into the bait al-sha'ar and the bait al-hajar. You live in the houses of stone or the houses of hair (that's goat's hair, the goat's hair tent). You are either aswad or abyad. If you live out in nature, as the Indians do, you get a swarthy, dark complexion, which they call "black." Then you're the black people. If you live in the houses and in the city on a delicate diet and keep out of the sun most of the time, then you are abyad. Then you are "white." This is the same pattern you find in the Book of Mormon. When the Nephites take up the Lamanite way of life, and vice versa, they change their general complexion and their culture. This is an interesting insight it gives into the early beginnings. There are a lot of recent new books that have come out on this very thing. We mentioned that one by Schutz, which is very good. It begins with the Willendorf figurine in 30,000 B.C. But this is the pattern you find all through there. And then he sees the seven lands of Enoch's mission, which very interestingly have names very much like the names of Hopi villages. We won't go into that either, but there's a story there anyway.

Then Enoch starts establishing the church among them. He starts baptizing them and organizing them. He calls upon them, they follow him, and he becomes their leader. In verse 13 he led the people of God, and they were attacked by their enemies who couldn't stand them. The enemies came to battle against them, not the other way around. "Ã‚Â…he spake the word of the Lord [and notice the terrible times we are in], and the earth trembled, and the mountains fled even according to his command; and the rivers of water were turned out of their course [that's mentioned in a number of the ancient sources-the rivers of water being turned out of their courses; and we mentioned this the last time]; and the roar of lions was heard out of the wilderness; and all nations feared greatly, so powerful was the word of Enoch, and so great was the power of the language which God had given him."

Remember the two giants, Ohyah and Hahyah. When the beasts roared, they were petrified and fell down with fear. And also you notice plate tectonics are at work. The sea retreats and comes up, and all this volcanic stuff going along. "There also came up a land out of the depth of the seaÃ‚Â…" And we are told again in numbers of sources, especially the Greek and Slavonic sources, that in Enoch's day (it was a great tradition) the sea retreated seven leagues and then came back again and drowned the land. But it retreated and new lands began to appear. It says, "There also came up a land out of the depth of the sea, and so great was the fear of the enemies of the people of God that they fled and stood afar off and went upon the land which came up out of the depth of the sea."

There's a very interesting legend about the island of Aegina which you see from Athens, which has Enoch's cloud over it when the weather is bad, etc. (all sorts of stories here we might get hung up on). "And the giants of the land, also, stood afar off;Ã‚Â…" Who were they? We are told when these Watchers came down and mingled with the daughters of men, what they begot were the giants. Well, there's mention of that in the Bible too. It doesn't mention the waters, but the sons of God and the daughters of men who produced a race of giants (there are such; again, we won't go into that). But when you go down into Montenegro every man is about seven feet tall. Kresimir Cosic looked small down there. That's where he went to draft basketball players because they are all enormous. Then you cross the border into Greece, and I walk down the street feeling very pleased with myself because I'm taller than almost anybody on the street. Now, these people would call the others giants. Alexander the Great fought a duel with Porus. The only way he could get at him was to get underneath his armor and punch him in the tummy. But Porus was about seven feet three and Alexander was about four feet six or seven. He was very short, and Porus was very tall. They had a duel and Alexander won, but Alexander always won. But you can see this idea of giants has that kind of reality. A giant is a relative thing, after all (we know what we are talking about here).

There was all this bloodshed. The people fight themselves as well. When nature is unstable and things become desperate, people start fighting each other. That is what happens here (these great upheavals, total uproar). There have been such times. And then in this the Saints are gathering. It's high time they were getting out of it, so they start pulling together. They start gathering together "blessed upon the mountains, and upon the high places." Notice that difference always between the high and the low places. "And the Lord called his people Zion, [I wonder what it meant then; of course, it means adornment, beauty, perfection] because they were of one heart and one mind, and dwelt in righteousness; and there was no poor among them." I've had students tell me that this absolutely proved that only the well-to-do were admitted to Enoch's city of Zion because there were no poor among them. Of course, if you were poor, they wouldn't let you in. That's how we can rationalize, as St. Augustine says, "Oh, human interest, what a powerful argumetrix you are." What powerful argumetrix; that's a person given great skill at argument. "Nobody can out-argue money," he says. Then there's a long passage of time (these things take time). They talk about these long ages, etc. We get all disturbed about millions of years when geologists talk about it (some Latter-day Saints do). But we are not disturbed at all when we speak of people living eight or nine hundred years and having children in their seven hundreds and eight hundreds, and things like that. What's it talking about? There's a good rational explanation for these things. As a matter of fact, the ages in this literature are all quite different. Even the Septuagint, which is the oldest and best of the Greek texts, gives quite different chronologies for Genesis from what we get the Masoretic text (the Hebrew text). Which one do you follow? It doesn't make much difference. Chronology has never worried me. Well, Satan takes over the earth now. (Oh, boy, have we got to move to get to Abraham.)

This is like today. Notice verse 24 here. This is a marvelous passage. He looked up and the power of Satan was upon all the face of the earth. Satan held a great chain and it veiled the whole earth in darkness. This passage actually occurs in some of the other texts too. He looked up and laughed and his angels rejoiced. One passage says that while he laughed, God wept. And God weeps very soon here, in verse 28. In verse 26 Satan laughs, and in verse 28 God weeps. Then those are brought together in one verse. It's very striking thing. So this is like l:35, where it says the time is not now but is near at hand when peace shall be taken away from the earth and the devil have control of his own dominion. We're living in that kind of a time now. But they start this shuttle service going. The angels descend and they preach and give a chance to everyone to hear the gospel and clear out while there is still time. The Holy Ghost fell on many, and they were caught up by the powers of heaven into Zion. So what is lost is only the residue of the people, as we are told in verse 22. Nobody gets drowned in the flood who didn't deserve to be drowned. That isn't because only Noah was righteous but because all the rest had made a break for it.

Notice verse 28: "And it came to pass that the God of heaven looked upon the residue of the people, and he wept; and Enoch bore record of it, saying: How is it that the heavens weep,Ã‚Â…" There's a new book out by Peter Kuhn called God's Weeping and Sorrowing in the Rabbinical Tradition. There was very little of it actually, but the idea that God weeps is in the Jewish Book of Enoch. Enoch asks him, Why are you weeping? He says, If you don't want to let me weep, I will go where you can't see me and then I will weep all I want. It's none of your business why I'm weeping. If I choose to weep, I will weep; but who are you to ask? I'll go where I want. The point is that God is weeping and Enoch just can't understand it. He's completely nonplussed. It has to do with the destruction of the temple in the Hebrew literature. When the temple was destroyed, God wept. Then they brought Enoch into the picture because Enoch was the one who asked God why he wept. That's the way it has it here. He says, I can't comprehend. You, after you have created millions of earths like this. What does this matter to you? Millions of earths like this couldn't begin the number of thy creations. Thy curtains are stretched out still. Why do you worry about this earth? He said, Because they are all connected.

And then this marvelous passage here. "And thou hast taken Zion to thine own bosom [they are safe now], from all thy creations [that's very interesting; Zion is brought out of all creations; they all have their problems, and they all have Zion; the whole system is pervasive everywhere], from all eternity to all eternity; and naught but peace, justice, and truth is the habitation of thy throne [very interesting passage]; and mercy shall go before they face and have no end; how is it thou canst weep?" Then the Lord explains why he has very good reason for weeping here. He says, I gave them the three things they needed. In the day I created him, I gave him knowledge so that he wouldn't be stupid. (He would know what the situation was.) I gave him his agency so he could choose to do anything he wanted. But, of course, you can have knowledge and agency and still do the wrong thing because you don't have the wisdom and understanding. You don't know everything. He gave them knowledge of good and evil. So he said, I gave him commandments; I gave him specific orders as to what he should do. In the Old Slavonic text, he told them, This you should do and this you should not do, but (just like this) "Ã‚Â…they are without affections, and they hate their own blood."

Son against father, father against son, and all against all-the whole human race is in complete uproar.

Then the mingling of fire and water that goes with the flood is interesting. Notice the language that is used all along here. "And the fire of mine indignation is kindled [well, fire and kindling are fire words, aren't they?] against them; and in my hot displeasure [there's another one] will I send in the floods upon them, for my fierce anger is kindled against them." We are often told that the fire and the water came together. It could have been a giant meteorite or something like that. Everything broke loose at once, and it was a tremendous thing. Enoch is completely overcome. He says, "I will refuse to be comforted;Ã‚Â…" Then the Lord shows him the plan of salvation. In the other text they make more of the fact that "a prison have I prepared for them." They are to be safe until the time of the judgment when they will be judged; and then they will be given a second chance. They will hear the gospel and be given a chance to repent. This is the preaching of the spirits in prison which, as Peter tells us, were disobedient at the time of Noah. That's what the temple work is for, to take care of these people who rejected the gospel and were destroyed. So the patterns all fit into each other very nicely. Joseph Smith calling more of his shots here. Look at the end, he says and be glad. The end will be a sanctified and eternal state, but when and how will it be, says Enoch. Then three times the Lord says, These are "days of wickedness and vengeance." Then he says, What will happen after that? Then he sees the world going bad again. His heart is broken and he says, Oh Lord, will you come again? He says, Yes, I will come again in the days of wickedness and vengeance. Then he sees the Messiah come and the Son of Man lifted up upon the cross and it's worse. All the creations of God mourned (the same thing all over again). The chains of darkness are there, and they are kept in judgment until the great day. Enoch wept again [this is for the time of Christ] and said, When will you come again. "And Enoch beheld the Son of Man ascend up unto the Father;Ã‚Â…"

Then this passage in verse 59: "Ã‚Â…thou hast made me, and given unto me a right to thy throne,Ã‚Â…" This is the one that really knocked Professor Black over. He said, "This is it." This is the one thing he thought he had discovered-that God gave Enoch a right to His throne. Here it is. When he saw this here, it really staggered him. Enoch is promised the throne (this is a very important thing). Enoch is called Metatron. That's commonly interpreted today as meaning "the one with the throne." The third time, He will come again. He says, Yes, I will come again in the last days, the time of wickedness and vengeance, where we are today. (That will bring us up to the twenty-first. Tomorrow is the first day of spring in these days of wickedness and vengeance.)

We have to get on to Abraham now, so we have to skip the marvelous story about Methuselah who took honor to himself and brought calamity on the world. The stories about Methuselah, the sons of God and the daughters of men here, and Noah continuing Enoch's work, and Methuselah's boast-there is a great literature backing this up. But this goes way back. This isn't in the apocalyptic stuff here. This goes back to the earliest Babylonian and Egyptian literature, especially the Babylonian story of Gilgamesh and Humbaba tells the very story that we have here of Enoch. The Lamech Scroll was discovered telling the same thing. See, Lamech was the father (too much; I'm sorry, we can't tell you). But we will start with Abraham the next time. We have to be thorough and careful in examining every one here. Maybe we put it off too long, but the other was relevant. [p.1]

Lecture 23

Abraham

In Jewish tradition and all tradition, it's a complete leap from Noah to Abraham. But they are aware of that because there's nobody that comes in between, so it is very proper that our Book of Moses (which is really the Enoch story leading up to Noah and the Flood) is followed by Abraham. The eighth chapter is the Book of Noah. There's a whole page from the Book of Noah, so we get actual excerpts from books from each of the major dispensations. The thing we want to notice here, how does it end up? How does the whole thing end up after this first phase? You notice you divide just as you do in astronomy and in geology. You divide up into ages, into aeons, into epics (smaller and smaller ones) just as you divide into the epic, the trilogy, the single play, the acts, the scenes, etc. So it is here. This is a big ending, one of those big breaks. You notice it happens right at the end of our Book of Moses. The earth was corrupt before God and was filled with violence. This is a sign of of our world today, both corrupt and filled with violence. Moses 8:29 : "And God looked upon the earth and, behold, it was corrupt, for all flesh had corrupted its way upon the earth. And God said unto Noah: The end of all flesh is come before me, for the earth is filled with violence, and behold I will destroy all flesh from off the earth [that's what they want; that's what they'll get]." And that's what we are moving toward right now. But this takes us right to Abraham.

The Enoch story reaches its climax in the Flood. After the Flood, we just repeat the Adam story again. Just as Adam found himself in the lone and dreary wasteland outside and had to work his head off to make things go, Noah also finds himself in the wilderness. It uses the very same word in the Bible. After the Flood nothing was growing. He found himself in a midbar, a desert world. Then he built an altar. Remember the first thing he did and the affair of the rainbow. Then what did he do? He cultivated a garden. He became a gardener, as Adam did and went to till the earth. The next thing he did was plant a vineyard. Then comes a separation. His family grows up. They flourish and go bad immediately, just as in the time of Adam. Already the flesh becomes corrupt.

You would think that after the Flood, after they learned their lesson, it would be a long time before that happened again. But you go almost immediately to the tower, because very quickly in Noah's own generation they start misbehaving. So we get the tower and the period known as the separation. Now you notice you have the diaspora. This is the separation. The diaspora is the scattering of the Jews, after the temple was destroyed at the time of the First Temple (the time of Lehi). Then again, when the Romans destroyed it in A.D. 70. There have been scatterings and there have been gatherings, as the Book of Mormon tells us, from time to time. But this separation is the biggest of all. This is the scattering of all the people where it tells us about the Tower of Babel and the three sons in chapter 14 of Genesis. This is called "the separation," and there is no scripture for this.

This is very interesting-this huge gap between Noah and Abraham and the familiar formula goes: ten generations from Noah to Abraham, and there was no one who called upon the Lord. So then Abraham starts things moving again after this one.

It's very interesting that this is the best documented of all periods in the ancient records. This is where the Egyptian and Babylonian records, the Lamentation Literature, the Admonitions, etc. fill in the gap. We have all sorts of contemporary literature in this early period of the scattering of the people in all directions. And we have a scripture provided for it. Isn't that lucky? The only person that gave it to us was Joseph Smith. It was the Book of Ether. That fills that gap, you will notice. Fortunately, it is the most easily controlled of all because at this particular period, during this gap, the Jewish and Christian literature is silent, and all the rest is. But the non-Jewish, non-Christian, the great civilizations, they give us the fullest records of it. This is when the traditions are laid for all the myths, the Greek tragedies which Aristotle says are just naturally tragic by nature. The quarrels between the great houses, the scattering, the desolation, the horrendous crimes that go on-it's your Book of Ether story all over again, your Jaredite story. We get it in the Jaredite story and you can match this with all sorts of manuscripts. It's interesting that Joseph Smith has filled the separation for us. That's not what we're concerned with now. We've got to get up to Abraham. This is this rich subsoil of myth and drama. The Abraham story is told in Genesis from chapters 11 to 25. There are over 11 chapters devoted to the life of Abraham. We don't have any other biographies that long or that full. We read about Moses between the lines, but here we have 11 chapters (some say 12) of the life of Abraham. What more do you need? Why do we need a Book of Abraham here? This tells us a different story, which is a surprising thing. The thing is the other one has been edited by the doctors (we might as well mention that). Was more necessary? And what else do they tell us about Abraham?

We might as well mention what the Jewish sources are; we refer to them. We put down the various sources for the Enoch literature. Well, we also have the same type of stuff (totally different stuff) for Abraham. Of course, we begin with the Torah, which are the first five books of Moses. Now, this is a thing that is recognized very widely. Moses is simply recapitulating Abraham. He tells Abraham's story, and scholars today are inclined to think the Book of Genesis should really be called the Book of Abraham (the creation story of Moses) because he picked it up from Abraham. Of course, it was revealed to Moses too, revealed in each particular dispensation. But he recapitulates Abraham all the way through. Anyway, the Torah goes back to Moses. The five books of Moses are studied by the Soferim, the "book people," the "learned ones." These are the Scribes and Pharisees of the New Testament. They discussed and they argued about things, and they produced a repetition or review. (Incidentally, next week we will review the whole course and try to agree on some feasible subject for a definitive essay so we can decide what people are up to. We will review the stuff, and then we will decide what would be a good subject to write about.) So they repeated it, and Mishnah means a "doubling or repetition." They would examine the Torah and go through it again.

Why do you have to tell the story again? Because there are many things therein that are full of contradictions and leave lots of things unsatisfied. Well, we get other stories in the Pearl of Great Price that fill in lots of those gaps. We started out by saying that what the Pearl of Great Price did was fill in a lot of gaps. Well, these gaps are recognized, and they are filled in by the Mishnah where they discuss and they speculate on what might have been. The Mishnah was the work of the Tannaim, the teachers teaching of the Mishnah. Incidentally, this goes from the third century B.C. to the third century A.D. There were 148 great doctors that worked on it. The doctors were rewriting the Bible practically. The last thing was finally sealed up in the third century by Judah Ha-Nasi. Judah the Prince gave us the last version of the Mishnah, but it is still studied. These things are all being reprinted today. They are being studied as never before because there is lots of information here.

So you have the Mishnah, but were the doctors right about anything? You can argue until the cows come home, and that's what the doctors of the schools loved to do. So you had to repeat the Mishnah, and in that you have the Gemara (which is the same as the Talmud). It means filling out, completion. We are going to give the final, definitive answer now, which is the same thing as the Talmud, the teaching. (What's happened to the Bible all this time?) This runs in two issues. There's the Palestinian, which ceases in the year 370 A.D. (that's the last installation). The Babylonian is the great one. It's three times as long as the other one. We have all these texts here, but you won't be required to read them before the end of the semester because they are very long. The Babylonian Talmud goes down to the fifth century A.D., almost a hundred years later. They are discussing these, and they are discussing these. The people who take care of this are the doctors of the church. Sobarim means "to be patient." What they (the Tannaim) did was go through the Mishnah line by line, verse by verse. They went through everything with a fine-toothed comb. They were patient, plodding Sobarim. The Arabic word for patience is sabbar (Job the Sabbar). So these were the Sobarim.

This Talmud is divided into two parts. The one is the main part, the halakah, which is the law. Moses gives the law to the people, the fasts and the festivals, the moral laws, the judgments, and the courts. They are legalistic. That's an important part of it. The other part is the haggadah. These are the stories that illustrate things they could have picked up. I heard Rabbi so-and-so say so-and-so. They will give a long line of authorities to determine where this story originally came from. But they are supposed to illustrate and confirm arguments and explain what is going on. They have some wonderful stories in them, and the stories are gathered in the well-known Midrash (not too well-known). Like Talmud it means "teaching," darshan, the professor, the teacher. Darash is "to teach." Midrashah is the council. The Midrash started right in the beginning with the Tannaim in the third century B.C. As soon as they started telling these stories, they started remembering them. The Jews are great on that. So from the third century B.C. it went on down to the year 1040 A.D. when the schools of Babylon were officially closed by the Moslems. They could no longer have any schools and discussions (so it runs to there).

But all these give sources, and the most interesting of all are the ones that are being revived today more than anything else, along with this. We call this the Rabbinical literature. Of course, these people are Rabbis too, but there is the Hasidic, which is very important.

There are various arguments as to who the Hasidim are. Would it have been the original Rabbi Hasid of Regensburg in 1200, or was it much older? They say it is the Cabala; it's Cabalistic. But hasidam means "grace." They have the old tradition that went back to Adam, the one that these people have been lousing up all along with their discussions. The reason they had so many gaps in the first place was that the doctors, the Soferim (Scribes and Pharisees) threw out so much they didn't like. It turns up, and they argue about things. But the Hasidic is the Cabala, and there are two main sources of that. The first, the basic one of all, is the Sefer Yetsirah, the "Book of Creation," supposedly written by Abraham (Abraham's original story of creation). It's considered today by nearly all Jewish scholars as the most important book in existence. The Sefer Yetsirah is a complete mystery. It's divided into two parts, and it's all in code: the meaning of the particular letters and then the "emanations" that go forth, the sefirot, the 10 emanations that created the world. The 10 sefirot and then the 22 sacred letters. So we have Cabala, Sefer Yetsirah, and then the Zohar, which has the most important section about Abraham. These are all good Abraham sources. They are just full of stories about Abraham. So even with 11 chapters in Genesis, what were they missing? Well, it is that they have missed the point. Today, there is a new look. (We have to watch the clock like crazy now.) I'm going to mention again the series on Abraham, which is a long one. It ran from 1968 to 1970 in the Improvement Era. It's 200 pages. You can get it at F.A.R.M.S for three bucks. That's less than two cents a page. There's some useful stuff in here, and they have paged it properly which makes it very easy to find things. It can save you a lot of trouble by reading from these, which we won't have time to do. But first of all this new look on Abraham. This is the way they talk about him now.

We quote a lot of recent Jewish scholars, and we have been visited often here, as you know, by eminent rabbis who come to discuss things. We get along famously with them. "With these new-found documents the whole picture has changed now. Whereas, we are told, only one mortal being, Moses, does the holy scripture state that God spake to him face to face. With all the other prophets, the Deity speaks in dreams, in visions, in riddles, but only to Moses face to face.
