Today, we are told that the covenant made with Moses on Sinai is 'but fulfillment of the covenant made with Abraham,' which is the true foundation of the life of Israel ever since. Whereas it has hitherto been taken for granted that everyone knows that it was Moses who first knew the Eternal One, we now learn that Abraham and not Moses was the founder of Israel's monotheism. God is always described as the God of Abraham, Isaac and Jacob, not the God of Moses." We are quoting certain rabbis writing today. "The covenant of the B'nai B'rith (that's the Society of the Covenant in Israel) is today considered to be the covenant that God made with Abraham, the first Jew, afterward renewed with Moses [Moses simply renewed what Abraham already had; there's nothing new, so this is a new attitude], the central deathless theme and constitution of Judaism."

This goes on and on. And notice here. They say, "Ah, we have discovered something now, that Moses is simply continuing Abraham." Well, we could have told you that all along. What is now becoming clear is that Abraham is simply doing what Noah did, and Noah what Adam did. That famous saying, "ten generations from Noah to Abraham" is repeated in the Book of Abraham. The Lord tells Abraham here, "It shall be with thee as it was with Noah." You are going to carry on the work of Noah, which Abraham does. So Noah, and then Adam. The whole idea, as we mentioned before, is that Christians and Jews alike say it's all just a one-act play. We are quoting standard Jewish sources that tell us it was Moses and Moses alone to whom God appeared. He gave him the whole law and that was it. They no sooner came out with that than they began to spawn this.

Incidentally, these books go into volumes and volumes, the Babylonian Talmud (they are huge). They could never be satisfied, so more goes on. From here, are there no prophets after Moses? We are talking about dispensations here. Then in our Pearl of Great Price the next one is the Little Apocalypse (Matt. 24 ) Jesus Christ speaking about the dispensation that is to follow him in the last days. And we just saw in Enoch (we could include Enoch here with Noah; he was his great grandfather) "in the days of wickedness and vengeance." These were days of wickedness and vengeance. These were days of wickedness and vengeance (Jesus Christ), and these latter days are the days of wickedness and vengeance. So we have this totally different pattern here. If the world would recognize that, it would save them a lot of trouble, a lot of arguing about scriptures. Abraham is regaining the esteem which he once held with the Jewish doctors who called him arbah, the greatest of the faithful, the tzadik tamim. That's what belongs down here, the subject of the Cabala, the Hasidic literature. Cabala means "transmission, to receive." With a double b, it means "to pass on from one to another." The whole theory of the Hasidic literature, with these very important writings in it, is that the whole tradition, the whole plan or pattern was passed on among holy, chosen men (men of God) from one dispensation to the next, ever since Adam-that we have exactly the same teachings that Adam had. That's the teaching of the Cabala. The Jewish doctors reject it completely.

When Jerusalem was destroyed in 70 A.D., the Jewish doctors, led by Johanan ben Zakkai, went out in a delegation to the Emperor Titus (he was the father of Vespasian) and asked him for permission to go beyond Jordan to Jamnia and found a school because they hated the temple. They rejoiced to see the temple burn. That's a surprising thing, but they did. The rabbis held no priesthood. They were just learned men who were elected by the community and taught in the school. A rabbi has no priesthood at all. Some are very proud of bearing the name Levi. Then they claim that they are Levites cohens. But to be a rabbi is simply to be a learned man. They went out and established the school of Jamnia. Then you get the two great schools, the school of Jamnia and the school of Pumbeditha in Mesopotamia. These are the schools that flourished until the middle of the eleventh century. They went out and taught their own stuff and built up the whole thing. Of course, it was based originally on this, but they just "went to town" with this and condemned all this. Some say it was Baal-Shem in 1700 who was the first [p.6] Hasid. Others say it was Isaac, the Hasid of Regensburg in 1200. Others say it was others. But they say, No, it wasn't this or that one or that one. The Cabala has been handed down since the very beginning. There is this tension here, so we have very much need for a Book of Abraham.

We could go on here: "He is the first and greatest of those whose coinage is current in the entire world, who colonized the world for God so that whereas before Abraham the Lord was king of heaven only, with Abraham he became the king of heaven and earth." God depends on Abraham to make him king of earth. "Abraham entered into a covenant on which the world is based," says the Zohar. "And thus the world was firmly established for his sake [if it hadn't been for Abraham, God wouldn't have created the world]." So this new status of Abraham is an important thing, the new view of him. This is about the new evidence that has come out. Here's an interesting statement: "Abraham qualifies to stand as the most pivotal and strategic man in the course of world history." Everything pivots around Abraham; he makes all the difference. And you see in this second verse of the Book of Abraham how loaded it is. Notice how long it is, one single sentence. There's not a break in it. You can't put a break; it all goes together. It's a very strange sentence, very potent, very powerful. This gives the whole mission of Abraham and it explains his importance when the world gets lost. As I say, the ten generations again. He starts out: "In the land of the Chaldeans, at the residence of my father, I, Abraham, saw that it was needful for me to obtain another place of residence."

We haven't ended with it by any means here. Now, we have the apocalyptic literature for Abraham which has been found since the middle eighteenth century and nineteenth century. Two special works, the Apocalypse of Abraham and the Testament of Abraham , are enormously important works, the most important yet discovered. They are very old and very closely related to our Book of Abraham. They give us a complete surprise. They are being gone over today. They come in in the apocalyptic writings, the Apocalypse of Abraham and the Testament of Abraham . There are many other things, but these are the most important ones. This writing comes out very early and it gives us this picture. The haggadah are extremely rich in stories of Abraham's childhood and what he suffered, how he protested with his family, how they did try to put him to death and why they did it. We go into all the stories about that here. There's no need repeating it now. They were up in the North there, and he protested with his family. He says here, "They utterly refused to listen to my voice." They were going to have the annual celebration when they have to make a proxy sacrifice for the king, who doesn't want to be sacrificed himself. They would always choose the proxy at this particular time and place. And all the details are very well set forth in the Book of Abraham. Notice this first chapter is greatly concerned with ritual (the whole thing is ritual ceremony and religion). This answers the historical question: could this really happen? You bet it happens. This is exactly what they did.

Wainwright has written a very good book on the old Star Religion of Egypt, which is revived from time to time, especially would be at this time. Nobody can date Abraham. They date him between 600 B.C. and 2600 B.C.

There is a gap of 2000 years in the present-day dating of Abraham. One scholar at Yale puts him 600 B.C. Others, because of Ebla, put him 2600 or 2800. That puts him way back. Well, with that sort of a gap, time doesn't mean a thing. You're not going to get anywhere arguing about chronology. You might say, "Well, look, this puts Abraham in the wrong time." Nobody knows when Abraham lived. These things happen ritually every year all the time, but it is also history. What is performed on a national scale with great panache and splendor is an historical event. In fact, it is the most historical event. War is merely a side issue. Their wars with Ramses II were small-time affairs, but they were ritual. It is especially very clear in Rome. Every part of the act of war is part of the annual rites of going forth, etc.

So here he starts out by saying that he has to leave home. All his life he has to keep going (the ten trials of Abraham). He was never able to settle at all. Remember, he has to rent ground to bury his wife and a tomb at Hebron where Abraham is supposed to be buried. He had to buy the land from the Hittite, Ephron. The Hittites owned that territory at the time. Everywhere he has to keep moving. That's why the book about Abraham in the Zohar, as in the Midrash, is entitled Lekh Lekha, meaning "get up and get going." Abraham always has to keep going. Remember, the famine drives him. There was corn in Egypt, and so they keep moving. He always has to settle on foreign land. He's always in trouble wherever he goes. And he always does the generous and magnanimous thing. What a marvelous man! He has every trial you can imagine. And this is his motivation here, what keeps him going. He has a vision of something else. He knows something else besides the regular routines of this world. A new place of residence; well, he looks around and he finds that there is greater happiness and peace and rest than he has at home. He has to find another place of residence because he hasn't got enough happiness and peace and rest. That's what he wants, and frankly, that's what he is seeking for himself. "Greater happiness and peace and rest for me." Nothing wrong with that. That's the formula the Egyptians use after the name of the king. Some people put it after their names, when they are buried. "Life, security, peace, prosperity." So he seeks for the blessings of the fathers. That's the only way it can come, in the blessings of the fathers. Now he emphasizes the fathers here and the patriarchal order. "Ã‚Â…the right whereunto I should be ordained to administer the same [happiness, peace and rest];Ã‚Â…"

I think we might describe the world of Abraham. It's a very important thing. It's the same as the world in these others, but it is particularly vivid in the case of Abraham. Maybe we will read a few examples of what was going on in the world of Abraham. First, hard times, very very hard times he had to go through: the ten trials of Abraham. Listen to the situation he has in the Bible here. This is on page 149, what he goes through. His life is an unbroken series of tests right up to his death. The saying is "Rabbi was like a son, a devoted son, who was ceaselessly beaten by his father." But he says, "Let him go on beating him; he knows what he is doing." Abraham knew the law of obedience. First of all, "But Sarai was barren; she had no child [Gen. 11:30 ]." Then Gen. 12:1, "Ã‚Â…Get thee out of thy country, and from thy kindred, and from thy father's house,Ã‚Â…"

He has to get going. Nine and ten: Going on still southward, and there was famine in the land (nothing to eat). That's one of the reasons everybody is moving now.

Then he gets to Egypt. Then what happens? The Egyptians beheld the woman, and the woman was taken to Pharaoh's house. His wife was taken away from him. Then when Pharaoh found that Abraham was her husband after all, he was indignant and said, "What is this that thou has done unto me?" And they sent him away. In chapter 13: "And the land was not able to bear them,Ã‚Â…" There was strife. His men fought with his nephew Lot's men because there was not enough grass for the flocks. They had a terrible time (poison wells and things like that going on all the time). Then the five desert chiefs came and made war, the way they are always zeroing in on Israel today. They took Lot and his goods away. Then Abraham had to bring them back again. In Gen. 15:2 he said, "Ã‚Â…I go childless, and the steward of my house is this Eliezer of Damascus [still no child]." Then the sacrifice of Abraham. He was still a young man at this time when he was sacrificed (our Facsimile No. 1 here). "Ã‚Â…lo, an horror of great darkness fell upon him." The other accounts of it describe what the sacrifice was.

Then his wife has this terrible affair with Hagar (all these squabbles at home). In chapter 16 she says to him (the great and marvelous Sarah, his wife), "My wrong be upon thee: I have given my maid into thy bosom; andÃ‚Â…I was despised in her eyes: the Lord judge between me and thee." She wouldn't judge, but because Abraham was childless she allowed him to take another wife, Hagar. Then Hagar started making snide remarks because she had a son, and making life miserable for Sarah (not a happy domestic situation). Then Sodom and Gomorrah, those terrible cities. But he pleads for them. He tries to get them saved, as you know. If there are only ten righteous people, will you please save them? And he abases himself before the Lord, "Wilt thou also destroy the righteous with the wicked? Oh let not the Lord be angry." Then came the big nuclear blast, and the next morning he looked out from his camp high above. He had chosen the bad ground above so that Lot could take the good ground below. It's a good 1500 foot level, and you look down on the whole valley. He looked down, and it was just like a furnace. It says flickering fires all full of smoke and dust, as if there had been some tremendous blast going (and there was). It's the most active volcanic and earthquake zone in the world down the rift there. The Gulf of Aqaba leads right up into the Djauf. "Ã‚Â…and lo the smoke of the country went up as the smoke of a furnace." So he gets out of that. He moves to the coast to be safe. After that explosion, it wasn't safe to be where he was. Then he took his family and went to the coast. The king of Gerar, Abimelech, was in charge there. They got along all right, but he took Sarah to wife also. Sarah was a raging blond and a terrific beauty, they tell us, but that wasn't the reason. They wanted to raise up a royal line by her (her name means "princess, queen"). Then he is in trouble again. "Ã‚Â…They will slay me for my wife's sake." Then he has to get rid of Hagar, his wife who had a son, Ishmael. "And Abraham rose up early in the morning, and took bread and a bottle of water, and gave it unto HagarÃ‚Â…and sent her away:Ã‚Â…" That was no fun. "And Abraham reproved Abimelech because of a well of water, which Abimelech's servants had violently taken away." Then comes the supreme test, the sacrifice of Isaac (Gen. 22:2 ). "Take now they son, thine only son Isaac, whom thou lovestÃ‚Â…and offer him there for a burnt offering." And then finally at the end of his life in chapter 23: "I am a stranger and a sojourner with you: give me a possession of a burying place with you, that I may bury my dead out of my sight."

Well, that's the career of Abraham from the beginning to the end. He's a wanderer. In the book of Deuteronomy you are supposed to come forth and make a basket of fruit (all the goodness the Lord has given you) and you are supposed to bring that to the altar. Every man is supposed to do this and say, "Our father was a wandering Syrian, an outcast, a tramp [an Amorite is the word they use], and you brought him to this blessed land and gave him this." But this was Abraham. He was the "outcast." That's what Abr means. It says, my father was a Hebrew, which means "a person from the beyond, a wanderer, an alien, a stranger, a person without a passport." The Hebrews have been that ever since. That's what they are-wandering, settling, getting into trouble, etc. It's an interesting history. It all goes back to Abraham. So why the sacrifice of Abraham here? This is the situation because of this confounded drought in this period. There have been periods, we know now, of major climatic change in the earth. In Abraham's time it was terribly bad. Remember, it says there was famine in the land, and they moved on. They could not settle in Canaan. They still had to move on into Egypt. The reason they went into Egypt was because there was corn in Egypt. The only place they could buy corn was in Egypt, and that's why Joseph sent his sons into Egypt later. There was a terrible time of drought, that whole period. We have the lamentation from this time. When the Nile failed to rise (if Egypt was the only place to find grain and they didn't have any in Egypt, you were hard up). And the vivid, full descriptions it gives from these early times in the Egyptian record: the Famine Stela, and the one called the Admonitions of an Egyptian Sage, and Neferohu. These are descriptions of how bad things could get in Egypt and what happens. They are very interesting ones. But this is the world of Abraham.

They have to make sacrifice. They have to do something about it, and they do. Abraham goes up, and he is to be offered. The whole theme of the Abraham story in the Book of Abraham is the rivalry between Abraham and Pharaoh as to the priesthood and kingship. That's why Pharaoh, we are told in the Midrash, wanted to marry Sarah-to raise up a righteous line, which is what Abraham wanted to do too. So he took Sarah away because she was the princess. He was to raise up a line. But there is this constant conflict between the two. And you notice in the first part Abraham is sacrificed on the altar in the place of Pharaoh, which was considered a great honor at this time. This goes back to the Sed-festival. The most important festival in Egypt was the Sed-festival. From prehistoric times you find it. The earliest monuments of Egypt have the Sed-festival. There are Sed-festival monuments over in the museum there now. The Sed was the celebration, originally at the end of thirty years, when the king started to get old and feeble. The king had to be sacrificed, and then he would be restored in his son Horus who would be regarded as the resurrected version of the king. He would disappear for three days, and suddenly he would come out as Horus (as himself). It was convenient also to come forth without being put to death that way but just disappear for a while and then come forth, and [p.10] have somebody else put to death in your place. So you have the sacrifice. You have the proxy, which is done. The rules are very strict and they were followed here. There are a great many instances of this happening. Abraham was chosen. As I say, it was considered a great honor. You had to be a noble and you had to be a stranger, and you had to have either yellow or red hair. They preferred red hair to anything else. So you were in real danger if you visited Egypt and were a redhead.

You notice it starts out by giving four gods in Canaan. Those were the four gods of Canaan that ruled the four parts. It says in the Book of Abraham, "Now, at this time it was the custom of the priest of Pharaoh, the king of Egypt, to offer up upon the altar which was built in the land of Chaldea, for the offering unto these strange gods, men, women, and children." Well, what was the Egyptian king doing in Chaldea? In the eighteenth dynasty there was Ramses II. What was he doing up there at Megiddo? Well, he conquered that. That was Egyptian country in those days. That's where this was, Chaldea. It was a temporary time. At the time Abraham wrote this, or at the time his children were supposed to read it, apparently it was no longer part of the Egyptian Empire. It came and went, depending. In the eighteenth dynasty Thutmose III took the whole shebang clear over to the Euphrates. Then they lost it all. Then they got it back again in the twentieth dynasty. Then finally, in the twenty-second dynasty the great Sheshonk went out and conquered it all again, and even more. Then it was lost again. Then Alexander the Great took the name of Sheshonk, said he was the son of Amon, made himself Pharaoh, and went back and conquered the whole thing again following the very same pattern. Each one of these conquerors called himself a cosmocrator. The story of the cosmocrator is important because Abraham's rival, Pharaoh, always calls himself the cosmocrator. You know what that would be. The cosmos is the universe, and the crator is a person who rules. It's a Greek title, but this is a Latin form. The cosmocrator was the man who claimed to rule the world as God's successor on earth. The mortal rival of Abraham was a cosmocrator who went by the name of Nimrod. All the legends say that the Pharaoh who tried to sacrifice Abraham was this Nimrod. You know Nimrod, the mighty hunter of the Bible. Is it the same one? That's a long story; we won't go into it. But the point is that Abraham in the legends has this mortal rivalry with the person who claims to have the priesthood and the kingship. Does he have the right to rule, and does he have the priestly right? Well, that's what Abraham had, and that's what Pharaoh coveted.

When he tries to sacrifice Abraham (this is the story that is told in the Midrash here), Abraham was put on the altar, and the fire was ready. You first cut the throat of the victim and then you cast him on the fire. It is always done that way. You notice in Abraham's sacrifice of Isaac, Isaac was carrying the wood for the fire. But Abraham raised the knife. It was the sacrificial knife, and then the fire follows. Well, as the priest raised the knife, a great, mighty earthquake struck. It overthrew the altar. It killed the priest, and Abraham wasn't touched. Well, this impressed the king, who immediately let him go. And he didn't have to sacrifice Abraham anymore because the sacrifice had already been made. The high priest was the substitute.

Now that someone had been sacrificed, he had no argument with Abraham. And he did honor Abraham by letting him sit on his throne. The Pharaoh had ordered the people all to bring cedar wood and make an enormous bonfire to burn Abraham on. Then the priest's knife was cast from his hand. The priest was overthrown and killed and Abraham was let go. Then he ordered them to gather wood and build a special place to put Abraham's throne on. Then he said he had 365 of the highest nobles of the land come with their children to Abraham to sit on the throne and be taught the principles of astronomy by Abraham. Well, that's what we have here in Facsimile No. 3. Figure One: "Abraham sitting on Pharaoh's throne, by the politeness of the king, with the crown on his head, representing the Priesthood, [notice the two feathers; that is the spiritual crown, the shew feathers] emblematical of the grand Presidency in Heaven; with the scepter of justice and judgment in his hand." Then it says, "Abraham is reasoning upon the principles of Astronomy, in the king's court." We are told that's exactly what he did. We are told in the Bet Ha-Midrash (I have a Bet Ha-Midrash text), page 140 in the fifth volume, that he sat upon the throne by Pharaoh's permission, and all the court brought their children to be instructed in the principles of astronomy. We are told he began his preaching, "Blessed be God who created the sun, the moon, and the planets." That's the way he began his sermon to the children of the Egyptians after Pharaoh had accepted Abraham. He accepted Abraham at court, and Abraham became a great favorite with him. Then you have the story of Sarah and the like.

Pharaoh took Sarah to wife. Remember, Abraham said, "She's my sister." This has lots of meanings. But he wanted to raise a posterity by Sarah, who was the princess, the queen. Sarah was true to the law of her husband. With this we have a source we forgot (the most important of all). We are always mentioning the Dead Sea Scrolls. In 1950 we discovered the Genesis Apocryphon, the Genesis written by Abraham. This is the Genesis, first thought to be the Book of Lamech because of the outside. This tells the story of Abraham in Egypt with Sarah (it really goes into things). You notice there are insights into Egyptian doings. This is from the first century. This goes back 2,000 years; it's an old story. This is all much later than that. Why did the rabbis throw this away? They had it. This is in a Jewish source. This happens to be written in Aramaic. This tells the story.

Of course, Pharaoh wanted to have Sarah for his wife, but Abraham and Sarah had made a covenant to each other, and Abraham in this story (1Q GenApocr, the Genesis Apocryphon) is outside the palace walls all night and prays to God to spare Sarah. And Sarah finds herself on the king's bed. Now, this is a bed (it's the standard bed). It's also an altar, the standard form of Egyptian altar. It is also an embalming table. You can use it for all these things. It is also a table for birth. We have many representations where queens give birth. Queen Hatshepsut, for example, is being born, and her mother is on one of these beds. So this is where life is conceived, where it begins, where it ends. Everything happens on a couch, always shaped like the lion here. It's the lion couch that represents the processes of destruction in the earth, represents the second principle where everything returns back to the earth. It's the fierce lion that consumes all things.

So he prayed outside and she prayed inside, with the understanding that if she didn't submit to Pharaoh, she would be put to death (that was the understanding). You don't refuse Pharaoh that way. So she prayed and Abraham prayed. Just as Pharaoh was to approach her, an angel came to the rescue at the last minute, exactly as he came to the rescue of Abraham at the last minute. This is told in the Genesis Apocryphon. It's not an ancient text, as far as we know. It's the oldest we have, and it's the most recently discovered. Just like this, it goes through the whole thing. The angel comes with a whip, and he forbids Pharaoh to take another step. The Bible tells us this Pharaoh and all his house became barren, including animals, until he had finally delivered Sarah and returned her to Abraham. Then he sent Abraham out of the land loaded with royal gifts as a prince. He recognized him as a prince. Especially, he recognizes Sarah. He sends her out as a queen with a crown and a robe, and all the rest of it. And he sends him on his way. In a story in the haggadah, it tells us that once Pharaoh was riding in his chariot with Joseph, and he says, Joseph, you're my best friend, but I'm going to have to ask you to step down from the chariot because when the people cheer as we go through the street, I don't know whether they are cheering me or you. I understand that you are very popular, but in Egypt there can only be one king; there can only be one high priest; there can only be one ruler as there can only be one God. So we don't want to confuse the issue. Will you please get down. (This is the thing that happens all along. We have the same thing here.) This is a Babylonian version, not from the Talmud, but from Babylon.

When Abraham is miraculously delivered from the altar, the Pharaoh (who is Nimrod over there too) says, I recognize your God; I want to make a sacrifice to him. I want to sacrifice 4,000 oxen to him (it's an embarrassing situation). Abraham says, I'm glad you do recognize him, but you can't do that. You don't have the priesthood. You're not authorized to do that. You're blessed as to the kingship but cursed as to the priesthood (which is what we have here in verse 27). "Now, Pharaoh being of that lineage by which he could not have the right of Priesthood (notice, it was his lineage to blame), notwithstanding the Pharaohs would fain claim it from Noah, through Ham," That would have been all right, but it happened that he claimed it through his mother. The Egyptian priesthood comes through the matriarchal, not the patriarchal line. In the verse preceding it says he was blessed with the blessings of the earth (notice, he was a good man). "Pharaoh, being a righteous man, established his kingdom and judged his people wisely and justly all his days, seeking earnestly to imitate that order established by the fathers in the first generations,Ã‚Â…" Now, that's a very important word, "imitate." You say, but look, did the Egyptians have the gospel? They have so many ordinances and things like that resembling ours. No, they didn't, but they had a darn good imitation. So if you want to know what the original was like, this is the best chance you have (studying this) because the imitation was a good one. But he knew he didn't have it. I have a section in that book on Abraham in Egypt, where there are many texts in which Pharaoh is fretted and worried and doubts his authority. But here: "that order established by the fathers [the patriarchal order]." He is seeking earnestly to imitate that patriarchal order of the first generations, "Ã‚Â…the first patriarchal reign, even in the reign of Adam, and also of Noah, his father, who blessed him with the blessings of the earth, and with the blessings of wisdom, but cursed him as pertaining to the [p.13] Priesthood." He couldn't have it because he came through the wrong line, it says. Through Ham? Well, Ham we are told was a righteous man and walked with God too. But it was through the line of Cush and Nimrod. They rebelled and left the priesthood. It's the famous story of the stolen garment. The priesthood garment of Adam was stolen. It should have belonged to the other brethren. They all had an equal claim, but when they were coming out of the ark, Ham stole the garment, made a copy of it, and claimed he had the priesthood. For that reason he was denied the priesthood until all the others should have it first. But this says he would fain claim it from Noah. That's the very thing we have here and the story we have in the Genesis Apocryphon. This Babylonian I was mentioning where Abraham says to him, I'm sorry you can't make the sacrifice because you're not authorized. You don't have the priesthood. You are blessed as to the kingship but cursed as to the priesthood. And again, the king must do what Pharaoh does here. He says, I'm sorry, I'll have to have you leave the country because we can't have two kings. If you have the true priesthood, then I would have to bow to you in Egypt. I would have to accede to you. So he invites him to leave with all honor, you understand. He recognizes his kingship and his priesthood (but it's uncomfortable to have you here because we can't have a rival Pharaoh; we can't have a sub-Pharaoh). Well, Joseph almost held that job. His great-great grandson, Joseph, was going to hold the same job and be almost equal with Pharaoh. Later on in Exodus we find that the people esteemed Moses as Pharaoh.

So we have this dealing with Egypt all the way through. People ask, "Why are we interested in Egypt?" The Egyptian delegates that came over here asked, "Why are you people so interested in Egypt?" That's why they let us have the exhibit because we seemed to have a genuine interest in Egypt. It comes out all over the place. Nephi starts giving us his record, the Book of Mormon, "in the language of the Egyptians and the learning of the Jews." It was at that time. That was the time when Demotic flourished. It was just a short period, and it was marvelous writing. It was a shorthand-the greatest little space saver you ever saw. Up in room 22 in the Cairo Museum, they have a remarkable inscription. It is above here; it is in Egyptian here; it's in hieroglyphic. Down here it's in Greek. Big blocks, so much Greek, so much hieroglyphic. In between there's just a little strip about eight inches wide that has the whole record on it of both these others in Demotic. It was that conservative. That's why Nephi, Lehi, and the others who wrote on the plates say it was very difficult. (It was hard to do; my hands got tired.) Mosiah makes his sons learn to read it so they can keep the record; they keep the record in a different language. Well, the Anglo-Saxon Chronicle was kept in different language-the archaic keeping of records. (Oh boy, the time is up now.) So we have all these things mixed up. The best thing to do is read the Book of Abraham. Of course, we have this week on Abraham. It's next week we're going to talk about the other. Not the next time because we still have a good deal to do, but this isn't very long. You notice the interesting thing is he starts out in chapter 3: "And I, Abraham, had the Urim and Thummim,Ã‚Â…" And that's how he found out about the creation. Then he starts giving us this valuable cosmology we can't go into here. I'm still sloshing around with this stuff. This is one of the most remarkable documents in the world, and we can't talk about that either. But the last part then is the creation, a different creation. It's the best creation story we have probably and most scientific. There's the Moses creation story, so there's not so terribly much to read. But oh how it's packed with stuff. The information in there is dynamite. It's encapsulated, believe me. What do we do, unravel it?

We can break it up now, but I'll tell you more if you want.

Lecture 24

The Destruction

Now we are on Abraham. We don't have much time for Abraham, or anything. But we have original statements by Adam, Enoch, Noah, and Abraham in the first person. Abraham is written in the first person. It is interesting that the apocalyptic Abraham literature that's come up in the latter part of the nineteenth century is in the first person, "I, Abraham." That sort of thing had not been known before. Apocalyptic literature was always in the third person as in the Book of Moses. But Abraham speaks in his own person here. And we have Moses. And we have a summing up by the Lord himself of the whole situation from an inspired interpretation by Joseph Smith that really makes sense of things for the first time. Then we have Joseph Smith's story itself. So we have all those dispensations in there, each represented by an original source.

All the leaders of dispensations, like the dispensations themselves, follow a certain pattern. But each leader and each dispensation is also quite distinctive. It is different from any of the others in certain ways. So that would be true of ours too. Abraham, as we saw, has been called the most pivotal. With each one of these you get a description of the world in which the person found himself and the way he reacted to it. But he can't do it on his own; he has to receive a call. He doesn't just get the call; he has to seek for it first. He finds himself a stranger and a loner in the world. He's in the position of Lehi. All the heroes in the Book of Mormon are one against the world, a man who can't get anyone to follow him at all as the prophets of old. That's the normal pattern. And Abraham is the most lonely of all. He doesn't have a single friend at times.

It's a time, we mentioned before, of natural disasters. First of all, signs in the heavens. I am going to read a few excerpts from each of these headings here. You notice this is characteristic of other dispensations too-in fact of all of them, including ours. So we start out with signs in the heavens as number one. At this time a great comet appeared periodically in the North and destroyed the crops. Jewish tradition here tells of a time when a great dragon-like monster (now we have Halley's Comet with us) had taken over the earth until God cut it off suddenly; and also the planet that comes out of Scorpio and spews gall and drops of unhealthy blood and fouls the waters of the earth. A great comet appeared periodically in the North and destroyed the crops and the kings of the East and the West. The Greeks called this one Typhon. In Palestine it was called Resheph. Resheph and Typhon were the same person. He is the Canaanite Resheph, the sky god, who came to Palestine and to Egypt also as a fiery meteorite rushing through the heavens (it was probably a comet-now we realize that comets are real things and really can hit and make trouble) whose sacred symbol was the iron meteorite in his shrine. Resheph is closely bound up with Abraham, and we are told that the stars fought for Abraham the night he marched against the marauding kings of the desert and slew his enemies by the almighty power of God.

The Egyptians were, according to Wainwright, convinced that the destructive falls of meteorites were, according to Wainwright, convinced that the destructive falls of meteorites were an affliction peculiarly reserved for the wicked. But the heavens were very active at the time of Abraham. This is a thing that has been commented on a good deal: the great interest in astrology and astronomy. A recent book by Vermes, a Hungarian scholar, points out the enormous importance that was placed on Abraham as an astronomer and astrologer by the Jews. Though the rabbis fiercely deny it, it's constantly connected with him. Ancient sources suggest quite often that disturbances in the skies, especially these blazing meteorites, were the cause of this intense preoccupation. People were watching the heavens like never before at this particular time. They concentrated on them. Notice that the Book of Abraham is very much occupied with cosmology and astronomy. He has given us a chart there, and he explains things. All these things are very closely connected together. This is part of the picture in Abraham's world beginning with the disturbances in the heavens. Remember in Enoch all the heavens shook; everything was in disturbance (the universe itself). The earth shifted on its axis is the explanation to that one. It was a time of earthquakes and volcanoes too.

According to our Book of Abraham the Lord broke down the altar of Elkenah and the gods of the land and utterly destroyed them. Here we read in the Pseudo-Philo, a very important source: "Just as Abraham prayed on the altar [for God to deliver him], there was a violent upheaval of the heavens and the earth and the mountains and all the creatures in them. Then God sent a great earthquake, and fire gushed forth from the furnace and broke out into flames and sparks of fire and consumed all them that stood around, 83,500 of them. But upon Abraham there was not the least hurt by the burning of the fire." This attempt to sacrifice Abraham is often connected with Nimrod who tried to build the Tower of Babel. It was overthrown by the wind, by a great destruction. The attempt to sacrifice sometimes takes place at the site of the tower in northern Mesopotamia where the rites are interrupted by "a vast burst of roaring flame which destroyed many people and saved Abraham, but it did not bring the people to repentance. Traditions constantly associate earthquakes with fires bursting out of the earth." That's Sodom and Gomorrah, of course. That's part of the Biblical story of Abraham. They were overthrown, Gen. 19 tells us, while fire enveloped them both from above and below. And the rivers of the region turned to bitumen. They still are bitumen. The five cities of the plain were at the south end, and they were destroyed. The bitumen pours out here. At En-gedi is where you will see the Jewish people come to vacation. They bathe in the water there and rub this bitumen on them. It's black pitch that comes out of the sea there, very black and very heavy. They think it cures rheumatism, so you will see these fat old Jewish ladies completely covered with this black stuff from head to foot to cure their rheumatism, wading around in the powerful, briny waters of the Dead Sea. Everything is active; there are fumaroles and everything going on there. This was very active at that time. It's very shallow here. You can walk across there at the Lisan, that tongue of land that sticks out into the water there. The Arabs know the way, and they smuggle contraband, cigars and things like that, across at night. You must be very careful. If you miss your footing and fall into that water, it's much brinier than the Great Salt Lake (it's terrible). It was very active at that time.

This is the great rift that goes down to the Gulf of Aqaba and goes right on up into the Djauf and the Bekaa depression. The rift that matches it and runs right along with it, is the Nile Valley. Before they had plate tectonics, they didn't know what caused them, but now we see what they are. This makes it the most active earthquake zone in the world, along here. Things are always going on here. That's what you get in Abraham. The cities of the plain are destroyed. It's very dramatically described in Genesis. We can't go into that, but it's really a great story. "And earthquakes and fumaroles and fissures, rumbling sulfurous smells, all go together in the story as they do in nature. For 52 years, according to a well-known tradition, God warned the people of the cities of the plain with preliminary rumblings and earthquakings. He made the mountains to quake and tremble, but they hearkened not to the voice of admonition. [It's like St. Helens, isn't it?] The last 25 years were particularly ominous with the earth subsiding and quaking almost continuously. All through the life of Abraham, even before the fall of the cities of the plain, we meet with these earthquakes."

We have quoted the Clementine Recognitions so much. They are so old, and they connect with Enoch and all the rest. We use them all the time. Here they are again talking about this. "In Abraham's time the world was afflicted by fire which, beginning at Sodom, threatened to destroy the entire world. After the destruction of Sodom and Gomorrah, Abraham had to leave his beloved Mamre." The plain of Mamre was up here. This is where Lot was, and the road immediately goes up here. It mounts 1500 feet. You look down on the whole plain all along here. These are the great cliffs that go along. This is Qumran up here. There's no break in them. But these great gorges are very much like our Red Rock country, or like Rock Canyon. They are very deep, very precipitous. That's where you find hundreds of manuscripts in the caves, up there in a place you would least expect them, all along here. But the road only goes up here. Then you look down and you can see the whole darn thing, right from one end to the other. It says when Abraham woke up in the morning and looked out over the plain, he said it was all just covered with dust and a vapor of smoke, with fires flickering here and there. It had just been wiped out completely. It sounds like a nuclear holocaust, doesn't it? But he had to leave. He was camping up here, and he had to leave and go over to the coast where it was safer. There he ran into King Abimelech and had to go through the same routine he went through with the king of Egypt-taking his wife Sarah, telling him she was his sister, getting her back again, all the other things. So we are dealing here with ritual patterns that go on, but they really took place is the point. Visiting another monarch you had to fulfill certain obligations. Abraham was in constant danger here of being dragged in. You notice the Book of Abraham makes that clear. He lives in a terrible world. He hates it, and he tries to disengage from it. They not only want to sacrifice him; his fathers have turned to idols again. He tries to dissuade the family. They won't hearken to him. On the other hand, they volunteered him for the annual sacrifice. He had to get out, leave the land and go to Canaan. It was just as bad there.

We are told here, "Because the plain of Mamre had been completely blighted by the catastrophe [it's pretty blighted today; it's a very dusty plain; they've tried to save it by planting eucalyptus trees here and there, but it's not a pleasant place], all plant life was destroyed, and seeds transplanted from Sodom would not grow anywhere. No wonder Lot's daughters, hiding in a cave, thought they were the only surviving mortals. The entire landscape was desolation, we are told. There were almost no travelers. Everything stopped moving." Archaeology confirms that grim picture here. And along with earthquakes, volcanoes, etc., you are going to have food troubles, world famine. This is an important thing. We just go to our scriptures, and especially to the Book of Abraham, for this. "Far more conspicuous in the Abraham tradition than these raging storms and floods is the blasting heat and drought that bring famine to the scene. In the Book of Abraham, the prophet, even before the conflict with the people of Ur of the Chaldeas, learns from the Lord that there is going to be a famine in the land." (Abr. 1:29-30 ) After his escape from the altar the famine descends in earnest, blighting the whole land of Chaldea. He leaves the country, and his first act on crossing the border into Canaan is to sacrifice to God, praying we are told (Abr. 2:17), "that the famine might be turned away from my father's house, that they might not perish." He hoped that all of his folks back home wouldn't die of hunger. It's a very severe situation. Does he find rescue in Canaan? No, not at all. Even in Canaan the famine got worse and worse, forcing the patriarch to go clear to Egypt for food. In Abr. 2:21 it says "the famine became very grievous." It's a world famine. It's everywhere; you can't escape. It was bad in Egypt as a matter of fact. Of the ten great famines which afflict the world, according to Jewish tradition, the greatest was that which hit in Abraham's time, the first world famine. This was one of the ten trials of Abraham, the hunger he had to face. Everywhere he goes, he is hungry. The flocks have trouble grazing. They are driven out, etc.

This takes us to the troubled nations and the social problems. When things are desperate and insecure, people get dangerous, insecure, nasty and mean with each other. This is very characteristic of Abraham's world here. Just an excerpt here: "It was because of the wickedness among the people that the birds came to destroy their crops when Abraham was a boy." He had a job driving the birds from the fields. The birds were desperate, but he made an agreement with them. He devised a special kind of plow, and he was famous for his inventiveness. Remember, he was a scientific wizard. This is emphasized all through the Abraham literature, and we get it here in our Book of Abraham (after all, he is the star man). He is the man who discusses things with the learned men of Egypt on these subjects. This plow would plant seeds and at the same time turn over the earth to protect them from the birds; but only after he had made an agreement with the birds and set aside some food for them too. It was because of wickedness among the people that the birds came to destroy their crops when Abraham was fifteen. As it was in the days of Noah, so in the days of Abraham, a very old Christian writing explains. The world was ripe for destruction according to the principle that whenever men fall away completely from God, destruction must follow. "Indeed, the people had sunk so low," says one very old source "that God caused their civilization to degenerate back into the stage of cave dwelling and brought Abraham out of the land." Things went right back to everyone for himself, and it was a desperate situation. This was after the Flood, and for a long time people had been haunted by fear, knowing that it could happen. What could we do in case of another such world disaster? After the Flood men were haunted by an understandable feeling of insecurity. To overcome this, they undertake great engineering projects, some of which are very famous.

This Nimrod we talk about was great for his technological knowledge and engineering projects. "Among these was the famous tower which was to be a symbol of man's ultimate mastery of nature. It was so ingeniously designed and solidly constructed as to be absolutely safe against flood, fire, and earthquake." But we are told in many sources what happened to it. You will get that from the Book of Ether too, the Jaredite story. Remember, the winds did never cease to blow for 344 days. It was the terrible winds. We are told the tower was overthrown by winds or tornadoes, a violent force, and most of it covered with sand. This happens, and there are some very interesting archaeological and geological studies by the Dutch on that particular subject. Notice, it was to be safe from flood, fire and earthquake, but they didn't mention wind. There are three floods: the water flood, the wind flood, and the fire flood. They come in that order. The water flood came in the time of Noah. The wind flood came in the time of the tower, long after. And the fire flood (the great overburn) comes at the end of this dispensation, in the last time. "Within the walls of the tower was to be stored the sum total [they had computers in there] of man's knowledge of the physical universe enabling him to meet and master any situation that might arise." It was all done, says our writer here, out of fear of another flood.

That's interesting, that awareness of the weather, etc., because Sister Blackmer from Alaska, a very competent woman who works with the Eskimos, was down here and she left me some very interesting material. It's so close to what you find with the Hopis and Navajos. They have professional weathermen who are very skilled at predicting the weather. They know all about it. The people pay great heed to them. They don't undertake any great project unless they consult the weatherman. They compare their notes, etc. That's what these people were doing back there. None of this is implausible. This sort of thing really happens. "A great economic boom and commercial expansion enabled them to undertake all kinds of engineering projects for controlling a dangerous nature. But the Lord fooled them by altering the course of nature and creation." All the Lord has to do is shift it a little bit. As Derchain says, the temple is like a control center or a power house. All you have to do is move a lever or shift a switch and tremendous powers are released. You don't have to do very much if you have the control there. That's the way God does it. All he has to do is wiggle a finger. But men can duplicate that performance. That means we are not safe. If we overlook one little tiny factor, that will spoil everything and our machine blows up. So that was in Abraham's day. The Nimrod legends are full of marvelous gadgets and structures-super buildings, mechanical thrones and altars, flying machines, etc. It was a time of great scientific and technological progress. The Abraham stories, including the Book of Abraham, are unique in their concern for a scientific understanding of the cosmos as against purely religious and moral teaching. Remember, this is a physical science that Abraham is teaching us in that Facsimile No. 2 and the others and, of course, in his text. Those hot winds were breathing down everybody's neck. In desperation men turned to worshipping idols. Why idols, of all things, in a scientific age?

It was, we are told, because in the whole world people were without a teacher or a lawgiver or anyone who could show them the way of truth. Of course, there was Abraham, but they didn't want him. So this was the situation. And willpower renders things quite dangerous.

Here's a famous saying from the midrash, "When you see the great powers fighting against each other, look for the feet of the Messiah. The proof is that in the days of Abraham, because the great powers fought against each other, greatness came to Abraham." The root of all this, as you might suppose, was the desperate greed (there are some marvelous passages on this). Everybody was out for himself, so we get this situation. Here we have the Jordan Depression that goes clear down, and then you go right over to Egypt. It was a veritable paradise when Abraham first visited it, before the Lord destroyed Sodom and Gomorrah, it tells us in chapter 13 of Genesis.

"It's not surprising that the men of Sodom were a wealthy and prosperous people on account of the good and fruitful land whereon they dwelt, for it supplied them with every earthly need of man. Nor is it very surprising that they did not trust in the shadow of their creator, but in the multitude of their wealth they trusted for wealth thrusts aside its owner from fear of heaven." Here Rabbi Eliezer seems to be quoting the same sources as Samuel the Lamanite. Then he goes on, "They had no consideration for the honor of the real owner of their wealth." Samuel the Lamanite says the same thing, You do not give thanks to the one who gave you your wealth. "Behold, you do always consider your riches [you are always thinking of the economy]," he says. "Therefore, your riches shall pass from you. They shall become slippery that you cannot hold them." Samuel the Lamanite is quoting from Enoch there though (very striking passage). But here this sounds like the same situation certainly. They were mean. This is thing that Abraham would not tolerate, any kind of meanness. He was just the opposite of that. "They had no consideration for the honor of the real owner of their wealth by distributing food to the wayfarer, but they even fenced all the trees on top above the fruit so that no one else could have any, not even the birds of heaven." This is the authentic Babylonian tradition, eye witness accounts.

We have documents from this time, and from earlier and later times, that describe just such situations. They tell how the people of Babylon "oppressed the weak and gave him into the power of the strong. Inside the city was tyranny and receiving of bribes. Everyday without fail they plundered each other's goods. The son cursed his father in the street, the slave his master. They put an end to the offerings and entered into conspiracies." The picture is just as lurid from the lamentation literature of Egypt at the same time. "The people of Sodom and Gomorrah were not condemned for their ignorance of the God of Abraham, but rather for their meanness, their immorality, and their greed. They were destroyed because they did not strengthen the hand of the poor and heeded not the needy. For them everything existed for the sole purpose of being turned into cash." There's a whole cycle of stories from the Midrash. They put a toll on their bridges with a double toll for wading the river. They would let a merchant come into the city, but they wouldn't allow any foreign merchant in town to buy food while he was there. They would wait until he starved, and then they would take it all over.

There is the story of Lot's daughters who were kind to some poor people in Sodom. Immediately, they were subject to severe punishment for it. "Of course, the richer a man was, the more favored he was before the law, for it was wicked to encourage idleness by helping the poor. Anyone helping the poor in Sodom got thrown into the river." And so it goes.

Here again from the Ugaritic Letters on the coast here. You just go up a little ways here to Ugarit where they found in 1929 and 1930 the great Ugaritic library, the Ras Shamra library from the fourteenth and fifteenth centuries B.C. It's very old stuff and very full. This is merchants' accounts, etc. But here we read the same thing: "The records of Ugarit, which some [including me] hold to be contemporary with Abraham, show that the practice of killing merchants was widespread in that part of the world, even as the Amarna Letters show us the world in which it was every man for himself. [The Amarna letters are at the same time.] Having no love for the stranger, the people of Abraham's homeland had even less to waste on each other. Finally, there was so much crime and murder among them that everything came to a complete standstill. Being grossly materialistic, they rated the hardware high above the software [famous saying]. If a man was killed in working on the tower, he was ignored; but if a brick fell, they sat down and wept. Abraham, seeing this, cursed them in the name of his God." One cannot help thinking of the church builders in the Book of Mormon. You adorn yourself with that which has no life and allow the poor, the sick, the afflicted and the needy to pass by you and notice them not.

Another quote, "They were dwelling in security without care and at ease without fear of war, sated with all the produce of the earth, but they did not strengthen the hand of the needy or the poor. And it is said, 'Behold, this was the iniquity of thy sister Sodom.'" We think of Sodom in terms of sexual depravity before everything else, but in all the stories and all the accounts it's meanness in any particular regard you're talking about. Well, what else do we have here? So there is this wicked world and all these greedy people and all the troubled nations. The result is economic collapse, as we see here. Everything comes to a halt, and everybody has to move (migrations). It's a restless world. Everybody is on the move, going somewhere else. Abraham is the Hebrew, the first person to be called the Hebrew. Hebrew means "displaced person." It means a person with no home, one of the avarim who comes from the other side of nowhere and who has no claim on local hospitality. So we have Abraham's order to move on here. He migrated because God told him to. If it was a test, it was a severe one. Professor Albright has recently pointed out that the ancient pioneers, far from finding a golden West awaiting them, were ethno-political intruders in the West. As such they were not well received, but were closely watched and were usually driven away by the local inhabitants who bitterly resented any attempt on the part of outsiders to move in and take over their fields or pastures. So it was a rough time, and you didn't find hospitality anywhere. Even in Canaan, the Babylonian threat followed the patriarch, who was forced to leave Damascus (according to a very ancient source) because of military and political pressure from the East. In Canaan Abraham's nephew Lot, catching the spirit of the times, declared that he preferred the suburban Sodom to the society of his uncle, saying, "I want neither Abraham nor his God" and moved down into the crowded and prosperous plain, down here, which was about to be destroyed.

So the theme for Abraham is, and the Book of Abraham is usually titled Lekh Lekha, "Get up and keep moving; get going; on your way."

This is the world of Abraham. Now Abraham himself. As a leader of dispensations, he stands alone. This is the great mystery, the great excitement, about Abraham: he is so absolutely alone. He starts things moving all on his own. How is that particular thing? Students of Abraham are impressed by the way in which he seems to start from scratch. The whole world had ground to a stop, was dead center. Who could possibly get it moving again? Well, of course, God could send an angel or something like that, but regarding Abraham is the famous saying, "There must be a stirring below before there can be a stirring above." You have to make the first approach, as we read in Abr. 2:12, "Thy servant has sought thee earnestly; now I have found thee." But he had to seek diligently first. You must seek before you find. You must ask before you receive. You must knock before it is opened to you. So Abraham seems to start from scratch. With the world all going in one direction, he steadily pursues his course in the opposite direction. This marvelous independence of Abraham is wonderful. Granted that the tradition of the fathers, of which the Book of Abraham speaks so eloquently, was still known; yet his own father and grandfather had lost faith in it and departed from it. We mentioned before there were "ten generations from Noah to Abraham, and not one of them had walked in the ways of the Holy One until Abraham our father," says Rabbi Nathan. He asks, "Where then did Abraham get the idea of starting things moving? The common explanation is that Abraham was self-taught. God appointed the two reins of Abraham to act as his two teachers. That still doesn't make him a privileged character for all men have the same promptings of the Spirit if they will only listen to them. For charity was also asleep and he roused it. The power was there, but it lay dormant from neglect. 'When all the inhabitants of the earth had been led astray by their own pride and self sufficiency, Abraham still believed on me,' [and so I made a covenant with him because he was true and faithful in the first place]. Abraham received his covenant only after he had made the first move." Speaking of him the Zohar says, "The prophetic spirit rests upon a man only when he has first bestirred himself to receive it." Again, "the stirring below is accompanied by a stirring above, for there is no stirring above until there is a stirring below." That's the rajjar; that's from the Zohar too. But who is to start the stirring? This is the point. You say, Well, nobody does it. You justify yourself by what everybody else is doing, etc. Lehi, alone in Jerusalem, said, "Which of the prophets was not alone?"

"It was to Abraham's merit that he loved righteousness in a hard-hearted, wicked generation without waiting for others to show him the way." A wonderful illustration of this is set forth in the 1831-32 account of Joseph Smith which was just found in 1967. He recounts how for three years as a boy he sought diligently for someone that apparently interested nobody else. Finally he said, "I cried unto the Lord for mercy, for there was none else to whom I could go, and the Lord heard my cry in the wilderness." We get that in the last book of the Pearl of Great Price. This was exactly the case with the young Abraham. No one was on the track at all. "For generations the world had moved ever farther and farther from God until by Abraham's time it had become what the Pearl of Great Price describes as the worst of all worlds.

Then Abraham single-handedly reversed the trend. The Shekhina, the spirit of God came on the earth at the creation, but through human sin it removed itself farther and farther from the earth. Then Abraham brought it down again. He was, says the Midrash, "like a man who saw a building all on fire and no one willing to put out his hand to save it." Abraham was the first one to get involved. As people get more in a "me" generation, more self-centered, they don't get involved in another person's affairs. But Abraham is involved, so he gets himself in trouble all the time. He did the only thing he could do. Exactly like Joseph Smith, he appealed directly to God at an early age. It was he who made the first move, according to Abr. 2:12, "Thy servant has sought thee earnestly; now I have found thee."

The wonderful thing about Abraham is that he always does the right thing no matter what anybody else does. He had to get along with all sorts of people, most of them rascals, and he treats them all with equal courtesy. He never judges any man. And then there are various examples. We go through quite a number of them. And the saying from Midrash Rabbah, "If Abraham does not play fair, who will?" His passion for fair play breaks all records in his pleading for the wicked cities of Sodom and Gomorrah to whom he owed nothing but trouble. He knew all about their awful wickedness, but still, Josephus observes, he felt sorry for them because they were his friends and neighbors. He appealed directly to the Lord's sense of fairness. This is Gen. 18 ; remember the various steps. It's such an interesting psychological study here. And he says to the Lord, "Wilt thou also destroy the righteous with the wicked?" He knew God wouldn't do that. (Gen. 18:23 ) "The impressive thing is the way in which Abraham is willing to abase himself to get the best possible terms for the wicked cities, risking sorely offending the Deity by questioning his judges. He says in the next verse, Far be it from thee "to slay the righteous with the wicked. Shall not the judge of all the earth do right?" Then he says, "Behold, now I have taken it upon me to speak unto the Lord, which am but dust and ashes." He is abasing himself more as he gets less and less people. Fifty righteous people will spare the city. Twenty-five, yes. Ten, yes. How many righteous people will it take to spare the city? Oh let not the Lord be angry, and I will speak again. I have taken upon me to speak unto the Lord." Again, in verse 32, "Oh let not the Lord be angry, and I will speak yet but this once moreÃ‚Â…" It wasn't an easy thing to do, but he is doing it. So we have Abraham here doing all these things for everybody, and he is the one who helps everybody. Notice, in this second verse in which he sets forth his program, it sounds like sort of an ego trip, doesn't it? He wants to be the father of many nations and king and have the priesthood. Why does he want it? He makes it very clear here. "And, finding there was greater happiness and peace and rest for me, I sought for the blessings of the fathers, and the right whereunto I should be ordained to administer the same [he wants to administer the blessings, pass them around], having been myself a follower of righteousness, desiring also to be one who possessed great knowledge, and to be a greater follower [improvement] of righteousness, and to possess a greater knowledge, and to be a father of many nations,Ã‚Â…" The reason he was is that he would administer the same to them. "This is my work and my glory." It was Abraham's work and glory too. "Ã‚Â…and desiring to receive instructions, and to keep the commandments of God, I became a rightful heir, a High Priest, holding the right belonging to the fathers."

Then it was conferred to him, but he is seeking it. He wants to become the father of many nations and sought for the blessings of the fathers to administer them to the rest of the human race (and that he did). As we are told, through Abraham and his seed all the nations of the earth would be blessed. And we are all the seed of Abraham. So he stands alone.

Now this mystery of the paradox-how he could stand dead center and do all this good? How does he do it? What is he going to do in this world? This is a terrible world in which he is being pushed around everywhere, moves from one place to another all the time, never settles down, never can. By faith, of course. He receives revelation. He sought the Lord and finally finds him and receives direction. But just like the founders of the other dispensations, it's what we would call a holistic picture. He gives the big picture. The emphasis on family, the emphasis on cosmology, and the emphasis on eternity. It's not just here; it's the eternities he is interested in. Those three things dominate with all of these, especially with Abraham. They really culminate in him. So in our Facsimile No. 2 that's what we see-a holistic picture, a four-dimensional picture of the universe there (a marvelous thing which we can't go into now). But having received the revelation, what does he do? He goes forth as a missionary to spread the word. And this is going to be hard. This is why he wants it; he wants it to administer. In Abr. 2:14 it tells us, "Ã‚Â…and I, Abraham, was sixty and two years old when I departed out of Haran. And I took Sarai, whom I took to wife when I was in UrÃ‚Â…and the souls that we had won in Haran, and came forth in the way to the land of Canaan, and dwelt in tents as we came on our way." The souls are the ones they had converted in Haran. He and Sarai immediately started work at conversion. In Gen. 12:4-5 there is the puzzling statement that when Abraham left his home he took with him "the living things." The rabbis argue what can the living things mean? The puzzling living things they have named was interpreted by the rabbis exactly as it stands in the Book of Abraham. The Midrash explained that it means the people converted in Haran. He goes even further to explain that when we are told that Abraham called on the name of the Lord in Sichem (Gen. 12:6 ; the first big place he camped in Canaan) it means he summoned the people to the Name. He began to preach the gospel to them and convert them.

According to the Sefer ha Yashar, the people of the land of Haran saw that Abraham was good and just toward God and man. Men from among the inhabitants of the land of Haran came to him and attached themselves to him, and he taught them discipline of the Lord and his ways. We have him here in Facsimile No. 3 teaching astronomy and the principles in the court of Pharaoh. "As he moved on his way, each altar raised by him was a center of activities as a missionary, he and Sarai diligently preaching and making proselytes wherever they went and pitched their tents." Midrash 39 says, "Abraham converted the men and Sarah the women." There was a tradition that all proselytes and heathen are the descendants of the infants of the pagan mothers, whom Sarah nursed. But all the followers of Abraham, including Abraham himself, were converts. It doesn't come by blood. As Lehi and Nephi explained to their sons, he who is righteous is favored of God. But you had to accept it. Abraham founded his Zion, and those who wished to follow became the followers of Abraham. By special rites and [p.11] ordinances they were adopted into the family. All the following of Abraham were adopted, which means the whole world is eligible. So he founds the church with the ordinances of the temple. This is a thing that is being played up a good deal these days here. "Today, Abraham is being described as the restorer rather than the initiator of the knowledge of God [so it came earlier than Abraham]." First Moses restores Abraham's knowledge. Now we are told that Abraham is restoring earlier knowledge of the holy rites and ordinances. He both "recapitulates all that went before and anticipates all that is to come," as one Catholic writer points out. This is in perfect agreement with the declaration at the opening of the Book of Abraham that Abraham's immediate ancestors had fallen away from the order established by the fathers in the first generations in the days of the first patriarchal reign. According to Maimonides, the great Rambam, it was Abraham who found his way back from idolatry to monotheism.

Incidentally, Maimonides is their greatest philosopher, but he wrote in Arabic, not in Hebrew. This return to the old faith is symbolized by his rebuilding of the ancient altar of the first fathers, especially Noah. Remember, from Abraham to Noah and Noah to Abraham. According to the Samaritan tradition, in Abraham the covenant of Noah is renewed, just as the covenant of Adam was renewed by Noah when he built his altar after the Flood. In the Book of Abraham that patriarch is also represented as resuming the work of Noah, in Abr. 1:19 and 26-27. Where Noah left off, you will take up. Like Noah, Abraham's work represents "the restoration of harmony that was broken when men fell away in the preceding dispensation."

According to Jewish theology, Mount Zion, on which Abraham built an altar to sacrifice to God, is the cosmic rock uniting heaven and earth, and all the great sacrifices of the past were offered there (it was the primal temple) on an altar which was demolished by the generation of the division (that's that separation, we mentioned before, after the Flood at the wicked times of the tower) but it was restored by Abraham. This was supposed to be the very spot whereon Adam had brought the first sacrifice and Cain and Abel offered their gifts to God; the same whereon Noah raised an altar to God after he left the ark. Abraham knew it, and that was the place appointed for the temple to be built later. We are told there were seven who built altars before the temple existed: Adam, Noah, Abraham, Isaac, Jacob, Moses, and Aaron (notice this succession again). The most important of these was Abraham. "It was he," according to Maimonides, "who recommended that Mount Moriah be consecrated as the place of the ordinances forever after and who dedicated the spot for the future temple [as Moroni dedicated a spot for the future temple at Manti, we are told]. For when Abraham was shown all the dispensations of the future in a vision, God also showed him the future temple and the service and the law." He knew all about it.

"Before he went down into Egypt," wrote Baring-Gould, a famous English Jewish scholar and very important statesman, "Abraham was shown the entire temple worship by God, after which he returned to Haran and instructed the people in the true religion. He erected three altars and gave thanks to God." Remember, on the Mount of Transfiguration, Peter said, "Let's set up three altars here," representing the three degrees of glory, the temple, etc. So Abraham gets the temple going here, simply restoring what had been there before (as a missionary and with the ordinances). Like others, his rival Nimrod is for kingship, the priesthood, and the heavenly order. This comes out all along. There is an imitation of this going on all along. Abraham has restored it again in competition. Nimrod thought he had it. We shouldn't go into the Nimrod story, but it connects very closely with the name of Abraham everywhere, and also (strangely enough) in our facsimiles in the Book of Abraham. But like the others, Abraham also left a full written report. Here it is in the first person. It is necessary to leave that, and we have it. In every dispensation, we are told, the gospel of the kingdom is preached for a witness, and then the end comes. So this is the next step. The world has always rejected the message. Remember, Adam and Eve mourned before the Lord. Their sons fell away from him. Adam's successor was Seth, and the children of Seth all fell away and joined the Cainites and the like. What happened was the great blight on the world, blackness. It was a sad place. Enoch was rejected, and he was inconsolable. "I will refuse to be comforted," he said. But he did select a people. He gathered them out. Those that were willing to go were gathered together, and in the process of time they were taken away. So there were none left behind at the time of the Flood. Incidentally, Rabbi Ishmael says that the Book of Enoch was written to prove to the world that God was not cruel in sending the Flood. They asked for the Flood because only the wicked people were left behind. The rest were given fair warning. The question is, well what about the innocent animals? There's a very interesting passage on this subject in 2 Baruch and 4 Ezra which says, "What is the fear of death? What is it that's bad about death?" It's the anticipation, the fear of it. He says that animals don't have that at all. They don't worry about that. They don't see it coming at all. When it is there, it's there, and that's all there is to it. "No animal can even imagine its own death," says Ezra. That's the thing we think about all the time. Human beings brood about this thing, whether they are wicked or not, I suppose. So He was not cruel to the animals. In fact, He changed them in a twinkling, if you judge by some of the remains that are found from the Great Freeze.

This gospel shall be preached for a witness, and then shall the end come. The gospel is always preached, not to convert (it doesn't convert) for a witness, as the Lord says, so they will be left without excuse. "Now, they have both seen and hated both me and the Father," he says. It was for a witness, not that he expected to convert them. Enoch was rejected and his Zion went away. Noah warned in vain, and then came the Flood. After Abraham came a tremendous world collapse. Awful things happened then ending the period. Again, it's the idea, where do you date him? But the nature of it is very clear from the Abraham record, the great droughts and the great winds, which did wipe everything out. Then again after Moses, the Exodus is part of a period of world upheavals again.

Then came the time of Christ who was completely rejected. "Despised and rejected, a man of sorrows and acquainted with grief," he had no place to lay his head. How did he end? Being publicly executed with not a friend to stand by him. This was being alone really. Then what happens? Well, the Roman Empire was just one continual war of annihilation, but the point is that shortly after there came the great plague in the time of Marcus Aurelius which wiped out eighty percent of the population of the whole Western World. Things were really happening. So this is the pattern that we follow here. We ought to bring it up-to-date.

Next time we will have to talk about chapter 24 of Matthew, which follows here. This is a very important document summing up dispensations. We've got onto the dispensation theme here, but it would not be complete if we didn't bring it up-to-date. So I'm giving you an assignment. These are two talks by President Kimball. The last one was printed in the Ensign exactly one year ago at President Kimball's express request. 1 So that's important, and this is important here. This is a description of our dispensation, a very good one. Of course, you are going to find that in Joseph Smith 2, but this brings things up-to-date. There was some empty space, so I have inserted a recent piece from a newspaper in January of this year. It's a statement by someone which will give you an idea of the condition of our situation here. This is required, and it will help you with the final. We want to bring it up-to-date. If you want a picture of our world, this is it. It is seven pages long and costs 35 cents. I have change here, and I'm open for business at a small profit (no, there's no profit). This is necessary if you want to see the picture because he is going to quote the very passages we have been quoting and relate them to our dispensation, bringing them right up-to-date. [p.1]

^A copy of this talk from the June 1976 Ensign has been included with the lectures.

Lecture 25

Joseph Smith-Matthew

Now we have the very important next to the last book, the one about Matthew. Matt. 24 (the so-called Little Apocalypse) is an extremely important document that Joseph Smith has given us. This is where the Lord summarizes the three major times of wickedness and vengeance, including his own. Matt. 24 is apocalyptic because the Lord tells you what is going to happen to the world. Notice that the Joseph Smith version of Matt. 23 is quite different. The verses are arranged differently, and that's for a very good reason. He talks about the loss of the gospel, the falling away, etc. here numbers of times (when it comes).

This is a thing, of course, which the later church would not tolerate-the idea that it got lost, that there was a gap. If anything, the Lord had brought it. He had saved us, and now it is the triumphant march of the church. The human race is saved, and that takes care of that. They did not like to admit the gap. They did not like to admit that he would come more than once. That was known as chiliasm, and that was absolutely out. In fact, 400,000 Donatists were slaughtered in North Africa with the approval of St. Augustine because they preached that there would be a Millennium and the Lord would come again. That's chiliasm (chili is the Greek for thousand). If you believed that sort of thing, you were out. So the verses had to be rearranged to make only one coming of Christ. That's why the thing is all mixed up. It is such a mess that nobody can make any sense of it now. For that reason, in the Catholic Church, this is not recommended (in fact, it is not permitted) as a subject for sermons.
