You can't give a sermon on this because it is so confused, and you might lead people astray. I have some interesting documents on that. We won't refer to this here.

In Matt. 23 the Lord sums up his dispensation. Remember, it's at the temple at Easter time. They are there for the Passover, and he gives this great sermon in the temple. As he leaves the temple, then the apostles come with the question (Matt. 24 ). But notice the situation here in Matt. 23. The dispensation has rejected the message, absolutely and completely. The very people who should have accepted it are the ones who have scorned it and rejected it and caused all the others to reject it as well. How many times does he repeat here "woe unto you, scribes and Pharisees, hypocrites"? In verse 15: "Woe unto you, scribes and Pharisees, hypocrites. Verse 23: "Woe unto you, scribes and Pharisees, hypocrites." Then again in verses 27 and 29. So that's the refrain. The scribes are those who study the scripture. He is talking to the people, and he says, "They sit on Moses' seat, and you harken to what they say." Because they preach the gospel, they are the very people that should be most ready to welcome the Messiah. The scribes (we mentioned the Soferim before) are those who study the scripture and who teach it. And the Pharisees are the Parishi, "those who are set apart." Parasha means "to set apart." Our word is part, parat, meaning "consecrated, chosen, sanctified."

They regard themselves as especially pure. The scribes and the Pharisees are the ones who should lead. They should be the ones expecting the Messiah and the first to welcome him. They were the only ones who didn't. They would have absolutely nothing to do with him, and they turned the people away from him. So he goes down the list. We don't want to linger on it here, but it is very much to the point. He repeats that refrain.

"Then spake Jesus to the multitude, and to his disciples saying, The scribes and the Pharisees sit in Moses' seat: [do everything they tell you to do, but don't do the way they do; that is don't do their works]." (Matt. 23:1 ) So every time after this he refers to them as hypocrites. Hypocrite is simply the Greek word for actor, a person who is playing a role which is not his real role, who is pretending to be somebody he isn't. Every time he refers to them on those grounds. They play a good role; they talk good talk, but they are hypocritical about it. They do all these things simply for appearance. He goes down the list of various things about them (the scribes and Pharisees, the sanctified, etc.). Title, rank, office, and authority count for everything, he says, among them. The authorities are the great obstacle instead of the great aid to progress. They are crazy about statistics, he says in verse 15. The temple is merely a matter of statistics with them. They are wholly engrossed in business, the accounting, the record. Remember, the mint, the rue, the tithes, and the taxes. They are so minute and careful about keeping the records that they ignore the main law. He says that's important (this you should do) but you should not neglect the other. They simply reverse things. They are blind leaders of the blind. Three times he calls them "blind leaders" here. They don't know where they are going themselves, so he says they are very careful. Administration is great with them. Administration is important, but they have completely inverted the values, he says in verse 24. They have exploited their position. They seem very sincere and are very impressive (verse 28), but they are false and unscrupulous. They identify themselves with the prophets, the founding fathers.

From the beginning though, the true messengers have been rejected and thrown out. From the beginning in every other dispensation, he says, from Abel on down that has been the way it has gone. His dispensation has fared no better than the others. When the Messiah finally came for his dispensation, they would certainly accept him this time. But how did they treat the Son of the Lord of the house? They treated him worse than any of the others had been treated (remember the parable on that subject). So the famous saying (verse 37): "O Jerusalem, Jerusalem, thou that killest the prophets, and stonest them which are sent unto thee, how often would I have gathered thy children together, even as a hen [bird] gathereth her chickens under her wings, and ye would not! [you didn't want it]." In the next verse he says it is all over. This is the last chance you are going to have, he says. So now you are finished, he says in verse 38. I won't bother you again.

Now Joseph Smith's story begins here with this different verse arrangement. You notice that verse 1 of the Joseph Smith text is verse 30 of chapter 23. Jesus had never talked as strongly as this before. It's his farewell. He knew he was going to be crucified, and he said astonishing things. This is very strong against the scribes and Pharisees. The apostles are really upset. They are worried about him; and they come to him secretly (embarrassed) and ask him, Lord, just what will happen? What is going to happen? And this is what he tells them. (This is how it happens.) This is the pattern that is laid down here. This is very important in our list of dispensations because this is the Dispensation of the Meridian of Times, the most important of them all. He tells them, as did Enoch, about the three times of wickedness and vengeance. First, what will become of the Jews; what will become of the Christians; and then what will come in the next dispensation. He talks about the Restoration and what is going to happen then, about our own dispensation. So it brings us right down to date.

So we begin with verses 1-20, and they deal with the end of Jerusalem (what is going to happen to the Jews, the fate of them). But notice the verses are different here. Our verse 2 is verse 1, and I have underlined here in red all the words that are not found in our text of Matthew, which makes a great deal of difference. They are hollow without them, and they become extremely perplexing. But when you realize what he is talking about in this first verse, it makes sense. He says, you will never see me again. This is the last you will see me until I come again. All the Greek text says is, "I will not come again until you say, Blessed is he who comes in the name of the Lord." What did he mean by that? "For I say unto you that you shall not see me henceforth and know that I am he of whom it is written by the prophets [they rejected him], until you shall say: Blessed is he who cometh in the name of the Lord, in the clouds of heaven, and all the holy angels with him. Then understood his disciples that he should come again on the earth after he was glorified and crowned on the right hand of God." (Joseph Smith 1:1). In the Christian record in the New Testament he appears 27 times after the resurrection. The resurrection wasn't the last time he appeared. But when he appears to the Nephites, he is accompanied by angels. In 3 Ne. 17:24, he appears accompanied. But they didn't like "until I come again," so they just rubbed that part out because they didn't believe he would come again. St. Augustine said, To think he would come again is an insult to the church, because after all, didn't he come and found it? Where are we if we need him to come again? Until very recently (now everybody is talking about it), they considered the idea of Restoration or restoring the gospel quite insulting. They would say, "Don't we have the gospel? Wasn't it given to us?"

So we start out: "And Jesus went out and departed from the temple; and his disciples came to him,Ã‚Â…" They said, What about the buildings? As they went through the temple, they commented. There was a lot of building activity going on at that time. They were asking about what was going to happen here. "Master, show concerning the buildings of the temple, as thou hast said-They shall be thrown down, and left unto you desolate." That's not in the King James, that part. "And Jesus said unto them: See ye not all these things? Verily I say unto you there shall not[beÃ‚Â…[that's the King James]" But here he says, "See ye not all these things, and do ye not understand them? [as it was in all the prophets] Verily I say unto you, there shall not be left here, upon this temple [that's what they are talking about specifically; that being left out in the third verse made argument as to what he was talking about], one stone upon another that shall not be thrown down." (Joseph Smith 1:3) And this fourth verse is very important. It says, "And Jesus left them, and went upon the Mount of Olives." Somehow puzzlingly, the next verse in the King James says that they came to him when he was on the Mount of Olives. Well, they were just leaving the temple with this conversation. But here it says that they came to him when he was on the Mount of Olives, and they came to him (as it says here) privately. Each one wanted to know for himself. They are puzzled by it. What's really going to happen? Will you please tell us? Can you let us know? "Tell us when shall these things be which thou hast said concerning the destruction of the temple, and the[Jews;Ã‚Â…[fourth verse]" This is the first destruction he is talking about after his own time, the destruction of the temple and the Jews. Notice that "temple and the Jews" is not in the King James version. "Ã‚Â…and what is the sign of thy coming and of the end of the world,Ã‚Â…"

Now the end of the world is mentioned three times here, and each time it specifies what it means by the end of the world. The King James simply says "end of the world." Well, you say, that's the end of everything. Everything just passes away or is vaporized. There's no universe; there's nothing. No, that's not it at all. As it says here, "Ã‚Â…and of the end of the world, or the destruction of the wicked, which is the end of the world?" That's what we mean by it. That is the perennial question. They are always asking that, right through the Middles Ages. The year 1,000 became a year of great panic because they said it's the end of the world; all things will end. Time and again that has appeared, but especially in the year 1,000. A Romanian scholar wrote a book on that, on the year 1,000 (what was his name?). But in the English Chronicles, the year 1,000 was a time of panic and alarm because they thought everything would come to an end. But what do you mean by the end of the world? This is what you mean: the destruction of the wicked, which is the end of the world. And then this comes in. He is going to give it in chronological order. Again, it is very important for him to say, "Take heed that no man deceive you," because this subject was more argued about than any other subject throughout the centuries to follow. When is the end of the world? When is the Lord coming? Just what's going on here? So the Lord says, you must get this straight; it's very important. What is given in Matthew here is a very confused document that makes nothing clear, so people decide they'll just leave that alone. But "take heed that no man deceive you."

Now it comes in chronological order. First, "For many shall come in my name, saying-I am Christ-and shall deceive many;" Now this is Christ, the Messiah. Other false Messiahs came as Christ immediately after Christ and during this very time. This is what the Jews do to the apostles: "Then shall they deliver you up to be afflicted, and shall kill you, and ye shall be hated of all nations, for my name's sake." This is the seventh verse here, but it is the ninth verse in the King James Version. The verses are going to be moved around here. They shall say, I am the Messiah and deceive many. Well, who came? There was Hanan ha-Nehba, which means "the hidden one." He had a career in Babylon as a clerk. He came to Jerusalem and ingratiated himself with the scribes and Pharisees, with the high authorities in the temple, etc. He started giving out that he was the hidden one, that he was Messiah. People started following him, already at this time. And there was Elchasai and Honi who spent seventy years in a cave and gave out that he was the Messiah. Everyone was asking in that day, Josephus says, "Where is the holy one? When will he come?" Everyone was looking for him. Herod claimed to be the Messiah, as a matter of fact. Herod Agrippa claimed that very thing. Well, his brother Pheroris was the one who first put forth the claim that he was actually the Messiah, and he promoted a Persian by the name by Bagoas who was a Carian eunuch who claimed to be the Messiah. Brother Welch has my two big volumes of Eisler on that subject. Robert Eisler wrote a huge work on this-all the false Messiahs in the time of Christ. The woods were crawling with them. It was no crime to claim that you were the Messiah at the time of the Passover, because who would know which was? When the Messiah came, he was not going to be recognized by many. So it was no crime for the Lord to say he was the King of the Jews. Many people claimed that and got away with it.

This is the condition you find in Josephus, what follows immediately after the crucifixion. It becomes all hell, just like it is in the Middle East today. "And then they shall be offended, and shall betray one another;" Everyone becomes offended here. Here we have a blank, blank, blank. "And then many false prophets shall arise, and shall deceive many;" This is what we were talking about, and they did in this time of stress and hysteria. "And because iniquity shall abound [what happens? the usual reaction], the love of many shall wax cold;" It's everyone out for himself, and everyone is desperate. Iniquity abounds, and the love of many waxes cold. "But he that remaineth steadfast and is not overcome, the same shall be saved." Again, "remaineth steadfast and is not overcome" is not in the King James. They will be martyred (they expect to be). Now, this is his instruction for the Jews, what is going to happen for the people living at that time. This is verse 15, and here it is verse 12: "When you, therefore, shall see the abomination of desolation, spoken of by DanielÃ‚Â…" That's khormah or khereba. Khormah is abomination, desolation. The land desolation was the land that had been destroyed by an enemy and was cursed and could not be inhabited again. It figures in the Milkhamah in the Battle Scroll in the same way it does in the Book of Mormon, where they use desolation the same way. Bountiful is the other side of it. There was the dar al-harb. Harb is hereb. That means "desolation." It's also the Arabic word for war. And the dar al-Islam, the land which is saved, the land which is submitted. Those are the two parts of the world. You are either in dar as-salam and the Roman ager pacatus, the peaceful world, or ager hosticus, the hostile world.

The abomination of desolations is the one who is on the other side and who will take over. Time and again Israel was occupied, taken over completely, by the great powers and utterly wiped out. When it was destroyed, I mean they destroyed (almost as thoroughly as we can do it today). They couldn't touch our techniques, but they were thorough about it because they had plenty of time to work on it. Anyway, "Ã‚Â…concerning the destruction of Jerusalem, then you shall stand in the holy place; whoso readeth let him understand [this is secret, you see]. Then let them who are in JudeaÃ‚Â…"

Notice specifically that's where Jerusalem was. These were the Jews; this was the kingdom of Judea. Every time Jerusalem is seized, what do they do? The place is surrounded by hundreds and thousands of caves and mountains. The deep gorges there, that's where the Dead Sea Scrolls were found in several hundred caves. You notice those gorges-the Wadi Hever, Nahal Hever and Qumran, all those places. The huge cave, the Muraba'at cave, in which so many documents were found is quite near Jerusalem. Nobody even knew it was there, and it was packed with documents. All these things had been so near to Jerusalem all this time and nobody knew about it. Well, you flee to the caves is what you do. It's the safe thing to do, and you hide out until it gets over, just as Lot did when he escaped from Sodom and Gomorrah at that time of great destruction. "Then let them who are in Judea flee into the mountains;" And when you are on the housetops, you flee, but this time you don't return to take anything with you. You are not coming back this time.

We have some wonderful letters. Nahal Hever is right behind En-gedi. The gorge steeper than Rock Canyon on the sides. Barefoot Arabs climb up the cliffs there and discover these caves full of documents (the Cave of Records is up there) of the time the people left Jerusalem. It was the destruction at the time of Christ. They went up there, hid out there, and died in the caves (they couldn't get out). The Romans camped on the hills above them and watched everything they did from the opposite side of the valley, didn't make any effort to get to them. They tried to get out by night down these very dangerous paths, etc. They buried their records deep in the dust in that cave, hundreds of documents describing that time. One is particularly interesting because it's a deed to a farm owned by a man called Alma , the son of Judah. We've always laughed at that name before, but this man's name was Alma, the son of Judah (and there is that deed). The first thing you see on your left-hand side as you enter in the house of records there, the Shrine of the Scrolls, with a light behind it shining through the parchment is this deed to a farm owned by Alma, the son of Judah. They left all their farms and everything. One very rich woman thought she would make it to Egypt. They were trying to get out the same as in the Book of Mormon, fleeing to Egypt in the time of Lehi. They couldn't make it; they died up there. A tremendous tragedy was going on, and the records are very moving.

So this is what's going to happen. Don't return to take anything out of your house. Don't go back for a watermelon; it won't do you any good. And if you are in the field, don't turn back at all because there is no point to going back. Don't go back to town once the city has been taken; it's not going to be freed again. "Neither let him who is in the field return back to take his clothes; [just go]." This sounds like a cry of woe and a threat (even gloating) but it isn't; it's a fair warning. The Lord is telling them ahead of time. "And wo unto them that are with child, and unto them that give suck in those days;" Make sure you won't have to leave in a time of pregnancy. Make sure you don't have to be there in the same way. "Therefore, pray ye the Lord that your flight be not in the winter, neither on the Sabbath day;" Arrange things so that you won't have to flee in winter. Look forward to it, but I'm giving a fair warning. It's no time for pregnancies and no time for winter. It's giving a fair warning so you can make your proper arrangements and prepare. But this is the way you do. You do what the Jews have always done.

Incidentally, these caves with the scrolls go clear back, some of them, to the first kingdom of Egypt. They have been hiding out in those caves for three thousand years. Every time it was invaded, they would go to these caves. Fleeing to the mountains is what they did (it was routine). So that's what the Lord tells them to do now, but don't come back this time. This is the worst that has ever happened or ever will happen to the Jews. They became a non-people for two thousand years after that. They became nothing. They were completely dissolved. "For then, in those days, shall be great tribulation on the Jews, and upon the inhabitants of Jerusalem [notice he's is talking about that destruction, the destruction that's to take place at that time, not the apostasy], such as was not before sent upon Israel of God since the beginning of their kingdom [all this is supplied by Joseph Smith and is very necessary to have] until this time; no, nor ever shall be sent again upon Israel." It lasted for two thousand years. It says here in verse 19, "All things which have befallen them are only the beginning of the sorrows which shall come upon them." The King James simply says, "These are only the beginning of sorrows." It was. The fall of Jerusalem, the driving out of the Jews, was to go on in sustained misery for the next two thousand years. They had no citizenship, no rights anywhere (pogroms and everything else everywhere all the time). So this was the worst. There could never be anything worse than that.

Why didn't it wipe the Jews out? It so very nearly did. Look at the Holocaust. That wasn't the only holocaust; there were many of them. In 1648 there was a tremendous holocaust under the Polish Hetman Shmielnitski. He was really not Polish; he tried to overthrow the Polish government. But he wiped out the Jews of Poland in the great pogrom of 1648. Why weren't they wiped out? He says just for this reason. They would have been He says. "And except those days should be shortened, there should none of their flesh be saved [again, you notice Joseph Smith adds very valuable notes here; no, the Jews wouldn't survive unless their captivity was shortened]; but for the elect's sake [for the righteous among them], according to the covenant, those days shall be shortened." The King James says only, "Ã‚Â…for the elect's sake, those days shall be shortened." But it's because of the covenant He made with the fathers.

For example, in the Book of Enoch he covenanted with Noah that his descendants should be on earth until the end. And he makes it very clear in verse 21. This looks rather puzzling because this stands in the King James this way: "Behold, these things I have spoken unto you concerning the Jews [that's what I'm talking about now, the Jews]; and again, after the tribulation of those days which shall come upon Jerusalem [he's talking about that particular one], if any man shall say unto you, Lo, here is Christ, or there, believe him not;" The Christians are in for it now. Then there would be false Christs come, and they were a dime a dozen. Of course, this is the whole Gnostic movement. The whole second century is the century of heresy, and Irenaeus lists some 88 different heresies (Gnostic and otherwise) led by men like Valentinus and Marcus who pretended to be the Messiah.

Simon Magus, one of the great Gnostics, loses his temper in the Clementine Recognitions and claims, "I am Christ; I am the creator of the world." Simon Magus appears in the New Testament and argues with Peter. So as it says here, "Ã‚Â…if any man shall say unto you, Lo, here is Christ, or there, believe him not; For in those days there shall also arise false Christs,Ã‚Â…" Note, he has talked about false Messiahs before among the Jews (using the same word for both). There would be false Messiahs and Christs among the Jews, and false Christs also after that (which is what happened). "For in those days there shall also arise false Christs, and false prophets, and shall show great signs and wonders,Ã‚Â…" Oh, they were good at that-Marcus and Apollonius, for example. Fire breathing; he had sulphur in a walnut and things like that, and special magic tricks. One of his great ones was turning water to wine (or turning it to blood) and back to water again. It was a well-known magician's trick. He would pour out the white liquid, and then it would turn red. It was a transformation, a miracle, etc. They were doing those things right in the time of the apostles. You can get those stories in Irenaeus. "Ã‚Â…false Christs, and false prophets, and shall show great signs and wonders, insomuch that, if possible, they shall deceive the very elect, who are the elect according to the covenant. Behold, I speak these things unto you for the elect's sake;Ã‚Â…" It's to encourage them. This is why because this is not good news. Here the Lord has come. He has brought the glad message. He has founded the church, etc. Why should things go bad? Why doesn't anything turn out happily? Why doesn't this have a happy ending? He says, I don't want you to be discouraged. I'm telling you these things ahead of time so that you won't be upset when they happen and say, well, it's false, and leave the church. People expect sudden blessings now. "Ã‚Â…you also shall hear of wars, and rumor of wars; see that ye be not troubled, for all I have told you must come to pass; but the end is not yet." So don't let it upset you; expect the wars, etc., and the end isn't yet.

This is very important because this is a touch that didn't come out until 1948 when they discovered desert sectaries crawling all over the place with the Dead Sea Scrolls. These sectaries would go out in the time of John the Baptist, and in the time of Christ they were there, numbers of them: the Damascus Covenanters, the Qumran communities, Raqim-people all over the place that would form these conventicles waiting for the Lord to come and their leaders would claim to be Messiahs. It says here, "Wherefore, if they shall say unto you: Behold, he is in the desert; go not forth:Ã‚Â…" And that's what they did; the Jews weren't allowed to stay in Jerusalem after the year 70 A.D. After the year 130 no Jew was allowed to go there at all. So they went to Pella in the desert (a very interesting place to visit) in Jordan, and they made that their capital. They went to all these desert places, but he said, Don't go into the desert; you're not going to find the church out there. You won't find it anywhere. If anyone says he's the Christ, don't believe him. They are false Christs. There won't be anyone qualified. "Behold, he is in the secret chambers; believe it not;" Again, these secret conventicles that met in Jerusalem and elsewhere in secret. If they didn't flee to the desert, they could hide out. There is the famous rescript of the emperor Hadrian on such conventicles in Asia Minor. He says, don't believe it.

If you are looking for the gospel, this is what to look for. This is what will be found. And then we come to the Restoration here. This is how it is going to be: "For as the light of the morning cometh out of the east, and shineth even unto the west, and covereth the whole earth [this is added and is very important] so shall also the coming of the Son of Man be." The gospel will spread in this manner, as the sun comes. Gradually and imperceptibly it spreads, but rapidly. I'm going to give you a parable how it will be, he says. This is the one that puzzles so many people because it sounds so unpleasant to compare the elect gathering in the Church to a carcass, a rotting corpse in the desert. But the purpose of every image, or parable, or metaphor is to emphasize one particular point right across the way. In verse 47 he says that the Lord will come as a thief in the night. Does that mean he is going to come to rob people, that he is going to come over the wall, that he is going to be sneaky, that he is going to be dishonest? Of course not. It means he will come in the manner that a thief comes and takes you by surprise when you are not expecting it. In that sense he will come, and in that sense only. We get the same thing here. Here he says, "And now I show unto you a parable. Behold, wheresoever the carcass is, there will the eagles be gathered together;" That's all the New Testament says. What do you mean, wheresoever the carcass is, there the eagles will be gathered together? That isn't a pleasant image. So we have to be told here: "so likewise shall mine elect be gathered from the four quarters of the earth."

What happens when there is a carcass in the desert, or anywhere else? The eagles come, or the vultures, or the buzzards (whatever they are going to be) from all directions. All of a sudden they appear out of nowhere. It's quite miraculous to see. How do they know? How can they see? Their eyesight is absolutely fabulous. From miles away you see the specks coming, and where the carcass is they gather in a mysterious way from all directions (in a way that can't be explained). This is the way the Saints are going to be gathered in the last days. First, the light breaks in the East. We have that hymn, "The Morning Breaks, the Shadows Flee." The light breaks in the East in that manner. It doesn't necessarily have to be the East. Oh how they argued about that East business. What do you mean about the East? Did it come from the Orient? No, not necessarily, I'm talking about how the light begins. It always begins in the East in the dawn. That's the way it is. It spreads in that manner, it says, and covereth the whole West. "For as the light of the morning cometh out of the east," that's the manner in which these things will take place. Then the people will be gathered together to Zion. How? In the same miraculous manner in which the eagles are gathered together to an object nobody else can see. So likewise shall they be gathered. But it will be a bad time, unfortunately. Now wait a minute. Verses 1-20 are the end of Jerusalem; verses 20-25 are the fate of the Christians and the sectaries. The sectaries are those in the desert and those in the secret conventicles. They were very common.

Now we come to verses 26 and 27 describing their reformation in this metaphor, this parable-emphasizing the one point, the way in which it comes, the way in which it spreads, the way in which the gathering takes place, and the circumstances under which they take place (which are very unpleasant, of course). Verses 28-31 describe the bad times of the Restoration, the times we live in, the time of the gospel being restored. "And they shall hear of wars, and rumors of wars." And again, it's important that Joseph Smith adds, don't let these wars and rumors of wars make you think that the Lord has failed in his purposes, or that the purpose in restoring this glorious work is just to lead to world into a bathos of war and trouble. No, "Behold I speak for mine elect's sake; for nation shall rise against nation, and kingdom and against kingdom; there shall be famines, and pestilences, and earthquakes, in divers places."

Did you notice that in the year 1985 ten thousand children a day died of hunger in Africa? That's real famine; we've never heard of anything like that. All the troubles and the earthquakes are piling up. It has happened this week: three earthquakes in San Francisco, just like that. Statistically, they have kept a chart of frequency over in the Geology Department here, and they are just piling up at an alarming rate. And again because of iniquity; we are getting plenty of that. Notice again, this is the second time. It happened in those ancient times; now it is happening in the final times, both among the Christians and the Jews. "And again, because iniquity shall abound [for that reason], the love of many shall wax cold;Ã‚Â…" A wicked person doesn't have true love in him-a person who is lecherous, vile, evil, dishonest, etc. Naturally, his love waxes cold. He can't deal with people the way he does and live with himself, unless he takes a very cool, calm, and detached view of everything. "Ã‚Â…but he that shall not be overcome, the same shall be saved." That's important: he that shall not be overcome by this wickedness shall be saved. "And again this Gospel of the Kingdom shall be preached in all the world, for a witness unto all nations, and then shall the end come, or the destruction of the wicked;Ã‚Â…" But not yet, you see. The gospel has to be preached to all the world. Well, it hasn't quite yet.

Then we have the Khormah. Then we have the desolation again. "And again shall the abomination of desolation, spoken of by Daniel the prophet, be fulfilled." These verses are all different numbers from those you find in the King James. "And immediately after the tribulation of those days [ah, after the big bump; after we get over to the other side of the bang, then what happens?]," After the tribulation of those days, then is going to happen this phenomena: "the sun shall be darkened [what would darken the sun, except something in the atmosphere?] and the moon shall not give her light [the moon is blocked out entirely by something in the atmosphere; I'll let you guess what] and the stars shall fall from the heaven [then meteoric showers and the like; a star is any shining object in the sky] and the powers of heaven shall be shaken." This looks something like nuclear winter if you want to look at it that way. "Verily, I say unto you, this generation, in which these things shall be shown forth [they come rather close together], shall not pass away until all I have told you shall be fulfilled." There will be a post-nuke age then. "Although, the days will come, that heaven and earth shall pass away; yet my words shall not pass away, but all shall be fulfilled." But this isn't the time when heaven and earth shall pass away. He is talking about something else there.

So we have the Restoration and then the bad times, verses 28-31. Then 31-37, the great destruction again with the fearful process (D&C Section 88) by which this takes place.

These things are described in D&C 88 in the same vivid language, very fully. That's a good one to look up. "And, as I said before, after the tribulation of those days, and the powers of the heavens shall be shaken, then shall appear the sign of the Son of Man in heaven, and then shall all the tribes of the earth mourn;Ã‚Â…" This is world affliction. The whole earth is smitten now. There has never been a war or destruction like that. All the tribes of the earth, the inhabitants, wherever they are, will all be mourning. "Ã‚Â…and they shall see the Son of Man coming in the clouds of heaven, with power and great glory." But notice, the Son of Man, and then shall all the tribes of the earth mourn. This is something that covers the whole earth. The Book of Mormon says that. There will be a vapor of smoke, and it shall cover the earth. These things are going to happen; we are determined on it right now. "And whoso treasureth up my word, shall not be deceived, for the Son of Man shall come,Ã‚Â…" People will say, Well, he is not coming. These times are too terrible. We're having no good news at all. "Ã‚Â…and he shall send his angels before him with the great sound of a trumpet, and they shall gather together the remainder of his elect from the four winds [the remainder is added there, just like the gathering to the carcass] from one end of heaven to the other. Now learn a parable of the fig tree-Ã‚Â…"

Now, here again, this is important, verses 38-40. How about the timing of this thing? He says there are no clocks. There are no stop watches, but there are indicators. In economics you have market indicators. You can't be specific; you can't set figures, etc. But there are indicators of which way the market will go. In the same way, the Lord says, I will give you indicators how you can know where you stand in this thing and how things are going. But don't try to time it because that's dangerous, as he tells us later here. If you say, I know exactly when he is going to come, then we can enjoy ourselves until ten minutes before, and then we will be ready. If we know the general is coming to inspect, we can clean up the barracks and make everything perfect before he comes. It's the surprise inspection that catches you as you really are. That's what the Lord is going to do. It says here he is going to come as a thief in the night and catch you the way you really are. So don't try to prepare for this.

And this is very important when he talks about this destruction. "So likewise, mine elect, when they shall see all these things, they shall know that he is near, even at the doors [Mark 13 here]; But of that day, and hour, no one knoweth [no calendar or clock is going to do you any good; don't try to predict it that way] no, not the angels of God in heaven, but my Father only [Luke 17:22 there]." But business as usual is the theme now. Things may be going pretty well as far as that goes. "But as it was in the days of Noah [this is the way it went], so it shall be also at the coming of the Son of Man;Ã‚Â…" In Luke 17:23 and following, he adds another thing. He says they were buying and selling; they were giving in marriage; they were eating and drinking. All the normal activities of life were going on; it was business as usual. Then the Flood came and hit them like that. That's the way it is going to be, so you can't tell by those signs, he says (but as it was in the days of Noah). "Ã‚Â…for until the day that Noah entered into the ark they were eating and drinking, marrying and giving in marriage [Luke says buying and selling]

And knew not until the flood came, and took them all away; so shall also the coming of the Son of Man be. Then shall be fulfilled that which is written, that in the last days,Ã‚Â…" How do you prepare for this? How did he tell the Jews to prepare? He says flee to the mountains, hide in the caves (they got out that way). But don't go back to Jerusalem. Well, that's exactly the way it happened. What do we do now? How do you prepare for this? Do you hide in a cave? Do you build yourself shelter? The Lord says it's not going to do you any good at all. That is not the way you prepare this time, he says. As it is written, "Ã‚Â…two shall be in the field, the one shall be taken, and the other left;" There are not survival schemes. Which one? They are equally prepared or equally expecting. The New Testament says two women shall be grinding at the mill (two women in the kitchen), one is taken and the other is spared. How can you figure that? They are side by side, the two women. It happens that way in war time too. You never know who. "And what I say unto one, I say unto all men;Ã‚Â…" So what can you do about it? Don't prepare ahead of time and say, I'm going to be perfectly safe. I've got a plan that will make me perfectly safe. No, two can be together in the same situation. One is taken and the other is spared, and there is no way they can calculate which one it will be.

But there is a way. He says, I'm going to tell you what you should do. This is the plan of procedure. This is the direction. No strategies for survival. They are not going to do you any good, but from 46-54 he is going to tell us what should be done. Instructions for the Saints here: First of all, you watch. You act as if the Lord were coming all the time. If the barracks are in spic and span condition all the time, then you don't worry about inspection. "Ã‚Â…watch, therefore, for you know not at what hour your Lord doth come." There is nothing you can do about that. "But know this, if the good man of the house had known in what watch the thief would come, he would have watched, and would not have suffered his house to have been broken up, but would have been ready."

All right, if you knew when the Lord was coming (you had been watching), you would make special preparations to receive him. But since you don't know, the only thing to do is to be prepared all the time as if he were coming any moment, tomorrow. That's what he says because he will come as a thief in the night. "Therefore be ye also ready, for in such an hour as ye think not, the Son of Man cometh." The only answer is to be ready all the time. Well then how are you ready all the time? In verse 49 he says, "Who, then, is a faithful and wise servant, whom his lord hath made ruler over his household, to give them meat in due season?" Notice he has made them ruler over his house. The ruler is supposed to take care of the rest of the household. He is the Dominus; he is the lord. That's what both those words mean. Dominus means lord of the house, and hlafweard means keeper of the loaf. So he says the same thing here. The servant who has been appointed must be responsible and take care toward his fellowman. "Blessed is that servant whom his lord, when he cometh, shall find so doing; and verily I say unto you, he shall make him ruler over all his goods. But if that evil servant shall say in his heart: [here it is, see] My lord delayeth his coming [He is not coming; I'm safe [p.13] for quite a while at least],Ã‚Â…" See, if you knew when he was coming, you could say, "My lord delayeth his coming." This is what is likely: He will begin to smite his fellow servants, and to eat and drink and be drunken and take advantage of them, be cruel and greedy and all the things that people are when they don't expect the Lord to really be coming for quite a while. Remember in the Book of Mormon, He will beat us with a few stripes and then everything will be all right. "The lord of that servant shall come in a day when he looketh not for him, and in an hour that he is not aware of." He is going to be surprised.

But this is what you are supposed to do: Behave toward your fellowmen in a manner the Lord would have you if he were here himself in Zion. What we should be like in Zion (as you get in that writing of President Kimball)? I have some copies for any who didn't get them. (I'm going to give the subject now for the final. Since it's April 11, it will give you ten days to think about it. I think it's a good subject; we'll see.) "And shall cut him asunder, and shall appoint him his portion with the hypocrites [there are the hypocrites again]; there shall be weeping and gnashing of teeth."

Now this is important: notice the last verse. This is not the end of the world as people conceive it. "And thus cometh the end of the wicked, according to the prophecy of Moses, saying: They shall be cut off from among the people; but the end of the earth [now he uses a geological, cosmological, scientific term] is not yet, but by and by." That's not the end of the earth or the planet. This isn't the time when the earth will be vaporized and the heavens rolled up as a scroll. That's another thing. When does that come? That doesn't concern you now, as he tells Moses, but by and by. "And thus cometh the end of the wicked, according to the prophecy of Moses, saying: They shall be cut off from among the people; but the end of the earth is not yet,Ã‚Â…" We are not talking about that at all. That is by and by, but these are the ends that will concern you (and this is the end that concerns us).

Next time we will review the whole thing. Still I think it would give us more time to think about it if we consider this. Some have been worried about assignments here. You notice I've passed over the books of the Pearl of Great Price rather lightly-the assignment being for you to read them more carefully along the lines indicated. It's a short enough book, after all. It's a short book but a great one, and you are justified in spending a lot of time with it. That's a standing assignment. I don't know that any more should be necessary. Notice, I have deliberately omitted the most important book of all, with President Kimball's updating-the Joseph Smith story, the last one of the lot, the last of the dispensations. That's 156 years ago now. What's happened? Well, President Kimball is bringing us to date on the present situation. So I deliberately left that out because I have been saving that for the big assignment. This is it, the essay due on April 11 by 6:00 p.m. This is the subject, and the essay will be judged as an essay, of course. I think the subject is glaringly obvious in view of that, and it is how does the Pearl of Great Price testify to the mission of Joseph Smith? You notice it can be philosophically, historically, literary, religious, documentary, scientific (all those things about the creation). How does the Pearl of Great Price testify to the mission of Joseph Smith?

Remember, this is the last book in the Pearl of Great Price? Do they all belong together in the same book? How do they support each other? There are so many points of view here. There are so many angles from which you can treat this thing that you should take some time to think about it. Read through the Pearl of Great Price if you've never read it before and ask yourself-.

The Lord comes and gives us the three dispensations (the times of wickedness and vengeance) just as it had been put down in the days of Enoch. He tells us about that time of wickedness and vengeance; then the time against the Christians, the long Apostasy. First, the destruction of Jerusalem (that horrible thing) then the Apostasy, then the Restoration. Remember, this was given at a very early time. It doesn't say, well, now the gospel is restored; this time it will be different. This time everything is going to be beautiful because the gospel is restored and the Prophet Joseph has come. It's worse than ever. That's a remarkable thing, isn't it? When was this given? This is the 1838 one, but in 1969 they discovered one much older than this written in the handwriting of Frederick G. Williams, which the Prophet dictated when he was 26 years old. It is a much better account than this one. This one is the one they chose to publish because they didn't have that one. That one wasn't discovered until 1969. That was printed in the BYU Studies in the spring of 1969 along with all the other versions, five different versions of the first vision. It's very important to fill out the picture there. You can still get that 1969 issue. It is gray with a picture of Joseph Smith on the front of it. But this is the theme of the whole thing. This would answer everything else. As far as the big question is concerned, where do we find our comfort? What joy is there in this? What is the reassurance of the gospel? And the big question we were talking about all the time in the earlier part was, what's hereafter? What's coming? Are we going into the dark? If anyone answered that, it was Joseph Smith. Did he answer it? Does this give us reason for confidence in his mission? You think about that for writing an essay on it, and then the next time we can sort of review the course. [p.1]

Lecture 26

Joseph Smith

Here is a very important statement. It pretty well sums up everything I have been trying to talk about. It's quite a recent one too, 1983. There's a large handbook titled Prehistoric Germanic Europe that begins way back with the Aurignacian, which is Upper Paleolithic, which is also Upper Pleistocene. One is paleontology; the other is geology. The Willendorf Figurine goes back older than that. It's the period roughly from 27,000 B.C. on (not 2700, 27,000 B.C. on). It's goes right through the whole thing. It's very well documented, very full. It goes through the various periods, and it covers all of Europe too. There are thousands and hundreds of thousands of artifacts now from those ancient times, and this is the very interesting conclusion these people draw at the end of their book. They go through the works. It's very good if you want illustrations and reproductions.

"From the material evidence surveyed, culture appears to be a collective attempt at providing answers to the questions posed by manÃ‚Â…" About what? About his economic survival; about his dominance; about sex, or something like that? Not at all. That's not what keeps it going at all. Of course, we, being a materialistic society, have always given a materialistic interpretation of it. Naturally, what primitive man was after was to save his life, to avoid wild beasts, to bash his neighbor over the head and all this sort of thing (always for survival). That was always the first rule. But that isn't the point at all, as he says here. From all this evidence culture appears as what? Not from that but from a "collective attempt to provide answers to the questions about his position in this life and the next life." That's the thing.

"As long as a distinct set of answers was satisfactory, the distinctive aspects of the culture remained constant and offered that degree of continuity which made for stability." You had a stable, continuous society as long as they had something they would believe in regarding this life and the next life. Of course, this is the age of sophistication, and science is supposed to abolish those things. That's exactly what you get in Lucretius and the others. As long as they had that, they had continuity and stability. "It appears, on the other hand, that experiment, innovation and change [they are necessary; they are forced on them] are a response to inadequacy or outright failure in some sector of the culture's general view of the world."

Can you answer the big question? (You know what that is; it's important for the final.) As long as they have some answer to that that satisfies them in any degree, the society can survive. And this talks about world civilization because it goes the same way throughout the whole world all the time. It's not localized. These ancient civilizations (primitives) were not localized. You find the same culture in most areas which we associate with Neanderthal. You find it clear from China to Solo Man. You find it in Choukoutien. You find it in Ireland. You find it everywhere, Heidelberg man and the rest. But they are world civilizations and they hold their coherency. They have dispensations as long as a dispensation is going. But when people lose their faith and don't have any particular plan, then they wander and stray off in all possible directions. It's the answer to that question that keeps you on an even keel. Otherwise, you are simply seeking for power and gain and influence or whatever you can get in the moment here.

Well, now this takes us to Joseph Smith. This is that account I was talking about of the First Vision in the Spring 1969 issue of BYU Studies, which they tell me you can still get at the Bookstore. It gives five different versions. This is the most important, the one of 1831-32. They have reproduced it here in the handwriting of Frederick G. Williams. This is the part I want to point out about the First Vision The Wentworth Letter is also included here. We have our own version in our Pearl of Great Price, the last chapter and the most important. It ties everything together. The spelling and punctuation are standardized in the following quotation from Joseph Smith's First Vision: "At about the age of twelve years, my mind became seriously impressedÃ‚Â…" Notice, he was very precocious, and all the accounts of his enemies from the time tell the same story. I collected a lot of them years ago in a book called The Myth Makers, all the various accounts of people who knew Joseph Smith (his enemies). There's a very good collection from those who were not his enemies by Hyrum Andrus. It's called They Knew the Prophet. You can get that; it's very good. There are 150 accounts of people from journals, etc., not written in response to anything, just their personal reflections in their lives about Joseph Smith and their connections and their knowing him. It's a very remarkable collection of documents. The other one is the other side. Anyway, they all tell this, that when he was very young, he was always thinking about things, asking questions about things. He was seen often walking in the woods talking to himself, etc. They thought that was, as Harding says, "an evil, groveling, self-centered nature." He was planning more evil tricks as he wandered around talking and feeling people out with questions.

"At about the age of twelve years, my mind became seriously impressed with regard to all important concerns for the welfare of my immortal soul which led me to searching the scriptures believing, as I was taught, that they contained the word of God. Thus applying myself to them and my intimate acquaintance with those of different denominations [and he did go around and converse with all the ministers] led me to marvel exceedingly for I discovered that they did not adorn their profession by a holy walk and Godly conversation agreeable to what I found contained in the sacred depository. This was a grief to my soul. Thus from the age of twelve years to fifteen I pondered many things in my heart concerning the situation of the world [this is the Abraham story too, as you notice] of mankind, the contentions and divisions, and the wickedness and abominations and darknessÃ‚Â…" What, in upstate New York in the 1820s? This was the condition of the world? What would he know about a wicked world? Well, he knew all right. You might say, what a very negative, sour nature, but Joseph Smith was notorious for his sunny, cheerful, optimistic nature. He was everybody's friend. His worst enemy said he never heard him say an unkind thing about anyone (very interesting).

He said, Oh, he told all sorts of lies, exaggerations, deceptions, everything else, but he never said an unkind thing about anyone. With all the provocation he had, imagine that. Well, that would be the acid test as far as I'm concerned. He goes on here, "Ã‚Â…which pervade the minds of mankind. My mind became exceedingly distressed for I became convinced of my sins, and by searching the scriptures I found that mankind did not come unto the Lord, but that they had apostatizedÃ‚Â…" You know, there's no mention of the Apostasy in the Articles of Faith. It's not necessary. The Article of Faith is an assertion of positive doctrines, the things we believe in, not the fact that other people are astray and wrong. It says that these things are true; then everybody else must be wrong. You don't have to include that as an Article of Faith, but he was aware of it, "that they had apostatized from the true and living faith, and there was no society or denomination that built upon the Gospel of Jesus Christ as recorded in the New Testament. And I felt to mourn for my own sins and for the sins of the world, for I learned in the scriptures that God was the same yesterday, today and forever, and no respecter of persons for he was God."

Now he describes the world which God has given us (this we get a lot of in the Pearl of Great Price) and then the world which men have made-what men have made of the world that God has given us. He looked around the world in upstate New York, a sublimely beautiful part of the country. It was gorgeous then, you can be sure. And also notice the concern with cosmology. He's talking about sun, moon and stars here. "Ã‚Â…I looked upon the sun, the glorious luminary of the earth, and also the moon, rolling in their majesty through the heavens, and also the stars shining in their courses, and the earth also upon which I stood, and the beasts of the field, and the fowls of heaven, and the fish of the waters, and also man walking forth upon the face of the earth in majesty and in strength of beauty, whose power and intelligence in governing the things are so exceeding great and marvelous, even in the likeness of Him who created him. And when I considered upon these things my heart exclaimed, well hath the wise man said, 'It is a fool that saith in his heart there is no God.' My heart exclaimed, all these things bear testimony and bespeak an omnipotent and omnipresent power who maketh laws and decreeth and bindeth all things in their bounds, who filleth eternity, who was and is and will be from all eternity to eternityÃ‚Â…" The interesting thing is that this is the contrast to the world which men had made (when I looked upon the situation of mankind, the contentions, the division, the wickedness, the abominations, the darkness). They were mobbing and misbehaving at this time badly.

We have a very interesting account from a woman revivalist. She preached in England and in New England, especially around this part, on the Geneva road and near Palmyra. The ministers gave her a bad time. Nancy Towle was her name, and they kicked her around. These people weren't very nice if you disagreed with them. The division, wickedness, abomination and the contrast with that-the stars, the glorious setting in which we are placed here-all these things bear testimony. And then "Ã‚Â…when I considered these things and that that being seeketh such to worship him as worship him in spirit and truth [he wants to be worshipped] therefore, I cried unto the Lord for mercy for there was none else to whom I could goÃ‚Â…"

The situation of Abraham, you see. When he starts out, the world has stopped dead center. Who can ever get it moving again? There must be a cry below before there can be an answer above (a stirring below, as the Talmud says). And the same way with the table. You do not ask for a blessing on an empty table. You set the table, then ask for the blessing. "Ã‚Â…there was none else to whom I could go and obtain mercy, and the Lord heard my cry in the wilderness. While in the attitude of calling upon the Lord in the 16th year of my age [changes the age] a pillar of light above the brightness of the sun at noon day came down from above and rested upon me, and I was filled with the spirit of God. And the Lord opened the heavens upon me, and I saw the Lord and he spake to me saying [this is a direct quote; it's an indirect quotation in the other account-he told me; he said to me, etc.-but this is what the Lord said, and it actually quotes Him here]Ã‚Â…saying, 'Joseph, my son, thy sins are forgiven thee. Go thy way, walk in my statutes and keep my commandments. Behold I am the Lord of glory. I was crucified for the world that all those who believe on my name may have eternal life. Behold, the world lieth in sin at this time, and none doeth good, no not one. They have turned aside from the gospel, and they keep not my commandments. They draw near to me with their lips while their hearts are far from me. Mine anger is kindling against the inhabitants of the earth to visit them according to this ungodliness and to bring to pass that which hath been spoken by the mouth of the prophets and apostles. Behold and lo, I come quickly, as is written of me, in the cloud clothed in the glory of my Father'" And then Joseph says, "And my soul was filled with love [that was his reaction]. For many days I could rejoice with great joy [remember Lehi was so depressed; and after he saw the vision, then he rejoiced, even though the news they had was bad] and the Lord was with me, but I could find none who would believe the heavenly visionÃ‚Â…."

Nobody would believe him. This is the situation in a dispensation. But without the important answers to go by, as this book by Herbert Schutz says, any world civilization simply crumbles and dissolves. You remember what the poet Yeats says, "Things fall apart; the center cannot hold; mere anarchy is loosed upon the world." Things fall apart today. The center can't hold. Everything is dissolving. That's the only difference, you see. We don't have a central doctrine. We don't have the gospel to believe in. Well, now as to our question: How does the Pearl of Great Price testify to the mission of Joseph Smith? Notice, testify to it, not for it or against it. It can be either one. Remember, the Pearl of Great Price, especially the Book of Abraham, was taken for years and years (and still is) as the number one evidence against Joseph Smith, as overpoweringly strong evidence against him. We went into quite a "song and dance" about that in the Improvement Era back in 1968. We might as well mention some things here because this is relevant. We've overlooked this part of it.

In 1912 S.A.B. Mercer at Chicago spearheaded an attack which initiated with Franklin S. Spalding, the Episcopal bishop of Salt Lake City. Mercer was a 32-year-old minister who wanted to be an Egyptologist (oh, how he wanted to be!). He was never very good; in fact, he was terrible. We know because he sold us his library, his notes and all that sort of stuff. How ignorant the man was; it was just a laugh. But the point is he was to spearhead this attack on Joseph Smith. In about 1953 (I had just come to BYU) he retired from Toronto. He collected a great Egyptian collection there, a library of books, and he offered to sell it to BYU for practically nothing. Tyler was our librarian then. He went back to Paris to see him and ask if he was kidding. No, he wanted the Mormons to have this library after this vicious attack on them. Harvard immediately offered four times the sum, but we got it. We have the Mercer collection here, which is the core of a very good Egyptian library, one of the few in the country that could really be used for advanced study. So he was in charge of this attack, and it was very carefully engineered. He sent the letters to a lot of Egyptologists. He got answers from eight very eminent ones. Sending the three facsimiles, he said, "This is what Joseph Smith says; is he right?" Instead, he should have said, What do these facsimiles denote? Well, none of them knew what they denoted. They just had one answer to give. Was Joseph Smith right in his interpretation? Well, all they had to do was say, "no." None of them believed that Abraham had ever lived. Now if you are going to say, Is this a true autobiography of Abraham and ask a man who firmly believes there never was an Abraham, what answer is he going to give you?

These are the people that tested it, and this is an amusing thing. The answers were very good actually; Brother Widtsoe, R.C. Webb and others answered back with some very pointed things showing that they disagreed among themselves, etc. But since that wasn't their particular field, these people naturally had a field day. Here's an editorial in the Church newspaper. They point out that there are some obvious contradictions and discrepancies in the views of the experts, and the Mormons might be at least permitted to ask for a stay of final judgment, as B. H. Roberts wrote then. He said, "These questions that depend on special scholarship are questions that require time and research, and the conclusions of the learned in such matters are not as unchangeable as they seem." The New York Times exploded when they read that. They had a big headline: Mormons Revile Scholars and Scholarship. They said, "The Deseret Evening News has spent its entire editorial page reviling scholars and scholarship." Just because they asked some perfectly natural questions that was reviling scholars and scholarship. It was pure authoritarianism, nothing else. These are the authorities. They used this, and then when the Church got the Joseph Smith documents in 1967, they brought all this up again (the big 1912 thing). They said these authorities were the ones; they said this that and the other. Two days after this, incidentally, the Deseret News showed the position the Church took then and still takes.

They were very much impressed because of the fact that as soon as the Church obtained the eleven fragments of the Joseph Smith papyri, it published them. They sent slicks around of every one of them. Without any comment or anything else, the Church made them available to everybody and asked anybody to make any comments they wanted to about it (the Egyptologists or anyone else). We have never argued with them. Well, that's exemplary; nobody ever does that in Egyptian. When you've got a document, you hug it to your bosom until you edit it, etc. and live for the next ten years on it. This was really impressive. But this is the policy the Deseret News takes. It says, "The Latter-day Saints court inquiry such as this. They want to know the truth and only the truth. There's no important issue that they are not glad to face [p.6] whether presented by friend or foe." Now in this testifying to the mission of Joseph Smith you can say there are things you find in the Pearl of Great Price that would show Joseph Smith must be faking or where he must be getting this, that or the other. But they thought they really had him dead to rights in this. Way back in 1845-that is very soon, you know, when you consider that the first attempt even to decipher it was 1838. This was only seven years later. They hadn't got to first base at that time, but this is what they say about Joseph Smith and the facsimiles in the Book of Abraham here: "Well, all these figures in the facsimiles are familiar and now understood. Joseph Smith in coming out with these things has confidently defied inevitable exposure. The Champollions of the Bibliotheque de Rei [of course, they got that absolutely wrong; there's no such a bibliotheque] and the British Museum have the subject well in hand. It's already apparent to the learned that the whole thing is too gross to bear patiently, too painful to laugh at." Just don't even consider it, the experts say, just brush it aside.

Then an interesting thing here. Mercer says quite frankly-this comes a little later here. We'll go into a lot of spiel about this. Well, that should have settled the matter with the authorities, but, of course, the Mormons were not convinced and would have done well to have done a little study on their own. In fact, Joseph Smith and Brigham Young both said (and Parley P. Pratt especially was strong on this): This is a signal that we should get going and do something on our own here. This is an invitation to look further. They pointed the way for Latter-day Saints to prepare themselves for just such eventualities, pleading with them to take heed of themselves and use their brains. I notice Brother Maxwell recently said that we can expect new documentary discoveries because they have been promised. They are part of the prophecies. "Even during the grim days of December 1844 the leaders of the Church advised the elders to get up schools that all might be taught in the branches of education and prepare themselves, that they at the least might be fully competent to correspond with the wise men of the world." But they neglected it because they were very busy with other things.

Now this Mercer headed this enterprise in 1912, and this was all revived and all republished by the Salt Lake Lithograph and Companies got up by the Tanners. They make a big thing of it. It's highly funded from various parts. Walters and people like that are still traveling around giving lectures on it and making a lot of money (not here, but in Arizona and back east). Night before last I met with four people from Denver. The pressure is really on there because they are building a temple there now. They make a big thing of it. Here Dr. Mercer frankly admits that he and the other scholars "did not seem to take the matter very seriously and devoted very little time to it indeed." Now they were supposed to pass their judgment on it. They said, "Well, it's just nonsense." But they didn't bother to read it because that's not necessary. You say Joe Smith, and that's all there is to it. You've tipped your hand and there's no argument. And then he says, "The haste was justified in the minds of the scholars by the simplicity of the task. Even less time could be expected. They probably felt as I did," says Mercer, "that their time was too valuable to spend on such scientific work as the Joseph Smith guesses." Whatever the reasons, they never intended to do any real work. They depended entirely on their credentials to get them through.

This is the sort of thing that has been going on for 150 years now, and we let them get away with it. But we have lots of evidence here.

We are talking incidentally about the subject here: How does the Pearl of Great Price testify to the mission of Joseph Smith? It's a subject that has very broad latitudes. You can write from many points of view. It's just how good the essay is. You can consider it from a doctrinal point view, historical, philosophical, literary, scientific; you name it. And there is internal evidence and external evidence. As Friedrich Blass says in his classic work on the criticism of documents to determine their antiquity (whether they are genuine or not), you rarely ever have to use any external evidence for the Book of Mormon, Pearl of Great Price or anything else. He's not talking about them, of course. He's talking about ancient documents in general, a massive volume on the subject. In the first place there's the fact that you have the document. You must give it the benefit of the doubt. That's the very first thing. But the point he makes here is that the evidence is internal. You rarely have to go outside of the internal evidence of the document itself. You don't need anything else. You don't have to refer to the time, to its setting, to anything else. Well, you know those things. They are important.

We haven't done anything at all as far as external evidence is concerned. We mentioned other sources for Enoch, other sources for Abraham. Some of them match. Which ones match? Which don't match? Well, that's a long story. You can take a lot of time on that. We haven't had time for that. We have been talking about the general thesis here. The thing I would be concerned with if I were writing this would be the internal evidence. How many books do we have? What do they pretend to be? What do they pretend to show forth? What kind of an accomplishment is this? Again, Brother Maxwell calls it an "Everest" for anyone who could set up a thing like that. What about a few mistakes and slips here and there? How do you know they are mistakes and slips, and all these other things? Well, we have the criticism. That's an interesting story. We go into a long thing, but we'd never bother to do it again.

We said we would have a review here. Well, here are the various lessons we have gone through (26 of them) and each one had a subject more or less, and they are connected more or less. Remember, the first lesson was what was the main concern of religion? This could be a perfectly legitimate question to write an essay for the final. What is the main concern of religion? That's indicated in the Pearl of Great Price. You could use the Pearl of Great Price as your text. Or, what is the main source of our religious knowledge? Where do we get it from and what's the type? We talked about definitions of religion and the like. What's the source of our knowledge? What serious defects are there in our knowledge, and what's the result of them? We made a big thing of this. In the religion of Israel, what's missing in the books? And in the religion of Christ, in Christianity? What's the evidence? How do you know these things were lost? What evidence do we have that these things were lost? We provided some of that. What has Joseph Smith restored? What does the Book of Mormon restore? What does the Pearl of Great Price restore?

This restores some very interesting things, and we talked a lot about them, beginning with the literal sense of things in the second lecture. Why was the literal sense of the scriptures rejected? Why would they do that? Why wouldn't they accept them as literal? What is the big question? We talked a lot about that. Remember Clement of Rome asking the big question wherever he went. How is it connected with other things? Remember, it is necessarily cosmological, deals with the creation of all things. But the main thing is what's going to happen to me? Where was I? Why does this big question overshadow all the others (that's the big one)? Can you give some examples, literary and historical, of the big question? Well, wherever you look you'll see it. Men are haunted wherever you go. Why are they behaving so desperately, so hysterically, so irrationally everywhere throughout the world today?

This takes us right back to our original thesis here from this recent survey of prehistoric Europe. As long as people have some satisfactory answer to the questions regarding the position in life and the next life, the society will keep going. People will go along and will not become desperate. The Egyptians had a pretty good answer to that, and this accounts for the unique stability of Egyptian society. You can see that. They never changed it; they never lost it. They were always naive and left the door open. They knew they didn't have the answer, but they felt they had enough tradition and they had enough evidence on hand to justify a grand jury and say, Yes, this thing is worth a test.

"Positivism" is the word invented by Auguste Comte in the middle of the nineteenth century. That is the positivism of science: what you see, what you hear, what you can feel-that's real and true, and all the rest is myth and superstition, and it is vicious. It's not a thing that you want at all. So positivism will not allow any of this. On the one hand you have the superstition of religion, and on the other hand you have the light of science. These things are absolutely as they are, and they are still very strong. A person like Carl Sagan is rabidly positivistic, and so is most of science. Remember that passage from C. P. Snow about the great physicist of his time, Rutherford. They were positivists, and they knew they had the answer. And they knew just as surely as they lived (just as surely as they knew Rutherford's atom) that they were all destined for annihilation and were going nowhere. When they were in public, they presented a brave front, but to themselves they were panicky. The dark night was over them. They had not only moments of doubt. "But men at times are sober. They think by fits and starts. And when they think, they fasten their hands upon their hearts." We don't escape that at all. It comes to everybody, strongly. It worried Darwin sick. He almost became a mystic in his last days. There is a very good book on that by Malraux. Well then, that was this one, this literalism that they got rid of. Why did they get rid of it? Joseph Smith brought it back. Then in the third lecture we talked about preexistence. This has to be part of the picture (it's central). We could ask this as the question for the final: Why is preexistence central to the discussion of religion? What is the present Christian and Jewish attitude to the teaching? Well, it is coming back, actually. But why were they against it? And what evidence is there for or against a preexistence?

I guess you can quote Wordsworth there, but we have better evidence for that. Plato's anamnesis; we mentioned that. Everything we know is a memory of what we learned before. We know what's right and wrong because it's implicit in us. Anamnesis, a memory of former times, and Zinzendoff "The Poem," etc. What was the early Christian view of it? Remember, we read that poem called The Pearl, how he leaves his heavenly home. He actually begins by saying, "In my first primeval childhood." He was raised by his parents in a palace. He came down here, got into trouble and went back. There was a welcome by the people back home.

Then the fourth and fifth lectures. If you have literal preexistence, that means you've got to take the cosmos into it. Cosmism is considered a great crime in Christianity and Judaism alike. The rabbis and the fathers of the church both condemn it, the physical cosmos. That's Hermeticism; that's Pythagoreanism. But why is it basic to religion? They can't get away from it, of course. In Gen. 1 that's what you get. What is the present attitude toward what they call cosmism or cosmology and why? And why does the Pearl of Great Price make such a big thing of it? What evidence is there for the concerns of the primitive for cosmology? Well, any references we have to the primitive (their stone circles, their glyphs, their drawings) all indicate their awareness of the four regions and the processes of nature and their observance of the sky. They are very much centered in the four directions (their dances and things they left behind in stone). But they still have them. Go down and visit the Hopis and Zunis now, and you see cosmology. They have some terrific cosmology things such as the visiting star. You go down in the kiva and a fellow comes in with a big silver star on his head. He comes down the ladder and says he has descended from another star. There are some very dramatic things that they do, but the cosmology is with it. They had megaliths and temples just like our own.

What about the religious elements that appear in science fiction? Why would they be significant for this course? Look at the titles of science fiction books. What makes science fiction interesting? The fact that it's plausible that there are other worlds that people might visit. There are troubles among them, etc. It goes back to the old theme, Councils in Heaven. You have high councils meeting in other worlds, and you have wars between the worlds, and you have the good influence and the bad influence, the good guys and the bad guys, the dark side and the light side (tsid akher as it is called in the Cabala in the Jewish mystics). The tsid akher is the "other side." There is the other side of things. Well, all these things are the sum and substance of science fiction because they have no ideas of their own. All they can do is draw on the scriptures. All throughout you will see the most popular ones, like Star Wars, are those that have that element of plausibility. It starts out "in a galaxy far away and long ago." This sort of thing could happen. We have been teaching that these things have always been happening. There has always been something going on. So cosmology is a big thing here.

The sixth lecture was on the big question. Why was, and still is, creation the number one issue of controversy today? Why would they consider that the most important of all? Once we're here, we're here. That's the main thing, but why be so exercised about how we happened to get here if you don't know. Just accept the fact that you are here and go on from there. As Professor Wormuth used to say up at the University of Utah, "Mormonism is the only non-speculative religion." We don't ask, is it right that we should be here, or were we created at all? These are problems of solipsism and entomology, etc. Do I really exist, or should the world be here? Well, I don't know; but it is here, and we begin with it as a given quantity. It's not speculative. That's what you speculate about, things like that.

What is the clash of Mormonism with fundamentalism? You know what that is there. We don't believe things were created instantly, simultaneously-that all things instantly came into existence out of nothing, not at all. That's the fundamentalist view. That's the standard view of creation, which is why science objects to it. Well, where do we clash with science? Where do we clash with Darwinism? We say it is both a process and a plan. They say there is no processing and no plan; it just happened. The only process would be chance (pure chance). Both coming into existence and whatever progress takes place after that is pure chance one way or the other. So we don't agree with either one of them. But why do we take sides, just the same? There's a psychological study you can go into. How does the Pearl of Great Price answer both of these? Remember, we read those passages from John Gordon Bennett and William Bartlett (those eastern editors) about how Joseph Smith impressed them. It was especially his cosmology that impressed them enormously because it wouldn't accept either side. How can ancient writings contribute to scientific support of Joseph Smith? We have a lot of them.

We come to the seventh lesson then. What is the concept of beginning in the Christian world? That is, beginning out of nothing? That's important because it takes us to the Council in Heaven. Where is the Council in Heaven described in ancient writings? Well, in all sorts of ancient writings it's described. We went into this one, the Shabako Stone, the oldest book in the world. That's what it told us about, this Council in Heaven at the creation of the world. It was not mumbo-jumbo or primitive superstition at all. It was a very rational and important document. It is regarded as still having powerful influence on Jewish literature and on Christian literature alike, down to modern times. What's the rationale of the Council in Heaven? Why did they have it?
