What was the reason for it? And the implications in the Shabako text with regard to cultural and religious evolution and the sources of the Pearl of Great Price. What are the implications there? Then the eighth lecture was still the Memphite Theology as we compared it with the Enuma Elish. Remember that's the Mesopotamian version of it. What are the main teachings in the Memphite Theology and in the Enuma Elish? And in the eleventh lesson, what are the precious things removed from the scriptures? We are back to that issue again. Who would do such a thing? Who removed them and why would they possibly remove them? When did they remove them? We know very well when they removed them. The councils of the church are very plainly dated. Where did it happen and how did it happen? These processes are all well known, but that's in Christian history (in the early church actually). What is the hermetic tradition?

Then we talked about the eight religious permutations, different philosophies. Remember the light-dark-light, dark-light-dark, the dark-dark-dark, the light-light-light, etc. Like the Chinese I Ching, there are the eight possible combinations. Which one are we, and why are we the only one that has all three alike? And what is the standard? What is the one the world usually accepts of those three? A bright preexistence, a bright world, or a bright hereafter? You can rearrange those in eight different combinations. There's the tragic combination, there's the standard, there's the Pythagorean or mystic, there's the conventional Judeo-Christian, there's the monastic, there's the romantic, and there's the gospel.

Then we have the doctrine of plurality of worlds. That goes along with cosmology. (I keep watching the clock for a reason here.) Why should the plurality of worlds be a major issue in religion, or in science? What's its place in the gospel, and what are the classic arguments for it and against it? That's a good subject for a final. How does it relate to the preexistence, and how does the preexistence relate to the problem of evil? This is the problem that no religion solves at all. The Pearl of Great Price is the only one that really handles it, really comes to grips with it. What religious issues are raised by geology, cosmology, biology, psychology? We know those. How does the Pearl of Great Price meet them? What is the connection that scientists are making between religious beliefs and quantum physics? You can get that in one of those books by Gary Zukav or Fritjof Capra.

Then lecture 15 was on the creation. How can there be a new world on old ground? Remember, we believe the episodic idea, which is very important. This is chronology. They always talk about the time. Is the earth only 6,000 years old? What a fuss! What is the significance of the tektites, of the iridium layer? What are the main phases through which the earth passes in the process of creation, according to the Pearl of Great Price? The geological process, and then it goes through the biological phases in the Pearl of Great Price again, beginning at a particular phase. At what moment does creation begin, and at what moment does creation end? What is the significance of the mountain in religion, as an image, in reality? Then back to Moses 1. How does Moses, chapter one apply to individual human beings? What are ascension texts? In lecture 18 we talked about them. What takes place in the Prologue in Heaven and what's the role of Mephistopheles? What are the main acts and scenes in Moses 1 (because it's a drama). What is the argument of the play? How does it resemble the classic year drama? What is the purpose of handing down this account?

Lecture 20 was on Moses 3:19 . What light does the Pearl of Great Price throw on primitive man? Where is the line between man and animal? When do you step over the line? What are the timeless episodes in the Book of Moses? There are timeless periods in which time is not measured at all that way. Then we come to the Lamech story. Why is that significant? Of course, there is Cain and the problem of evil. What is the pact with the devil? Who made it? What was Cain's motive? What was his message? What was his defense?

Then what was Enoch's mission? Who were the Watchers? Why is baptism central to the Book of Abraham? What evidence is there that supports the authenticity of the Book of Enoch?

What's the message of the Book of Enoch? What was the last straw that brought on the Flood? Violence, of course. How does Noah repeat the experience of Adam, as he does? How does Abraham resume the work of Noah? What is the basic pattern of dispensations presented in the Pearl of Great Price? Why must the founder of each dispensation stand alone? Why are his instructions binding on others? What were the trials of Abraham? The merits of Abraham? The merits of every dispensation? Abraham is typical, you see. How does each dispensation end? We're dealing with this dispensation thing. By 6:00 p.m. on the Friday, the eleventh, have something written on this subject to hand in so we'll know how to give you some kind of grade, which is absurd.
